item.title: A 27-year-old woman, 27 million viewers and a thousand-year-old ceremony - this is why Queen Elizabeth II’s Coronation in 1953 was the most famous royal event in history
item.dc:creator: Laura Silverman
item.description: Queen Elizabeth II was crowned in 1953 in a ceremony of pomp and splendour. We tell the story of this momentous day in her life
item.content:encoded:
The coronation of Queen Elizabeth II was the most British of days: a day of glory and spectacle, steeped in tradition and uniting the Commonwealth. And it rained.

The day was 2 June 1953, the new Queen Elizabeth II had acceded to the throne on 6 February 1952, when her father, King George VI, passed away. The coronation, following a period of mourning and meticulous preparation, marked the occasion. It was to be a day of celebration and solemnity, the centrepiece a ceremony that had remained largely the same for 1,000 years.

There have been 39 coronations held at Westminster Abbey since William the Conqueror's in 1066, and the bones of the ceremony remain little changed in 1000 years. If you'd like to know more about the ceremony itself, I'd recommend Ian Lloyd's brilliant The Throne: 1,000 Years of British Coronations, which is available on Amazon and details every little bit of history about this ultimate British display of pomp and circumstance.

Queen Elizabeth II's Coronation

How old was the queen when she was crowned?

The Queen was 27 at the time. But she had been thinking about the day since she was 11. In May 1937, her father, King George VI, had asked her to record his coronation at Westminster Abbey so that she would appreciate it. “I thought it all very, very wonderful,” she wrote. “The arches and the beams at the top were covered in a sort of haze of wonder as Papa was crowned, or at least I thought so.” Then an admission. The service became “rather boring” towards the end because “it was all prayers”.

The young Queen was unlikely to feel bored when her turn came around, but rather a heady mix of excitement, pride and, perhaps, a little trepidation – not that she showed it. Lady Rosemary Spencer-Churchill, one of her Maids of Honour on the day, recalls her appearing “very confident”. Speaking on BBC Radio 4 in 2013, Lady Rosemary said: “She made us feel very confident too. The minute she arrived, everyone just felt everything was going to be all right.”

Preparations for the day begin

Hundreds of people camped on the pavements the night before in the cold and wet, so that they could catch a glimpse of their new Queen the next day. “The Queen’s subjects remained impressively British in their patient, companionable good nature,” wrote The New York Times. As her subjects waited for the Gold State Coach, wending its way from Buckingham Palace to the Abbey, they sang ‘Singing in the Rain’ and ‘It Ain’t Gonna Rain No More’ with hope and joy at a new Elizabethan era. The crowds would grow on Coronation Day to number three million.

The Abbey, the venue of every coronation since 1066, opened at 6 am to about 8,000 guests. Members of the Royal Family, peers and heads of state from across the world glided in after 8.30 am. Then, at 11.15, came the Queen with a procession of 250 people to verses from Psalm 22.

What did Queen Elizabeth II wear for her Coronation?

She wore a gown and a robe of white satin, made by Norman Hartnell, the fashion designer who had also designed her wedding dress. It was embroidered in gold and silver thread with Commonwealth motifs, from the English rose to Canadian maple leaves. The dress is on display as part of the new exhibition Queen Elizabeth II: Her Life in Style this year.

As glorious as the Queen looked, the gown and robes were so heavy that they got caught in the thick gold and blue carpet as she walked. “At one moment, I was going against the pile of the carpet, and I couldn’t move at all,” she said in a BBC documentary in 2018.

What was so special about Queen Elizabeth II's Coronation?

The Coronation was the first to be televised – and the first event many saw on television. About 27 million people tuned in across the UK, with millions more around the world. About 11 million people tuned in on the radio.

What happens at the Coronation of a British monarch?

The three-hour service, conducted by the Archbishop of Canterbury, comprised six parts: the Recognition, the Oath, the Anointing, the Investiture (including the Crowning), the Enthronement and the Homage.

Every object was rich in significance. The Queen was crowned in St Edward’s Chair, made in 1300 for Edward I and used in every coronation since. During the ceremony, the Queen was handed the four symbols of authority: the Orb, the Sceptre, the Rod of Mercy and the Sovereign’s Ring. The Orb, a globe of gold surrounded by a cross with diamonds and emeralds, shone in the light. The Ring, also known as The Wedding Ring of England, glittered with sapphire and rubies.

At the moment of coronation, the Archbishop placed the St Edward’s Crown, one of the most iconic symbols of royalty, on the Queen’s head. Dating back to 1661, it is made of solid gold and encrusted with 440 precious and semi-precious stones. A cry of “God save the Queen” went up, and gun salutes were fired as crowds outside cheered.

What happened after Queen Elizabeth II's Coronation ceremony?

The Queen held a coronation luncheon for 11 in the Abbey annex, where she and the Duke of Edinburgh ate smoked salmon, sausage rolls and chicken sandwiches. A banquet was held for guests, many from abroad, in the Great Hall of Westminster School, next door to the Abbey. The star dish was coronation chicken, a recipe created for the occasion.

For many people outside, the royal procession back from the Abbey to the Palace meant almost as much as the ceremony. At 7.2km, the route was designed to be seen by as many people as possible and took the participants, in their thousands, two hours. The Queen looked resplendent in her Purple Robe of Estate, embroidered with a cipher of the Queen and a border of olive branches and wheat ears, representing peace and plenty.

The Imperial State Crown

Now she wore the Imperial State Crown, a crown she had also worn at the end of the ceremony. It featured her favourite jewel, the Black Prince’s Ruby, worn by Henry V at the Battle of Agincourt. Both this crown, which the Queen later wore on State Openings of Parliament, and the St Edward’s Crown, were heavy, and despite her grace in the carriage, she endured a feat of strength. As she pointed out in the BBC documentary, “You can’t lean down to read your speech [wearing the crown]... Because if you did, your neck would break, and it would fall off.” To protect her posture, she practised wearing it beforehand. Lady Anne Glenconner, another Maid of Honour, recalled: “She used to wear it when she was writing letters. I think Prince Charles says he remembers going in and seeing her wearing it.”

In 2019, Lady Anne Glenconner published Lady in Waiting, which is available on Amazon. As a Maid of Honour at the Queen's Coronation and former Lady in Waiting to Princess Margaret, this is a fascinating look into behind the scenes of royal life.

The Gold State Coach

On the day, the Queen had the added challenge of wearing the crown in a bumpy carriage - the Gold State Coach- a ride she described as “horrible”. She said, “It’s only sprung on leather, not very comfortable.” Just as she found the crown weighty, the horses had similar troubles with the four-ton coach. “We could only go at a walking pace,” said the Queen. “The horses couldn’t possibly go any faster.”

Observers cheered men from the Royal Navy, the Royal Air Force and the Army in the parade, as well as waving to figureheads from across the Commonwealth. But it was Queen Salote of Tonga who captured their hearts, refusing to put the roof up on her carriage, despite the British rain.

The evening celebrations

That evening, for the finale, the new Queen stood on the balcony of Buckingham Palace and turned on a show of lights along the Mall. She wore a silver evening gown, an ermine stole and a diamond diadem, and smiled at the thousands of people looking back at her. Beside her stood her husband, the Duke of Edinburgh, in a dinner jacket. At 10.30 pm, fireworks lit up the sky. “Portraits etched in lines of fire depicted the Queen, the Duke of Edinburgh and their two children, Prince Charles, Duke of Cornwall, and Princess Anne,” recorded The New York Times. “Each display brought a surge of cheering and applause.”

It was a fitting end to a remarkable day. Recalling her coronation, the Queen later said: “It’s the…beginning of one’s life, really, as a sovereign.” What a life it was.

Many people there on the day have managed to keep and preserve the Queen Elizabeth II Coronation Day book, made to commemorate the day in 1953, and there are copies available to buy on Amazon. As we mark 100 years since she was born, there have been several new biographies of Queen Elizabeth II in the last year as well. The world's most knowledgeable royal biographer, Hugo Vickers, has just released Queen Elizabeth II, which is also available on Amazon, and is a must-read this anniversary year.

This article was first published on DiscoverBritain.com

item.title: 19 kitchen splashback ideas to steal for a bold and beautiful kitchen update
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: Keep your kitchen walls safe from splashes while giving the whole room a lift with these practical yet stylish design ideas.
item.content:encoded:
The secret to a successful kitchen refresh is often found in the vertical plane, which is why exploring new kitchen splashback ideas is the perfect place to start.

A well-chosen backdrop does more than just protect your walls from the rigours of a Sunday roast; it acts as a visual anchor. These surfaces are the ideal canvas for your most creative kitchen ideas.

From luxury marble to the artisanal charm of hand-painted ceramics, here we explore the best ways to finish your space. To ensure your new focal point stays pristine, we recommend investing in a high-quality brush like this tile scrubber from Amazon.

1. Choose a timeless marble slab

For an unapologetically luxurious look, a solid marble splashback is peerless, offering a seamless expanse of natural beauty that tiles often cannot match. 'Marble splashbacks can be a very striking feature within the kitchen and elevate more pared-back designs, especially using a Calacatta marble with strong veining,' explains Isabella Pacelli, junior sales designer at luxury kitchen brand Roundhouse.

Lee Thornley, founder of tile brand Bert & May, adds that 'a marble splashback is an easy way to add a bit of interest and pattern to your kitchen. The variation in tone and natural veining gives the tiles a considered feel without having to worry too much about uniformity. It’s also a practical choice, offering a durable, easy-to-clean surface that works well in everyday use.'

While it is undeniably elegant, it does require a bif og love to stay looking its best. 'As long as you take care of your marble splashback by wiping it down after cooking and maintaining cleanliness, it will remain beautiful and not stain; however, for some, this is too high maintenance for everyday life,' Pacelli notes. Purchasing a purpose-made cleaner like this Method granite and marble spray from Amazon will keep it looking pristine.

2. Tile an alcove behind the cooker

If your kitchen features a chimney breast or a recessed cooking area, use it to your advantage by tiling the entire internal alcove. This creates a dedicated cooking zone that feels separate and specialised, almost like a hearth in an old English manor. By wrapping the tiles around the sides of the alcove as well as the back wall, you create a cocoon of colour and texture that makes the range cooker feel built in.

3. Install reflective mirrored tiles

Mirrored tiles offer a rhythmic, decorative approach to reflecting light. Whether you choose bevelled-edge shapes or small mosaics, mirrored tiles break up reflections into shimmering fragments, creating a jewellery box effect in the kitchen.

4. Decorate with authentic Delft tiles

Bring a sense of heritage and storytelling into your home by opting for hand-painted Delft tiles, which provide a quintessentially English country aesthetic. 'We often think of the splashback as a starting point for the palette, particularly with hand-painted or artisanal tiles, which can bring such charm and character,' says Sophie Pringle, co-founder of Surrey-based interior design studio Pringle & Pringle.

These iconic blue-and-white ceramics offer a charming, artisanal feel that pairs beautifully with classic Shaker cabinetry and wooden worktops, and each tile acts as a miniature work of art. 'The classic blue and white palette also tends to reflect light, helping to keep the kitchen bright while creating a backdrop that feels timeless,' adds Carly Allison, head of brand and product at tile brand Fired Earth.

5. Extend your worktop up the wall

Creating a wrap-around effect by using the same material for your worktop and splashback gives the space a streamlined, contemporary look. Whether it's a rugged, honed granite or a sleek modern composite, continuing the surface vertically eliminates the visual noise created by changing materials as the eyeline.

6. Create a focal point with a tiled motif

For a truly bespoke and whimsical touch, consider incorporating a figurative tiled motif into your design, much like the beautifully detailed heron seen in this scheme by interior designer Laura Stephens. By setting a specific illustration within a larger expanse of plain tiling, you treat the splashback as a piece of art. It's a lovely way to celebrate local wildlife or personal interests, ensuring your kitchen feels totally personal and full of character.

7. Opt for tactile zellige tiles

Moroccan zellige tiles have become a staple in many English homes because they feel far more personal than a standard machine-made tile. These are made by hand from clay, meaning every piece has a slightly different thickness, unique glaze tone, or perhaps even a welcome chipped edge.

When you see them on a wall, they create a shimmering, slightly uneven surface that catches the light in a way that feels organic rather than clinical. Award-winning interior designer Laura Stephens is a fan, noting that Zellige tiles 'add texture and interest without feeling too busy.'

8. Take tiles to the ceiling

Don't feel restricted by the standard height of your wall cabinets; extending your tiles all the way to the cornice can radically change the proportions of the room. 'Taking tiles higher up the wall, even to the ceiling, is also a lovely way to create a sense of presence, especially with more textural finishes like zellige, which add depth without overwhelming the room,' says Pringle.

9. Coordinate colours with patterned tiles

If you decide to go for something more intricate or Mediterranean-inspired, remember that careful colour coordination is what separates a professional design from a disjointed one. 'I’ve always loved using patterned tiles as they instantly create a focal point, especially behind a range,' says Stephens. 'But it’s all about balance, so if you’re going bolder, I’d keep the cabinetry a little quieter.'

By picking out a specific pigment from the tile and repeating it on your cabinetry or kitchen island, you create a visual thread that ties the entire scheme together perfectly.

10. Colour drench for a cohesive look

For a kitchen that feels quietly impactful and serene, consider matching your splashback tones exactly to your surrounding walls and woodwork. 'To keep the look cohesive, I like to colour-drench the cabinetry and walls in the same shade, sometimes introducing a limewash finish for added depth and texture,' notes Carina Raymond, founder of London-based interior design firm Studio Raymond.

By choosing a splashback in the same hue - perhaps a matte porcelain or a back-painted glass - you create a sophisticated monochromatic backdrop that allows your hardware, like unlacquered brass taps, to truly take centre stage.

11. Introduce warmth with copper sheets

For a glow that softens a kitchen's harder edges of stone and steel, a bespoke copper splashback is a wonderful choice. Copper brings a warmth to the room, reflecting light with a rosy hue that is particularly beautiful in the evening under low-level task lighting.

Over time, the metal will develop a natural patina, shifting through various bronze tones, or it can be polished regularly to maintain its original fiery shine for a more industrial-glmaour aesthetic that looks particularly beautiful paired with deep blue cabinetry.

12. Select a bold herringbone pattern

Standard-shaped tiles feel instantly more bespoke when laid in a classic herringbone formation. This simple change in orientation adds a sense of movement and energy to the wall, breaking up the horizontal lines of the cabinetry.

To make the most of this look, use a slightly contrasting grout to highlight the geometric precision of the layout, or choose a tile with a handmade, tumbled edge to give the traditional pattern a more relaxed feel that suits a country kitchen.

13. Mix and match different tiles

If you can't decide on just one style, why not embrace a curated, mismatched aesthetic? Integrating a few patterned hero tiles into a larger field of plain white ones creates an eclectic, bohemian feel that is full of charm. You could scatter them randomly for a relaxed look or cluster them in a specific area to create a patchwork-style focal point.

14. Use tongue-and-groove panelling for country charm

Nothing says farmhouse style quite like vertical wood panelling, which offers a soft, tactile alternative to the coldness of ceramic tiles. Painted in a durable, wipeable eggshell finish - perhaps a soft sage, deep navy or classic cream - tongue-and-groove boards add a verticality that can make low ceilings feel higher. It's a lovely way to inject a sense of cosy, rustic warmth into a kitchen. and it serves as the perfect partner for a classic AGA, a farmhouse sink and open shelving.

15. Embrace the drama of antiqued mirror panels

If tiles feel too fussy, a large-scale antiqued mirror panel is the ultimate way to add a touch of vintage glamour. Unlike a clear mirror, the foxed or 'mercury' finish adds a layer of depth and mystery, softening the reflection so you aren't staring at a crystal-clear image of yourself while cooking. It's an excellent way to make a narrow galley kitchen feel twice as wide while adding a boutique hotel finish to the heart of the home.

16. Go all-white

There is something so refreshing about an all-white scheme, where white tiles are paired with crisp white walls. To prevent the look from feeling too lab-like. play with different finishes; imagine a glossy, white-tiled splashback set against a matte-painted wall. This subtle shift in sheen levels creates a sophisticated, layered effect that feels bright and airy.

17. Lay tiles vertically for a modern twist

Try rotating your tile layout by 90 degrees. Laying tiles vertically - often called stack bond - draws the eye upwards, which is a clever trick for making small kitchens feel bigger. It offers a clean and contemporary aesthetic that feels a little more architectural than the standard horizontal layout.

For a softer look, choose tiles with a slight tonal variation, or keep it strictly minimalistic with a crisp white tile and matching grout.

18. Create a classic checkerboard effect

Checkerboard patterns aren't just for floors; they make a fantastic, high-contrast splashback that feels both traditional and playful. While the classic black and white is undeniably bold, you can achieve a more subtle English look by using two tones that are closer in value - think a soft cream paired with a sage green or pale terracotta.

This geometric layout adds a sense of order and rhythm with a little bit of modern charm, acting as a decorative ribbon that runs along your worktop and injects instant personality.

19. Match your splashback to your kitchen island

For a design that feels truly anchored, try mirroring the material or colour of your kitchen island on your splashback. If you have a kitchen island in a deep navy or a dramatic veined stone, using that same finish behind the hob or sink creates a visual link between the two main focal points of the kitchen.

It's a clever way to pull a large, open-plan space together, ensuring the working part of the kitchen ties in with the island's social hub.

The best choice of splashback for your home is one that balances your personal style with the practicalities of daily life - whether you are leaning towards the grandeur of marble or the charm of hand-painted tiles. Taking the time to research how to choose tiles will also ensure your kitchen remains a joy for years to come.

Once the installation is complete, you can turn your attention to the smaller details; if you've gone for a stone finish, consider echoing the material with these marble salt and pepper shakers from H&M to sit on your table or shelves.

item.title: 17 hallway paint ideas designers swear by for a stunning first impression
item.dc:creator: Charlotte Dunford
item.description: Reinvent your entrance with a sophisticated palette that creates a seamless, intentional transition from the front door to the heart of the home.
item.content:encoded:
Finding the right hallway paint ideas is the perfect way to make sure the first area your guests see feels both considered and wonderfully curated.

These versatile hallway ideas balance the unique architectural demands of a busy transitional space with that timeless, effortless elegance we love in English interior design.

Here we explore 22 inspiring ways to reinvent your entrance with colour, but before you dive into the pigment, don't forget to invest in a good brush like this cutting-in brush from Amazon – it is the best way to ensure those sharp lines between your walls and woodwork look professional.

1. Soften the welcome with neutrals

While crisp whites and cool greys have long been the default for brightening darker hallways, there is a notable shift toward warmer, more grounded neutrals. These shades, like oatmeal, biscuit and soft taupe, offer the airy quality of a traditional neutral but carry subtle yellow or red undertones. This prevents the space from feeling clinical in the low light often found in central corridors.

Shades like Farrow & Ball's Jitney or Little Greene's Rolling Fog provide a gentle glow that pairs beautifully with natural materials like sisal rugs and aged oak flooring.

'In hallways, a soft, neutral wall tone creates a timeless foundation. Then, you can introduce personality through bold artwork, a vase of flowers, or a patterned runner,' says London-based interior designer Rebecca Hughes. 'The goal is harmony – a space that feels curated yet comfortable, polished yet welcoming – setting a refined tone for the rest of the home.'

2. Create a seamless look with colour drenching

For those looking to make more of a statement, the technique of colour drenching has moved from a niche design choice to a contemporary classic. This involves enveloping the walls, woodwork, radiators and even the ceiling in a single, cohesive hue.

Carina Raymond, founder of London-based interior design firm Studio Raymond, notes that the transitional nature of the space makes it the perfect place for such a bold move, suggesting that hallways are an opportunity to be a bit braver with design precisely because they aren't rooms we linger in for hours. 'I love to fully wallpaper a hallway or colour drench it from skirting to ceiling,' she says, 'as it creates an immediate sense of atmosphere and makes the space feel intentional rather than overlooked.'

3. Opt for darker colours for a cosy hallway

While many of us may avoid dark colours in narrower rooms such as hallways, deeper tones can be effective in creating a space that feels welcoming and full of personality. 'Darker shades can work really well in functional areas of your home, and can create an earthy and dramatic entrance,' says Cathryn Sanders, head of creative at Earthborn Paints.

'If this is too bold for your taste, consider painting the lower half of the wall in a dark shade and pair this with a lighter shade on the upper half – this can also heighten the look of your ceiling, making it appear more spacious.' When it comes to the best dark paint colours for a hallway, jewel-toned hues such as emeralds and burgundies are particularly effective in bringing a richness to interiors.

As Sophie Pringle, co-founder of Surrey-based interior design studio Pringle & Pringle, also highlights, leaning into these depths can actually enhance the architectural joinery of the home. 'Darker blues work brilliantly in hallways or snugs, particularly where natural light is limited,' she says. 'Leaning into deeper tones in these spaces can feel intentional and cocooning, especially when they lead into lighter, more open rooms - the contrast exaggerates the play of light and makes both spaces feel stronger.'

4. Define period features with two-tone walls

Transient spaces, hallways can benefit from bright or intense accent colours to bring a smile as you walk through each day. Bold colours can work particularly well for highlighting architectural or period features. 'For a strong scheme that showcases architectural detailing, use contrasting colours across the walls, panelling and woodwork to highlight these structural details,' says Andy Greenall, creative director at Paint & Paper Library.

5. Elevate the look with a painted ceiling

The ceiling itself is a powerful tool for hallway design. In spaces with high ceilings, painting the 'lid' in a soft, sun-drenched terracotta or a pale yellow can mimic the warmth of the evening light. This prevents a tall, narrow space from feeling like a chimney and brings the proportions down a little. Or, pulling a colour from an adjacent room's wallpaper or all-over colour drenching the space creates a visual thread that ties the ground floor together.

6. Add decorative touches to hallway doorframes

If you’re more of an experienced painter, hallways offer the opportunity to be a little more experimental and decorative. One of Annie Sloan, founder of the eponymous paint brand’s, top hallway paint ideas is taking inspiration from ancient Greek and Roman architecture. 'A playful take on grand architectural detail, painted pediments are particularly effective in properties which have had their period features stripped away – but this idea would also look brilliantly bonkers in new builds, which I’m all for!'

7. Create a painted statement hallway with painted woodwork

Hallways offer numerous areas to experiment with paint, from walls to ceilings to floors to woodwork. One of Laura Jackson's, style consultant to COAT, favourite hallway paint ideas is to add a pop of colour on bannisters and spindles. This can contrast beautifully against neutral walls and is an effective option for small hallways. 'By painting the spindles on the stairs and the steps, it elongates the stairway, drawing your eyes up. The drama of this bold colour against the neutral makes a real statement but in a really elegant way,' explains Jackson.

8. Use contrasting paint finishes

If you’re looking for hallway paint ideas that create interest without introducing too many different colours, try using varying paint finishes on painted walls and surfaces. 'One colour may sound slightly underwhelming, but used in two different finishes, you will get textural changes and light that will respond wonderfully,' explains Patrick O’Donnell, international brand ambassador at Farrow & Ball. The resulting look is beautifully simple and elegant.

9. Paint your hallway in colours that uplift and inspire

One of the most important things to consider when selecting paint for a hallway is how it’ll make you feel. 'Hallways are a passing point, so it is important to create an environment that makes us feel happy,' explains Francesca Wezel, founder of Francesca’s Paints. 'I always advise people to choose their favourite colours – shades and tones that make them feel comfortable. I don’t believe in colour rules; I believe in choosing whatever colours make you feel good. For some, this might be an acid yellow, whereas for others it’s a gentle pink.'

10. Play with colour juxtaposition

When designing a hallway, it’s essential to consider not only the hall itself but what you can see through open doors into the rooms that come off of it. While coordinated colour palettes can look polished and chic, contrasting shades bring a touch of playfulness to interiors. 'Colour juxtaposition works brilliantly in a hallway to create a sense of drama and depth – reds and pinks through to greens and blues, for example,' says Betsy Smith, colour consultant to Graphenstone.

11. Bring the outside in

'Hallways are the literal transition from outdoors to indoors, so they are a wonderful space for extending the beauty and atmosphere of the natural environment,' explains Ruth Mottershead, creative director at Little Greene. Shades of green are a great choice of paint for hallways. From dark forest to fresh fern, these colours will bring the outside world in and create a calm, tranquil space. For a cocooning effect, apply the same shade to the ceiling, woodwork, and doors.

12. Create a calming hallway with softer shades

When it comes to hallways, many experts advise choosing a shade that brings joy and provides a retreat from the busy world outside as soon as you step through the door. 'It should be a colour which is a comfort to come home to,' explains Dominic Myland, CEO of Mylands. Gentle yet warming shades such as lavender or sage are an optimum choice, 'these subtle shades will offer a welcome sense of calm and tranquillity upon entering the space.'

13. Opt for lighter shades to make narrow hallways look bigger

If you’re looking for small hallway ideas to create the illusion of a bigger space, opt for lighter paint shades on doors, ceilings, and walls. 'Decide at the start whether you want to try to make your hallway look lighter and brighter, or if you want to go for a dark and dramatic scheme. The lighter the colour, the more it reflects available light – the darker the colour, the more it will absorb the light. So if you want to make a hallway seem larger, you will need to go for lighter shades,' explains Justyna Korczynska, senior designer at Crown.

14. Use bright tones for an inviting hallway

Helen Shaw, UK marketing director at Benjamin Moore, suggests one of the best paint colours for hallways is a warmer neutral or white. 'It’s important to remember your hallway connects to each room, so the colour chosen should feel harmonious with the rest of the house. With this in mind, opting for a warmer white is highly effective, especially when paired with colourful accents on furniture or paintwork to add personality and charm.'

15. Use vertical stripes for added height

For hallways with low ceilings, use paint to create subtle vertical stripes in alternating finishes of the same or complementary colours. This Regency stripe effect adds a layer of texture and the illusion of height without the busyness of wallpaper. To ensure you get perfectly crisp lines, it's worth investing in something like this Frog Tape from Amazon for a professional finish.

16. Frame the space with a painted border

A highly effective way to add architectural interest to a hallway at a low cost is by painting a border. This can take many forms: a thin, contrasting line painted just below the ceiling to mimic a cornice, or a wider band of colour that wraps around the room at picture rail height. By using a vibrant hue like a rich ochre or an electric blue against a more neutral wall, you create a graphic element that leads the eye through the space.

17. Upcycle furniture with a statement hue

If you aren't quite ready to commit to painting the walls, why not introduce a splash of colour by upcycling your hallway furniture? Painting a console table or a radiator cover in a bold, statement colour like a royal blue or a striking emerald green can act as a lovely anchor to the space.

Not only does this give a tiered piece of furniture a second life, but it also creates a deliberate focal point that draws the eye as soon as you step through the door. For decent furniture paint, we recommend Annie Sloan's range. This olive chalk paint is available from Amazon and would look beautiful against a neutral wall.

Choosing the right paint ideas for your hallway ultimately comes down to the atmosphere you wish to evoke the moment you turn the key.

To complete the look, consider adding a sculptural lamp like this ceramic column table lamp from M&S to your console; the warm glow will beautifully highlight your chosen pigments and ensure your newly painted hallway feels like home.

item.title: This is the one garden buy everyone will be talking about: OKA’s new parasol is basically outdoor art – and the detail underneath is what really sets it apart
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: I’m trading grey skies for marine blue ikats with the new OKA discovery that has completely transformed my outdoor space.
item.content:encoded:
The sun is finally out, and in true British fashion, I'm already searching for shae - which is how I just spotted this beautiful new OKA parasol.

After such a long, wet winter, I've been trying to beautify my garden. This is easily one of the best parasols for a quick patio glow-up.

I'm already picturing it paired with this bistro set from Amazon for the perfect al fresco coffee spot. Here is why this new arrival is the ultimate shortcut to a high-end outdoor retreat.

OKA Hanway Garden Parasol

This brand-new parasol from OKA is a Mediterranean-inspired masterpiece. Crafted in Italy, it features a generous 300cm canopy and a unique rattan-clad pole. While the topside is a classic, understated plain, the real magic happens when you step underneath; it reveals a stunning, intricate Bhukara ikat print in marine blue that completely changes the feel of the space.

RRP£995
Shop now

What really wins it for me is that stunning underside; while the top is a classic, simple cream, the interior reveals a beautiful ikat print in a kaleidoscope of green-blue, red and pale yellow. It's so intricate that it feels like looking up at a piece of art while you're lounging. I also love that the design is as practical as it is pretty, with four height settings to help you track the sun.

I'm styling mine to create a Mediterranean escape in my own Cotswolds back garden, layering in weather-resistant textiles, like this block-print outdoor cushion from OKA, and some oversized terracotta pots. I think that the earthy orange of the clay will be a lovely contrast against that lovely marine blue pattern.

Though it definitley gives off a more exotic vibe, it is perfectly suited for gardens on home soil. Charlotte Dunford, The Englsih Home's houses editor, has spotted how well this design translates to a classic setting. 'Oka’s new Hanway parasol is a wonderful choice for an English country garden; I’m particularly impressed with its sophisticated, yet decorative feel,' she says.

'The generous, plain canopy provides ample shade, be it from sun or rain, while the contrast of the patterned underside adds an unexpected, playful twist that’s sure to bring a touch of style to any garden.'

Just keep in mind, this particular parasol doesn't come with a base, though it is pictured, so you'll want to pick up a sturdy one to keep this generous parasol secure come rain or shine. Something like this Charles Bentley base from Amazon should do the trick.

I know this particular model is on the pricier side, but it is truly a forever piece for the garden. If you want to capture a similar boutique hotel look for a little less though, here are some of my favourite affordable alternatives:

Habitat x Scion Garden Parasol

RRP£30
Available from Argos
Shop now

Neptune Classic 3m Parasol

RRP£325
Available from Neptune
Shop now

Cox & Cox Floral Fringed Parasol

RRP£125
Available from Cox & Cox
Shop now

Whether you're investing in a forever piece like the OKA Hanaway or opting for one of my hand-selected highstreet gems, the right shade to accompany your garden furniture is the foundation of any outdoor retreat.

For the ultimate golden-hour glow, without any messy wires, opt for wireless lighting, like this rechargeable lamp from Amazon. It's the perfect way to keep the conversation going well after the sun has set.

item.title: From coastal colours to themed wall decorations - how living by the sea inspired the decoration of this family home
item.dc:creator: Charlotte Dunford
item.description: By adding playful elements and nods to its coastal setting, Megan McCluskie has transformed this Edwardian house into a vibrant and welcoming family home
item.content:encoded:
Megan McCluskie’s theory that ‘it’s the little things that make a house a home’ is clear to see in the early 20th-century property she shares with her husband, Gareth Williams, and their three children, Lachlan, Laurie and Lana.

It is this ethos, of choosing personal and nostalgic over prescriptive pieces, that means her aesthetic doesn’t fall neatly into any one of the many established interior design styles.

Instead, from the ground floor to the third up in the eaves, in every nook and cranny, the interiors are an ode to the family - a reflection of their personalities, interests and new-found love of living by the sea.

‘We lived in London and we always loved day trips to the beach. I'd never heard of Folkestone until a colleague mentioned it, so we came for the day and we loved it,' recalls Megan. 'We had no intention of moving and were really happy where we were, but there was just something about Folkestone and living by the sea that we couldn't resist.'

Despite not having been looking for a new home at the time, the couple started house hunting. They had set their hearts on a property in a different area of the coastal town, but it fell through and they were left feeling deflated. 'I was in two minds about leaving London, but we were coming to do a viewing and, honestly, we came to see this house out of boredom,' adds Megan.

Having seen the property, Megan and Gareth left and walked to the end of what would soon become their new road. 'There is a little bench there where you can sit and look out to sea. We just sat there and thought, "this is it, that's the one",' recalls Megan.

They were won over, in part, by the house's elegant Edwardian architecture and features. Original fireplaces had survived in the playroom and living room, and the beautiful coving and wooden floors were in great condition.

Another appealing factor was the minimal work the house required. 'We didn't have to do anything majorly structural,' Megan says. 'We did rewire and that was the very first thing we did. It was the sort of house where there was one plug socket in every room and it wasn't functional for our kind of modern life. One thing I slightly regret is how quickly we did that, I think you need to live in the house a bit longer.'

Further to rewiring, the couple updated the bathrooms and kitchen, as well as knocking through the kitchen into a redundant lean-to which now houses the dining area. They also addressed the 'more cosmetic side of things', taking up the existing carpet and removing wallpaper before replastering.

When searching for inspiration for redecorating, Megan was led by her new surroundings. 'Waking up every morning and seeing the sea makes you feel very lucky. I've become quite addicted. In fact, my son and I are often down there shell hunting at 6am. We've definitely become quite seaside people,' she laughs.

The palette of her home undeniably reflects the Channel colours. Shades of blue and green flow through the house, from soothing green-grey walls in the living room to the fresh blue-and-white scheme in the family bathroom.

However, there is one area where Megan has been slightly more restrained. 'With the kids' rooms, I didn't want to go too bold. Most of the bedrooms are white with coloured woodwork so that they can add their stamp to them as they grow,' she explains.

Indeed, Megan's children were front of mind in many ways throughout the redecoration. 'It's very much a family home, so I've tried to add a little bit of fun and make it a bit playful. I try to make sure everyone is reflected because it's everybody's house,' she says. Small tributes to her children are cleverly woven into the interiors, such as the frequent banana motifs to honour Lana's endearing nickname, bugs and shells to reflect Lachlan's interests, and sea creatures for Laurie.

It is a home filled with personality, something Megan has achieved through hours of searching for the perfect pieces. 'We moved from a two-bedroom flat, so we did have to get a lot of things, mostly preloved and antique,' she says. While a number of antiques came with them, Megan has since also sourced several more treasures. 'I went a bit mad. The thrill of finding something gets too addictive!'

Among the larger items, such as the furniture in the main bedroom, found in an antique store in Cornwall, there is an eclectic mix of wall decorations which bring colour and charm - from decorative sconces and plates to paintings and posters. 'My favourite things are probably mirrors. I'm averaging two or three per room - it's my weakness,' Megan admits.

One of her frequent ports of call is Instagram. There are so many amazing sellers on there. Some of my favourites are Wallis Vintage, Kept London, Shiny Things London, and there's an antiques dealer called The Antik Store, who's actually since become a friend in Folkestone.'

Many of these treasures have found a spot in Megan's own sanctuary. 'In our bedroom, we have a little en-suite bathroom, which is sort of just for me. I'm definitely a bath person, so I'm very grateful for that little space,' she says. Yet, her most loved place to spend time is hidden away at the top of the house.

'Lana's bedroom is one of my most favourite rooms. There's something about that feeling of being tucked up in the eaves, especially when you live by the sea and the wind is crazy. We only have a little sea glimpse from the house, but we can see it from up there and we love to watch the ships go across the Channel together,' smiles Megan.

There is an overwhelming sense of warmth in this Edwardian house. Walking around you get a true sense of the family at its heart, thanks to the lived-in interiors, each layered with an array of personalised and considered touches. For those looking to bring character to their own family homes, there is plenty of inspiration to be found, especially children's bedroom ideas to create a joyful and uplifting space where imaginations can run wild.

item.title: 18 Beautiful utility room ideas that actually work – finally get organised without wasting time or money
item.dc:creator: Molly Malsom
item.description: From calming greens to busy wallpapers and natural materials, these utility room ideas will add function and beauty.
item.content:encoded:
The secret to a serene home often lies tucked away behind the laundry door, where well-considered utility room ideas can turn the relentless cycle of daily chores into a brief moment of domestic bliss.

As the true engine room of the British household, this space has to be a bit of a multitasker. Whether you're taming a sprawling country boot room or a compact urban cupboard, the magic is in the details.

Below, we explore 18 ways to transform your service space into a room you might actually want to linger in.

1. Add personality with pattern

Just because a utility room is a functional space doesn’t mean you can’t make it look as beautiful as the rest of your home. A patterned tile, wall or textile can completely transform the room – not only does it add ample personality, but it makes the room feel homely and more enjoyable to spend time in.

2. Create a calming utility room with green

A space for completing household chores, a utility room can often feel like a stressful part of your home. Choosing a calming colour palette will make the room feel more relaxing. A sage green shade is a great choice – not only will it create a peaceful haven, but it’s also a timeless colour you’ll never tire of.

3. Conceal essentials behind bespoke cabinetry

'A well-designed utility room should work as hard as the rest of the home, blending practicality with thoughtful storage solutions,' says Tom Howley, creative design director of the eponymous kitchen company. 'I always recommend maximising vertical space with full-height cabinetry.'

In many English homes, floor space is a luxury, but wall space is often a goldmine waiting to be tapped. By taking your cabinetry right up to the rafters, you can stow away those once-a-year items - think picnic baskets or the Christmas tableware - leaving the lower, easy access shelves for the stuff you reach for daily.

Utility rooms are home to a lot of necessary but noisy equipment, from the boiler to bulky vacuum cleaners. To maintain a sense of calm, opt for bespoke, full-height cabinets that can swallow these items whole. Tucking your appliances behind beautifully crafted doors ensures that the room feels like an extension of your living space.

4. Save space with a ceiling-mounted rack

The average clothes dryer takes up a lot of space and can make your utility feel like laundry chaos. Instead, opt for a rack hung from the ceiling – not only will it save space, but because heat rises, it will help to dry your clothes quicker.

With space at a premium, every element needed to work hard - the hanging clothes rack is a particularly useful addition, allowing for air-drying without taking up floor space, while also drawing the eye upward and making full use of the room’s height,' says Melissa Hutley, co-founder of London-based interior design studio Hutley & Humm, of her project, pictured above. 'Combined with open shelving, simple hooks and natural materials, the space balances practicality with a quiet, lived-in charm.'

Garden Trading Chalford Ceiling Dryer

RRP£135
Available from Garden Trading
Shop now

Mustard Made Hangers

RRP£26
Available from Mustard Made
Shop now

Garden Trading Chalford Wall Dryer

RRP£170
Available from Garden Trading
Shop now

5. Integrate wicker baskets for textural storage

If rigid, wall-to-wall cabinetry feels a little too showroom for your taste, introducing wicker baskets into your shelving is the perfect way to add soul and texture. They are brilliant for corralling mismatched cleaning cloths or pet supplies, and they bring a lived-in character that suits a period home perfectly. Using baskets on open shelving provides a charming, tactile alternative to heavy drawers and allows the room to breathe while keeping smaller items tidy.

The Cotswold Co. Antique Wash Tray

RRP£20
Available from The Cotswold Company
Shop now

Matalan Laundry Window Basket

RRP£8.50
Available from Matalan
Shop now

Habitat Seagrass Wave Storage Basket

RRP£18
Available from Habitat
Shop now

6. Embrace creamy neutrals

Neutral paint colours are a great choice for a utility room. Off-white, stone or tallow shades help a windowless utility feel light and airy. These tones provide a clean, crisp backdrop that makes it easier to spot stray items, and they provide a lovely canvas for a few well-placed houseplants to thrive.

7. Stack your appliances

In most utility rooms, you will likely find the washing machine and tumble dryer stacked on top of each other. In some cases, it’s done to fit both appliances in the space; there are also many practical advantages to this. Stacking them makes it incredibly easy to move laundry from one to the other, without knocking into open doors.

8. Embrace a patterned wallpaper

A busy patterned wallpaper may feel daunting in other rooms, but a utility room is the perfect space to experiment with unique schemes. A floral, garden-themed print paired with contrasting joinery creates a delightful jewel box effect that makes opening the utility door a genuine pleasure.

9. Invest in quality hardware

Hardware is the jewellery of the room. Swapping out basic plastic or chrome handles for solid brass cabinet pulls or hand-finished porcelain knobs can instantly elevate basic cabinetry, giving the entire space a bespoke, high-end feel. Match the finish to your taps or lighting to create a cohesive look.

10. Add a pop of colour

As a room that’s kept somewhat separate from the rest of your home, the utility is a great place to add that daring colour you’ve been dreaming of, such as a loud, zesty orange or a beautiful sunshine yellow.

11. Create interest with floor tiles

Utility rooms benefit from durable, easy-to-clean flooring thanks to their functional uses. This doesn’t mean you have to opt for something boring – a patterned floor tile is a fun option that ticks all the practical boxes.

12. Introduce industrial design features

Celebrate the room's hardworking nature by leaving certain elements exposed. Brass pipes, oversized Belfast sinks and industrial-style bulkhead lighting add a refined, utilitarian look. These materials are incredibly durable and only look better with age, developing a beautiful patina that tells the story of a busy, happy home.

13. Organise with glass jars

Plastic detergent bottles are rarely a design triumph. Decanting your powders, pods, and wooden pegs into large glass storage jars removes visual clutter instantly. Not only does this look beautiful on open shelving, but it also allows you to see at a glance when you are running low on supplies - no more mid-wash realisations that the powder has run out. Or, you could invest in some premium supplies like Daylesford's new laundry collection, which comes already beautifully packaged, see below.

The Laundry Co Clear Bottle

RRP£8
Available from Matalan
Shop now

Daylesford Natural Laundry Starter Kit

RRP£60
Available from Daylesford
Shop now

Garden Trading Washing Tablet Box

RRP£20
Available from Garden Trading
Shop now

14. Install a dedicated folding station

If space allows, a clear stretch of worktop specifically for folding clothes is a total game-changer. Using high-quality oak or marble worktops ensures the surface is smooth enough for delicate fabrics while adding a luxurious finish. It also provides a versatile spot for other tasks, such as arranging flowers or sorting through the morning post.

15. Add character with tongue-and-groove panelling

For a classic boot room aesthetic, tongue-and-groove panelling is unmatched. It offers a tactile, heritage feel that protects the walls from the rigours of a busy household.

'This utility and boot room was designed to feel cohesive, calm and quietly characterful,' notes Sophie Pringle, co-founder of Surrey-based interior design studio Pringle & Pringle, of her project, pictured above. 'We used a rich teal across the joinery and continued it into the tongue-and-groove panelling, running it high up the walls to draw the eye upwards and give the space a sense of height. Repeating the colour across both areas helps to unify the spaces.'

16. Create a cosy built-in dog bed

Integrating a pet station into your joinery ensures the utility room remains an organised hub. By concealing a dog bed within the cabinetry, you provide your pet with a secure, quiet retreat while maintaining a clean aesthetic for the room.

'This utility room was designed to make everyday tasks feel uplifting rather than mundane,' says award-winning interior designer Laura Stephens of her project, pictured above. 'A discreet cupboard provides essential storage, while a gathered fabric curtain conceals a built-in dog bed, creating a charming, comfortable nook without compromising floor space.'

Dusk Striped Pillow Dog Bed

RRP£30
Available from Dusk
Shop now

Wovenhill Rattan Oval Pet Bed

RRP£87
Available from Wovenhill
Shop now

Barbour Snuggle Dog Bed

RRP£105
Available from Barbour
Shop now

17. Install a dedicated dog wash station

For country homes, particularly, a raised dog wash station is a transformative addition. Tiling the area with waterproof tiles creates a splash-proof zone. A dedicated hand-held shower attachment allows you to wash off mud and grit before your pet enters the rest of the house, keeping your carpets pristine and your dog comfortable after a soggy walk.

18. Soften the look with a fabric sink skirt

If your utility room feels a little too hard, a fabric skirt is a charming way to introduce softness and a dash of English cottage style. It's an excellent trick for concealing bulky appliances or ugly plumbing under a freestanding sink. By choosing a grounding, cohesive fabric - perhaps a classic ticking stripe or ditsy floral - you can wrap the space in a warm tone that softens the joiney.

The most successful utility spaces are those that borrow the best kitchen ideas - think durable stone surfaces, smart lighting and high-quality joinery - to create a room that is as sophisticated as it is serviceable.

To illuminate your workspace with a dash of heritage elegance, this Morris & Co. brass wall light from Habitat will provide a warm glow that elevates the atmosphere of your utility room in an instant.

item.title: WIN! A three-night stay in a beautiful beach hut in West Sussex, worth up to £1500
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: Set directly on the shores of the Sussex coastline, Beachcroft Beach Huts offer a stylish take on the classic British seaside escape.
item.content:encoded:
The English Home is delighted to partner with Beachcroft Beach Huts to offer one lucky reader a three-night B&B stay for two in a Beach Hut Suite. With uninterrupted sea views and direct beach access, this is the perfect coastal retreat for rest, relaxation, and fun.

On arrival, guests are welcomed with complimentary rosé wine and sloe gin - perfect for long evenings watching the sunset - along with a Nespresso coffee machine to enjoy those early morning sunrises.

Great facilities include complimentary bike hire for exploring the local area, paddleboards for those feeling a little more adventurous, giant chess, a snooker table, table tennis and plenty of games. Guests can also enjoy an indoor heated swimming pool, available to book privately. There are plenty of food and drink options, from dining in the Tamarisk Restaurant, to casual dining in Monty’s dog-friendly bistro, or Blakes Beach Bar for alfresco lunches and stone-baked pizzas in the evenings.

Explore the Sussex Coast

The hotel also makes a great base for exploring the surrounding area, with world-class attractions nearby. These include the 11th-century Arundel Castle, home to the Duke of Norfolk; the historic Roman city of Chichester, with its 3rd-century city walls and Fishbourne Roman Palace, home to some of the most impressive early Roman mosaic excavations in the country.

Just a short drive away is the naval city of Portsmouth, home to the world’s oldest dry dock, Henry VIII’s Mary Rose and Admiral Horatio Nelson’s HMS Victory, which fought in the Battle of Trafalgar. For those who fancy a walk, the hotel is nestled close to the South Downs National Park, perfect for nature enthusiasts and walkers alike – and the hotel even offers a walking guide for guests to take on their own self-guided walks.

Dogs are welcome too. Dog beds, bowls and toys are provided for four-legged friends, and they can even join their owners in Monty’s Bistro for breakfast and dinner.

What's included in the prize?

One lucky winner will win a three-night B&B stay for two in a Beach Hut Suite. The prize includes:

  • Breakfast in the mornings (delivered hamper or hot breakfast in the hotel restaurant)

  • Full use of hotel facilities, including Tamarisk Bar & Restaurant, Bistro Garden and indoor heated swimming pool

To enter, simply fill in the form below. Competition ends 23:59 pm GMT on Thursday, 21 May 2026:

View this article on our website to enter

item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: From classic French styles to contemporary, trendy options, we’ve curated a list of our favourite garden bistro sets to suit every outdoor scheme.
item.content:encoded:
Hunting down the best garden bistro sets is the ultimate shortcut to turning a small balcony or forgotten patio corner into your new favourite morning coffee spot.

Even the most compact spaces deserve the best garden furniture that balances a bit of Parisian flair with a sturdy, weather-resistant design.

To keep the atmosphere glowing long after the sun dips, we suggest popping something like this rechargeable lamp from Dunelm on your table – it's the perfect final touch before browsing our favourite finds for the season below.

RRP£250
Available from Nkuku

21. The Cotswold Co. Folding Bistro Set

RRP£899
Available from The Cotswold Company
Shop now

How to style a garden bistro set

The trick to styling a bistro set is to treat your patio with the same editorial eye as a cosy indoor reading nook. Since these sets are petite, every piece has to pull its weight.

Start by softening those hard edges. Seat pads are the ultimate quick win for turning a metal chair into a spot that you'll actually want to linger - like these beautiful Sanderson X National Trust seat cushions from Habitat. To give the area some height and much-needed shade, a parasol acts as a temporary ceiling, making the corner feel more private; this parasol from Habitat will suit any garden style.

As the sun dips, it's all about the ambience. A sculptural chiminea keeps the evening chill at bay and provides a lovely, cosy glow - we've got our eye on this classic terracotta chiminea from Amazon. To finish, toss in a tactile cushion, like this outdoor cushion cover from H&M, to make the whole set-up feel like an extension of your living room.

Things to consider when buying a garden bistro set

In order to determine the best material for your bistro garden furniture, factors such as specific preferences, climate and maintenance expectations must be taken into consideration.

Metal (wrought iron, aluminium):

  • Pros: Durable, sturdy, and resistant to rust. Metal bistro sets are reflective of Parisian origins, often having an elegant and timeless look.

  • Cons: Can become hot in direct sunlight, may require periodic maintenance to prevent rust and are heavier compared to other materials.

Wood (teak, eucalyptus, cedar):

  • Pros: Natural, aesthetically pleasing, durable, and resistant to decay.

  • Cons: Requires periodic sealing or staining to maintain appearance, and it may be heavier than other materials.

Plastic (polyethene, resin):

  • Pros: Lightweight, easy to clean, resistant to weather conditions, and affordable.

  • Cons: It may not have the same aesthetic appeal as metal or wood, and can be less durable over time.

Rattan/Wicker:

  • Pros: Lightweight, stylish and often used for a more casual, tropical look.

  • Cons: May require occasional cleaning, not as durable as some other materials, and may not be suitable for all climates.

Whether you're looking for the best garden chairs for a morning espresso or an area for a full-blown evening meal, the right bistro set makes every square inch of your garden work harder.

For those moments when you want to trade the table for total relaxation, why not hang up this hammock from Amazon? It's the ultimate way to turn a quiet corner of the garden into your very own sanctuary.

item.title: A golden sausage roll recipe with a zesty marmalade glaze and aromatic spices for a spring picnic
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.content:encoded:
These marmalade-glazed sausage rolls offer a playful update on a classic picky staple, using a note of preserve to cut through the richness of the pastry.

As the days brighten, spring recipes should be getting lighter and more picnic-friendly. Easily replicated with plant-based alternatives if you wish, these sausage rolls are finished with a glossy glaze, making them perfect for a garden lunch alongside a crisp salad.

For a perfectly crisp base, using a surface like this perforated baking tray from Amazon will ensure the pastry stays light and flaky; discover how to recreate these savoury treats below.

Overview

Serves: 8

Ingredients

  • 8 sausages

  • 1 small red onion, finely chopped

  • 1 garlic clove, minced

  • 1 tsp smoked paprika

  • 1/2 tsp ground cumin or allspice (optional)

  • 1 & 1/2 tbsp marmalade (such as La Vieja Fabrica No Peel Marmalade)

  • 1 tbsp chopped fresh parsley or sage

  • Salt and pepper, to taste

  • 1 sheet puff pastry (ready rolled)

  • Milk, for brushing

  • Optional: sesame seeds or nigella seeds for topping

Method

  1. Preheat oven to 200°C (180°C fan). Line a tray with baking paper.

  2. In a small pan, sauté red onion and garlic until softened (about 5 mins). Add the paprika and optional spice, stir for another minute, then remove from the heat.

  3. In a bowl, mash the vegan sausages with a fork. Add the onion mixture, marmalade, herbs, salt, and pepper. Mix well.

  4. Unroll the puff pastry and cut into 2–3 long strips, brushing lightly with marmalade (depending on size preference).

  5. Spoon the filling into a log shape down the centre of each strip. Fold over and seal the edge with a fork. Slice into rolls.

  6. Brush with plant milk and sprinkle with seeds if using.

  7. Bake for 20–25 minutes, or until golden and crisp.

Recipe and image sourced from La Vieja Fabrica

If you are showcasing these delightful pastries to guests this season, you will want them to look their best. Here are some of our favourite serving boards to display your creations at a spring garden gathering:

TOAST Splatter Enamel Platter

RRP£36
Available from TOAST
Shop now

The White Company Serving Board

RRP£
Available from The White Company
Shop now
