RRP£72
Available from The Roost
Shop now

To ensure your savoury bakes stay as fresh and wholesome as possible, it is worth considering how they are transported. Stowing your lunch in something insulated like this beautiful striped picnic bag from Amazon ensures that your sausage rolls remain their best until you reach your destination.

For more inspiration, this soft courgette and chickpea recipe offers a delicious way to celebrate the season and can be easily packed for a picnic.

item.title: How to make art feel personal, not placed – lessons from The Chancery Rosewood’s 800-piece collection
item.dc:creator: Niamh Collins
item.description: From how to find a piece that speaks to you, the mistakes to avoid when making a purchase, and the artists making the most exciting work of the decade, Daisy Bell’s insights will help guide your approach to empower you to choose a piece of art you will cherish for decades to come.
item.content:encoded:
Buying art for your home can fill one with dread. On the face of it, it’s an exciting opportunity to select a piece you adore — one that will hold sentimental meaning, bring life to a plain wall, and make you smile every time you dash by.

However, the pressure to make the ‘right’ decision when choosing art can boggle us into not making any decision at all. I could not have been more impressed, therefore, by art consultancy founders Daisy Bell and Bella Cramer of Cramer and Bell, who were tasked with curating and assembling 800 pieces for Mayfair’s sparkling new hotel, The Chancery Rosewood. An astounding collection has been amassed, tastefully hung, and delights visitors who flock to bask in assembled works of Grayson Perry, Christopher Le Brun, Hormazd Narielwalla, Sussy Cazalet, and Caroline Isgar.

The magnificent commercial space, suffused with personality and beautifully thematically tailored to the hotel’s history, can teach us a thing or two about selecting and commissioning art for our own homes. From how to find a piece that speaks to you, the mistakes to avoid when making a purchase, and the artists crafting the most exciting work of the decade, Daisy Bell’s insights will help guide your approach to empower you to choose a piece of art you will cherish for decades to come.

Lesson 1: Work with an art consultancy

If resources allow, having the guidance of a specialist who has all the art-world knowledge at their fingertips can be a worthwhile investment, especially if you are renovating and are considering multiple pieces. The steer of an art consultant can be invaluable. The process is not intimidating, but rather can uncover your taste and reveal pieces that may never have been discovered through gallery browsing or the internet alone.

‘The way in which we, as Cramer and Bell, start with the client is to go and speak to them about what their requirements are. A lot of people don’t know what they want, they don’t know where to start, they find the whole world intimidating,’ explains Daisy.

‘You’ve got your big commercial galleries around London that are squeaky clean, you can hear a penny drop, they are scared and also I think some of those big galleries are very contemporary and so you don’t necessarily know where to find the more traditional or more modern, mid-century pieces — the artists you may want to have on your walls, or that don’t have four hundred naughts by them, or that you can envisage in your home.’

If you’re working with an interior designer, it can feel challenging to marry an interiors brief with your paintings and sculpture, but working with an art consultant can help to create harmony. ‘A lot of the clients we work with have worked with interior designers,' says Daisy, ‘so it might be that we've had elevations sent through or floor plans, or we know the style which they like, but a lot of our clients have no idea what they like. The first initial meeting is to discuss whether they have any interests or what artworks they already have in their home. Do they like those? A lot of people say that they got given a work of art as a wedding present, but they don’t like it anymore. These conversations are so useful to help build up the knowledge of what they like.’

Once Daisy and Bella have explored this with you, they will create a presentation bursting with ideas, a moodboard presentation with a range of media or styles bespoke to you. When you have fed back, a range of available artworks for each location will be suggested for you to consider.

When your pieces have been chosen, the logistics will be handled entirely by the consultancy. They will sort the imports and any licensing, the framing, transportation, and installation. Such support can give you valuable peace of mind and help to ensure that the entire process, not solely the choice of artwork, runs like clockwork.

Lesson 2: Finding artists to buy art from

One of the most commonly asked questions is where to find art to buy. If you’ve scoured the high street galleries, it can be difficult to know where exactly to turn to find pieces to shop. ‘We source artists through Instagram, and  through art fairs,’ says Daisy. It's worthwhile to pay a visit to The London Art Fair and the Affordable Art Fair.

‘Artists we work with introduce us to other artists, but we also have key galleries that we work with. Someone like Lyndsay Ingram, who’s got a beautiful eye, has really interesting artists, and Jenna Burlingham has very mid-century style artists, and she works with lots of artists' estates.’

‘We also work with print studios like  Glasgow Print Studio. They've always got really interesting artists on their books, and they're printing all of them in their studios in Glasgow. It's a constant drive for anyone in the team to find a new artist because it’s so exciting to discover someone new.’

Who is Daisy eagerly watching at the moment? ‘Andrew Cranston, he’s had a museum show. I love Robbie Fife, who is a very good up-and-coming painter. I love Katherine Jones, she’s Royal Academy, and I’ve been following her for 15 years. She’s a printmaker who really breaks the boundaries of printmaking with her techniques. I love Aimée Parrott, who is a great up-and-coming artist who stitches within her canvases, and she uses a really beautiful colour palette. Mary Ramsden is represented by Pilar Corrias; I think the way in which she has evolved through her career is amazing.’

Lesson 3: Commissioning artists

Commissioning is another way to approach finding a piece if you have a very specific idea in mind, although it’s always worth bearing in mind that artists will bring their flair, imagination, and creativity to what they paint.

The Chancery Rosewood required many bespoke commissions because of the scale of the project, and this can also happen with residential clients who are working with huge spaces and find it hard to find a piece that fits such stature. Above the fireplace when you enter the hotel, there is an extraordinary commissioned piece by Christopher Le Brun, both textural and colourful, it’s a stunning focal point above the rich burgundy marble fireplace.

‘Christopher was the president of the Royal Academy for seven years, and so this was such an exciting commission to do because he is an artist of huge merit,’ enthuses Daisy. ‘With both Christopher Le Brun and Stephen Chambers, they painted more than one piece for us to pick.’

‘Within a commercial space, storytelling is so important. Hotels want to have an overarching theme. What is the art telling? Is that a sense of place? Is it spearheading emerging artists?’ With residential projects, it’s often the feelings and personality of both the client and their home that propel the choice of a certain work: ‘A client might say that the entrance hall is grand, and so they would like a more traditional feel with gilt frames, but when they go into the family room, they want it to be more playful. Residential projects can be more organic,’ says Daisy.

It’s worth noting that some artists may refuse commissions, so do your research and begin a dialogue with an artist to see whether they are willing to work to specific parameters.

Lesson 4: What are you trying to achieve

‘The first thing to consider is what you are trying to achieve from that space,’ says Daisy. If you want a calm atmosphere, perhaps you could bring in art that invites another layer, like tapestry, texture, or sculptural wall art. But then if you have a very neutral hallway, you may want to have that sense of a focal point.’

Obviously, budgetary constraints can play into your decisions, too. ‘If you don’t have tens and thousands to spend on art, then my advice is to focus on a few spaces that you really want to fill first, especially the bits you are living in most frequently, like your living room and your sitting room,’ advises Daisy. Cramer & Bell have an online shop, Cramer & Bell curates, which offers pieces between the £150-£3,000 mark.

‘If you look at The Chancery Rosewood, we spent the majority of our budget on the public spaces where there was the highest footfall. When you are carving up the art budget for your home, you don’t want to be spending thousands on the guest bedroom or bathroom artworks. You might want to put the pieces that you have that you are not so fond of in those spaces.’

Lesson 5: Mistakes to avoid when buying art

Mistakes can inevitably be made on the journey of acquiring art for your home. Whether it be choosing a piece impulsively or for the wrong reasons, there are common traps many of us fall into that leave us with buyer’s remorse. With a few considerations, these slip-ups are avoidable, and sometimes, you can transform a work you’re weary of into one you fall in love with through fresh eyes.

  • Buy a work because you love it, not because you hope it will make money

‘You need to like it,’ insists Daisy. ‘You're going to be living with this artwork, and you might move house. Although a lot of our clients are buying art for homes they want to live in for a long time, realistically, it may not be your last house, and this art will travel with them because it’s something you can and likely will move.’

If you are concerned about art holding value, ‘buy a print from a really well-known artist,’ suggests Daisy. ‘Buy a Hockney print, buy a Howard Hodgkin print. My husband and I very nearly bought a print when we were 27 by Howard Hodgkin. We never bought it, and we really regretted it. We then bought it six years later for three times the amount of money, and it’s there to remind me of the mistake I made. However, I think the most important thing is that you love it, and if it is an investment piece, that should come secondary.’

  • Don’t hang work too high

‘Everyone has everything too high,’ says Daisy. This is where bringing on board art consultants can really help, as they will be able to ensure the perfect positioning when the art arrives.

  • Choose a suitable frame

‘One of the most common mistakes I see is that a lot of art can be framed so badly,’ explains Daisy. ‘I have a client with a semi-abstract landscape, and it has a thick black frame that kills it, whereas if it were put on a nice linen mount with a smooth dark wood frame and a tiny bit of gold slip, it would completely change the whole thing, which is what we're planning to do.’

To explore the art collection of The Chancery Rosewood is an experience like no other. For art lovers, it is simply a marvel, along every corridor and in every room and suite, original pieces abound that would not be out of place in a leading gallery. I have no doubt it will spur other hotels and hospitality venues to revisit their collections with renewed interest, not merely as decoration, but as artistic and cultural hubs. The lessons we can learn from the tasteful and highly personal ways in which the curation of this collection has painstakingly happened are manifold. If you are keen to learn more about choosing art, delve into Art at Home: An accessible guide to collecting and curating art in your home by Rachel Loos, available from Amazon, or The Art of Buying Art: How to evaluate and buy art like a professional collector by Alan Bamberger, also from Amazon.

item.title: The Anatomy of an English Home: The art of the bookshelf – Why a house isn’t a home without a well-stocked library (and how to get the look)
item.dc:creator: Amelia Thorpe
item.description: The soulful layers and storied stacks that turn simple shelving into the creative heartbeat of the British home – and how to curate your own.
item.category: The Anatomy of an English Home
item.content:encoded:
Welcome to the first instalment of The Anatomy of an English Home, a new series exploring the essential elements of British style. We begin with the bookshelf: a feature that serves as the soul of any room.

By utilising in-between spaces like chimney breasts or sloped eaves, you can transform awkward corners into purposeful focal points.

From bold colour to bespoke builds, here's how to master the art of the bookshelf.

1. Utilise hidden spaces

‘When space is at a premium, we integrate shelving into the architecture of the room,’ award-winning interior designer Laura Stephens says. ‘We look for the “in-between” spaces, such as above doors, within chimney breasts, under sloping ceilings or inside deep wall reveals. Step back and think about the room in three dimensions, not just at eye level, as higher or awkward areas are often the most successful places to add storage without dominating the scheme.’

She recommends a minimum shelf depth of about 30cm for books; shallower shelves work well for paperbacks or decorative objects.

2. Add custom details

To house three French horns, accommodate a piano and provide display space, Sophie Pringle, founder of Surrey-based interior design studio Pringle & Pringle, designed characterful custom joinery, featuring reeded panels on the door fronts and shelf profiles. The shell motif at the top is a nod to the owner’s love of the sea. ‘Even existing or off-the-shelf joinery can be elevated with thoughtful detailing,’ she says. ‘Adding mouldings, such as those from Decora, to frames or vertical or horizontal elements can instantly give cabinetry more presence.’

3. Go for a classic alcove

Many homes, particularly period ones, feature alcoves on both sides of a fireplace. ‘Bespoke bookshelf joinery makes efficient use of the alcove space, adding valuable storage, while also creating a beautiful opportunity to display books and decorative objects,’ notes Carina Raymond, founder of London-based interior design firm Studio Raymond. To keep this classic look fresh, she used bold blue to coordinate with the fireplace and added a playful injection of yellow with a pretty wall light. ‘To reinforce the sense that the joinery has always belonged in the room, we ran the skirting and cornicing in front of the bookshelves,’ she adds.

4. Create a whole-wall focal point

A bookcase wall can make a striking focal point, adding plenty of interest to any room. ‘Think about practicality first – what needs to be stored, should it all be on display or some items be hidden away?’ advises Laura Hanbury, co-founder of Berkshire-based interior design studio Vanrenen Hanbury. ‘Then we think about the design concept and detailing.’

Here, false books on bi-folding doors conceal a TV screen, shelves display the owner’s collection of antique delftware, and cupboards and drawers are for media equipment. ‘We used the same colour on the joinery as the skirting, doors and architraves to anchor the bookcase as an architectural detail,’ she adds.

5. Make a cosy nook

‘The under-eaves space naturally invites intimacy,’ says Henriette von Stockhausen, creative director of luxury interior design studio VSP Interiors. ‘Rather than seeing the slope as a limitation, I always see it as an opportunity to create something that feels enveloping and quietly special.’ Here, the backs of the shelves are painted in a deep, warm red to create depth and contrast against the softer painted framework. Paperbacks are arranged by colour as another considered touch. ‘Shallow shelves can look mean, but overly deep ones waste space where head height is limited,’ she adds.

6. Integrate fireplace shelving

Shelving above a fireplace can make an impact, provided there is sufficient clearance between the fire opening and the bottom of the shelf for safety reasons. ‘Here, the shelving and fi replace were conceived as a single, unified elevation, rather than a series of stepped elements, allowing the composition to read as one considered piece of joinery rather than a collection of parts,’ explains Sabah Ashiq, associate director of architecture at London-based interior and architecture studio Own London. Shelving aligns with the mantel height to avoid visual heaviness and to introduce clear horizontal lines that bring a sense of calm and order to the space.

7. Use mirrors for depth

Mirror is a deceptively simple tool that can be used to enhance a bookcase design, as Pringle explains. ‘Here, the joinery was existing when we took on the project, and the central square element felt quite heavy within the composition,’ she recalls. ‘Adding a mirror introduced reflection and depth, creating the illusion of looking through the joinery.’

Pringle recommends using decorative glass, rather than a standard ‘flat’ mirror. ‘Antiqued mirror, which we used here, softens the reflection and adds warmth, preventing the glass from looking cold or sharp in a sitting room,’ she adds.

8. Choose freestanding furniture

A freestanding bookcase or piece of display furniture is a flexible choice. ‘It can allow a room to breathe, and feel lighter and less fixed, particularly in transitional spaces like hallways, advises Raymond. ‘Pieces like this can easily be repurposed elsewhere in the home as lifestyle and needs change, something that is especially useful in family homes where spaces need to evolve over time.’

If off-the-shelf bookcases are too large or small for requirements, another option is to consider a bespoke piece, made to suit the dimensions of the room, and unlike fitted joinery, it can be taken to a new home in the future.

9. `Add bookshelves near the bed

If reading in bed is a luxury, then adjacent bookshelves are a must. ‘Here, bespoke recessed shelving on both sides of the bed brings storage and display together effortlessly, adding character and warmth through books and personal objects,’ says London-based interior designer Nicky Dobree. ‘We kept the shelving light in colour so that it doesn’t visually dominate the room or feel too heavy around the bed.’ The lower sections are slightly deeper to accommodate drawers for extra storage, with tops that double as bedside tables.

10. Add bespoke bookshelves

In a study or library, the best solution may be to line the walls with custom-made bookcases and cabinets. ‘Bespoke storage, especially in a narrow room like this, works best because it is designed specifically for the space and the owner’s individual needs,’ says Cath Beckett, co-founder of London-based interior design studio Yellow London.

Beckett recommends a mix of closed storage and open shelving for personal items like artwork, objects, books and paperwork, which can be neatly, carefully and attractively arranged for display and keeping everything organised. ‘Choose an uplifting colour like blue or green to keep the room feeling light and consider using gallery lights to add dimension,’ she says.

item.title: A seasonal flourless chocolate meringue cake recipe with a fudgy base and a crisp topping for an elegant spring celebration
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: A rich and decadent flourless chocolate cake topped with a delicate cocoa meringue that creates a striking centrepiece for any seasonal gathering.
item.content:encoded:
This flourless chocolate meringue cake is a delicious choice for those seeking a gluten-free dessert that doesn't compromise on taste.

This rustic spring recipe is a celebratory alternative to traditional sponges, an effortless win for a Sunday when paired with crème fraîche to balance the deep cocoa.

You can find this recipe in the Cooking Made Simple cookbook from Amazon, which is well worth buying if you’re looking for more seasonal recipe inspiration. Discover how to recreate this indulgent, fudgy chocolate dessert below.

Overview

Serves: 12

Ingredients

(For the chocolate cake)

  • 300g dark chocolate (70% cocoa), chopped

  • 150g unsalted butter, cubed

  • 5 large eggs, separated, plus 2 large egg yolks

  • 175g caster sugar

  • Pinch of coarse sea salt flakes

(For the meringue topping)

  • 2 large egg whites

  • 120g caster sugar

  • 1 tbsp unsweetened cocoa powder

  • 1 tbsp cornflour

  • 1 tsp apple cider vinegar

(To serve)

  • Crème fraîche (optional)

Method

  1. Preheat the oven to 170°C fan and line the base and sides of a 20-cm springform cake tin with parchment paper.

  2. First, make the chocolate cake. In a heatproof bowl set over a saucepan of simmering water, melt the chocolate and butter together, stirring to combine. Remove the bowl from the heat and allow the chocolate mixture to cool slightly.

  3. Meanwhile, whisk the 5 egg whites. In a large, grease-free bowl using an electric hand mixer, beat the egg whites with the sugar until they reach a marshmallow consistency with slightly droopy peaks. Set aside.

  4. Once cool, beat all 7 egg yolks into the chocolate mixture along with the sea salt flakes. Now fold in one-third of the beaten egg whites to loosen the chocolate mixture. Next, transfer the chocolate mixture into the larger bowl with the remaining egg whites and gently fold together until everything is well incorporated and a mousse-like texture.

  5. Scrape the cake batter evenly into the prepared tin. Bake the cake in the centre of the oven for 20 minutes or until slightly risen and a crust has formed on top. (Don’t worry if the middle of the cake wobbles a little.) After 20 minutes, remove the cake from the oven and increase the oven temperature to 180°C fan.

  6. While the cake is cooling slightly, make the meringue topping. In a large, grease-free bowl using an electric whisk, beat the remaining 2 egg whites until thick and frothy. Slowly add the sugar, a tablespoon at a time, continuing to whisk until you have a strong meringue that holds stiff peaks. Sift in the cocoa powder and cornflour, then gently fold in the apple cider vinegar using a metal spoon.

  7. Spoon the meringue mixture over the cake and return to the oven for a further 35 minutes or until the meringue puffs up and has a shiny top.(Don’t worry if the meringue cracks or slightly mushrooms over the top.)

  8. Transfer the cake still in the tin to a wire rack and allow it to cool for 5 minutes. Using your hands, push up any meringue that has collapsed over the edge of the tin to encourage it to collapse inwards – there is no need to be too precious with this, it is meant to look a little dramatic. Run a knife around the inside edge of the tin to loosen the sides of the cake and release the sides of the springform cake tin. Allow the cake to cool before removing from the tin and serving.

  9. Serve slices of the cake with crème fraîche, if you like.

Extracted from Cooking Made Simple by Alexandra Dudley (Ebury)

Giving the dessert the spotlight it deserves starts with the right presentation. A glass or ceramic cake stand is a wonderful way to display this cake and bring a touch of occasion to the table. Here are some of our favourites:

Available from Guzzini

To ensure your bake stays as wholesome as possible, it is worth considering the tools you use. Preparing your ingredients in a plastic-free bowl like this stainless steel mixing bowl from Amazon ensures your mixture remains pure.

Whether you're serving a thin slice after dinner or hosting a larger party, this flourless cake is a decadent choice. If you're looking for more sweet inspiration, try out this pistachio tiramisu recipe.

item.title: A simple spiced shortbread recipe for the ultimate chocolate-dipped Easter treat
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: A buttery, spiced Easter shortbread that offers a refined take on a seasonal classic with a delicate chocolate finish.
item.content:encoded:
These Easter Shortbread biscuits offer a refined twist on a classic, balancing a buttery, spiced crumb dipped in indulgent chocolate.

As we look for more seasonal spring recipes, these biscuits provide a delightful alternative to richer festive bakes. Whether shaped into classic bunnies or festive eggs, they make a charming addition to an Easter Sunday table.

For a perfectly even bake, using a heavy-duty baking tray like this one from Amazon will ensure the biscuits stay crisp and golden; discover how to recreate Daylesford's sweet, chocolate-dipped treats below.

Overview

Total Time: 1 hour

Serves: 12

Ingredients

  • 300g plain flour

  • 200g unsalted butter

  • 100g golden caster sugar

  • 1 tsp mixed sweet spice

  • A few drops of cold water

(For the decoration)

  • 200g white, dark or milk chocolate

  • Crystallised ginger, cacao nibs, dried rose petals, goji berries

  • Biscuit cutters of your choice

Method

  1. To make the shortbread, simply place the flour, butter and sugar in the bowl of a food processor. Whizz briefly (with a stand mixer or a hand mixer), adding a few drops of cold water, until the dough just comes together. Turn out of the bowl and knead very lightly to an even dough. Shape into a thick disc, cover and chill for at least 30 minutes.

  2. Dust your worktop with a little flour and roll out the dough to 1/2 cm thick. Use your cutters to cut out biscuits, placing them on a lined baking tray with plenty of space between each. Lightly knead any leftover dough together and roll out to shape more biscuits. Chill for 30 minutes before baking.

  3. Preheat the oven to 160°C and bake the biscuits for 15 minutes or until they are just set and have turned a light golden colour. Carefully transfer to a cooling rack.

  4. Break up the chocolate of your choice and place it in a glass bowl set over a pan of simmering water until melted. Chop up the ginger, cacao nibs or goji berries, if using.

  5. Once the biscuits are cool, simply dip them into the chocolate or use a teaspoon to drizzle the chocolate over each one. Place back onto the cooling rack and sprinkle with your chosen decorations.

  6. We have made seasonal egg and bunny-shaped biscuits, but you can use any cookie cutter or let your children make shapes of their own. Delicious dipped into melted chocolate.

Giving these biscuits the spotlight they deserve starts with the right presentation. Here are a few of our favourite Easter-themed plates to help you display these seasonal favourites and bring a touch of festive elegance to the table.

Emma Bridgewater Mini Eggs Plate

Available from Emma Bridgewater
Shop now

For those inspired by the season, try these classic hot cross bun recipes to further celebrate the arrival of spring.

item.title: The best cream paints for timeless English homes – expert-approved shades that never go out of style
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: 10 of the best cream paint colours for your home, recommended by interior designers and paint experts.
item.content:encoded:
Finding the perfect shade to breathe warmth and light into your home begins with discovering the best cream paints currently favoured by interior designers.

Whether you are looking to refresh a dark hallway or create a serene, sun-filled kitchen, these versatile paint ideas offer an airy alternative to stark white or drab beige.

Before beginning your transformation, ensure you have the right tools for a professional finish. We suggest this cutting in brush from Amazon for flawless lines. Here, we explore the most elegant cream hues for a timeless home, guided by leading interior designers and paint experts.

1. Clay, Paint & Paper Library

Award-winning interior designer Laura Stephens identifies this architectural neutral as a foundational favourite. 'A fail-safe neutral for me is Paint & Paper Library's Clay. It sits beautifully in that perfect middle ground, neither too grey nor too yellow, which makes it incredibly versatile,' she says.

In a recent project, Stephens utilised the brand's graduated system to enhance the sense of journey through the house. 'We used it across adjoining spaces, with Clay I in the dining room (pictured) and Clay III in the adjacent snug. Using the same colour family in different strengths is a wonderful way to create flow between rooms while still giving each space its own distinct character.'

2. Slipper Satin, Farrow & Ball

A firm favourite for both Hattie Hansgard, interior designer at London-based interior design studio Joanna Wood Interiors, and Cotswold-based designer Sean Syimington, Farrow & Ball's Slipper Satin is the definitive off-white cream for the modern English home.

'Slipper Satin is our go-to white; it's a lovely, creamy tone that comes alive in English light,' says Symington.

It avoids the yellow heaviness of traditional creams, offering a chalky, silk-like finish that Hansgard notes 'has enough depth to feel considered but remains incredibly versatile.' She adds that, used well, it 'can unify a space in a way that brighter whites often can’t - it feels more forgiving and more liveable.'

3. Pointing, Farrow & Ball

For those who find standard creams a touch too heavy, Symington suggests Farrow & Ball's Pointing as a 'beautiful step up.' Named after the colour of lime mortar, it is a fresh shade that feels 'a touch crispier' than Slipper Satin but remains a warm base that prevents it from feeling clinical.

It is an ideal choice for ceilings and cornicing to provide a soft contrast against slightly deeper cream walls, ensuring the room feels bright and lifted.

4. Linen Tint, Laura Hammett Living

Laura Hammett Living's Linen Tint represents an elegant and understated character. 'Cream paint is often perceived as simple, but achieving the perfect cream tone requires real depth and nuance - and this is where the difference between premium and trade paints becomes particularly noticeable,' notes Laura Hammett, founder of her eponymous interior and lifestyle brand.

Her matte emulsion is beautifully matte to the eye with a low sheen level of under 5%, but it has a unique advantage that it is wipeable, where many others are not, making it a practical choice for most rooms in the home.

5. Stone-Pale-Cool, Little Greene

David Mottershead, managing director of paint brand Little Greene, looks to the 18th century for inspiration with Little Greene's Stone-Pale-Cool, a historically significant shade. 'It is a lovely shade: it is a Georgian colour, and represents the palest variant of the cool powdery colour based on Portland or Bath stone in the 1730s,' he explains.

It provides an architectural quality that feels historic and grounded, making it the perfect choice for those seeking a cream that feels stony and powdery rather than buttery.

6. Hay, Farrow & Ball

For a slightly richer, more traditional feel, Hansgard points toward shades with genuine depth. Farrow & Ball's Hay is warm and inviting, working particularly well in rooms that lack natural light. She notes that she looks for creams with a 'gentle warmth rather than anything too yellow or too cold,' specifically for how they behave in 'London light, which can be quite grey,' bringing a necessary softness without feeling heavy or dated.

7. Canvas IV, Paint & Paper Library

Interior designer Rebecca Hughes views the selection of high-quality cream paints as a smart investment. 'Selecting the right paint is more than just picking a colour – it's about achieving lasting beauty and harmony throughout your space,' she says.

She frequently turns to Canvas IV to achieve smooth, even coverage with a flawless finish.

As a final note, Hughes says that 'luxury doesn’t always mean the most expensive option – it's about smart investment. A well-chosen mid-range paint can look just as elegant as a premium one, if applied with care and intention.'

The beauty of a thoughtfully chosen cream lies in its ability to soften a room, providing a gentle glow that serves as the perfect foundation for a refined home.

If you are eager to learn more about selecting the right tones for your own home, we recommend the book Farrow & Ball: Living with Colour, available from Amazon, a wonderful resource for understanding how heritage shades can transform a space. To discover more ways to build a restful palette, explore our guide to the best neutral paint colours.

item.title: Farrow & Ball’s spring 2026 palette is here – and it’s a soulful mix of new neutrals and citrus hits that are set to define the season
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: From lime greens to plaster pinks, this season’s collection is the soulful, sun-drenched refresh our homes have been waiting for.
item.content:encoded:
With the light finally changing, I've been diving into the new Farrow & Ball spring colours for some much-needed new season inspiration.

I'm currently obsessed with the latest Farrow & Ball paint colours, which favour warmth and colour over clinical neutrals.

Before I dive in, don't miss the brand's gorgeous new book, Farrow & Ball Colour Stories: The Art of Living with Colour from Amazon, which is out in September but available to preorder.

View this post on Instagram

The overall palette this season is a joy to behold – it's a cheery, quintessentially spring collection that feels like a garden in mid-bloom.

You can see from Farrow & Ball's Instagram post above how these shades sit together; they aren't just bright for the sake of it, but have a layered, pigment-rich depth that is bound to shift beautifully as the day goes on.

One of the standout shades for me is Scallop, a beautifully pale, terracotta-tinged pink that mimics the look of raw plaster. I think this colour would work beautifully in any room of the house, but specifically a south-facing living room, where it acts as a warm neutral and the perfect backdrop for antique furniture or a curated gallery wall.

For those seeking a sense of serenity, editor of The English Home Alison Davidson is looking toward Dix Blue. 'Like a lot of people, I find blues calming, and I think this would be perfect for a hallway, so as you walk in the door, it instantly makes you feel relaxed and ready for some 'me-time,' she says.

On the more vibrant end of the spectrum, Babouche is proving that yellow is back in a big way. 'I am definitely drawn to strong colours too, and I find yellow energising, I think this would be a great colour for a dining room to encourage conversation, or in a home office to get you in the mood for work,' Alison adds.

If you want to add even more juice to a dark corner, Acid Drop is a total revelation. Chartreuse has always been one of my favourite colours, and this version is so playful but definitely do-able in a home – it also looks suspiciously like the signature lime hue that took over the world a few summers ago, thanks to a certain female British pop star. It's a punchy, irreverent green that I can see bringing instant life to a small bathroom, a utility room or even the inside of a cupboard.

For a slightly more traditional take on that lush energy, Yeabridge Green offers a garden-inspired feel that is quintessentially British for a kitchen – the ultimate shade for a green kitchen ideas mood board.

Even the new blues are becoming more relaxed; Kittiwake, for instance, has a tiny bit of black mixed in, which gives it a denim-like, lived-in quality that instantly reminds me of spring. It doesn't feel too cold or 'show-homey', which makes it incredibly peaceful for a bedroom scheme.

If these paint ideas have inspired you to start a project, remember the best spring updates often extend beyond the walls. To truly lean into the season, I love layering colourful tones with accessories that mirror the garden's awakening.

You might want to pair your new palette with something like this floral embroidered cushion from M&S, which perfectly mirrors the punchy, organic feel of Farrow & Ball's Acid Drop and Yeabridge Green. After all, the joy of spring is in the little details – just like the way a soft fabric catches afternoon sun against a fresh coat of paint.

item.title: 15 beautiful ensuite bathroom ideas for a flawlessly finished personal nook
item.dc:creator: Suzanna Le Grove
item.description: Get inspired by these chic ensuite bathroom ideas designed to maximise your floor plan and make even the smallest space feel bright, open, and incredibly high-end.
item.content:encoded:
Whether you are working with a grand master suite or a clever converted cupboard, these ensuite bathroom ideas will help you create a space that feels like a natural extension of your bedroom.

Finding the right balance between luxury and utility is key when researching bathroom ideas for smaller, more intimate spaces.

To elevate the sensory experience, consider incorporating small but impactful details like this Neom scented candle from Amazon, which instantly signals a shift from the bustle of the day to a state of relaxation. Here we explore 15 ways to transform your ensuite into a sophisticated retreat.

1. Harmonise the atmosphere

To make an ensuite feel like a true suite rather than an afterthought, focus on a shared mood between the two rooms. 'I feel like an ensuite should feel like a natural extension of the bedroom rather than a purely functional space,' says Hattie Hansgard, designer at luxury interior design firm Joanna Wood. 'The most successful ones strike a balance between practicality and atmosphere.'

Even if you choose practical stone or tile over bedroom carpet, ensuring the colour palette and feel of the decor remain consistent allows the eye to travel uninterrupted.

2. Contrast varying scales of pattern and colour

In smaller ensuites, don't be afraid to experiment with patterns and colour that may feel overwhelming elsewhere. 'if the space is small, I love mixing and playing with varying scales in a scheme, whether that be large prints, small ditsy florals, bold stripes, or solid expanses of colour,' suggests Rebecca Hughes, creative director of London-based studio Rebecca Hughes Interiors.

This layered approach creates a curated, English home aesthetic that feels effortless and comfortable rather than trend-driven.

H&M Floral Shower Curtain

RRP£14.99
Available from H&M
Shop now

Pooky Frill Mirror

RRP£185
Available from Pooky
Shop now

M&S Striped Bath Mat

RRP£15
Available from M&S
Shop now

3. Streamline the layout

Unlike a high-traffic bathroom, an ensuite is a personal territory that allows for a more refined, edited space. 'An ensuite bathroom benefits from a more streamlined approach,' says Laura Hammett, creative director of Laura Hammett Living.

'Unlike a large family bathroom, it doesn’t need to accommodate multiple users or extensive bathroom storage, so the focus can remain on refined finishes and an intuitive layout that supports daily routines without feeling crowded.' By stripping back the clutter, you can focus on a restorative environment.

4. Layer your lighting

Lighting is perhaps the most critical element in shifting a bathroom from clinical to cosy. 'I tend to favour a layered approach, soft ambient lighting for overall warmth, and a flattering light, combined with more focused task lighting at the mirror,' says Hansgard.

Hammet agrees, noting that thoughtful bathroom lighting creates a 'flattering glow for getting ready in the morning while also allowing the space to feel calm and relaxing in the evening.'

Laura Ashley Semi-Flush Light

RRP£60
Available from M&S
Shop now

M&S Wicker Wall Light

RRP£39.50
Available from M&S
Shop now

Pooky Ceramic Shade

RRP£82
Available from Pooky
Shop now

5. Envelope the space in tile

If your ensuite is small, go bold with your tiling strategy by extending it beyond the floor. 'Extending the same floor tile into the shower helps create a seamless feel,' notes Hansgard. Tiling the rear wall of the shower or the sides of the bath in the same material as the floor creates a continuous envelope of colour.

This approach prevents the space from feeling chopped up or cluttered by different finishes and grounds the room, providing a high-end, bespoke finish.

6. Integrate recessed storage

In tight ensuites where a bulky cupboard simply won't fit, a slim marble or stone ledge is a lifesaver. Running a shelf behind the basin and toilet provides the perfect spot for decanted toiletries and fresh flowers without encroaching on floor space. As Hansgard advises, 'Storage should be integrated as discreetly as possible,' ensuring the room remains visually clean.

Rockett St George Storage Jar

RRP£30
Available from Next
Shop now

Dunelm Slimline Storage Box

RRP£10
Available from Dunelm
Shop now

Shelf Life Amber Storage Jar

RRPfrom £16
Available from Not On The High Street
Shop now

7. Define the shower zone with contrasting tiles

By swapping standard flooring for a bold, decorative tile inside the shower, you create a distinct feature zone. This visual break from the rest of the room makes the walk-in area feel like a bespoke, high-end design choice

8. Position towel rails symmetrically

If wall space is at a premium, look upward. Positioning twin heated towel rails at either end of a bath - or even above it - is a clever use of dead space. This layout feels balanced and grand, turning a basic heating element into a decorative feature. It's a beautifully convenient position that ensures you never have to step out into the cold to find a dry bath towel, maintaining that essential sense of bedroom-like comfort.

Christy Cotton Guest Towel

RRP£12
Available from Christy
Shop now

H&M Striped Bath Towel

RRP£9.99
Available from H&M
Shop now

Rebecca Udall Scallop Hand Towels

RRP£54
Available from Rebecca Udall
Shop now

9. Personalise with wallpaper

'Wallpaper can work beautifully in an ensuite, provided ventilation is good,' says Hansgard. 'I tend to use it more in areas away from direct water exposure, where it can bring softness and personality that tiles alone sometimes lack.' Moisture-resistant wallpapers are a game-changer for bringing the charm of the bedroom into the bathroom. This brings a softness and personality that tiles alone sometimes lack, creating a beautifully composed retreat.

10. Prioritise high-quality brassware

Size should never compromise the space experience. Even in a compact corner shower, a high-quality oversized rainfall head provides an indulgent experience that makes a quick morning rinse feel like a luxury. Well-crafted brassware instantly elevates the space.

11. Rethink the room entrance

When designing an ensuite, the way you move between the sleeping and bathing areas dictates the entire flow of your morning. Instead of sticking with a standard, narrow door, consider how moving the entrance or widening the threshold can create a grander sense of arrival.

By treating the entry as an architectural transition - perhaps through a sliding door, an open walkway or a curtain - you allow the bathroom's best features, like a beautiful vanity or a freestanding bathtub, to become part of the bedroom's visual landscape.

12. Select calming neutral colours

For larger ensuites, a sense of serenity is paramount to ensure the space feels like a spa-like retreat. 'Selecting the right scheme for an ensuite bathroom is more than just picking a colour – it's about achieving lasting beauty and harmony throughout your space,' says Hughes.

'I tend to embrace classic English influences with a relaxed, layered approach. In a larger ensuite bathroom, however, I like incorporating calming, neutral colours such as creams, off-whites, that create a serene and relaxing atmosphere.'

13. Maximise natural light

Natural light is the ultimate luxury in a private space, making even a tiny ensuite feel airy. Hansgard suggests looking to the ceiling when wall space is limited. 'Maximising natural light wherever possible makes a huge difference. Skylights or carefully positioned windows can transform an en-suite, particularly in smaller or more enclosed layouts.'

A successful ensuite relies on treating the room as a true living space rather than a utility zone. When looking for small bathroom ideas that provide both character and function, remember that even the most compact layout can feel like a grand hotel suite with the right textural finishes.

To finish the look with a touch of luxury, consider placing this geranium-scented handwash from Bamford on your vanity, which adds a subtle, botanical fragrance. By focusing on these quiet luxuries, your ensuite will become a harmonious extension of your bedroom.

item.title: From a crimson red kitchen to a lemon yellow bedroom - this London flat is a masterclass in bold paint application
item.dc:creator: Charlotte Dunford
item.description: Keen to move away from the muted palette of her previous home, Ruth Corbett has transformed this London flat with a beautifully bold use of colour
item.content:encoded:
It was the generous proportions and rare garden access that drew interiors editor and journalist, Ruth Corbett, to this flat in London. Set in an Edwardian mansion, the rooms were spacious, the ceilings were high and sizeable windows allowed natural light to flood in.

The property had previously been bought by a French actress at the age of 80 and, impressively, she had continued to live there for the next 22 years. The rooms had been peppered with theatrical details, including feathered slippers and top hats, as well as family heirlooms. Yet, today, the home is a stark contrast to its former incarnation, adopting a blend of classic and contemporary interior design styles.

The schemes feel well-curated, sparingly decorated with characterful pieces layered upon elegant, highly-pigmented hues. Its bold transformation is down to the creative partnership of Ruth and luxury British paint company, Mylands.

With over 25 years of experience in interiors journalism, including writing about beautiful homes around the UK, Ruth was well prepared to tackle the renovation. She discovered Mylands in 2020, originally aware of it as a supplier to the film industry before it later began to crop up in numerous houses she wrote about.

It was Mylands' fourth generation CEO, Dominic Myland, who offered Ruth guidance on the redecoration of her home. 'Dominic carries on the family tradition of perfection in paint, still creating colours that are lustrous, flawless and with guaranteed quality,' says Ruth. 'I love the romance of the story behind this paint!'

Early conversations, discussing and refining palettes, took place as the flat underwent a complete refurbishment. Electrics, plumbing, plastering and glazing were reworked, all before a drop of paint was added.

Ruth's vision for the interiors centred around the idea of an elegant European apartment. Embracing the impressive amount of natural light and space throughout was essential, as was a bold application of colour. 'Our previous house's colour palette was muted, so I've loved bringing bolder shades to this place,' says Ruth.

A variety of hues appear throughout the flat, bringing personality and a defining sense of character to each space. In the hallway, walls in Chester Square are paired with the ceiling and woodwork in Mint Street, to set a calm and welcoming tone for the home. Meanwhile, Egerton Place - an earthy pink that transforms in different lights - lends warmth to the guest bedroom.

A particularly special shade, however, takes pride of place in the main bedroom. The walls are painted in Lemon Salts, a pale yellow that brings brightness and has won its spot as Ruth and her husband's favourite colour in the flat.

Arguably, the most dynamic room is the kitchen. Bold tones complement one another, creating a vibrant place to cook, entertain and gather. Ruth had selected Huguenot, a crimson red, for the cabinets before the kitchen installation had even been finished. Its depth and richness made it the perfect partner to the black granite worktops.

It was Dominic's suggestion to then bring Rose Theatre, a soft pink-grey, and Millbank, an earthy brown, in via the walls and woodwork. The blue zellige tiles and a statement range cooker in an ocean blue finish were the perfect finishing touches to the striking scheme.

The transformation of this Edwardian flat proves the power that colour holds. It also boasts a wealth of inspiration when it comes to creative paint ideas. Colour has been applied beyond the walls, including on window frames, kitchen cabinets and ceilings, infusing extra layers of character and drama into the interiors.

item.title: Safer inside: How to protect your pets on the road this season
item.dc:creator: Sally Coffey
item.description: It is time to rethink how we travel with our four-legged friends, says Tavo.
item.content:encoded:
As we head into lighter, warmer days and nights, we will all be spending more time outdoors in the fresh air. Whether that is staycations, days out or quick trips to some of our beautiful parks and nature trails.

For many of us, a trip would not be complete without the company of our beloved four-legged friends. But, despite travelling with our pets more often, are we prioritising their safety any more than we used to?

Tavo does not think so. Its research has found that 26 per cent of motorists with dogs in the UK allow their pet to travel with its head out of the car window.

That is around 3.5m dogs that are being put in serious danger (based on a 13.5m pet dog population). It is a familiar sight, but it is a big risk to not just the dog's safety but for everyone inside and outside the car.

So why do we do it?

A staggering 71 per cent of dog owners who allow this think the dog enjoys it, and 70 per cent let their dog put its head out of the window to give it fresh air. Just under half (43 per cent) think it puts the dog at ease and, most alarmingly, 48 per cent do not even consider the dog's enjoyment! Almost a third (28 per cent) do it because they think it looks cute, and a further 20 per cent, around 700,000 dogs, travel like this because owners believe pedestrians enjoy seeing it.

What are the risks?

When a dog travels with its head out of the window of a car, it is at risk of being hit by anything airborne, such as flying debris, not to mention potential eye abrasions from the wind. If that car is involved in a collision, there is even more danger to the dog.

The experts come together

To help tackle the issue, television vet and animal welfare advocate Dr Scott Miller, and dog behaviourist and trainer Adem Fehmi have joined forces with Tavo to launch the 'Safer Inside' campaign. This aims to raise awareness of the danger dogs are in when they are travelling with their heads out of the window - reinforcing the message that they are simply safer inside.

Dr Scott Miller is among those calling for tighter rules for motorists who drive with a dog sticking its head out of the car window. He explains why: "You don't want to imagine the injuries I see caused by dogs jumping out of car windows. If the dog is anxious or stressed, it adds further complexity to the scenario. I'm working to raise awareness that what many people in the UK think looks 'cute' can end in tragedy. It's time pet safety in cars is given the same priority as human safety!'

Shining the spotlight on dog behaviour, Adem Fehmi, said: 'Dog behaviour is complex. While some owners might think that a dog panting with its head out of the window is a sign that they are enjoying the fresh air, panting can, in fact, be a sign of stress. Sitting inside a crash-tested carrier inside the vehicle is the best option for creating security and a calm and happy dog.'

Setting a new standard in safety

To help pet owners secure their dogs in comfort, style and safety, Tavo has redefined pet travel with its range of premium car seats and carriers. Importantly, every Tavo travel product is occupant crash-tested to the latest United Nations ECE child-restraint standards, ensuring the highest level of protection.

From compact carriers for pets up to 20lbs to robust travel crates for dogs up to 100lbs, Tavo delivers safe travel solutions across the spectrum, utilising the car's integrated ISOFIX fittings to ensure ultimate safety and prevent rotation in the event of a collision.

Because a dog deserves the same protection as the rest of the family.

Find out more at tavopets.com/uk

item.title: Quick! The H&M Home sale is ending – Here’s how I’m styling the ultimate summer tablescape
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: I am prepping for al fresco season and raiding the H&M Home sale for the whimsical sculptural pieces I need to elevate my summer hosting before the event ends this Friday.
item.content:encoded:
I have been keeping a very close eye on the current H&M home sale, which offers the perfect opportunity to refresh your homeware for the new season.

As the weather warms, I’m searching for fresh summer table decorating ideas to move my hosting outdoors. There is nothing like a sun-drenched brunch to celebrate the return of the light.

If you’re looking to update your al fresco collection, you must act fast; the event concludes in less than 72 hours. I’m convinced these vibrant new accessories would look beautiful on a white embroidered tablecloth like this one from Amazon, which is the perfect canvas for the pieces I’ve picked out from the sale below.

Figurative Stoneware Jar
