The trick to a great outdoor table is making it feel effortless rather than over-styled. I’m moving away from anything that looks too set or precious this year, choosing instead to mix and match different textures and pieces to create a relaxed, gathered feel.

Personally, I’m using this sale to lean into some of the more charming, quirky details that H&M does so well. I’ve always been a fan of playful fruit and vegetable motifs, so I was very excited to see the grape and artichoke pieces make their way into the markdowns. I’ll be styling the grape storage jar as a trinket pot for salt flakes or olives, or even as a sculptural centrepiece.

I’ve also picked up a couple of these seagrass tealight holders. The natural weaving adds such a lovely, low-key texture to the table, and they give off a much softer, more organic glow than a standard glass jar when the sun begins to dip.

As we head into the season of long, lazy lunches and garden gatherings, having a few hero pieces can make all the difference to your outdoor space. Whether it’s a characterful serving bowl or a simple linen runner, these small updates are the perfect additions to spring table decorating and summer al fresco dining. summer table decorating.

And don’t forget the glassware! These colourful wine glasses from Amazon are a brilliant way to add a touch of jewel-toned sparkle to your setting. Remember, the H&M sale ends on Friday morning, and there are only a limited number of homeware pieces left to snap up!

item.title: Easter decorating ideas: 13 tasteful ways to bring the spirit of spring indoors
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: Ditch the seasonal clichés for an edit of Easter decorating ideas that bring the luminous, budding energy of the English garden indoors.
item.content:encoded:
As the first blossoms start to peek through the frost, there is no better excuse to shake off the winter blues than with a collection of cheerful Easter decorating ideas to brighten every nook and cranny.

The transition from the cosy depths of winter to the airy brightness of spring requires a delicate touch. Easter brings with it a sense of renewed energy and the promise of longer, sun-drenched days, inciting us to bring that sense of vitality into the home. To achieve a look that feels elevated yet inviting, balance heritage charm with a seasonal spirit – think soft pastels, animal motifs and botanical patterns.

To begin your styling journey, consider a simple Easter table decorating idea, such as these mini Easter crackers from Amazon. Whether you are hosting a sprawling family lunch or simply wish to add a seasonal nod to your mantelpiece or front door, these ideas offer simple ways to layer your home with a bit of personality.

1. Change up your textiles

As the light changes, so should the weight and feel of your fabrics. It is time to evict those heavy velvets and wools in favour of breezier alternatives like ticking-stripe cottons and muslins.

This is also the perfect excuse to have a bit of fun with your functional pieces. Many brands release Easter-themed tea towels, oven gloves and napkins that can instantly lift a kitchen’s mood. For the living room, introduce a few cushions featuring spring motifs, and for the dining room, an embroidered table runner to add a soft, tactile finish that feels perfectly in step with the season.

Emma Bridgewater Windblown Daffodils Tea Towel

RRP£13
Available from Emma Bridgewater
Shop now

The White Company Easter Egg Embroidered Napkins

RRP£35
Available from The White Company
Shop now

Sophie Allport Chicken Double Oven Glove

RRP£30
Available from Amazon
Shop now

2. Embrace pastel tones

To evoke the soft, clear light of a spring morning, later a palette of butter yellow, duck-egg blue and sage green throughout your rooms. For those ready for a total overhaul, a fresh lick of paint on the walls can completely transform the energy of a room.

If you aren’t quite ready for the paintbrush, introduce these colours through soft furnishing; patterned cushions or a lightweight throw draped over a sofa can work beautifully. Against a neutral backdrop of off-white or stone, these shades provide a calming environment that allows seasonal accents to sing.

OKA Stonewashed Linen Cushion Cover with Fringing

RRP£75
Available from OKA
Shop now

Bed Threads Lagoon & Crème Muslin Throw

RRP£170
Available from Bed Threads

Addison Ross Buttercream Lacquer Rechargeable Bobbin LED Lamp

RRP£125
Available from Addison Ross
Shop now

3. Hang an Easter wreath on the door

Extend your decorating efforts to the exterior by welcoming guests with a colourful wreath. For a natural touch, craft one from foraged greenery and sprinkle in some little faux Easter eggs.

If you’re not ready to get crafty, there are many artificial Easter wreaths out there that give the same effect. These everlasting designs feature silk blossoms, miniature nests and pastel foliage that can be brought out year after year.

Dibor Yellow And Green Easter Eggs Wreath

RRP£59
Available from Not On The High Street
Shop now

Lights4fun 50cm Pre Lit Easter Egg Wreath

RRP£34.99
Available from Lights4fun
Shop now

Pippa Designs Easter Spring Summer Door Wreath

RRP£44.50
Available from Not On The High Street
Shop now

4. Decorate with bunny motifs

While bunny motifs can easily become cluttered, a disciplined approach ensures they remain tasteful. Look for silhouettes in sophisticated materials, like a bronze hare for the mantel, a rabbit-adorned butter dish or a delicately illustrated cushion.

While the larger and more colourful bunnies can look beautiful as part of an elevated tablescape, choosing pieces that are a little more subdued can acknowledge the spirit of Easter while keeping the overall aesthetic rooted in a country, luxury aesthetic that feels international.

Neptune Haydon Felt Jumping Hare Hanging Decoration

RRP£15
Available from Neptune
Shop now

Cox & Cox Six Bunny Wire Napkin Rings

RRP£16.50
Available from Cox & Cox
Shop now

What a Host Arley Easter White Bunnies Set

RRP£29.99
Available from What a Host
Shop now

5. Put spring blooms in vases

Nothing captures the feeling of the season quite like the scent of fresh tulips, daffodils and hyacinths wafting through a room. For an elevated look, arrange your flowers in a relaxed ‘just-picked’ style. Rachael Kennedy, floral designer at award-winning floral design studio Acacia Creative Studio, notes that ‘Easter is the moment the season truly shifts. It's less about formality and more about freshness and movement.’

She suggests embracing the natural silhouette of the season. ‘We love filling vessels with spring stems and seeing how the softer stems of fritillaria, tulips and hellebores naturally curve and bend, giving a unique sense of character and sculpture to a vase. Enjoy embracing looseness and asymmetry. Let the stems speak. Choose one or two varieties and repeat them in abundance.’

To finish the look, Kennedy suggests varying vase heights across a table or a mantel to create a layered, immersive effect.

Oliver Bonas Green & White Striped Ceramic Vase

RRP£42.50
Available from Oliver Bonas
Shop now

The White Company Witcombe Frill Vase

RRP£45
Available from The White Company
Shop now

M&S Medium Splatter Ceramic Vase

RRP£15
Available from M&S
Shop now

6. Decorate an Easter tree

A staple of modern Easter styling, an Easter tree offers a lovely focal point. If you love a touch of foraged charm, go for a generous bundle of pussy willow or dried branches tucked into a heavy ceramic vase or a rustic stoneware crock. However, if you’re looking for something you can enjoy for years to come, consider an artificial twig tree. Much like your Christmas tree, you can bring it down from the loft each year, and enjoy decorating it with hand-decorated eggs, felt characters or glass baubles with family.

Where you put it matters just as much as what’s on it. To really get people talking, place your tree on a hallway console table to greet guests, nestle it onto a windowsill, or have it front and centre on a sideboard.

Emma Bridgewater Mini Eggs Small Tin Egg Decoration

RRP£3
Available from Emma Bridgewater
Shop now

The White Company Swirl and Spots Glass Egg Decorations

RRP£25
Available from The White Company
Shop now

Daylesford Clarissa Florist Rabbit Decoration

RRP£18
Available from Daylesford
Shop now

7. Bring the outdoors in with potted plants

Look beyond cut flowers and incorporate potted bulbs. Easter is the perfect moment to reflect on what is happening just beyond the window. ‘Bringing potted plants and spring bulbs indoors creates an atmosphere of warmth, reflecting those brighter mornings and the hummings of spring,’ says Kennedy.

She also suggests grouping bulbs such as hyacinths, tete-a-tete daffodils, muscari or crocus together rather than spacing them evenly, as clustering them creates impact and feels far more natural. ‘Vary the vessels to add depth and texture. Terracotta pots, zinc containers and lined woven baskets all work beautifully and keep the look cohesive. We like to leave bulbs slightly exposed, intentionally part of the display, showing their growth.’

8. Fill pots and vases with chocolate treats

Easter is a time for indulgence, and your favourite treats can become a part of your decor. Fill a large, clear jar with pastel-coloured eggs or gold-wrapped bunnies to place on a console table or kitchen island. This creates a playful and inviting display.

For a more subtle approach, nestle small foil-wrapped eggs into the moss of your potted plants, creating a minute egg hunt.

9. Decorate and paint your own eggs

The egg is the ultimate symbol of the season, and hand-decorating it can be a sophisticated pursuit. Rather than using bright colours, opt for pastel tones. Once dried, eggs can be displayed in a simple wooden bowl on a sideboard, on a table as part of the Easter tablescape or hung from an Easter tree.

10. Fashion a bunny napkin for the table

Add a touch of whimsy to your place settings by forming your napkins into subtle bunny shapes. This playful nod to the season feels sophisticated rather than kitsch when crafted from napkins in quality materials like linen or cotton, in muted oatmeal or dove grey.

Avoid stiff, synthetic materials; instead, let the fabric's natural drape give a relaxed, organic look. Secure the centre with a piece of jute twine or a delicate velvet ribbon in a complementary colour.

11. Make Easter bakes take centre stage

The kitchen is often the heart of all celebrations hosted at home, and your seasonal baking can double as a beautiful decorative element. Display a stack of golden hot cross buns or bunny-shaped pastries on top of a pretty cake stand or a show-stopping Simnel cake under a glass dome.

The warm aroma of cinnamon and citrus is as much a part of Easter decor as a bunch of fresh daffodils. Surround these treats with a few fresh sprigs of rosemary or scattered petals for a full-on sensual delight.

Ferm Living Fountain Scalloped-Edge Stoneware Cake Stand

RRP£85
Available from Selfridges
Shop now

M&S Glass Cake Stand

RRP£40
Available from M&S
Shop now

Wedgwood Royal Albert Polka Rose Vintage 2 Tier Cake Stand

RRP£75
Available from Wedgwood
Shop now

12. Throw in touches of gingham

Nothing says 'country spring' quite like the timeless appeal of gingham. To keep the look grown-up, use the pattern sparingly: a set of oversized gingham napkins, a micro-check lampshade, a neutral cushion or a simple ribbon tied around a bunch of tulips.

This cheerful print introduces a sense of nostalgia and refinement without veering into overly rustic territory, making it a great option if you wish to extend your decor past the Easter period and into true spring and summer.

Piglet in Bed Botanical Green Gingham Linen Blend Duvet Set

RRPfrom £184
Available from Piglet in Bed
Shop now

Walton & Co Gingham Blue Ruffle Square Cushion

RRP£22
Available from Harts of Stur
Shop now

M&S Full Pleat Gingham Table Lamp

RRP£49.50
Available from M&S

13. Add a showstopping hanging wreath

For those looking to create a true 'wow' moment, a large-scale hanging wreath suspended above a dining table is a breathtaking addition. This floral chandelier can be constructed from a light wire hoop adorned with trailing ovy, jasmine and delicate silk ribbons. It draws the eye upwards and creates an ethereal look for your Easter feast.

Dashing a few small glass baubles or lightweight egg ornaments within the greenery can add a magical, shimmering quality as they catch the light from the light below.

The beauty of these Easter decorating ideas is that they aren't just for now; you can refer back to them year after year, taking an edited and curated approach to your decor as your style evolves. It's all about building a collection of seasonal staples that feel as fresh each spring as the day you first brought them home.

To really delight your guests this year, why not incorporate some edible decor into your tablescape? This milk chocolate hare lollypop from Daylesford would make for a sweet finishing touch and is a simple way of incorporating a bunny motif into your seasonal decor.

item.title: WIN! A luxury silk bundle for spring from Carpe Diem Beds, worth over £500
item.dc:creator: The English Home
item.description: Shut out the light and drift to sleep with ease in the most luxurious silk bundle from Carpe Diem Beds.
item.content:encoded:
Spring is a season of change, but your sleep shouldn't suffer as the days get longer. We are giving away a curated collection of luxury Mulberry silk essentials by Carpe Diem Beds, designed to transform your rest into a high-end retreat.

What's included in the prize?

  • Silk Sleep Mask

  • Silk Robe

  • Silk Pillowcase

  • Bedrooms as Sanctuaries

Whether facing increasing daylight, travelling by air, or indulging in a relaxing afternoon nap on the couch, the silk sleep mask is the perfect companion. It provides total darkness and comfort, ensuring sleep remains deep and undisturbed.

Crafted from the finest 16-momme Mulberry silk, these items offer an exquisitely soft, sumptuous feel against the skin. Because silk naturally regulates temperature and absorbs moisture, it keeps the body dry and comfortable – ideal for the unpredictable temperature shifts of springtime.

A silk pillowcase does more than just aid a calmer sleep. Unlike other fabrics, silk absorbs less moisture, helping the skin retain its natural hydration. The smooth surface reduces pulling and creasing on delicate facial skin, allowing for a refreshed morning glow.

The exclusive silk robe adds a touch of elegance to any morning or evening routine. Perfect for relaxing at home, it allows the wearer to enjoy the irresistibly soft feel of genuine luxury against their skin.

The Bedrooms as Sanctuaries book explores the bedroom as a place of stillness, ritual and renewal. Featuring dream spaces by famous designers, architects and visionaries, alongside expert guidance on sleep and interior principles, the book invites you to discover how design and rest intertwine.

To enter, simply fill in the form below. Competition ends 23:59pm GMT on Monday 11 May 2026:

View this article on our website to enter

item.title: 19 beautiful country bathroom ideas to master the traditional look
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: We round up 19 country bathroom ideas to make your bath time all the more soothing after a long walk in the British countryside.
item.content:encoded:
Creating a scheme that feels both timeless and lived-in is the ultimate goal when gathering country bathroom ideas.

This aesthetic ranges from the cosy, rustic look of a cosy cottage to the grand, traditional elegance of a stately home, offering endless bathroom ideas for any home, country cottages to urban apartments.

To start, invest in foundational pieces like this antique-style soap holder from Amazon, which instantly adds a sense of history. From there, it's all about the layers. Here, we explore 19 ways to bring that English country charm to your bathroom.

1. Design the space as an extension of the living room

'I like to keep a bathroom timeless by making it feel like a proper room - though usually not with carpet, unless a client specifically asks for it,' says Henriette von Stockhausen, creative director at luxury interior design studio VSP Interiors.

By introducing elements usually reserved for the living room - like a comfortable armchair or a decorative rug - the bathroom becomes a destination rather than just a stop-off, helping it feel like a cohesive part of the home rather than an isolated, tiled box.

Ruggable Calabria Multicolour Rug

RRPfrom £99
Available from Ruggable
Shop now

La Redoute Intérieurs Rattan Armchair

RRP£195
Available from Next
Shop now

M&S Gingham Bolster Cushion

RRP£19.50
Available from M&S
Shop now

2. Soften the walls with heritage wallpaper

Apply a beautiful print to create an instant sense of character and depth. 'I use wallpaper (yes, even in bathrooms) - it really helps create that ‘room’ feeling,' notes von Stockhausen. This technique instantly breaks up the monotony of tiles and makes the walls feel wrapped in history and texture.

3. Hide the shower with frameless glass

Choose a frameless glass enclosure to help the shower blend in. 'A glass shower enclosure helps it disappear into the background, and sometimes I’ll even back it with glass to continue the wallpaper behind,' says von Stockhausen.

This ensures the modern necessity of a walk-in shower doesn't interrupt the traditional visual flow or the beautiful patterns of the room.

4. Incorporate antique furniture

Repurpose a vintage chest of drawers or a sturdy washstand. This avoids the off-the-shelf look and ensures your bathroom feels like it has a personal history. It is the perfect way to introduce high-quality timber and unique craftsmanship into a space that often feels dominated by mass-produced porcelain.

5. Ditch the downlights for decorative wall sconces

Swap out harsh, recessed spotlights for elegant wall sconces placed at eye level. For the ultimate country charm, choose bathroom lighting with pleated shades. 'I love using fabric shades on wall lights to soften the glow,' says von Stockhausen.

This eye-level glow is far more flattering for the morning mirror routine and infinitely more inviting for a relaxed evening.

Neptune Scalloped Raffia Wall Light

RRP£95
Available from Neptune
Shop now

Pooky Paragon Wall Fitting

RRP£122
Available from Pooky
Shop now

M&S Wicker Scalloped Wall Light

RRP£39.50
Available from M&S
Shop now

6. Hang artwork in vintage frames

Elevate the room by displaying paintings or framed sketches. von Stockhausen believes the functional elements should take a backseat to the decor. 'I treat the necessary bathroom elements as incidental - there because they have to be - so I try to visually soften them,' she says. A landscape oil painting in a gilt frame can transform a simple washroom into a curated space.

7. Mask plumbing with a gathered fabric skirt

Embrace a touch of nostalgia by using fabric to cover your basin's underside or an open vanity unit. 'A gathered skirt underneath a sink can soften the feel of a bathroom and hide a myriad of things,' says Hampshire-based interior designer Emma Sherlock.

It's an ideal solution for a rustic home where you want to introduce a playful floral pattern while keeping cleaning supplies and spare towels neatly out of sight.

8. Opt for understated, timeless marble

Select marble finishes that feel timeless rather than trendy. While bold, heavily veined stones are popular in modern design, they can be overwhelming in a classic setting. 'I tend to keep marble fairly understated so it doesn’t date too quickly - you can tire of a bold marble,' says von Stockhausen.

Stick to subtle Carrara or honed limestone for a look that will still feel elegant and sophisticated in 20 years' time.

9. Add upholstery for a touch of luxury

Include an upholstered stool, a bench, or a linen-covered chair if the space allows. This is the ultimate cosy touch for a country-inspired home, providing a comfortable place to sit while the bath runs or to lay out a fresh robe. This adds a soft texture that instantly balances the harder surfaces of the tub and floor.

Dusk Nyla Accent Chair

RRP£189
Available from Dusk
Shop now

Dunelm Woven Stripe Stool

RRP£49
Available from Dunelm
Shop now

M&S Sherwood Dark Oak Footstool

RRP£119
Available from M&S
Shop now

10. Frame windows with full-length curtains

Swap out functional rollers for full-length curtains or Roman blinds in a heritage fabric. Fabric helps to dampen acoustics, making the room feel quieter, more private and less echoey.

Curtains in a classic ticking stripe or a botanical print help bridge the gap between the interior and the rolling garden views outside.

11. Utilise wooden shelving or built-in ledges for rustic charm

Look upward to find extra storage space that doubles as decor. 'Antique shelving above a bath can add real charm to a bathroom and be useful for storage,' says Sherlock. Using a weathered timber shelf to display glass apothecary jars and sprigs or dried lavender keeps your essentials within reach without sacrificing that authentic, historical feel that is central to the English country aesthetic.

Ferm Living Cotton Pad Container

RRP£25
Available from Nordic Nest
Shop now

The White Company Glass Jar

RRP£25
Available from The White Company
Shop now

H&M Marble Soap Dispenser

RRP£24.99
Available from H&M
Shop now

12. Incorporate woven and natural textures

'If you want to soften the feel of a bathroom, using natural materials (like baskets for storage) will help,' suggests Sherlock. Replace clinical plastic containers with natural materials to ground the design and give it a rustic touch. Wicker laundry baskets or small rattan trays for soaps provide that vital texture that defines the rural look.

H&M Seagrass Laundry Basket

RRP£59.99
Available from H&M
Shop now

M&S Rattan Storage Box

RRP£15
Available from M&S
Shop now

Neptune Ashcroft Rectangular Tray

RRP£19
Available from Neptune
Shop now

13. Keep the daily product count low

Maintain a sense of tranquillity by keeping surfaces clear of modern plastic bottles and accessories. By decanting essentials into glass bottles or tucking them away, you allow the natural materials and period of the bathroom to truly take centre stage and shine.

14. Source living-finish brassware for authenticity

Select taps and hardware in unlacquered brass or aged bronze. These materials will naturally tarnish over time, adding a sense of age and authenticity that shiny chrome cannot match in a country-inspired home. Unlike modern finishes, these living metals develop a unique patina that perfectly complements the weathered textures and warm, original woods of an English country home.

15. Lay flagstone flooring for a grounded feel

Ground the room with natural stone tiles to create a sense of permanence and architectural weight. Tumbled limestone or slate flagstones offer an earthy foundation that feels perfectly appropriate for a rural scheme. When paired with underfloor heating, they provide a wonderfully tactile experience on chilly mornings while maintaining a rugged, heritage aesthetic that only gets better with age.

16. Experiment with soft heritage colour

Introduce gentle tones like dusty blue, sage green, or plaster pink to move away from a standard cream and wood palette. A coloured vanity unit can add a contemporary edge that still feels traditional. These soft hues sit beautifully atop exposed floors, bringing a breath of fresh air and personality into a bathroom.

17. Install a freestanding bath

Position a freestanding bathtub as the star of the show to instantly anchor the room's country credentials. Whether you opt for a traditional roll-top with claw feet or a contemporary tin boat bath, a standalone tub creates a sense of luxury and architectural interest. It provides the perfect place to fully submerge and wash away the day's stresses, serving as a beautiful focal point that invites you to slow down.

18. Find a substantial wood mirror

Hang a mirror with a substantial wooden frame to instantly reinforce the rustic context of the room. Whether the wood is sleek and polished or heavily antiqued and distressed, it serves as a necessary focal point. A bathroom mirror not only bounces light around a potentially dark room but also softens the tiles within its organic frame.

Oliver Bonas Mango Wood Wall Mirror

RRP£98
Available from Oliver Bonas
Shop now

Hastshilp Serena Oval Rattan Mirror

RRP£295
Available from The Roost
Shop now

M&S Marquetry Wood Wall Mirror

RRP£129
Available from M&S
Shop now

19. Install traditional wood panelling

Embrace the architectural charm of wainscoting or tongue-and-groove panelling to ground the bathroom's country aesthetic. This classic wood detail not only adds an immediate layer of warmth and texture but also serves a practical purpose in protecting your walls.

For a quintessentially English feel, paint the panelling in a deep heritage hue like a forest green or a muted charcoal that allows lighter porcelain and brassware to pop.

Whether you're lucky enough to have a sprawling master suite or are looking to inject charm into a small bathroom, the secret to the English country look is in the curation of thoughtful details. By stepping away from the clinical and embracing the 'proper room' philosophy, you create a space that doesn't just serve a function but offers a genuine place to unwind. To finish the look, consider an accessory that feels quintessentially pastoral, like this ruffled hand towel from M&S.

item.title: 13 beautiful small living room ideas to make the most of a snug floor plan
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: Beautifully simple ways to make a small living room feel bigger, brighter and more stylish.
item.content:encoded:
Finding the right small living room ideas is a balancing act between honiring a home's character and making sure a compact footprint feels liveable.

Whether you're navigating a flat or a snug cottage, you should embrace the proportions rather than fighting them. Success usually comes down to living room ideas that view a limited floor plan as the perfect invitation to create a cocooning space.

Of course cosiness usually comes from textiles, from cushions to super soft throws like this wool-blend blanket from H&M, but here we explore the more foundational ways of designig a small living room, from the use of colour to the clever placement of furniture.

1. Elongate with fabric

To draw the eye upward and stop a low ceiling from feeling heavy, hang your curtains higher than the actual window frame.

'Long curtains that gently pool on the floor can make the space feel taller and more expansive, especially when paired with layered fabrics,' suggests designer and founder of her epynomous textile brand, Tori Murphy. This adds a hit of vertical grandeur and avoids that awkward shunted look that short curtains often give a small room.

2. Reflect with strategic mirrors

Mirrors are the oldest trick in the book for a reason: they act like interior windows. Beyond hanging them, try balancing a large, ornate mirror on a mantelpiece to add depth to a chimney breast, or cluster several smaller antique frames together on a narrow wall to create a curated gallery feel that makes the room feel double its actual size.

M&S Wooden Rounded Overmantle Mirror

RRP£119
Available from M&S
Shop now

Oliver Bonas Mother of Pearl Mantle Mirror

RRP£265
Available from Oliver Bonas
Shop now

Next Twist Over Mantel Wall Mirror

RRP£150
Available from Next
Shop now

3. Layer textures and patterns

Don't fall into the trap of thinking a small room needs to be plain and beige to look bigger. 'Don't shy away from pattern, as it brings personality, making the room feel inviting and lived-in rather than relying on minimalism to try to extend the space,' notes Cath Beckett, co-founder of London-based interior design studio Yellow London. Mixing a bold floral with a classic stripe creates visual depth that makes walls feel further away.

4. Elevate furniture on legs

When shopping for sofas or armchairs, look for 'leggy' pieces. Being able to see the floor continue right to the skirting board prevents the room from feeling weighed down by chunky upholstery. It's a simple visual trick that lets light flow under your furniture, making the whole layout feel much airier.

5. Drench the walls in colour

Paint your walls, skirting boards and even radiators in the same shade to blur the room's edges. This colour-drenching technique means your eyes don't get distracted by the sharp contrast of white woodwork, which effectively makes the corners of the room blend into a seamless, wrap-around look.

6. Lean into cosiness

Instead of trying to make a small room look vast with bright whites, lean into its natural cosiness. 'We lean into the cosy atmosphere a smaller living room naturally offers, enhancing its sense of intimacy,' says Beckett. Deep blues or earthy terracottas can turn a tiny sitting room into a high-impact sanctuary that feels intentional rather than cramped.

7. Stripe your surfaces

Use vertical patterns to trick the eye into perceiving more height. 'Choose lengthening patterns, like our Harbour Stripe on curtains or blinds, as they naturally elongate the room,' suggests Murphy. Stripes act like a visual ladder, directing the gaze upward and adding structure to narrow walls.

Habitat Stripe Ruffle Cushion

RRP£12
Available from Argos
Shop now

TBCo Cotton Tie-Top Curtains

RRPfrom £179
Available from TBCo
Shop now

Oliver Bonas Striped Ceramic Vase

RRP£42.50
Available from Oliver Bonas
Shop now

8. Reclaim the window bay

In many homes, the window bay becomes a bit of a dead zone. Building a bespoke window seat with hidden storage underneath gives you extra seating without needing a bulky armchair.

If bespoke building isn't on the cards, including a regular armchair here will make the most of the space; by tucking it near the bay, you create a dedicated reading nook that keeps the centre of the room clear for moving around.

9. Upsize your rug

It sounds wrong, but a tiny rug actually makes a room look smaller by breaking the floor into islands. Go for a large, textured wool rug that allows all your furniture legs to sit on top. This anchors the room and tricks the brain into seeing the whole floor as one big, cohesive space.

Ruggable X Anthropologie Blue Rug

RRPfrom £119
Available from Ruggable
Shop now

Natural N. Premium Ashylyn Jute Rug

RRPfrom £110
Available from Next
Shop now

Dunelm Botanical Scalloped Washable Rug

RRP£99
Available from Dunelm
Shop now

10. Draw the eye skyward

Put a bookshelf or a picture rail just a few inches below the ceiling. This draws the eye to the very top of the walls, highlighting the height of the room rather than the narrowness of the floor. It's also a great way to show off your favourite books and homeware without using up any precious floor space.

11. Swap coffee tables for ottomans

Hard-edged coffee tables can be a bit of a stumbling block in a small room. An upholstered ottoman is far more versatile; top it with a tray for drinks and accessories, use it for an extra seat for guests, or just put your feet up. Plus, they're easier to shuffle around when you need extra space.

12. Optimise with built-in units

To save on precious floor space, swap free-standing furniture for bespoke cabinetry that spans an entire wall. Built-in units can be designed to include mirrored back-panels or glass-fronted doors, which act like secret assistants by bouncing ambient light back into the room.

Choosing a lacquered finish or adding polished brass hardware to the cupboard doors provides that essential touch of jewellery while keeping the overall look streamlined and clutter-free.

13. Anchor the space with a hero piece

Instead of filling the room with lots of small accessories that can quickly look like clutter, pick one standout hero piece. Whether it's a giant floor lamp or a large painting, having one bold focal point makes the room feel like a deliberate design rather than a collection of small things.

The most successful small living room ideas are thosethat prioritise flexibilit and a cosy atmosphere. From living room curtain ideas tothe creative use of colour and pattern, focusing on smart proportions and a few expert-led tweaks ensure your space can become a hard-working part of the home.

To keep your new refreshed sitting room feeling its best, a finishing touch like this bay and rosemary scented candle from St Eval can scentscape the space and acts as an easy coffee table styling idea too.

item.title: How to choose the perfect iron bed for your bedroom
item.dc:creator: The English Home
item.description: Choosing a bed is one of the most important decisions when designing a bedroom. It shapes comfort, atmosphere and visual balance.
item.content:encoded:
Interior designers often recommend iron beds for both character homes and contemporary interiors. These frames offer timeless elegance alongside practical durability. Their open structure also helps rooms feel lighter and more spacious.

Today’s homeowners are increasingly drawn to furniture with lasting value. British-made craftsmanship and heritage design are becoming central considerations.

For those exploring traditional styles, a wide selection of handcrafted designs is available from the British bed-maker Iron and Brass Bed Company.

Understanding the role of the bed in interior design

A bed naturally becomes the focal point of a bedroom. Its proportions influence how the entire space feels. Designers therefore consider scale, height and visual weight carefully.

Iron beds provide strong structure without appearing bulky. Their slender framework allows light to move around the room. This creates a sense of calm and openness.

In period properties, especially, iron beds complement architectural features beautifully. Decorative plasterwork, fireplaces and sash windows remain visible rather than overshadowed.

This balance between presence and lightness makes iron beds particularly versatile.

Choosing the right size for your space

Selecting the correct bed size is essential for both comfort and layout. Designers recommend measuring carefully before making a decision.

In smaller bedrooms, a double iron bed can maintain flow while still offering generous sleeping space. The open frame prevents the room from feeling crowded.

Larger bedrooms may suit king or super king designs. Taller headboards can enhance ceiling height and create a sense of grandeur.

It is also important to consider the walking space around the bed. A well-proportioned layout improves daily comfort and visual harmony.

High footboard or low footboard?

Footboard height can significantly influence bedroom style. Interior designers often use this feature to shape the atmosphere.

High footboards like the Emma iron bed by Iron and Brass Bed Company feel traditional and cocooning. They suit period homes, country interiors and more formal schemes.

Low footboards create openness and a relaxed mood. This style works well in contemporary settings or compact rooms.

The choice ultimately depends on personal preference and room proportions. Both options offer timeless appeal when thoughtfully styled.

Selecting the perfect finish and colour

Iron beds are available in a wide range of finishes. Colour choice plays an important role in defining bedroom character.

Soft neutrals such as ivory, stone or muted grey feel calm and versatile. They suit layered bedding and heritage interiors beautifully.

Darker finishes can introduce drama and contrast. Deep bronze, charcoal or black frames work particularly well in townhouse bedrooms.

Painted iron beds also offer flexibility. Frames can often be refreshed over time to suit evolving design schemes.

Considering craftsmanship and longevity

Quality construction should always guide furniture choices. Interior designers encourage investment in pieces designed to last.

Handcrafted iron beds are shaped using traditional techniques refined over generations. This creates frames that feel both robust and elegant.

British-made beds offer additional reassurance. Local production supports skilled makers while reducing environmental impact.

Importantly, some manufacturers like Norfolk-based Iron and Brass Bed Company now hold official Made in Britain certification. This mark confirms genuine UK manufacturing standards.

For homeowners seeking authenticity, this level of transparency is increasingly valued.

Styling your iron bed for lasting appeal

Once the ideal frame is chosen, styling brings the bedroom to life. Designers recommend layering natural textures to soften the metal structure.

Crisp cotton sheets, linen cushions and wool throws create warmth and comfort. Patterned quilts or heritage prints can introduce personality.

Lighting also plays a key role. Wall lights or classic table lamps help frame the bed while enhancing the atmosphere.

Thoughtful styling ensures an iron bed remains relevant as interiors evolve.

A timeless investment in comfort and character

Ultimately, the perfect iron bed balances beauty, practicality and longevity. It should feel appropriate to the home while supporting restful sleep.

Interior designers continue to favour iron beds because they offer enduring versatility. These frames work equally well in traditional and modern settings.

For those seeking authentic handcrafted British-made designs, explore the full collection at Iron and Brass Bed Company. A family company based in Norfolk, handcrafting iron and metal bedframes for over 20 years.

A carefully chosen iron bed can shape the feel of a bedroom for decades. It becomes both a design statement and a trusted foundation for everyday comfort.

item.title: A seasonal chicken and wild garlic pie recipe with a golden crust and aromatic filling for a spring weekend lunch
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: A golden, seasonal classic that captures the fleeting, fragrant arrival of wild garlic in a comforting farmhouse pie.
item.content:encoded:
This chicken and wild garlic pie is the ultimate celebration of the British foraging season, pairing tender roasted thighs with punchy, vibrant notes for a seasonal twist on a farmhouse classic.

As we look for more spring recipes, this dish is a rewarding project for a slow weekend. Whether served with buttered greens or enjoyed as a cold slice, it captures the very best of the early season.

For a perfectly crisp finish, using a steam vent like this ceramic pie bird from Amazon will ensure the steam escapes and the pastry stays golden. Discover how to recreate this savoury showstopper below, sourced from The Crown, Bildeston, a pub set in the glorious Suffolk countryside.

Overview

Serves: 4

Ingredients

  • 600ml whole milk

  • 300ml cream

  • 150g flour

  • 150g unsalted butter

  • 1 tablespoon Dijon

  • 1 tablespoon wholegrain mustard

  • 1 handful of wild garlic

  • 80g fine-grated Parmesan

  • Salt and black pepper to taste

  • 1 roll store-bought short pastry

  • 1 Egg yolk mixed with a tablespoon of cream

Method

  1. Preheat your oven to 180 degrees.

  2. Season your chicken and coat it in olive oil.

  3. Place the chicken on a tray, skin side up and roast in the oven for 25 minutes until golden and cooked through and leave it on the side to cool.

  4. In a heavy-based pan, melt the butter and add the flour and cook out like a roux.

  5. Add the milk and cream and whisk until smooth.

  6. Cook gently until it thickens (approximately 15 minutes).

  7. Add the mustard and parmesan and season to taste.

  8. Chop up the chicken into bite-sized pieces and stir through.

  9. Finally, add the wild garlic and stir it in.

  10. Season again to taste and chill the mixture.

  11. Line a suitably sized pastry tin with the pastry.

  12. Fill generously with the mix.

  13. Top the pie with pastry and crimp the edges with your fingers

  14. Brush the pie with the egg yolk mix and poke a little hole in the centre to let the steam come out.

  15. Bake for approximately 30 minutes at 180 degrees or a bit longer until golden and crisp.

  16. Let it sit for 5-10 minutes, then enjoy with your favourite Oaky Chardonnay.

Recipe sourced from Chef Greig Young, The Crown, Bildeston

Giving this dish the spotlight it deserves starts with the right presentation. Here are a few of our favourite pie dishes to help you achieve a perfectly crispy base and bring this seasonal favourite to the table.

Emma Bridgewater Enamel Pie Dish

RRP£40
Available from Emma Bridgewater
Shop now

Le Creuset Stoneware Round Pie Dish

RRP£52
Available from Le Creuset
Shop now

Neptune Bramley Stoneware Pie Dish

RRP£29
Available from Neptune
Shop now

To ensure your meal stays as nutritious as possible, it is worth considering the tools you use. Preparing your pie with the best non-toxic cookware ensures that your ingredients remain free from unwanted chemicals.

Whether you're enjoying a generous slice at the kitchen table or hosting a weekend lunch, this dish is all about healthy, hearty food. For those inspired by the season, this wild garlic recipe book from Amazon offers even more ways to use this fragrant ingredient, while our soft courgette and chickpea recipe is another vibrant way to celebrate spring's arrival.

item.title: Chandeliers, peacocks and stately Italian busts - this Suffolk home is filled with colour, history and a touch of the dramatic
item.dc:creator: Charlotte Dunford
item.description: As part of his book, The Layered Home: Inspiration for Creating Cozy, Collected Rooms, Benjamin Reynaert discovers the characterful Suffolk home of Ambrice Miller
item.content:encoded:
It is often the homes that tell a story, of pieces collected and of lives lived, that feel the most captivating and inspiring.

Far from prescribing to specific interior design styles or rulebooks about how a room should behave, editor and stylist Benjamin Reynaert believes the key to creating interiors that feel both beautiful and personal is to focus on what feels right.

In his first book, The Layered Home: Inspiration for Creating Cozy, Collected Rooms, Benjamin offers an aspirational guide on how to curate and transform interiors by embracing abundance, imperfection and craft. Amongst the pages are a wealth of homes exquisitely decorated by creative and curious individuals. Here, Benjamin talks us through one such home: a former malt master's house dating back to the 17th-century in Suffolk belonging to Ambrice Miller, founder of Relic Interiors.

Every room in Ambrice Miller's home features incredible vignettes, curated collections, and compelling artwork, seemingly on every tabletop surface and wall throughout. It feels sweetly unpredictable yet coordinated in how it all comes together. One thing I noticed was Ambrice’s unfettered approach to hanging art. Nothing feels overly precious or too measured, and there’s a sense that things can and will change if you blink.

I love how Ambrice promotes the idea that art, and collecting, is not just for one type of person or group. Students, young families, solo collectors, and everyone else has the right to chase the feeling of discovery, because what we bring into our homes is an extension of who we are as people and the places we’ve been or want to go.

The atelier that is Relic Interiors can be seen as a catchall for old-world finds destined for new beginnings. These rooms are ever-changing in the inventory they hold, sometimes only for the swiftest of stints before they're claimed by a buyer and shipped off to another address. It's the atmosphere Ambrice is used to, relocating these wares to make room for the latest antique market discoveries while assessing how these treasures will resonate with her audience and devoted clientele.

In the atelier, and in her private living spaces, absolutely everything is on display, from the family of chandeliers that dangles above a stately Italian bust, to the array of portraits, to an exotic painted wood peacock situated upon the dining room table (a set design remnant from a 1980s production at the Royal Albert Hall).

Growing up with interiors-obsessed parents was only the beginning for Ambrice. From those beginnings, what bloomed was a deep-rooted love of art and discovery that further evolved into a cultivated shop where fixtures, furniture, and artwork coexist in shambolic harmony waiting their turn on a wall or as a tabletop centerpiece.

Ambrice says that the goal of all this goes far beyond feeding a joy of collecting to also noticing something wonderful that's happening with younger generations looking to invest in their own collections. 'Everyone remembers the first couple of pieces they bought. Especially if you're a student or if you're a new entrant to the workforce and you buy something with your first couple of paychecks, you're like, wow, it's really something special.'

Ambrice believes that the best home life is one filled with colour, history, and a touch of the dramatic. Everything is cherished but not so overly precious that it's untouchable. Even with her young son bopping around, he is constantly exposed to the rotating parade of finds and wares that come inside. 'Our homes are meant to be lived in. I can't imagine anything worse than not being able to feel like you can properly relax in your own home.'

Ambrice's home is a prime example of the joy and personality found objects can bring. For those eager to begin sourcing hidden treasures for their own interiors, our guide to antiques offers expert advice from a whole host of renowned designers.

item.title: OKA’s faux florals are the ultimate interior hack – Here are our favourite everlasting blooms to shop now
item.dc:creator: Kate Freud
item.description: Homeware brand OKA’s faux flower collection will have even your most green-fingered friends fooled, creating floral arrangements to last forever.
item.content:encoded:
In a sea of unrealistic, plastic-looking fakes, OKA faux flowers are a total game-changer.

Forget everything you know about dusty artificial petals; these are hands-down the best faux flowers for anyone who wants a high-end look without the high-maintenance upkeep.

A sculptural base like this red and white stripe vase is the perfect vessel for your new faux arrangement. Here, we look at how luxury homeware brand OKA masters the art of the everlasting bouquet and share our favourites to shop now.

OKA stores are a constant rotation of fresh interior inspiration that regularly shows you how to layer a home. But between the sofas and cushions, you'll find the dedicated 'Bunched' florist stations, a creative playground where the focus is on botanically accurate stems - complete with the organic curves and subtle colour shifts you would find in nature.

Many people love the idea of faux flowers, but are daunted by the prospect of creating a natural-looking arrangement. But these aren’t your average stems – they’re so realistic even your green-fingered friends will struggle to spot the difference.

Once you've curated your stems, the magic happens in how you ground them within the room. A great floral arrangement shouldn't sit in isolation; it works best when it's part of the scheme. item.title: We’ve finally found the best faux flowers that actually look real and the only ones worth buying
item.dc:creator: Jill Morgan
item.description: Delicately crafted faux flowers and plants bring joy and beauty into the home all year round.
item.content:encoded:
Integrating life-like greenery into your interior scheme is the ultimate way to elevate a room, and finding the best faux flowers is the secret to maintaining a lush look year-round.

Of course, fresh flowers are lovely, but they can be a bit of a high-maintenance struggle in draughty hallways or those darker corners where nothing seems to manage to stay alive.

If you find yourself struggling to nurture 'virtually unkillable' plants alive, like this snake plant from Crocus, then this curated guide to the best faux flowers to buy is here to save your space.

Faux houseplants

As much as we love biophilic interiors, a plastic-looking tree can compromise the aesthetic of a room instantly. When you are hunting for a Fiddle Leaf Fig or a Monstera, have a close look at the 'join' - that is where the leaf meets the stem. In premium plants, this should be tapered and neat, not a chunky plastic socket.

You also want to avoid anything too shiny; the best houseplants have real leaves that have a natural waxy glow, but they shouldn't look as though they have been polished and buffed. 'The number of leaves or branches is a good indicator of quality,' adds Liz Igwe, interior expert at home decor brand Dowsing & Reynolds. 'A faux plant should look like the healthiest version of the real plant.'

Cox & Cox Faux Ceramic Yucca

RRP£85
Available from Cox & Cox
`Shop now

OKA Faux Fiddle Plant

RRP£225
Available from OKA
Shop now

M&S Artificial Monstera Plant

RRP£25
Available from M&S
Shop now

The White Company Olive Potted Tree

RRP£180
Available from The White Company
Shop now

House of Isabella Travellers Palm Tree

RRP£104
Available from House of Isabella
Shop now

M&S Artificial Money Plant

RRP£15
Available from M&S
Shop now

Faux peonies

Peonies are the superstars of the faux world because they are wonderfully blousy. The trick to making them look authentic is variety. Avoid any bunch where every single flower is wide open; it simply doesn't happen in nature. Instead, look for sets that include a few tight, round buds alongside the larger blooms.

High-quality faux flowers often have a special coating that makes them feel velvety to the fingertips, which is a total game-changer for realism. Achieving this requires an informed choice of materials, as designer Abigail Ahern explains that the best faux flowers 'are made from a range of different materials, like silk, fabric or paper, to best emulate the structure and movement of real flowers. Many of them are hand-painted and finished to capture the subtle nuances of fading petals'

OKA Faux Full Peony Stem

RRP£18
Available from OKA
Shop now

Neptune Peony Spray

RRP£40
Available from Neptune
Shop now

Pottery Barn Faux Peony Bundle

RRP£59
Available from Pottery Barn
Shop now

Faux dahlias

Dahlias are almost architectural, which makes them translate brilliantly into faux materials. For a realistic display, seek out Juliet or Pom Pom varieties that feature dozens of layered petals.

Look for dahlias with realistic foliage too; the leaves should have a matte finish and serrated edges rather than a glossy, plastic sheen. These are particularly effective when styled in a vibrant orange or deep burgundy to add a punch of jewel-toned colour to a mantelpiece.

OKA Faux Double Dahlia Stem

RRP£18
Available from OKA
Shop now

Cox & Cox Faux Miniature Dahlia Spray

RRP£32
Available from Cox & Cox
Shop now

Pottery Barn Faux Yellow Dahlias

RRP£49
Available from Pottery Barn
Shop now

Faux greenery

Foliage is the backbone of any professional arrangement. Investing in high-quality greenery, such as eucalyptus or olive branches, allows for the creation of a permanent structure that can be updated with seasonal blooms throughout the year.

Look for frosted finishes on eucalyptus or the silvery-green underside of olive leaves; these textural details catch the light just like the natural wax-coated leaves. Plus, high-quality stems usually have a wire running through them, allowing for organic, drooping silhouettes.

OKA Faux Maidenhair Fern Stem

RRP£15
Available from OKA
Shop now

The White Company Eucalyptus Branches

RRP£40
Available from The White Company
Shop now

Pottery Barn Faux Bay Leaf Branch

RRP£39
Available from Pottery Barn
Shop now

Faux hydrangeas

Hydrangeas are a designer favourite for their ability to fill space with soft, architectural volume. The secret to a successful faux hydrangea is choosing antique or dried hydrangea varieties. As these flowers naturally dry out in the garden, they take on muted, dusty tones of teal, mauve and burnt orange.

High-quality versions capture that papery, muted quality, making them almost impossible to distinguish from the real thing. Avoid the saturated, neon-leaning cyans often found in lower-quality replicas; instead, seek out the muted, sun-bleached indigos or dusty teals that mimic a flower transitioning into autumn.

Ett Hem Hydrangea Stem

RRP£21
Available from The Roost
Shop now

OKA Faux Mophead Hydrangea Stem

RRP£20
Available from OKA
Shop now

Neptune Potted Hydrangea

RRP£85
Available from Neptune
Shop now

Pottery Barn Faux Hydrangeas

RRP£49
Available from Pottery Barn
Shop now

OKA Hydrangea Paniculata Stem

RRP£20
Available from OKA
Shop now

Neptune Hydrangea Stem

RRP£22
Available from Neptune
Shop now

Choosing the perfect vase

With myriad vase and container options, heed expert advice for the best results. “I think your choice of container is extremely important,” says Sue Jones, creative director at luxury homeware brand OKA.

'I personally like to use a medium-sized vase with a fluted neck, as this creates the impression of fuller, more rounded display. Remember to think about the style too – if you’re planning to use a particularly vibrant collection of flowers, opt for a more minimalist vase to let your blooms stand out,' she concludes.

'We recommend something opaque that hides your stems and the absence of water,' Ahern says. 'Earthy oversized vases, rustic pots and textural baskets in contrasting or subtle tones that complement your gathering are simple but very effective.'

M&S X Kelly Hoppen Petal Vase

RRP£19.50
Available from M&S
Shop now

The White Company Terracotta Jug

RRP£70
Available from The White Company
Shop now

H&M Large terracotta vase

RRP£69.99
Available from H&M
Shop now

How to care for faux flowers and plants

Premium faux stems will last for years, but do require occasional attention to keep the illusion alive. 'Petals and leaves can be wiped gently with a soft dry cloth or blown gently with a hairdryer on its coolest setting,' recommends Ahern.

If you decide to rotate your display, store them in a box in a damp-free and dust-free environment. This longevity is what makes high-end fauxs such a sustainable investment for the home.

The beauty of a great faux display is that it bridges the gap between seasons, keeping your home feeling alive even when the garden is dormant- whether you want to lighten up your coffee table with spring flowers or want to warm things up with orange leaves in the autumn.

To really finish the look, try placing your vase on a tray like this rattan tray from H&M; it adds a lovely layer of natural texture that makes an entire arrangement feel a bit more put together and styled.

item.title: 10 beautiful bathroom lighting ideas to add instant character and warmth
item.dc:creator: The English Home
item.description: Beautiful bathroom lighting design ideas for every style and budget.
item.content:encoded:
Designing a sanctuary that balances morning efficiency with evening serenity is all about finding the right bathroom lighting ideas.

Whether you are embarking on a full-scale renovation or simply looking to refresh a tired ensuite, these bathroom ideas will help layer your lighting like an interior designer.

Of course, the most atmospheric glow comes from a flicker of flame, so we suggest a luxury scented candle like this one from The White Company to create a spa-like retreat. Here, we explore how to perfectly illuminate your bathroom.

1. Frame with eye-level sconces

The right fixtures act as the jewellery of an interior, pulling the entire decorative scheme together while serving a vital practical purpose. Beyond the aesthetic, lighting the face properly is key to a successful vanity setup. 'We always try to position wall lights either side of the mirror at eye level,' says Sophie Pringle, co-founder of Surrey-based interior design studio Pringle & Pringle. 'This avoids harsh shadows and provides the most flattering, practical light for tasks like shaving or applying makeup.'

Melissa Hutley, co-founder of Surrey and London-based interior design studio Hutley & Humm agrees, emphasising that you must 'mount wall lights at eye level beside the mirror to kill unflattering shadows,' as this ensures the face is illuminated directly rather than just having light shining down from above.

Rockett St George Green Wall Lamp

RRP£60
Available from Next
Shop now

Pooky Calico Wall Light

RRP£110
Available from Pooky
Shop now

Dusk Glass Scallop Wall Light

RRP£49
Available from Dusk
Shop now

2. Introduce a statement chandelier

Nothing beats the dramatic style of a large chandelier hung above a classic roll-top bath. For a contemporary twist, look for clear crystal shallow chandeliers that carry a laboratory-certified IP44 rating. This ensures your statement piece is as safe as it is stylish, even in areas where it may be splashed or hit by steam.

Dunelm Art Deco Chandelier

RRP£119
Available from Dunelm
Shop now

M&S Metal 6 Arm Chandelier

RRP£129
Available from M&S
Shop now

Next Brass 3 Light Chandelier

RRP£175
Available from Next
Shop now

3. Layer your lighting for maximum flexibility

A well-thought-out scheme must balance practicality with atmosphere. 'Layered lighting is essential in a bathroom to balance practicality with atmosphere,' explains Pringle. 'We always combine well-placed downlights for task lighting with wall lights, feature pendants, and softer accent lighting. This allows the room to transition from functional in the morning to calm and relaxing in the evening.'

4. Recess spotlights thoughtfully

For detailed tasks at the basin, it is useful to install bright lights, but placement is everything. 'Downlights should never be placed in a grid,' advises Pringle. 'Instead, think about what you are lighting below and position them accordingly to avoid creating shadows.' When used correctly, recessed lighting gives a clean, architectural feel while still being highly functional.

5. Integrate lighting into the shower zone

Incorporating lighting within the shower or bath area instantly elevates a bathroom, says Pringle. 'We often use LED strips within niches or low-level wall washers to create a soft glow.' This not only highlights materials and detailing but also provides a gentle light at night without needing to switch on a brighter overhead light.

6. Soften the space with textile shades

Bathrooms can often feel cold due to the abundance of hard surfaces like tile and stone. 'We love including curtains in larger bathrooms where space allows,' says Hutley. 'Embracing textile or fabric shades to soften the hard edges of the room.' Using a pair of wall lamps with fine-plated shades adds an immediate sense of warmth to the vanity, making the space feel more like a proper living area.

Lighting Direct Twin Wall Light

RRP£44.99
Available from Lighting Direct
Shop now

Dunelm Chiffon Pleated Lamp Shade

RRP£25
Available from Dunelm
Shop now

Kelling Home Alotablots Lampshade

RRPfrom £40
Available from The Roost
Shop now

7. Add an illuminated mirror

Bathroom mirrors and mirrored cabinets that feature integral lighting are a brilliant two-in-one solution for the modern home. Many versions operate via an infrared sensor for a touchless experience, and some even include heated demista pads to keep your reflection clear. These allow you to switch from a clear morning brightness to a warm evening glow at the wave of a hand.

8. Introduce a pendant

You may at first associate this type of light with a kitchen or a hallway, but a statement pendant can transform a bathroom. 'It introduces a sense of luxury, softness, and personality, helping the space feel more like a room within the home rather than purely functional,' says Pringle. Mixing materials and finishes, such as bronze or frosted glass, can add depth and visual interest to the ceiling.

Dunelm Mondez Bathroom Pendant

RRP£70
Available from Dunelm
Shop now

Pooky Milford Check Square Pendant

RRP£63
Available from Pooky
Shop now

Laura Ashley Ceramic Scallop Pendant

RRP£85
Available from M&S
Shop now

9. Always install a dimmer

The final secret to a successful scheme is the dimmer switch. It allows the room to transform effortlessly from brisk morning sessions to leisurely evening baths. Being able to drop the light levels by half instantly changes the mood from functional to foundational, letting you drift off into bubble-filled bliss without a care in the world.

10. Don't overlook natural light

While we focus heavily on artificial fixtures, the sun is your best friend in a bathroom. Take care to consider the availability and direction of the natural light during the planning stage. Whether it's a skylight above the shower or a large window by the tub, maximising daylight not only makes the room feel larger but also provides the most accurate light for your morning routine.

The best bathroom lighting ideas make your space feel confident and relaxed. If you are working with a more compact floor plan, these techniques can be adapted to suit various small bathroom ideas, too.

To keep your newly lit sanctuary looking its best, we recommend investing in a decorative tray, like this marble tray from Dunelm, to organise your essentials with elegance.

item.title: Small kitchen island ideas: 10 clever ways to anchor your kitchen without the bulk
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: Expert ways to add extra surface space and style to a modest room.
item.content:encoded:
The kitchen doesn't need a vast footprint to benefit from the charm and utility that comes with the right small kitchen island ideas.

While we often associate a central hub with sprawling country estates, certain kitchen island ideas can transform even the most modest of floor plans into a hard-working culinary station.

To keep your surfaces clear, a beautiful stoneware jar like this one from H&M is the perfect finishing touch for a compact workspace. Below, we explore how to maximise every inch of a small footprint without sacrificing style or essential prep space.

1. Take a creative risk with colour

Don't be afraid to let your island be the start of the show, even if its footprint is modest. Because it isn't physically attached to the kitchen units, it's the perfect candidate to add a bit of personality. 'A kitchen island is one of the most natural places in the room to take a small creative risk with colour,' says Cotswold-based interior designer Sean Symington. 'Rather than committing every surface to a single shade, painting the island in a contrasting tone grounds it as a distinct piece within the space, almost like a freestanding piece of furniture.'

2. Embrace the versatility of a butcher's block

If a fixed island feels too permanent for a tight layout, look to the past for inspiration. 'Back when deVOL began, we were very much in favour of butcher's blocks,' says Helen Parker, creative director of deVOL Kitchens. 'You could always squeeze one into most kitchens, even if you didn’t have the room for an island or prep table.'

3. Prioritise your 'clearance zones'

The secret to a small kitchen island isn't just the piece itself, but the air around it. 'It’s important to consider the proportions of your kitchen, as you don’t want the island overwhelming the space,' advises Fred Horlock, design director at Neptune. 'We also recommend a minimum of 1m between island and cabinets as a comfortable distance for opening doors and appliances.'

4. Opt for portability and movement

In a small kitchen, the ability to reconfigure your space is a game-changer. Parker notes that deVOL often uses 'chunky castors on the legs so you could wheel these little prep tables around the kitchen.' This allows you to 'pull your butcher's block to the end of your table for big gatherings where you need extra space to lay out your platters of food. '

5. Soften the silhouette with open shelves

In a smaller room, a solid block of cabinetry can sometimes feel a little imposing. 'Open shelving on a kitchen island is a great way to soften what might otherwise feel like a solid block of cabinetry,' suggests Symington. 'It gives you somewhere to display lovely items like baskets and lovely tableware.'

To keep this area looking its best, try mixing textures - such as stacking tonal stoneware alongside wicker baskets - to create a curated look that feels more like a piece of furniture than a storage unit.

M&S Ceramic Storage Jar

RRP£10
Available from M&S
Shop now

Dusk Seagrass Storage Basket

RRP£18
Available from Dusk
Shop now

M&S Ceramic Nesting Mixing Bowls

RRP£30
Available from M&S
Shop now

6. Choose freestanding over fitted

To keep a small kitchen feeling airy, avoid the built-in look. 'More people are opting for freestanding islands, creating a contrast to fitted cabinetry and offering you freedom to move, whether around the kitchen or from home to home,' says Horlock.

7. Be brave with material choices

Smaller islands are the perfect place to experiment with luxury materials that might be too costly for the entire kitchen. Instead of playing it safe, use this small space to introduce a statement element - like a beautiful slab of marble or a warm, tactile piece of recalimed wood - to create a distinct focal point.

8. Rethink the island sink

In a compact space, every inch of the worktop is precious. 'Consider carefully if you would like a sink on the island, as these areas can easily become cluttered and you may find yourself constantly tidying it,' says Horlock. Keeping the surface clear allows it to function as a buffet or a primary prep station where people actually choose to linger.

9. Allow space for seating

If you want your island to double as a breakfast bar, don't forget the human element. 'If you’re going for a seated area, make sure you allow for enough clearance when a bar stool is pulled out and occupied,' Horlock advises. In tight spaces, backless stools that tuck entirely under the counter are a great space-saving hack.

Nina Campbell Green Alder Bar Stool

RRP£179
Available from Next
Shop now

Dunelm Counter Height Bar Stool

RRP£69
Available from Dunelm
Shop now

Dusk Mia Set of 2 Barstools

RRP£149
Available from Dusk
Shop now

10. Use lighting to define the zone

In a small open-plan kitchen, an island needs visual anchoring. Hanging a single statement pendant light or a pair of smaller lanterns directly above the island helps to zone the area, making the island feel like a purposeful, architectural feature. It's a kitchen lighting idea that works to draw the eye upward, which can also make a low-ceilinged roomfeel loftier.

The White Company Pembroke Pendant Light

RRP£125
Available from The White Company
Shop now

M&S Frill Pendant Ceiling Light

RRP£59
Available from M&S
Shop now

Habitat Dimple Glass Cluster Pendant

RRP£90
Available from Argos
Shop now

The most successful kitchen ideas for smaller homes are those that prioritise flexibility and personal style over rigid design rules. By focusing on smart proportions, a few expert-led tweaks, and a dash of creative colour, your island can become a hard-working heart of your home - no matter the square footage.

To keep your new hub looking its best, a set of kitchen towels, like these ruffled tea towels from Dunelm, draped over a side rail adds a soft, tactile finish that is as practical as it is pretty. Whether you choose a mobile butcher's block or a slim, freestanding island, these small additions ensure your space remains a joy to cook, live, and gather for years to come.

item.title: I’ve always avoided fabric in the kitchen until I saw this one stunning detail from Made in Chelsea’s Binky Felstead — it’s the secret to a lived-in glow
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: This one simple detail is the key to turning a clinical space into a soulful, modern country kitchen.
item.content:encoded:
I have been completely captivated by the lived-in, soulful charm of Binky Felstead’s kitchen.

Her recent posts are a masterclass in modern country style, and I’m convinced that the secret to stealing that signature aesthetic is to move away from those cold spotlights and embrace more textured kitchen lighting ideas.

Bringing fabrics into the kitchen may feel like a bold move, but it’s the quickest way to inject personality into a room that is often dominated by cold cabinetry and stone surfaces. Starting with a lamp shade like this one from M&S.

View this post on Instagram

What I love most about Binky’s kitchen is how she styles it with the softness of a living room. Her choice of beautifully textured fabric lampshades above the kitchen island is a total game-changer – it’s a choice that breaks the rules about wipe-clean surfaces.

Pairing pleated linen with soft blue-painted cabinetry creates a soft, nostalgic glow that’s far more flattering and homely than the harsh metal or glass fixtures we’re all used to.

Binky’s third layer of lighting is the real secret sauce. If you look closely at her breakfast bar, there’s a lovely table lamp tucked behind a wicker fruit basket that perfectly matches the cupboards. It’s a great way of transforming a high-traffic counter into a cosy nook.

I’ve definitely been taking notes for my own kitchen; while I don’t have the luxury of an island to play with, seeing how textures can be used to soften hard edges has me rethinking my own bare corners. Here is what I’ve now got on my wishlist to recreate Binky’s look:

Chloe Stewart The Damaris Shade

Available from The Roost
Shop now

M&S Fabric Wave Scallop Shade

Available from M&S
Shop now

Dunelm Frilled Gingham Lamp Shade

RRP£22
