Shop now

Pooky Rechargeable Table Lamp

RRP£140
Available from Pooky
Shop now

Walton & Co Stripe Seat Pad

RRP£20
Available from Harts of Stur
Shop now

Byredo Tree House Candle

RRP£35
Available from Space NK
Shop now

Binky’s space proves that we shouldn’t be afraid to bring living room luxuries into the heart of the home. Ditching the clinical for the comfortable is one of those timeless kitchen ideas that turns a practical room into a sanctuary.

For further softening of surfaces, don’t forget those often-uncomfortable benches and stools- adding some cushions like these gingham seat pads from Dunelm is the easiest way to make even the most utilitarian seating feel cosy.

item.title: 11 beautiful blue bedroom ideas – from soft coastal hues to deep dramatic tones that instantly elevate your entire space
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: From serene sky-inspired sanctuaries to moody indigo retreats, explore these expert blue bedroom ideas to create the ultimate restful escape.
item.content:encoded:
Creating a calming retreat starts with exploring blue bedroom ideas – a palette that is naturally restorative as it mirrors the stillness of the sky and the sea.

From soft duck egg to moody navy, this timeless palette offers endless bedroom colour ideas for those seeking a restful night's sleep.

If you're allured by this hue but want to avoid the mess of paint, a simple addition like this wool rug from Dunelm covers enough surface area to instantly transform the room; these further ideas below will help you do the rest, guiding you through the art of layering tones and textures for a beautifully blue finish.

1. Go bold with dark navy

Commit to deep indigo or navy on all four walls to create a snug, jewellery box feel that encourages deep rest. 'Deep blues work beautifully in bedrooms, especially as darkness supports good sleep,' says Sophie Pringle, founder of Surrey-based interior design studio Pringle & Pringle. 'To keep the space feeling balanced, we tend to keep ceilings and woodwork lighter, which helps reflect light and prevent the room from feeling heavy.'

Dusk Santorini Muslin Bedding Set

RRPfrom £45
Available from Dusk
Shop now

M&S Cotton Velvet Quilted Bedspread

RRP£129
Available from M&S
Shop now

Addison Ross Navy Rechargeable Lamp

RRP£125
Available from Addison Ross
Shop now

2. Mix classic blue and white

Stick to the classic pairing of blue and white for a look that feels crisp and eternally fresh. 'We love working with Lulworth Blue by Farrow & Ball as it strikes a perfect balance between playful and calm,' notes Pringle. 'It pairs beautifully with crisp reds and whites – the combination feels fresh, layered, and timeless.'

Whether through a pinstripe or floral, it's a fail-safe way to make a room feel pulled together and timeless.

3. Layer different blue fabrics

Avoid a cold atmosphere by piling on the textiles to build warmth through touch. 'Blue is such a classic choice for a bedroom because it’s so peaceful, but the key to making it feel warm is all in the textiles,' explains Jodie Hazlewood, founder of bespoke furniture brand The House Upstairs. 'Mixing different shades of blue through linens and upholstery gives the room a really lovely depth that feels sophisticated without being too formal.'

4. Combine blue with green

Look to nature for a colour scheme that feels like a walk through a forest or along a coastline. Because blue and green sit right next to each other on the colour wheel, they naturally get along without any visual clash. You can achieve this look by pairing a blue wall with botanical prints or by layering green velvet cushions on a blue and white bed set up.

5. Add pops of red

Prevent a blue room from feeling too safe by adding a sudden hit of red as a clever design counterpoint. 'Blues are a natural choice for bedrooms as they bring a sense of calm, particularly in lighter, powdery tones,' says Cath Beckett, co-founder of London-based interior design firm Yellow London. 'Pairing blue with touches of red creates a bit of contrast that lifts the palette, with the red acting as an unexpected accent that keeps the design interesting.'

Rosie Gore Scalloped Candle Holders

RRP£37
Available from The Roost
Shop now

Dunelm Frilled Rectangle Cushion Cover

RRP£8
Available from Dunelm
Shop now

Oliver Bonas Velvet Photo Frame

RRP£20
Available from Oliver Bonas
Shop now

6. Choose mineral blue shades

Look for blues that have a grey or stony undertone for a space that feels calm but never boring. Friedrich and Johanna Gräfling, founders of art space Salon Kennedy, suggest favouring muted, mineral blues over bright, primary colours. 'They absorb light softly and shift throughout the day, which keeps the room alive without becoming restless. Blue is a strong presence, so there’s no need to overload a blue bedroom with objects,' they note.

7. Frame walls with white trim

Give the eyes a chance to rest when using bold blue paint by using neutral boundaries to ground the colour. The Gräflings recommend using white ceilings and floors to act as a frame for the pigment. 'In the image above, the white ceiling and floor act almost like a frame, sharpening the intensity of the walls while keeping the space light and breathable. The furniture follows this approach—simple forms that allow the colour to remain the main focus,' they explain.

8. Use aqua for warmth

Try an aqua or a duck egg if you find standard blues a bit too chilly for a north-facing room. 'I particularly like using softer, warmer blues in bedrooms – aquas with a hint of green, gentle duck egg tones, or powder blues. These shades feel calm and restful while still bringing a sense of freshness,' says Henriette von Stockhausen, creative director of luxury interior design studio VSP Interiors.

Pair them with rich walnut furniture to ground the look with an organic, earthy feel.

Addison Ross Small Scalloped Tray

RRP£80
Available from Addison Ross
Shop now

Sanderson Aqua Pyramus Throw

RRP£300
Available from Harts of Stur
Shop now

M&S Pure Cotton Velvet Cushion

RRP£19.50
Available from M&S
Shop now

9. Build a tonal scheme

Use multiple shades of blue at once to make the room feel cohesive rather than flat. 'Layering is key. Building up different tones of blue creates a cohesive and harmonious look,' says von Stockhausen. 'I like to pair blue with creams, soft pinks, and warm woods such as walnut to introduce warmth and balance,'

This ensures everything pulls together without looking too 'matchy-matchy'.

10. Start with a patterned wallpaper

Pick your bedroom wallpaper first and pull the paint colours from the print to ensure a professional finish. 'Patterned wallpapers are a great starting point – in our Hammersmith project (pictured above), we pulled out the blue from the wallpaper into the headboard, allowing the rest of the scheme to be layered and adaptable over time,' says Pringle. This is an easy way to build a room that feels effortlessly curated.

11. Introduce blue through accessories

Bring blue into the bedroom through smaller, intentional pieces if you prefer a more flexible approach to colour. The Gräflings suggest that accessories are a brilliant way to pick up the blue of the walls or bedding without overwhelming the senses. 'Accessories should remain minimal and intentional. A single-coloured glass, a few books, or a sculptural lamp is often enough. Ultimately, a blue bedroom works best when the colour is treated not as decoration, but as a material,' they say.

Timeless Traditions Ikat Table Lamp

RRP£97.92
Available from Dusk Lighting
Shop now

H&M Sea-Inspired Vase

RRP£12.99
Available from H&M
Shop now

Coco & Wolf Embroidered Oblong Cushion

RRP£135
Available from Liberty
Shop now

To finish the look, focus on elements that allow your chosen shade to breathe. While we have explored an array of paint ideas here, the most successful bedroom ideas always come down to the bed itself. Investing in a high-quality foundation, such as this pure organic cotton bedding set from Christy, provides the crisp, clean contrast needed to make any blue - from the palest aqua to the deepest indigo - really sing.

item.title: A comforting and glossy Carlin pea stroganoff recipe with earthy mushrooms for a nutritious seasonal supper
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: Swapping traditional beef for the nutty bite of Carlin peas, this velvet-smooth stroganoff is a sophisticated, pulse-led twist on a classic comfort dish.
item.content:encoded:
This carlin pea stroganoff is the definition of heartwarming. Using the bean stock to create a glossy sauce, it delivers a punch of umami that complements earthy mushrooms.

As we look for lighter spring recipes, this dish offers a nourishing alternative to hearty stews. Whether mopped up with crusty bread or served over silky mash, it is a versatile dish that celebrates simple, seasonal cooking.

This recipe is taken from this Bold Bean Co cookbook available from Amazon, an essential resource for anyone looking to make pulses the centrepiece of their cooking. Discover how to make this savoury classic below.

Overview

Total Time: 1 hour

Serves: 4

Ingredients

  • 500g (1lb 2oz) mixed

  • Mushrooms, roughly sliced (like chestnut, oyster and shiitake)

  • 2 tbsp olive oil

  • 2 onions, thinly sliced

  • 2 tsp fresh thyme leaves/1 tsp dried thyme

  • 1 tbsp tomato purée

  • 2 tsp sweet paprika

  • 1/2 tsp cayenne pepper

  • 2 garlic cloves, roughly chopped

  • 50ml (2fl oz) marsala wine or brandy

  • 500ml (18fl oz) veg stock

  • 2 tbsp crème fraîche or soured cream

  • 1 tsp Dijon mustard

  • 570g (1lb 4½oz) jarred carlin peas, with their bean stock

  • 1 tbsp Worcestershire sauce or

  • Henderson’s Relish

  • 15g (½oz) parsley, finely chopped

  • Knob of butter (optional)

  • Salt and pepper

(To serve)

  • mashed potatoes, cooked rice, baked potatoes or buttered tagliatelle

  • steamed green beans (optional)

Method

  1. Start by dry-frying your mushrooms in a large, deep-sided casserole pan over a medium–high heat. Do this in two batches, first frying half of the mushrooms for about five minutes until coloured and crisping at the edges, then removing to a plate and repeating with the other half. Dry-frying gives them a bit more texture.

  2. In the same pan, heat the olive oil over a medium–low heat. Add the onions, along with a pinch of salt, and cook gently for 20–25 minutes, stirring occasionally so that they don’t catch. Cooking the onions slowly like this will bring out a lovely caramelised flavour.

  3. Add the thyme, tomato purée, sweet paprika, cayenne pepper and garlic to the onions and cook off for another two minutes until some of the onions start sticking to the bottom.

  4. Deglaze the pan with the alcohol, then let it bubble off for a few minutes until it is reduced by half.

  5. Now, gradually add the stock, stirring the mixture to a smooth paste before adding more. Once all your stock is added, bring your mixture to a simmer for two minutes to thicken. Return the mushrooms to the pan, along with the crème fraîche, Dijon mustard and carlin peas, with their bean stock. Stir in the Worcestershire sauce or Henderson’s Relish, along with most of the parsley. If using, swirl through a knob of butter to give it a bit more richness. Simmer for about three minutes more to warm it all through and ensure the mixture is creamy and glossy. Season with salt and lots of cracked black pepper.

  6. Serve with the carb of your choice, topped with the remaining parsley, with some steamed green beans on the side, if you like.

Full of Beans: delicious beany recipes to obsess over by Amelia Christie-Miller. Published by Kyle Books.

To ensure this dish gets the spotlight it deserves, the right presentation is key. Here are a few of our favourite pasta bowls to bring your showstopper to the table:

Available from Liberty
Shop now

To ensure your meal stays as nutritious as possible, it is worth considering the tools you use. Preparing this stroganoff in the best non-toxic cookware ensures that your ingredients remain free of unwanted chemicals, keeping the focus entirely on those fresh, earthy flavours.

Because this dish is just as delicious the following day, it is an ideal candidate for a midweek lunch. For those on the go, this stainless steel food container from Amazon is a brilliant way to carry leftovers.

item.title: A nutty and decadent pistachio tiramisu recipe that offers a fresh and vibrant twist on the Italian classic
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: This sophisticated take on the Italian classic pairs deep, coffee-soaked sponge with a luxuriously nutty cream for an elegant spring finale.
item.content:encoded:
With pistachio appearing to have taken over everything from social media to high-end menus, this vibrant pistachio tiramisu is the ultimate answer for those wanting to get in on the craze at home.

As an indulgent spring recipe, it swaps coffee for the buttery, nutty depth of the season’s most coveted ingredient. This elegant twist on a classic creates a velvety smooth showstopper, perfect for an elegant dinner party or a weekend treat.

You can find this recipe in the Cooking Made Simple cookbook from Amazon, which is well worth buying if you’re looking for more seasonal recipe inspiration. Learn how to whip up this decadent treat below.

Overview

Total Time: 25 minutes

Serves: 12

Ingredients

  • 2 eggs, separated, plus 1 egg white

  • 100g caster sugar

  • 450g mascarpone

  • 175g pistachio paste or pistachio cream

  • 150ml strong brewed coffee, cooled

  • 75ml coffee liqueur (I use Kahlúa)

  • 75ml almond liqueur, such as Amaretto (or you can use Frangelico for a hazelnut flavour or Contreau for an orange flavour)

  • About 30 savoiardi (ladyfinger) biscuits

  • 75g pistachios, roughly chopped

  • You will also need a deep bowl or serving dish to serve

Method

  1. In a large bowl using an electric whisk, beat the two egg yolks with 50g of the caster sugar until pale and fluffy.

  2. Then, switching to a handheld whisk so as not to overbeat them, beat in the mascarpone and pistachio paste/cream until smooth.

  3. In a separate large, grease-free bowl, whisk the three egg whites until frothy. Add the remaining 50g of caster sugar and beat to firm peaks.

  4. Fold half the beaten egg whites into the pistachio-mascarpone mixture to loosen it, then gently fold in the rest until you have a light, fluffy mix.

  5. Combine the coffee, coffee liqueur and almond liqueur in a small bowl or measuring jug. One by one, briefly dunk each savoiardi biscuit in the liquid, turning once to ensure it is moistened evenly.

  6. Arrange a layer of the coffee-dipped savoiardi biscuits over the base of your serving dish. Spoon in half of the pistachio-mascarpone mixture and gently spread to cover the biscuits. Arrange another layer of the remaining coffee-dipped savoiardi biscuits over the top, then gently spread over the remaining pistachio-mascarpone mixture. To finish, scatter over the chopped pistachios.

  7. Allow the tiramisu to rest in the fridge for at least four hours or ideally overnight, before serving.

Extracted from Cooking Made Simple by Alexandra Dudley (Ebury)

To ensure this decadent dessert gets the spotlight it deserves, the right presentation is key. Whether you prefer a deep, glass trifle dish to show off those vibrant green layers or a wide, ceramic serving bowl for a more rustic feel, here are a few of our favourite ways to bring this showstopper to the table:

M& SSpring Meadow Footed Serving Bowl

Available from M&S
Shop now

Neptune Lana Glass Trifle Bowl

RRP£55
Available from Neptune
Shop now

Dibor Beaded Glass Bowls

RRP£22
Available from Amazon
Shop now

Whether you’re enjoying a generous scoop at the kitchen table or hosting friends, this dish is best enjoyed slowly, savouring each creamy layer. This recipe would make a perfect centrepiece for a seasonal gathering – perhaps served alongside these Easter biscuits for a complete spread. To elevate the experience, these polka dot dessert spoons from Emma Bridgewater add a playful element to the table, or you can find more inspiration in our guide to Easter table decorating ideas for a truly memorable lunch.

item.title: Bedroom wallpaper ideas – 13 ways to create a personal and cocooning sleep space
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: From immersive murals to tactile textures, use wallpaper to turn your four walls into a soulful, cosy sanctuary.
item.content:encoded:
From timeless botanicals to bold statement murals, the most inspired bedroom wallpaper ideas offer a transformative way to wrap a space in character and texture.

As a private sanctuary, the sleep space is the perfect canvas for more adventurous bedroom ideas, allowing for a level of expressive pattern that might feel too daring for the home’s more public areas.

To achieve a professional, bubble-free finish, a high-quality tool, such as this paperhanger’s brush from Amazon, is an essential investment. From romantic florals to quiet neutrals, these 13 ideas show how to give your bedroom a soulful feel with wallpaper.

1. Create escapism with immersive murals

A large, uninterrupted wall behind the headboard is the ideal stage for a mural. Unlike traditional repeating patterns, a mural feels like a window into another world – whether that’s an ancient forest or a sprawling English garden.

Designer Totty Lowther’s bespoke woodland designs, pictured above, offer a sense of dreamy escapism; these landscapes can even be personalised to include your most beloved landmarks, creating a unique piece of art that tells your own story.

2. Embrace the full room wrap

To make a room feel truly intentional, try moving beyond the single feature wall and papering the whole space. Anouska Cave, head of design at North London-based interior design firm Cave Interiors, loves this immersive look: ‘Wallpaper has an incredible ability to shape how a bedroom feels, both visually and emotionally. A full room wrap is my preferred approach. Feature walls can feel temporary, whereas a fully wrapped room has real presence and intimacy.’

3. Ground the space with stripes

Structured patterns bring a sense of stability and height to a room meant for rest. Vertical lines draw the eye up, making the ceiling feel softer. ‘I’m drawn to using pattern as a way to ground a bedroom, especially through a vertical stripe,’ says Svetlana Willems, founder of fabric and wallpaper design house Franquemont. ‘I love our Vine Blots wallpaper because the combination of a structured stripe with the organic trailing floral really softens the pattern.’

4. Go for elegant rose gold and metallics

Principal bedrooms really come alive with light-reflective metallic finishes, which give off a beautiful glow as the sun sets. A rose gold or soft champagne palette, like ‘Drunken Monkey’ by Divine Savages, pictured above, adds a bit of shimmer that feels classic but fresh. It’s a great way to show some personality while keeping things calm and welcoming.

5. Lean into sun-baked shades

For 2026, we are seeing a shift away from cool greys toward earthy, sunny warmth. ‘For a bedroom, don't be afraid to lean into richer, more sun-baked shades,’ suggests Willem. ‘They create warmth that feels very calming, like our Crimson and Fuchsia or Sunlit Amber colourways.’

These palettes look their best when paired with natural wood and aged brass.

6. Echo patterns across the room

For a polished, designer look, treat your wallpaper as one part of a bigger puzzle. ‘We like to use wallpaper as part of a wider, layered scheme,’ says Cath Beckett, co-founder of luxury London-based interior design studio Yellow London. ‘A playful way to incorporate wallpaper is to echo the same pattern across cushions and lampshades to create a sense of cohesion.’

A great bedroom scheme is all about how the walls and the bed work together. ‘Pairing wallpaper with paint and textiles is where a scheme starts to feel intentional and calming,’ says Cave.

Pulling colours from your wallpaper into your duvet covers or rugs creates that ‘put-together’ feel that’s essential for a relaxing suite.

7. Add structure with faux panelling

If you love a bold print but don't want it to take over, try using wallpaper inside faux-panelling frames. It adds depth and a period feel to a modern room without the pattern feeling overwhelming. It’s a smart way to frame your favourite prints like they’re pieces of art.

8. Don’t forget the ceiling

The ceiling is often ignored, but it’s the one surface you actually see most when you’re lying down. ‘Extending wallpaper onto the ceiling draws the eye upwards and gives the space an immersive quality,’ says Beckett. In small bedrooms, wallpapering the ceiling blurs the lines where the wall ends, making the space feel surprisingly big and cosy.

9. Stick with timeless, neutral and understated patterns

Sometimes, a subtle neutral wallpaper is the most luxurious choice. A high-quality paper in a muted tone is stylish forever and acts as a great backdrop for other decor. This lets you go bolder with things like headboards, knowing you can update them easily down the line.

10. Soften loft angles

Loft rooms often have awkward slopes, ceiling beams and sharp angles that can look a bit harsh. Using a small-scale, ditsy floral or geometric print on these surfaces softens the lines and makes the architecture look intentional. As Beckett notes, ‘smaller-scale prints are particularly effective for introducing detail and interest’ without making a small bedroom feel cramped.

11. Bring the outdoors in

Biophilic design is a huge part of creating a restful space. Wallpapers with deep forest greens, mossy textures or charcoal sketches of trees create a grounding environment. Pair this organic look with tactile materials like wool or reclaimed wood to help you switch off at the end of the day.

12. Use fabric-effect papers for extra warmth

To make a room feel physically and usually warmer, look for papers that mimic grasscloth or wool. These faux-fabric wallcoverings actually help with sound absorption and offer a soft-focus finish that makes a bedroom feel incredibly snug and private.

13. Match your wallpaper to the headboard

For a truly high-end look, consider the exact same fabric or pattern for both your wallpaper and upholstered headboard. This pattern-drenching technique blurs the boundaries of the bed, making it feel like an integrated part of the architecture rather than just a piece of furniture, creating a designer-led, polished finish.

In the end, it’s these thoughtful layers - the texture of the walls or the way the light hits a metallic print - that really turn a bedroom into a sanctuary. When you approach a space with care, you create a room that doesn’t just look finished but genuinely calm.

To pull the whole look together, plain bedding like this 100% cotton bedding set from Dunelm provides the perfect crisp, neutral contrast to let your new papered walls do the talking. If you’re ready to move on to the next part of your bedroom transformation, discover our guide to bedroom curtain ideas for more inspiration.

item.title: How to use 13 bedroom colour ideas to make your bedroom feel cohesive, curated and effortlessly elegant – and the unexpected combinations that actually work
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: From buttery yellows to earthy greens, explore the designer-led palettes that turn a simple room into a soulful, lived-in retreat.
item.content:encoded:
The best bedroom colour ideas are a direct reflection of your personal style, creating a space to help you decompress at night and feel energised come sunrise.

When exploring bedroom ideas, the goal is to find what actually draws you in and makes you want to unwind. Whether you’re into dark, moody walls or hits of bold colour, the best spaces should function as a retreat from the rest of the house.

Aside from investing in cloud-like bedding like this soft and silky bedding set from M&S, colour plays a vital role in creating total comfort in a bedroom. From colour-drenching to the unexpected red theory, here we explore the best ways to make your sleep space feel like a proper sanctuary.

1. Prioritise atmosphere over colour in isolation

A painting shouldn’t be just about a single paint swatch; it’s about the total feeling of the room. Henriette von Stockhausen, creative director of luxury interior design firm VSP Interiors, suggests moving away from picking a colour and instead thinking about the final mood: ‘We always think about bedrooms in terms of atmosphere rather than colour in isolation – it should feel calm, layered and quietly enveloping.

To achieve this, she recommends starting with a soft, warm neutral that has a bit of depth, and then building in richer tones through fabric and upholstery.

2. Shift the nervous system with soft blues

Blue is a calming classic for a reason. ‘Certain tones interact with the brain and the body’s circadian rhythm, which is why soft blues often feel instantly calming,’ explains Anoushka Cave, head of design at North London-based interior design firm Cave Interiors. Go for a misty, grey-toned blue to keep the space grounded and grown up.

H&M Quilted Bedspread

RRP£29.99
Available from H&M
Shop now

Striped Ruffle Bedding Set

RRPfrom £25
Available from M&S
Shop now

TBCo Cotton Cushion Cover

RRP£45
Available from TBCo
Shop now

3. Pair pink with green

If you want warmth without a ‘sugary’ feeling, try pairing soft pink with green ‘Soft pinks bring warmth, while darker tones add depth and keep the scheme from feeling too sweet,’ says Melissa Hutley, co-founder of London and Surrey-based interior design studio Hutley & Humm. This colour combination has become an interior design favourite because it creates a space that feels both balanced and restful.

4. Replace sage with moss and olive greens

If you want a nature vibe that feels more enclosed, look to the forest floor. ‘Deep olive is a richer, more protective alternative to sage,’ says Cave. It’s an easy, deep neutral that plays particularly well with wood furniture and brass hardware.

Dusk Ceramic Table Lamp

RRP£49
Available from Dusk
Shop now

Piglet in Bed Gingham Duvet Set

RRPfrom £184
Available from Piglet in Bed
Shop now

Sanderson Aril's Garden Cushion

RRP£50
Available from Bedeck
Shop now

5. Master the art of colour drenching

For a high-end seamless look, paint the walls, skirting and ceiling in the same colour. ‘This creates a cocooning feel, especially in rooms with high ceilings, where it helps bring the whole space together,’ explains award-winning interior designer Laura Stephens.

Plus, if you have awkward sloping ceilings or dormer windows, colour drenching is a lifesaver. ‘Taking one colour across all surfaces creates a seamless envelope that softens awkward angles,’ Hutley adds.

6. Forget the 60-30-10 rule

You may have read about the 60-30-10 rule – a classic design formula where 60% of the room is a primary colour, 30% is a secondary hue, and 10% is an accent. While it’s a helpful starting point, following it too strictly can make a space feel staged. Stephens suggests a more relaxed approach: ‘I focus on how a space feels rather than adhering strictly to proportions. It keeps the scheme from feeling forced.’

Instead of worrying about percentages, focus on how the light hits your walls. Using different shades of the same colour - like a darker velvet cushion against a lighter painted wall - adds a natural depth that feels much more comfortable and lived-in.

7. Introduce a visual anchor with a single bold accent

Instead of saturating a room in high-energy colour, you can use a single, sharp accent to give a soft palette some much-needed definition. ‘I love adding a punchy red stripe on a bed valance or a small ceramic lamp,’ says Jodie Hazlewood, founder of bespoke furniture brand The House Upstairs. ‘It instantly brightens a softer scheme without ruining the quiet vibe.’

This one flash of intensity acts as an anchor, giving the eye a place to rest and preventing a subtle room from feeling washed out or one-dimensional.

8. Layer different tones of the same colour

To keep a light room from looking bland, layer different depths of the same colour. ‘Layer with other tonal colours to keep it cohesive,’ suggests Cath Beckett, co-founder of luxury London-based interior design studio Yellow London. ‘It makes the room look considered while still being comfortable.’

For example, if your walls are a light stone, choose curtains in a deeper taupe and a rug in a soft mushroom to create natural depth.

9. Use dark tones to envelope small rooms

Don’t be afraid of dark paint in a small bedroom. ‘Smaller rooms benefit from deeper colours because they envelop the space and make it feel cosy, not confined,’ says Hutley. Darker tones like charcoal or forest green actually blur the boundaries of the walls, creating a snug feeling that white paint can’t replicate.

10. Avoid high-agitation reds on walls

A red pillow is fine, but a red wall can be a sensory nightmare. ‘Bright reds can feel energising or even slightly agitating,’ warns Cave. If you want that sense of warmth, look for earthy shades like dusty terracotta or deep ochre. These feel much more grounded when the lamps are on.

11. Build quiet luxury through warm neutrals

If you’re going neutral, stay away from clinical whites. ‘To achieve a space that feels both high-end and inviting, I look to a palette of warm, layered neutrals – think straw, honey, and caramel,’ suggests Esme Amberg, head of design at online luxury furniture retailer The Roost. ‘These tones add a natural richness that makes the room feel considered and expensive rather than stark or flat.’

12. Soften the scheme with tactile textures

Texture is the secret ingredient that prevents a bold palette from feeling flat. Materials like linen bedding, velvet headboards, or natural timber furniture add a third dimension to your colour choices, but remember that textiles can bring the colour themselves too. Instead of relying solely on paint, use a moss-green headboard or a terracotta linen throw to introduce hue through touch.

13. Brighten with sun-kissed yellows

If you want a room that feels energising in the morning but warm and candle-lit at night, look to soft, buttery yellows. 'Yellow is the colour of optimism,' says Cave. 'In a bedroom, it works beautifully as a warm neutral that mimics the glow of natural light, especially when paired with soft whites or natural wood tones to keep it feeling sophisticated rather than overwhelming.'

M&S Milk Glass Table Lamp

RRP£45
Available from M&S
Shop now

Oliver Bonas Yellow Striped Duvet Set

RRP£75
Available from Oliver Bonas
Shop now

OKA Imber Poppy Cushion Cover

RRP£95
Available frm OKA
Shop now

By layering these expert approaches - from the restorative power of living greens to the calming warmth of a layered neutral palette - you can transform your bedroom into a space that truly resonates with how you want to live. When your colour choices for your bedroom are intentional, they set the scene for genuine rest and rejuvenation.

To really bring those new tones to life, adding a warm glow with a lamp, like this portable one from Dunelm, can shift the atmosphere when the sub goes down. If you’re ready to start testing swatching, our paint ideas guide will inspire you to play with different finishes and techniques. For those looking for a specific starting point, we’ve also curated a list of some of the top interior designers’ best neutral paint colours.

item.title: Bathroom storage ideas – 13 clever fixes to turn a chaotic space into a calm retreat
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: From sleek built-in options to charming freestanding units, these ideas will help you maximise storage without sacrificing style.
item.content:encoded:
A well-organised room is the secret to a stress-free morning. By integrating clever bathroom storage ideas, you can transform a chaotic daily routine into one that is efficient and enjoyable.

Thoughtful layouts help maintain a serene, clutter-free atmosphere. When exploring different bathroom ideas, remember that purposeful storage is what allows a stylish design to be practical too.

To avoid the space being too utilitarian, look for elements that enhance the overall decor, from small, textured touches like this lidded seagrass storage basket from H&M to more substantial options like cabinets and vanities. Discover the best storage solutions below.

1. Add storage with a mirrored cabinet

A mirrored cabinet provides hidden storage while also serving as a key functional feature in the bathroom. 'A mirrored cabinet is an intelligent storage solution for a luxurious bathroom. It efficiently combines storage with a light-enhancing mirror, creating a sense of spaciousness,' says Rikki Fothergill, bathroom style expert at Big Bathroom Shop. 'Discreetly housing daily essentials, it keeps countertops clear, contributing to a polished and refined aesthetic.'

Choose a design with adjustable shelves to accommodate various items, from medicines to grooming essentials. Mirrored cabinets with integrated lighting offer an added luxury, perfect for illuminating your morning routine.

Melody Maison Wooden Mirrored Bathroom Cabinet

RRP£104.95
Available from Amazon
Shop now

Next Pebble Bathroom Wall Mirror

RRP£110
Available from Next
Shop now

Very Corrie Mirrored Bathroom Wall Cabinet

RRP£69
Available from Very
Shop now

2. Maximise storage with a vanity unit

A vanity unit is an essential bathroom storage solution, combining practicality with style. Opt for a unit with deep drawers and built-in organisers to store everything from toiletries to bath towels. If space allows, choose a double-sink vanity to accommodate multiple users while keeping surfaces neat. Adjustable shelving inside the vanity ensures everything from hairdryers to skincare products has a designated place.

3. Build shelving into your tiles

For a sleek, integrated appearance, built-in shelving serves as an excellent option. Recessed shelves within a shower or positioned above a bath keep essential items easily accessible while preserving a streamlined look.

Incorporating LED spotlights can improve the ambience and make these shelves both functional and visually attractive.

4. Incorporate a bath tray for everyday essentials

A bath tray is both a stylish and functional addition, allowing you to keep essentials close at hand while enjoying a relaxing soak. Whether used for holding scented candles, books, or bath oils, a well-chosen bath tray adds a spa-like touch to your bathroom. Opt for a wooden or bamboo design for a warm, natural look that complements any decor.

The White Company Wooden Bath Tidy

RRP£60
Available from The White Company
Shop now

Nkuku Indore Iron Bath Bridge

RRP£48
Available from Nkuku
Shop now

Nôsa Faro Stone Bath Caddy

RRP£89
Available from Nosa
Shop now

5. Introduce a freestanding unit

For those who love the charm of freestanding furniture, a dedicated cabinet or shelving unit offers both a stylish focal point and flexible storage.

A vintage-inspired linen cupboard with glass-fronted doors - perfect for neatly folded textiles - or a classic wooden bookcase repurposed for plush towels and toiletries can add character to your space while keeping everything neatly stored..

6. Install shelves to utilise wall space

Wall-mounted shelves are a simple yet effective way to increase storage without taking up floor space. 'A simple floating shelf above the toilet or the door can store towels, toiletries, and other supplies,' explains Maria Anderson, cleaning and organising expert at Henfield Storage. 'Sleek, narrow, and high-reaching cabinets can also be installed to store extra supplies or items that are not used frequently.'

Glass, metal or wooden shelving can complement different bathroom styles, from contemporary to traditional.

7. Conceal clutter with a skirted sink

While a pedestal sink undoubtedly lends a sense of period elegance to a room, its structural silhouette often lacks storage. Adding a fabric skirt around the sink instantly creates a concealed storage area for unsightly cleaning supplies, spare toilet rolls, or toiletries.

By choosing a high-quality linen or a block-print fabric that matches your decor, you can hide the clutter while still going for a cohesive, classic look.

8. Store bathroom essentials in baskets

Woven baskets are a versatile storage option, bringing a much-needed layer of organic texture to the hard, often clinical surfaces of a bathroom. Perfect for holding rolled-up towels, toiletries, or even laundry, they offer a soft tactile way to keep essentials within reach.

Whether you tuck them onto open shelving, slide them into the void under a sink or let them sit beside a bath, they provide an organised yet stylish approach to bathroom storage.

H&M Large Lidded Storage Basket

RRP£59.99
Available from H&M
Shop now

Neptune Alva Baskets

RRP£25
Available from Neptune
Shop now

Cox & Cox Hyacinth Storage Basket

RRP£65
Available from Cox & Cox
Shop now

9. Hang towels on a heated rail

A heated towel rail not only keeps your towels warm and dry but also serves as an elegant storage solution. Wall-mounted or freestanding designs help free up cupboard space while ensuring towels are always within reach.

"A well-placed towel rail offers both functionality and style. It ensures towels are neatly arranged and readily available, while also adding a touch of warmth," adds Rikki. "Particularly, a heated rail elevates the bathing experience, creating a spa-like atmosphere through simple, elegant design."

10. Repurpose bar cart for portable storage

A bar cart can be repurposed into a stylish bathroom storage unit, ideal for holding toiletries, towels, and decorative accessories. The advantage of a mobile unit is that it can be moved around as needed, making it a great solution for small bathrooms.

11. Keep everyday items handy with a storage caddy

A storage caddy is a handy addition to any bathroom, especially for keeping everyday essentials organised and within reach. Opt for a sleek metal or wooden caddy for a stylish touch, or choose a plastic version for a more practical, easy-to-clean option.

Beyond its functional appeal, a portable design allows you to effortlessly move your curated products from the vanity to the bath.

Wireworks Round Caddy

RRP£190

Shop now

Nkuku Mahi Bathroom Storage Caddy

RRP£130
Available from Nkuku

Next Chrome Hanging Shower Caddy

RRP£10
Available from Next
Shop now

12. Install hooks to optimise vertical storage

Hooks are a simple yet effective way to increase bathroom storage without taking up much space. Use them to hang towels, dressing gowns, or baskets filled with toiletries. Choosing stylish hooks in unlacquered brass or contemporary matte black metal can also add a decorative element to your bathroom.

Rowen & Wren Grove Hanging Rail

RRP£64
Available from Rowen & Wren
Shop now

Hastshilp Shell Brass Coat Hooks

RRP£75
Available from The Roost
Shop now

Pickpiff Self Adhesive Hooks

RRP£6.99
Available from Amazon
Shop now

13. Utilise the window ledge for storage

Don’t overlook your window ledge as a potential storage area. It can be the perfect spot for keeping small plants, decorative candles, or everyday essentials like cotton wool and skincare products –remember to decant your cotton pads and buds into a pretty container like this metal holder from H&M to keep everything looking put together.

If your ledge is deep enough, small storage boxes can help keep items tidy.

The best bathrooms are the ones that don't feel like an afterthought. They're the spaces that have been put together with intention, and clever storage is part of its foundation, creating a room that works like a dream when you're in a morning rush, but calm and curated when the day is done. Whether you're refreshing a family bathroom or maximising an en suite, when the space moves from being just a functional room to a sanctuary where everything has its own place, you know you've got it right.

As you pull your own space together, remember that it's often the little things that make the biggest difference. To instantly soften hard surfaces, we are loving this cotton scalloped bath mat from M&S, an elevated everyday essential.

item.title: ‘It’s a quiet detail that elevates the whole palette’ - how bold design and functional elements transformed this family kitchen
item.dc:creator: Charlotte Dunford
item.description: Highly practical and visually striking, this family kitchen combines playful design, hidden elements and clever storage solutions to create a space perfectly suited to modern-day living
item.content:encoded:
Often named the heart of the home, kitchens have to be highly practical spaces. Yet, this doesn't have to come at the expense of visual appeal.

The most successful kitchen ideas are the ones that marry form and function. This bold and innovative scheme in London is a prime example; elevating details, striking colours and playful patterns combine to create an interior that is both beautiful and hardworking.

The kitchen in question is the work of designer Sophie Pringle, founder of interior architecture and design studio, Pringle & Pringle. Established in 2015, the studio has worked across a wide range of projects and developed its signature style, uniting personality and purpose to create interiors inspired, at their core, by how people live in them.

When Sophie embarked on the renovation of this late-Victorian terrace house, she had somewhat of a head start. 'This was the fourth project we've completed with this client, which means we already had a strong understanding of how they live and what they respond to aesthetically,' she explains. The clients in question were a busy, creative family with three children.

It's no surprise, then, that the client's brief focused on functionality and ease of use. 'They wanted the kitchen to feel clean and uncluttered to support both everyday life and entertaining,' says Sophie.

Alongside these practical considerations, the client had a clear visual direction in mind. 'They had lots of reference images featuring a mix of rich textures and finishes such as ebonised wood cabinetry, heavily veined marbles and mixed metals, including copper sinks,' remembers Sophie. 'They also knew they wanted colour and a fun feel for family living.'

The project began with a full transformation of the ground floor. A large extension was constructed at the rear of the house and the floor layout was reconfigured. 'It created a more open-plan and light-filled space that better suits modern family living,' explains Sophie.

With alterations made, attention turned to practical elements to maximise the kitchen's functionality. The addition of a sizeable walk-in pantry and tall, bespoke cabinetry provided ample storage space, meaning the main working area of the kitchen could remain organised and efficient.

Sophie's designs were largely guided by the client's specific requests, such as a dedicated breakfast cupboard and an integrated fridge. As Sophie notes, 'Once we understood what they needed, we allowed those requirements to define the proportions and rhythm of the joinery.'

It is among this run of built-in cabinetry, that a secret hides in plain sight. 'The clients loved playful, clever moments, and we had used a hidden door with a floor-to-ceiling mirror in the hallway to access a home office. We applied the same logic here.' Sophie continues, 'The tall joinery run would have been broken up by a pantry door, so we concealed the pantry entrance within the cabinetry.' As well as being more aesthetically pleasing, this fun feature had its practical benefits. 'It allowed us to make the pantry slightly larger by bringing the door to the front of the joinery,' she adds.

Over the last few years, black kitchens have seen a surge in popularity and judging by their recurring appearance on the original vision boards, Sophie's client was a fan. The choice was made to go for black-stained cabinetry rather than painted, to introduce a subtle element of texture into the scheme as well as a more durable finish.

The hardware, while a small detail, was carefully deliberated over, too. 'To give the otherwise flat fronts a bit of personality and tactility, we chose bronze ring pulls, subtle in pattern but adding a distinctive shape that reads beautifully against the black timber,' says Sophie. 'It's a quiet detail that elevates the whole palette.'

While black cabinets and wood tones were the starting point for the kitchen scheme, the inspiration for the bold use of blue came from a textile. 'We selected Nicky Haslam's Shutter Stripe fabric for the blinds and this introduced a vibrant blue, which we echoed on the tall cabinets and through the checkerboard tiles. This injected fun and warmth into a palette that could otherwise have felt heavy.'

'We wanted to bring tiles into the kitchen in a less traditional way by using pattern to add interest without overwhelming,' says Sophie. A mix of blue and white tiles were installed along the side of the kitchen island to 'lighten and energise the space' and to balance out the black cabinets. While this tile idea was a new departure for the Pringle & Pringle team, the result has been a resounding success. 'The tiled island was a highlight,' she adds. 'It brings energy and personality to the heart of the home in a way that feels both surprising and wholly appropriate to the family's lifestyle.'

This kitchen artfully balances form and function. With a striking design and high-level functionality, it proves that practicality and personality can coexist. However, achieving a similar seamless style in your own kitchen doesn't necessarily require a full redesign; even a small step, such as investing in elegant and efficient kitchen storage buys, can have a big impact.

item.title: A bright and zesty courgette and chickpea recipe with lemon and mint to welcome the new season
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: This vibrant spring recipe transforms simple kitchen staples into a delicious, lemon-scented supper.
item.content:encoded:
This courgette and chickpea stew is made for those longer evenings that are just around the corner, to be enjoyed al fresco.

As a versatile spring recipe, it skips the fuss by using starchy liquid from jarred chickpeas to create a signature, glossy silkiness. Whether a midweek main or a simple side, it practically begs for a t-shirt-weather breeze and a cold tipple.

This recipe is from the cookbook Cooking Made Simple from Amazon, a beautiful staple for any seasonal cook. Discover how to recreate this sun-drenched dish below.

Overview

Total Time: 25 minutes

Ingredients

  • Olive oil

  • 2 garlic cloves, crushed

  • 2 courgettes, thinly sliced

  • Coarse sea salt flakes

  • 700g jar of chickpeas, including their liquid

  • 40g Grana Padano or Parmesan, finely grated

  • Grated zest of 1 unwaxed lemon

  • Juice from up to ½ lemon

  • Small bunch of mint (about 25g), leaves finely chopped

  • Freshly cracked black pepper

  • Crème fraîche, natural yoghurt, or ricotta, to serve

Method

  1. Heat a good glug of olive oil in a frying pan over a gentle heat. Add the garlic and cook for two minutes. Add the courgettes with a large pinch of sea salt and cook for six to eight minutes or until softened enough that they are flopping and folding over themselves.

  2. Pour the chickpeas into the pan along with their liquid from the jar, scatter in the Grana Padano or Parmesan and cook for about five minutes or until the chickpeas have warmed through and the sauce has thickened and emulsified slightly. Switch off the heat.

  3. Stir through the lemon zest, just a squeeze of lemon juice and the chopped mint, along with another good pinch of salt. Taste to check the seasoning, adjusting as needed. Drizzle over more olive oil and finish with some freshly cracked black pepper.

  4. Divide the courgette and chickpea mixture between bowls and serve with a spoonful of crème fraîche, natural yoghurt, or ricotta, if you like.

Extracted from Cooking Made Simple by Alexandra Dudley (Ebury)

Looking for the perfect vessel for this dish? To complement those unhurried evenings on the garden steps – or perhaps for a leisurely lunch at your bistro set – here are a few of our favourite bowls:

H&M Porcelain Serving Bowl

RRP£14.99
Available from H&M
Shop now

Sharland England Heather Soup Bowl

RRP£42
Available from Liberty
Shop now

Rebecca Udall Celia Pasta Bowl

RRP£26
Available from Rebecca Udall
Shop now

Whether you're packing tupperware into a picnic basket or sharing a relaxed meal at the kitchen table, this dish is all about slowing down and enjoying the season. It is simple, unhurried, and exactly what these brighter days call for.

To complete the seating, these colourful wine glasses from Amazon are just the thing for a chilled glass of rosé, or you can browse our guide to the best outdoor furniture or the best outdoor furniture for more spring inspiration.

item.title: 23 beautiful kitchen ideas for a stylish, lived-in space you’ll never want to leave
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: From clever freestanding furniture to the small tactile details that make a room feel like home, here are the best ways to create a kitchen that is as functional as it is inviting.
item.content:encoded:
Finding the right kitchen ideas is about more than just cabinetry; it’s about capturing the soul of your home. We’re moving away from showroom-perfect spaces and back towards rooms that feel lived-in and wonderfully personal.

For most of us, the kitchen is the true anchor of daily life – a place where we gather for morning tea, host friends and come together for Sunday roast.

From the seating to the lighting, every design decision plays a part in how the room functions and feels – whether you’re working within a small kitchen or are lucky enough to be playing with larger proportions. To help you navigate your next project, we’ve curated a range of design ideas to consider.

1. Embrace the unfitted look with freestanding furniture

Modern kitchen designs are increasingly moving away from heavy, blocky cabinetry in favour of pieces that feel like carefully crafted furniture. ‘Over the past few years, we’ve seen a clear shift towards baker’s tables and freestanding kitchen islands that feel more like furniture than fitted furniture, a move that redefines how storage is approached within the kitchen,’ says Tom Howley, founder of the eponymous luxury kitchen design company.

By opting for a more unfitted silhouette, you can prevent a large kitchen from feeling overly utilitarian. This works particularly well in country kitchens, where the mix of different textures and freestanding elements creates lived-in charm.

2. Create a coffee station

Carve out a dedicated nook for your morning ritual to streamline your routine. By housing the coffee machine, pods and mugs in a corner, you create a far more organised hub; alternatively, tucking them behind pocket doors keeps the main countertops entirely clear. Either way, it creates a boutique-hotel feel and keeps the traffic of coffee-making away from the main cooking zone.

Shop the look

Coffee Station Organiser for Countertop

RRP£22.99
Available from Amazon
Shop now

De'Longhi Stilosa Manual Espresso Coffee Machine

RRP£125
Available from Argos
Shop now

Pintail Studios Wooden Coffee Pod Holder

RRP£40
Available from Not On The High Street
Shop now

3. Celebrate reclaimed materials

To add instant age to a new-build kitchen, look to the history of reclaimed wood. Whether it’s a chunky oak lintel above a range or a ceiling crisscrossed with old beams, natural wood breaks up the flatness of painted units.

If you aren't ready to pull up the floorboard, simpler additions like a solid butcher's block or open timber shelving can do the heavy lifting. These smaller doses of raw texture help a space feel grounded and warm, especially when you use different wood kitchen ideas to balance modern cabinetry and add some history to the room.

4. Soften joinery with open shelving

Break up the visual weight of heavy wall cupboards by embracing open storage. It’s a design choice that prioritises airiness, allowing you to showcase heirlooms, ceramics, and even everyday essentials - like displaying dry goods in glass jars - that tell your personal story.

‘We don’t like a kitchen feeling too fitted or fixed, so adding open shelving creates a real opportunity for display. A pot rack, a run of open shelves, somewhere to put things that have a bit of character, it makes the space feel lighter and brighter,’ says Cotswold-based interior designer Sean Symington. This approach turns functional items into part of the decor, making the room feel curated rather than cluttered.

5. Experiment with colourful surfaces

While cabinetry often takes centre stage, the surfaces we touch every day offer a unique opportunity to be a bit more adventurous. Moving away from the obvious neutral stones, designers are now treating countertops as a canvas for personality.

‘A colourful countertop is much more fun and inventive,’ says Phoebe Holland, founder of London-based interior design firm Studio Hollond. ‘It's also great because it's more forgiving, and it's brilliant when it's quartz like this, as that material is indestructible, which is exactly what you want for a kitchen!’

6. Ground the room with patterned flooring

The floor is often a vast, underutilised canvas in a kitchen. A patterned floor draws the eye downward, anchoring the entire scheme. ‘Kitchens tend to have a lot of hard surfaces, such as joinery and ovens, so adding patterned floors can introduce a playful and impactful element,’ explains Holland.

If you aren’t in the position to change the flooring itself, look to textiles; a durable, patterned runner can achieve a similar feel without a full renovation.

Dunelm Byron Tile Geometric Runner

RRPfrom £55
Available from Dunelm
Shop now

H&M Checked Flat Weave Runner Rug

RRP£19.99
Available from H&M
Shop now

Ruggable Calloway Brown Multicolour Rug

Overall rating: 1/5

RRP£159
Available from Ruggable
Shop now

7. Emulate nature with green hues

If you are looking for green kitchen ideas that will stand the test of time, look no further than the garden for inspiration. Green has become the go-to neutral for the modern country home; whether it’s a soft sage or a deep forest hue, it provides a backdrop that feels immersive and grounding.

'Green brings the beauty of nature indoors and invites a calm, welcoming atmosphere perfect for kitchens,' says Fred Horlock, design director at British homeware brand Neptune. ‘In larger kitchen spaces, opt for incorporating green on a freestanding island to create an interesting focal point.’

8. Introduce character with wallpaper

Wallpaper is a transformative tool that adds instant depth to walls, and it’s often overlooked in the kitchen. ‘Wallpaper can be a wonderful way to introduce pattern and texture into a kitchen, particularly in spaces where soft furnishings are limited,’ says Sophie Pringle, founder of Surrey-based interior design studio Pringle & Pringle. ‘We often use it inside dressers, at the back of shelving, or above panelling to add depth and character.’

9. Integrate real greenery

Bring the outdoors in with an indoor herb garden or oversized potted plants. Not only does this add a burst of life and fresh scent, but it also softens the hard lines of appliances and stone tops. A few pots of rosemary and thyme on a sunny windowsill make the room feel alive – and very handy when needing to grab a handful mid recipe!

Shop the look

The Hairy Pot Plant Company Chives

RRP£7
Available from Burford Garden Company
Shop now

Le Creuset Stoneware Herb Planter

RRP£41
Available from Le Creuset
Shop now

Flowers & Plants Co. Boston Fern

RRP£29.99
Available from Selfridges
Shop now

10. Opt for ‘living’ finishes

Choose unlacquered brass or copper for your taps and handles. Unlike polished chrome, which is the standard, these living materials develop a unique patina over time, darkening and changing with use. This constant state of change means your hardware won’t look like a showroom display, but rather a collection of functional objects that grow more interesting the more they are handled. It’s one of those subtle details that make a kitchen feel like an established part of the house.

11. Design a built-in pantry

There is something so satisfying about a well-organised pantry; it is the ultimate workhorse of a busy kitchen. Whether it’s a grand, walk-in room or a clever double-door larder integrated into your cabinetry, a pantry allows you to keep the main kitchen calm and clear.

By housing everything from the stand mixer to the flour behind closed doors, you move the clutter out of sight.

Shop the look

M&S Set of 3 Wooden Stacking Boxes

RRP£30
Available from M&S
Shop now

Neptune Bramley Stoneware Butter Dish

RRP£29
Available from Neptune
Shop now

M&S Set of 5 Ribbed Glass Spice Jars

RRP£18
Available from M&S
Shop now

12. Zone with oversized pendants

Use lighting to define different areas in a large, open space. Hanging a trio of statement pendant lights over a breakfast bar or a large dining table creates an immediate visual anchor, signalling exactly where the social heart of the kitchen lies. These should be positioned low enough to create a sense of intimacy for evening drinks, but also high enough so they aren’t disturbed during day-to-day meal times.

Shop the look

H&M Rattan Pendant Light

RRP£49.99
Available from H&M
Shop now

Tobias Ribbed Pendant Light

RRP£55
Available from Dunelm
Shop now

M&S Wave Scallop Lamp Shade

RRP£45
Available from M&S
Shop now

13. Add a seating nook

Incorporate a window seat or a small bistro table in a quiet corner. It encourages people to linger long after the meal is over, making the space feel less like a laboratory and more like a true extension of the living room.

14. Layer your lighting sources

Lighting is the secret ingredient that transforms a kitchen into a retreat, but the real magic happens away from the ceiling. ‘Layered lighting makes a kitchen feel far more atmospheric and versatile, says Pringle. ‘We always combine task lighting, such as downlights or discreet under-cabinet lighting, with more decorative sources like wall lights or table lamps.’

By layering in different kitchen lighting ideas - such as a small lamp tucked onto a shelf or elegant wall sconces - you can dial down the big light and create a much more intimate atmosphere.

Shop the look

Pooky Double Chukka Wall Fitting

RRP£195
Available from Pooky
Shop now

The White Company Wimbledon Porcelain Pendant

RRP£175
Available from The White Company
Shop now

M&S Rechargeable Rattan Table Lamp

RRP£39.50
Available from M&S
Shop now

15. Switch to fluted glass

For glazed cabinets, opt for fluted or ribbed glass. It allows you to see the silhouettes of your glassware while artfully blurring any interior clutter – ideal for those who love the open look but want a little more privacy for their shelves.

16. Hang original artwork

Bridge the gap between utility and living by treating your walls with the same reverence you would a sitting room. ‘Kitchens are increasingly becoming the heart of the home, so we like to treat them as living spaces rather than purely functional rooms,’ says Pringle. ‘Introducing artwork instantly softens a kitchen and adds personality.’

Bringing artwork into the kitchen instantly removes that clinical edge and makes the room feel like a core part of the house’s personality.

17. Hang your copper and cast iron

There is something wonderfully nostalgic about a hanging pot rack positioned over a central island or above a range. Instead of hiding your best cookware in deep drawers, hanging your copper pans or seasoned cast iron makes them part of the room’s visual texture. It’s a practical move too, keeping your most-used kitchenware within arm's reach at all times.

Le Creuset Cast Iron Round Skillet

RRP£99
Available from Le Creuset
Shop now

DEMMEX Solid Copper Saucepan

RRP£104.80
Available from Amazon
Shop now

Barbary & Oak Shallow Casserole Dish

RRP£55
Available from Dunelm
Shop now

18. Reach new heights with a library ladder

In a kitchen with high ceilings, a library-style ladder is far more than a gimmick – it’s a clever bit of vertical thinking. It turns those impossible, hard-to-reach cabinets into a genuinely usable space, perfect for stashing away the Christmas tableware or appliances you only need once in a while.

19. Boldly apply colour

Don’t be afraid to make a statement with your palette to create a confident focal point that really draws the eye. While neutral schemes are safe, a deliberate splash of colour can transform the room substantially. Whether it’s a vibrant, jewel-box pantry cupboard door or a saturated, deep-toned kitchen island, colour allows you to express your personality.

20. Soften with fabric skirts

For a look that feels authentically gathered rather than mass-produced, consider swapping a few lower cupboard doors for fabric skirts. This is a classic hallmark of the English country kitchen, breaking up the usual hard cupboard doors with a hint of pattern and movement.

Aside from being a clever way to hide a bulky appliance, a pleated linen or ticking stripe skirt adds an immediate layer of softness that gives kitchens a charming, old-world feel.

21. Build in a banquette

If you have a spare wall or corner, an integrated banquette seating area is far more space-efficient than traditional table and chairs. This cosy, tucked-away space offers a cafe-like atmosphere, encouraging the family to gather, whether for homework or enjoying a glass of wine.

22. Add a tiled splashback

‘A tiled splashback is one of the most practical and visually impactful elements in a kitchen,’ says Jamie Robb, creative director at Wiltshire-based tile brand Marlborough Tiles.

Because it occupies that crucial eye-level space behind the cooker or sink, it serves as a natural gallery for colour and texture. ‘When choosing tiles for a splashback, durability and cleanability are essential. Glazed ceramic tiles are particularly well-suited because they’re resistant to stains, heat and moisture,’ notes Robb.

Doodlepippin Lovebirds Tile

RRPfrom £11.90
Available from Not On The High Street
Shop now

Ca’Pietra Green & Pink Porcelain Tiles

RRP£55
Available from B&Q
Shop now

Tumia LAC Ceramic Mexican Tile

RRP£19.95
Available from Amazon
Shop now

23. Display your decorative plates and finest silverware

A classic dresser, wall-mounted cabinet or a run of open shelves provides the perfect stage for those treasured pieces that often spend their lives hidden away in bubble wrap. Instead of banishing your fine chinaware or heirloom silverware to the back of a dark cupboard or up in the attic, bring them out into the light.

These pieces don’t necessarily need to be part of the Tuesday night dinner rotation; by displaying them, they become a core part of the room’s character and decoration. You get to enjoy your finest plates and teacups every day, while keeping them ready to be celebrated whenever the right occasion arises.

Shop the look

Daylesford Red Delicious Platter

RRP£90
Available from Daylesford
Shop now

Harrods Bone China Toile Sugar Bowl

RRP£28
Available from Harrods
Shop now

OKA Kintaro Mugs

RRP£50
Available from OKA
Shop now

The best kitchens don’t try too hard to be on trend. They’re the ones that feel like they’ve been put together over time, mixing the sturdy foundations of cabinetry and furniture with accessories and final flourishes. It’s about creating a room that works like a dream when you’re busy cooking, but still feels warm and inviting when the oven is off, and the wine is poured. When a space moves from being just a place to boil the kettle to the heart of the home where everyone naturally gravitates to, you know you’ve got it right.

As you begin pulling your own space together, remember that it’s often the little things that make the biggest difference. To instantly get that lived-in look, we are loving these Habitat X Sanderson & National Trust seat cushions from Argos. They are perfect for softening up uncomfortable wooden chairs – encouraging everyone to linger just a little longer around the dining table or kitchen island.

item.title: I never thought a coffee table could feel luxurious until I saw Rosie Huntington-Whiteley’s styling — some call it boring, but I call it chic
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: Swap the landing pad for a stage and master the art of ‘curated quiet’ with Rosie Huntington-Whiteley’s neutral-minimalist coffee table styling.
item.content:encoded:
I’ve always been drawn to a more minimalist look in my own home to offset the chaos of daily life, but Rosie Huntington-Whitley has just made me realise that I haven’t quite mastered the art of ‘curated quiet’.

While some may dismiss her neutral palette as boring, I find her coffee table styling ideas to be a masterclass in the warm-minimalist aesthetic. It isn’t about being cold or clinical; it’s just the confidence to allow a functional piece to serve as decor rather than a storage unit.

In her latest snaps, her coffee table acts as a stage for a few, highly deliberate objects: a stack of books (my eagle eye can spot this exact Damien Hirst coffee table book from Amazon in RH-W’s collection), a sculptural stone bowl, a black tray, a candle and some objets d'art.

View this post on Instagram

The real trick, though, is how Rosie lets the table breathe. By leaving a generous portion of the surface entirely bare, she creates a sense of calm that makes the whole room feel more open and inviting. It’s a vision I’m eager to emulate in my own home; even a minimal space can fall victim to the stuff of daily life that eventually smothers the curated look.

My own coffee table is a sturdy oak piece, and while I love it, it often disappears under a mountain of stray remotes, empty mugs and discarded post. I’m taking Rosie’s lead by swapping that daily chaos for a strictly tonal edit. I want the texture of the wood to be the star, not the junk on top of it.

I’ve already started trading my mismatched coasters for uniform, heavy marble ones, which I’ll stack on top of a statement tray, and I’m switching the pile of letters and magazines for a few beautiful linen-bound volumes. Here are some items that I’ve currently got in my virtual shopping basket:

Hanobe Serving Wooden Decorative Tray

RRP£22.48
Available from Amazon
Shop now

H&M Marble Tray

RRP£19.99
Available from H&M
Shop now

Diptyque Roses Scented Candle

RRP£179
Available from Selfridges
Shop now

The White Company Marlow Marble Orb

RRP£40
Available from The White Company
Shop now

A New English Style: Timeless Interiors

RRP£35
Available from Amazon
Shop now

M&S Lotus Rattan Floor Lamp

RRP£129
Available from M&S
Shop now

It turns out that what some call boring is actually just the luxury of breathing room and a few carefully considered pieces. My living room feels lighter, and for the first time, my coffee table feels like a choice, and less like a landing pad.

To start your own edit, I recommend grounding your surface with a structural anchor. This marble decorative tray from The White Company is the perfect base to store smaller items like remotes or even a box of matches, and the neutral colourway would slot perfectly into all interior design styles.

item.title: 19 dreamy bedroom ideas for a soft, character-filled sleep space
item.dc:creator: Suzanna Le Grove
item.description: Create a beautiful space in which to sleep soundly and awake with positivity with a dreamy bedroom full of indulgent details.
item.content:encoded:
The bedroom should be a sanctuary intentionally designed to create the right atmosphere for peaceful sleep, and the best bedroom ideas will also allow the room to function practically for daily routines and relaxed mornings on the weekend.

A comfortable environment for a good night’s sleep begins with a restful mattress, quality bed linen, and a suitable window dressing. The next important step is to plan sufficient storage to avoid clutter disturbing the calm. A beautifully layered look is achieved with finishing touches including characterful furnishings, soft lighting and meaningful accessories.

Below, we explore the foundations of a successful scheme, including expert advice on how to choose practical lighting and smart storage, along with style pointers such as mixing prints, using natural textures and choosing artwork.

1. Prioritise smart storage

Hidden and elevated storage helps the bedroom feel clutter-free, ensuring the space is organised and calm. ‘Think vertically and multi‑functionally: in small bedrooms choose compact, dual‑purpose pieces like ottomans with storage and under‑bed drawers, along with wall shelving and recessed niches to free floor space,’ says Anna Trinder, owner and designer at The Dormy House.

‘In larger rooms, balance wardrobes and tall storage with open shelving or display units to keep essentials organised and the scheme feeling light; always plan storage to suit how you live in the space rather than forcing items into ill‑fitting solutions,’ she concludes.

OKA Carena Ghini Feathers Upholstered Trunk

RRP£495
Available from OKA
Shop now

Rowen & Wren Archer Bedside Shelf

RRP£225
Available from Rowen & Wren
Shop now

Dusk Primrose Dressing Table Storage Stool

RRP£129
Available from Dusk
Shop now

2. Consider a natural mattress

If you often feel too hot or cold at night or suffer from allergies, the best mattresses are often those made from natural materials. Dhruv Nagpa, director of The Original Bed Company, advocates silk as an ideal option. ‘A silk mattress provides luxurious softness and comfort while naturally regulating temperature to keep you cool in summer and warm in winter. Its breathable, moisture-wicking fibres help maintain a dry sleeping surface while resisting dust mites and mould.’

Other natural materials to consider, which offer breathable and durable qualities, include wool, bamboo, cotton, natural latex and cashmere. Look for quality assurance labels from GOTS (Global Organic Textiles Standard) and GOLS (Global Organic Latex Standard) to certify the origins of the material.

The British Wool Collection 1500 Pocket Spring

RRP£599
Available from The Cotswold Company
Shop now

Dusk Luxury 80% Goose Down Duvet Collection

RRPfrom £50
Available from Dusk
Shop now

Piglet in Bed Merino Wool Mattress Topper

RRPfrom £189
Available from Piglet in Bed
Shop now

3. Use matching prints

Although currently popular, matching prints are also a timeless look that has endured since the Victorian era. ‘I love the effect that matching a pattern throughout a room scheme can give. I think it is particularly effective in a bedroom, as it creates a cosy and enveloping atmosphere. Layering different textures within the same pattern has great visual impact and creates a sophisticated and timeless feel,’ says decorative artist and printmaker Tess Newall.

To try a more subtle version of this look, match bed linen and cushions in the same pattern. Alternatively, for a more dramatic effect, coordinate wallpaper, lampshades, headboards and even rugs to create multiple layers of the same print. Use accessories in solid colours picked out from the repeated print to keep the look from feeling overwhelming and to show intentionality.

4. Mix colourful, bold prints

To create a cohesive, colourful bedroom, botanical print designer Clarissa Hulse suggests building a visual hierarchy through scale and colour. The process begins with selecting a lead bold pattern –typically on a duvet – and supporting it with mid-scale pillowcases and smaller-print cushions. This variation in scale prevents the space from feeling cluttered, ensuring the layers feel curated rather than chaotic.

The secret to balancing clashing motifs lies in a unified, concise colour palette; when different patterns share key shades, the heavy lifting is done for you. This principle extends beyond the bedscape to wallpaper, rugs and artwork.

To maintain a restful atmosphere, Hulse advises balancing high-energy areas with calmer backdrops: if the walls are bold, keep the bedding restrained. By repeating specific colours throughout the room, you create a harmonious flow that guides the eye without overwhelming the senses.

5. Use tonal colours throughout the scheme

The art of layering different tones of the same colour on multiple surfaces and items across the room (sometimes referred to as double drenching) has several desirable effects, says Deborah Fiddy, CEO and founder of luxury silk bedding brand Gingerlily. ‘Layering varying tones of the same colour across a bedroom is a beautiful way to create cohesion, cosiness and even a touch of drama. By working within a single colour family – whether that’s soft blues, rich mocha or inky forest greens – it allows the eye to move gently across the room.’

As a tester to see if you like the look, Fiddy suggests, ‘It works particularly well on the bed itself, where tonal bedlinen can foster a sense of calm, perfect for evoking a relaxing and serene atmosphere. Try lifting colours from your pillowcases and introducing patterned elements, such as an eiderdown, that thread the shade through the scheme while adding depth and decorative detail.’

6. Create the right window treatment

A suitable window treatment combines practicality with style. Debbie Leigh, design manager at ILIV, explains how to plan the right window treatment for any bedroom. ‘When approaching a bedroom window scheme, think first about the atmosphere you want to create. A bedroom should feel calm and cocooning, so focus on building soft, inviting layers that temper the light and add depth. Take cues from the view outside and the changing natural light throughout the day, allowing these to provide inspiration for a complementary colour palette.’

In terms of the specific types of curtains, she advises, ‘Sheer fabrics are great for a bedroom as they gently diffuse daylight, while lined curtains can help to introduce warmth and a sense of privacy in the evening. For a sumptuous, relaxed look, allow your curtains to drape generously on the floor, and when selecting a pattern, consider the scale of the window so that the design feels balanced within the room. Thoughtful details such as textured weaves, subtle trims and coordinating tiebacks can then be used to add quiet interest without detracting from the wider scheme.’

7. Layer lighting correctly

To achieve the best lighting scheme to meet all requirements, bedroom lighting should be divided up into the same categories as other rooms – task lighting, ambient lighting and accent lighting. Jo Plant, chief creative officer at Pooky, divulges, ‘The most successful bedroom lighting schemes aren’t just about seeing where you’re going; they’re about how the room feels as you wind down.

'To get it right, you need to think in layers: ambient lighting provides that soft, general glow that fills the room; task lighting - such as a well-placed bedside lamp or a directional wall light - is essential for bedtime reading; and accent lighting is the secret ingredient that adds depth, highlighting a favourite painting or a quiet corner to create real atmosphere.’

Consider how the bedroom is used from day to night to choose the right lighting designs. ‘By mixing pendants, elegant wall lights, and the occasional floor lamp, you create a space that transitions seamlessly from a practical morning start to a genuinely inviting, cosy sanctuary at night,’ summarises Plant.

8. Choose a standout table lamp

A bedside lamp should be practical and aesthetically pleasing. It is important to consider the size of a nightstand lamp carefully. Issy Granger, designer and owner of her own eponymous interior brand, explains, ‘When choosing a table lamp for a bedroom, I always begin with proportion and mood. The lamp should feel balanced against the bedside table and headboard, and height is essential. Ideally, the base of the shade sits around eye level when you’re sitting in bed, so the light feels soft rather than glaring.’

To choose an eye-catching style of table lamp, Granger says, ‘Shape is an important design tool – curved forms can soften strong lines, while a straighter silhouette will help balance a more detailed scheme. In a lighter, more neutral room, a lamp is also a wonderful opportunity to introduce a bold colour; it can act as a focal point without overwhelming the space. Above all, choose a piece that casts a warm, diffused glow to create a restful, intimate atmosphere.’

Oliver Bonas Alba Desk & Table Lamp

RRP£80
Available from Oliver Bonas
Shop now

OKA Lakadema Table Lamp Base

RRP£110
Available from OKA
Shop now

Pooky Wilma Table Lamp

RRP£108
Available from Pooky
Shop now

9. Create a cosy corner

If there is space in the bedroom, a seating area in the corner of the room can be enjoyed as a place to relax during the daytime. Jules O-Carroll, interior designer and founder of fabric house Parker & Jules, explains, ‘You’re creating a vignette to enjoy visually and simultaneously a cosy corner from which you can enjoy your bedroom.’ For a larger room, she says, ‘If space allows, a chaise longue is the perfect piece as it's a little decadent! It’s also vital to have a side table at the right height and a lamp.’

In a more modestly proportioned bedroom, the same effect can be achieved on a smaller scale. ‘If space is tight, a slipper chair is the perfect second option. I would add a little footstool and have pictures nearby that you can also enjoy. Finally, having a hanging pendant in that corner can make it really thought through and special,’ says O-Carroll.

10. Opt for complimentary artwork

Incorporating artwork into a bedroom scheme can enhance the desired mood, says Holly Atkinson from art, furniture and interior emporium Rowen Homes. Choosing the right piece of artwork should always be guided by feeling a personal connection to the piece, but there are some guiding principles that can help when searching for a suitable design for a bedroom scheme. ‘When choosing artwork for a bedroom, it’s best to look for pieces that work with your overall colour palette to create a calm and cohesive feel. Soft tones and gentle shapes help keep the space feeling relaxing, while subtle texture or dimension can add interest without overpowering the room,’ advises Atkinson.

Rosie Harbottle Fritillary A3 Print

RRP£80
Available from Not Another Bill
Shop now

Bertola Home Studio Stained Bobbin Frame

RRPfrom £27
Available from The Roost
Shop now

OKA Ovalis Framed Floral Prints

RRP£295
Available from OKA
Shop now

11. Create a romantic scheme

To achieve a romantic scheme that feels timeless and not old-fashioned, the trick is to mix design eras for a fresh feel. ‘You don’t have to limit yourself to one pattern; build up a scheme that incorporates lots of lovely designs that speak to you — embrace the new and old. Florals paired with bolder, more geometric shapes can really update a look. Small-scale patterns against large florals, decorative stripes, and global styles can create diverse looks, from country houses to modern townhouses,’ explains Rebecca Craig, lead designer at Sanderson.

12. Personalise your headboard
