Choosing your favourite fabric to cover a headboard ensures you will continue to love the design for years to come. 'Upholstered headboards are a great way of both personalising and softening a bedroom, ‘ explains Shauna Dennison-Taylor, creative director of Penny Morrison.

Consider both vintage and new designs to create the desired effect. ‘Whether it’s a fabric you source from your local showroom or design centre, or a piece of antique or heirloom fabric you’ve been waiting for a reason to use, upholstered headboards are both a practical and decorative way to introduce more detail and personality to a bedroom,’ says Dennison-Taylor.

13. Incorporate antiques

Including antiques in a bedroom scheme adds a soulful sense of depth and storytelling, but it requires the right mix of pieces, says Annabelle Holland, interior designer and creative director of luxury interior brand Anboise. ‘The key thing when incorporating antiques into your design is to strike a balance between old and new. A room that is too heavy on antiques can feel rather severe, while a space that only has modern furniture can feel cold and characterless. I find the best way to achieve this balance is by using antiques as accent pieces.’

To begin the process of sourcing specific antique pieces for a bedroom, Holland suggests, ‘Antique textiles such as tapestries, and in particular rugs, are also an effective way to incorporate antiques into contemporary designs. They can serve as a great starting point for creating a colour palette for the room, tying together different design elements and creating a cohesive look.’

14. Decorate dressing areas

If your dressing area is within the bedroom, focus on softening functional furnishings with decorative touches. For a dressing table, a stylish upholstered stool, elegant accessories, a plant or vase of flowers, and a decorative gallery on the wall above will stop the area from feeling stark.

For wardrobes, think about their exterior appearance, says Cotswold-based interior designer Sean Symington; ‘We don't like a bedroom to feel like you're sleeping in a dressing room, so we love using fabric or wallpaper on wardrobe doors to add softness, making them feel like their own stand-alone furniture rather than fitted wardrobes.’

Nina Campbell Willis Dressing Table

RRP£599
Available from Next
Shop now

Addison Ross Pink Lacquer Box

RRP£54
Available from Addison Ross
Shop now

Oliver Bonas Mother of Pearl Dressing Table Mirror

RRP£115
Available from Oliver Bonas
Shop now

15. Bring in natural textures with wooden furniture

Ground a bedroom scheme with wooden furnishings that celebrate nature’s organic patterns. To select the right wood, consider whether a smooth or tactile finish is desirable; both will bring a different texture and sense of sensuality to the scheme. For maximum impact, especially if you are just incorporating one piece in a natural wood finish, burl wood offers a unique and eye-catching pattern.

At the other end of the scale, furniture made from maple or beech will have a more subtle and light wood grain. Alternatively, for darker wood pieces, look to vintage and antique emporiums to find one-off furnishings in rich finishes such as walnut and mahogany.

16. Invest in natural bed linen

Similar to a natural mattress, bedding made of natural materials has moisture-wicking qualities to help regulate your body temperature year-round. To decide upon the right natural material, personal preference with regards to the texture and feel is important to consider – for example, wool has a thick, weighty pile, whereas linen has a lighter and softer feel.

When you have selected a suitable natural material, always check whether the bed linen is a hundred per cent natural and look for quality assurance labels – such as GOTS (Global Organic Textile Standard) and Woolmark, which ensure the material is pure wool- to ensure the origins of the material.

Piglet in Bed Sage Gingham Linen Blend Valance

RRPfrom £109
Available from Piglet in Bed
Shop now

Christy Pure Organic Cotton Duvet Set

RRPfrom £55
Available from Christy
Shop now

Secret Linen Store Patchwork Cotton Throw

RRP£169
Available from Secret Linen Store
Shop now

17. Create a cosy hideaway with a bed canopy

A canopy or simply creating an oversized headboard in a decorative pattern is also another way to create impact around the bed. Ensure it is beautifully and indulgently dressed, layering different linens, quilts, pillows, and decorative throws that are alluring and inviting. Pay attention to colour schemes, textures, and patterns to enhance the overall aesthetic, making the space feel cosy yet sophisticated, and truly luxurious.

18. Make the bed the focal point of the bedroom

The key part of the bedroom is, of course, the bed, and this should be given priority when considering layout and decoration. Make it the hero of the room and celebrate it. An impressive four-poster can be used in many rooms - not just large spaces; it can make quite the impact in a small bedroom, too.

Clever solutions for lighting, such as integrating into the canopy or into the bedsides, can negate the need for a bedside table or allow for the central space to be used to full height.

19. Place a bench at the foot of the bed

Positioning an upholstered bench at the end of the bed instantly elevates the room, lending it an indulgent, boutique-hotel feel. This simple addition provides a dedicated spot to sit other than the bed itself, transforming the space into a private sanctuary. It is the perfect nook for pausing to read a book or magazine, offering a quiet escape from the daily hustle and bustle of family life.

From implementing larger practical changes to making smaller style tweaks, simple bedroom alterations can improve your daily routines and well-being.

If there is one overarching design rule to bear in mind, it is to consider sensuality when selecting designs for the bedroom scheme. To choose an easy starting point, consider the feeling as you climb out of bed; a comforting rug underfoot – such as this scallop edge rug from Dusk – is a worthwhile investment.

item.title: How to design a principal bedroom – Expert tips for a restful sanctuary
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: How to transform a private space into a sanctuary for total renewal.
item.content:encoded:
In the new coffee table book, Bedrooms as Sanctuaries, Carpe Diem Beds explores a simple question: What does it mean to sleep well? While a guest bedroom is all about making others feel welcome, a principal bedroom is much more of a personal project. It is a space that should ‘hold us, body and mind,’ acting as a private retreat from the stresses of the outside world.

Drawing on the expert advice in the book's design chapter, co-written by Frida Ramstedt, here are five ways to shape a bedroom into a personal sanctuary.

1. Find a bed that ‘holds the body’

In a principal bedroom, the bed is the heart of the room. Rather than a standard mattress, it should be designed specifically for the individual. As sleep specialist Stephanie Romiszewski notes in the book, true rest comes from feeling safe and supported.

Whether choosing the adjustable technology of the Vindö or the classic feel of the Skaftö, a bed is a long-term investment and should allow you to wake up feeling refreshed and ready to start each morning.

2. Use the ‘2:8 rule’ to clear the mind

Interior expert Frida Ramstedt shares a brilliant secret for a peaceful bedroom: the ‘2:8 rule.’ The idea is to keep only 20% of belongings on display while 80% are tucked away out of sight. By hiding the clutter of daily life, ‘visual noise’ is reduced. This helps the brain switch off the moment you walk through the door, turning a busy room into a quiet, restful space.

3. Layer soft, ‘tone-in-tone’ colours

To create a sense of stillness, the book suggests moving away from bright, high-contrast colours. Instead, consider a ‘tone-in-tone’ palette – using different shades and textures of the same neutral colour. By layering natural materials like linen, wood and wool in similar tones, the room feels cohesive, calm and considered. This gentle environment soothes the senses and prepares the mind for deeper, more restorative sleep.

4. Invest in the ‘micro-climate’ of the bed

A bed should be comfortable in every season. While guest rooms might only be used once every so often, a principal bedroom needs a permanent solution for temperature control. High-quality mulberry silk linens and duvets are a natural choice because they are highly breathable. Silk helps to regulate body temperature, keeping it cool in the summer and warm in the winter, which is an essential ingredient for a restful night’s sleep.

5. Protect the REM cycle

The environment of a principal bedroom should be designed to support the body’s natural rest. To enjoy a full REM cycle - the stage of sleep where the mind and body truly recover - the room needs to be consistently dark and quiet. Simple choices, like using heavy sound-dampening curtains and plush rugs, help to cocoon the space from the outside world. As the book reminds us, creating a sanctuary is about protecting the one-third of our lives that we spend in sleep, ensuring we wake up feeling mentally sharp and restored.

Ultimately, a principal bedroom should reflect personal style. Designer Lars Wallin explains in the book that he approached his bedroom design ‘rather untouched’ by standard decorating rules: ‘It's just my vision and that made it very personal.’ This shows that while expert rules provide the foundation, the true sense of sanctuary comes from what you like.

The feeling of true sanctuary, though built on expert principles, is ultimately derived from personal preference. By combining these professional design principles with your own creative vision, you can create a space that feels uniquely yours.

Visit carpediembeds.co.uk to shop the collection and order your copy of Bedrooms as Sanctuaries.

item.title: Why interior designers choose iron beds for character homes
item.dc:creator: The English Home
item.description: The Iron and Brass Bed Company explores why interior designers choose handcrafted, British-made frames to create inviting spaces that truly stand the test of time.
item.content:encoded:
There is a quiet confidence to a beautifully made iron bed. Interior designers understand this instinctively. They often choose iron frames when working on homes with history and personality.

These beds offer structure without heaviness. They bring craftsmanship, heritage and visual elegance into the bedroom. Most importantly, they adapt to changing interior styles.

In character properties, especially, designers value furniture that respects the architecture. Iron beds feel authentic in period settings. Yet they also suit relaxed modern interiors.

Today, there is also a growing appreciation for British-made furniture. Clients increasingly ask where pieces are produced and how they are made.

For those exploring traditional craftsmanship, a wide collection of certified British-made iron beds can be viewed here.

A natural companion for period homes

Character homes often feature beautiful architectural details. High ceilings, original fireplaces and tall windows shape the atmosphere.

Interior designers aim to complement these features rather than compete with them. Iron beds work wonderfully in this context. Their open framework maintains sightlines and allows light to travel.

Unlike heavier upholstered frames, iron beds feel visually lighter. This helps rooms retain their sense of proportion. Even in compact bedrooms, the effect is noticeable.

Historically, metal bedsteads became popular during the nineteenth century. They were valued for durability and improved hygiene. Today, designers appreciate the same practical strengths.

They also admire the timeless silhouette that iron beds provide. Gentle curves, hand-formed castings and elegant proportions feel inherently English.

When paired with natural textiles and layered bedding, these beds create inviting spaces full of character.

The value of genuine British craftsmanship

There is renewed respect for furniture made close to home. Interior designers recognise the importance of supporting skilled British makers. They understand the difference this brings to quality and longevity.

Handcrafted iron beds represent a tradition that continues to thrive. Skilled makers shape metal using techniques refined over generations. This results in frames that feel both substantial and refined.

Importantly, some manufacturers now hold official Made in Britain certification. This mark confirms genuine UK production and trusted craftsmanship.

Designers often highlight this when advising clients who seek sustainable, long-lasting furniture. Locally made pieces reduce transport impact and support regional skills.

They also offer reassurance. Buyers know where their furniture comes from and how it was produced.

In an age of fast interiors, this sense of authenticity carries real value.

A designer’s perspective

Interior designer Charlotte Reynolds regularly incorporates British-made iron beds by Iron and Brass Bed Company into her projects. She believes they offer unmatched versatility in character homes. 'Iron beds create instant atmosphere without dominating the room. They feel rooted in history yet work beautifully in modern schemes. Clients also appreciate knowing their bed was made in Britain.'

This balance of heritage and adaptability makes iron beds a dependable choice. Designers can style them formally or casually. The frame provides structure while textiles shape the mood.

Paint finishes also play a role. Soft neutrals suit calm country interiors. Deeper tones add drama in townhouse settings.

The bed, therefore, becomes both a focal point and a foundation.

Designing for longevity rather than trends

Interior designers increasingly prioritise longevity when selecting furniture. They encourage clients to invest in pieces that will age gracefully.

Iron beds support this philosophy. Their strength ensures they remain stable for many years. Unlike trend-driven upholstered designs, they rarely feel dated.

Many metal bedframes can also be repainted or refreshed over time. This flexibility allows homeowners to adapt their interiors without replacing core furniture.

Designers view this as both practical and sustainable. Fewer replacements mean reduced waste and greater long-term value.

In period homes, especially, furniture that evolves with the space feels more appropriate. Iron beds offer this quiet permanence.

They become part of the home’s story rather than a temporary addition.

Styling iron beds for contemporary living

Although rooted in tradition, iron beds suit modern lifestyles. Designers often mix them with contemporary lighting and relaxed textiles. This contrast creates fresh, inviting bedrooms.

Layering plays an important role. Crisp cotton sheets, wool throws and linen cushions soften the metal framework. Patterned quilts can add personality without overwhelming the room.

Wall colours also influence the final effect. Gentle greens, chalky blues and warm neutrals highlight the frame's silhouette. Meanwhile, textured wallpapers bring depth to simple schemes.

In smaller spaces, keeping surrounding furniture light helps maintain balance. Slim bedside tables and wall-mounted lighting preserve visual flow.

Through thoughtful styling, iron beds move effortlessly between classic and modern interiors.

Supporting sustainable interior choices

Sustainability now shapes many design decisions. Clients want furniture that aligns with their environmental values.

British-made iron beds support this shift towards responsible interiors. Local manufacturing reduces shipping distances and associated emissions. Durable construction also extends product lifespan.

Designers often pair iron frames with natural mattresses and breathable bedding. This combination promotes comfort while reducing reliance on synthetic materials.

Choosing well-made furniture is an investment in both the home and the planet. It reflects a slower, more considered approach to interiors.

Iron beds, therefore, represent more than aesthetic appeal. They embody thoughtful design choices for modern living.

A timeless foundation for characterful bedrooms

Ultimately, interior designers choose iron beds because they offer balance. They combine heritage with flexibility and strength with elegance.

In character homes, this harmony feels especially important. Furniture should enhance architectural beauty while supporting everyday comfort.

Iron beds achieve this effortlessly. They create structure without heaviness and history without nostalgia.

For homeowners seeking authentic British craftsmanship and enduring design, these frames remain a compelling choice.

To explore traditional styles crafted in Britain, visit Iron and Brass Bed Co., a family company that has been handcrafting metal bedframes in Norfolk for over twenty years.

A thoughtfully chosen iron bed can shape the atmosphere of a bedroom for decades. It becomes both a design statement and a lasting companion in the home.

item.title: The best neutral paint colours – 10 expert-approved shades for a soulful, timeless home
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: From textured limewashes to soft shades of plaster pink, five interior designers share their favourite colours used to create timeless, atmospheric spaces.
item.content:encoded:
Finding the best neutral paint colours is like searching for the perfect white T-shirt; it is deceptively simple until you are faced with dozens of near-identical options.

Moving away from drab magnolias, today’s soulful paint ideas are designed to adapt to a home’s architecture. Mastering this look requires a discerning eye for the undertone – that elusive hint of moss or rose that shifts with the light. To achieve this, designers rely on paints that provide the depth necessary for an authentic, lived-in finish.

Since neutral pigments react to light, observe how shades change from daylight to evening illumination. An earthy colour can appear cold under LED light, so test swatches in sunlight and against directional light, like this wall light from M&S. To guide you through these nuances, we've asked top interior designers to share the tins they swear by.

1. Farrow & Ball Lime White

Many designers prefer paints that mimic traditional materials to add a sense of character and softness to a room. ‘Farrow & Ball’s Lime White is one of my favourite neutral paints,’ says Henriette von Stockhausen, founder of London and Dorset-based interior design studio VSP Interiors.

‘It has a wonderful, old-school quality to it that almost resembles traditional lime plaster, which gives it much more depth and softness than a standard white. In older houses, especially, that slightly chalky, nuanced tone feels far more sympathetic to the architecture than anything too crisp or stark.

‘I often use it on cabinetry because it works beautifully alongside natural materials like stone flooring and traditional features such as stone mullion windows. It creates a calm, timeless backdrop while still allowing contrast elsewhere in the space.’

2. Paint & Paper Library Leather

The timeless appeal of a country kitchen is rooted in its palette. To balance rustic charm with a polished finish, designers often choose sophisticated neutrals that offer a sense of history and depth.

‘My favourite neutrals all tend to come from Paint & Paper Library, as they have such a beautiful depth and subtlety,’ says award-winning interior designer Laura Stephens. She highlights Paint & Paper Library's Leather as a standout.

‘Leather is a particularly lovely example as it’s a very soft, warm neutral with a gentle richness to it. We used it in Shade I on the walls and Shade II on the cabinetry in our SW18 project, which created a layered look that still felt calm and cohesive while giving the room just enough contrast.’

3. Paint & Paper Library Clay

For a versatile ‘middle ground’, Stephens points to Paint & Paper Library Clay. ‘Another fail-safe neutral for me is Clay. It sits beautifully in that perfect middle ground, neither too grey nor too yellow, which makes it incredibly versatile. In our recent Herne Hill project, we used it across adjoining spaces, with Clay I in the dining room and Clay III in the adjacent snug. Using the same colour family in different strengths is a wonderful way to create flow between rooms while still giving each space its own distinct character.’

4. Bauwerk Limewash Hoar Frost

Texture is the secret weapon for turning a flat wall into a feature that feels lived-in and soulful. ‘Bauwerk limewash is another favourite because it adds a wonderful softness and texture to walls," says Sophie Pringle, founder of Surrey-based interior design studio Pringle & Pringle. ‘In our Chiswick project, we used Bauwerk's Hoar Frost with Farrow & Ball Shaded White in eggshell on the panelling below, which created a calm layered scheme.’

5. Little Greene Pearl

Pringle also highlights Little Greene Pearl as a restful choice. ‘Pearl by Little Greene is a lovely bedroom neutral. It feels calm and gentle while still providing enough warmth to support layered fabrics and furnishings, as seen in our Notting Hill bedroom,’ she notes.

6. Farrow & Ball Cromarty

The right shade can completely transform a sun-drenched room, turning harsh glare into a soft, atmospheric glow. ‘We also love Farrow & Ball Cromarty, particularly in rooms with strong south-facing light,’ says Pringle. ‘Its soft grey-green tone feels relaxed and atmospheric, and we used it in the sitting room of our Notting Hill project. We mixed it with Farrow & Ball Mizzle on the woodwork.’

7. Paint & Paper Library Architectural Colour Range

Using varying strengths of the same hue is the ultimate designer shortcut to a space that feels curated rather than cluttered. ‘The Paint and Paper Library architectural colour ranges are also really useful as you can use different strengths in the same palette,’ notes Hampshire-based interior designer Emma Sherlock.

Pringle agrees: ‘Paint & Paper Library's Slate IV works beautifully in townhouses where you want something calm but with depth... while Stone or Canvas suit country houses particularly well, bringing warmth and softness without feeling overly decorative.’

‘I used Stone I on the panelling in this sitting room in my house and then used a darker shade (Stone IV) on the bookshelves on the other side of the room,’ Sherlock adds.

8. Farrow & Ball Off White No.3

Whether you're looking to add character to kitchen or bedroom panelling, choosing a shade with a soft, chalky undertone is the key to a space that feels lived-in rather than brand new.

‘I’m a big fan of Off White No.3 by Farrow & Ball and often use it on woodwork,’ shares Sherlock. ‘It’s a really useful off-white and works especially well in country houses as it is quite chalky, warm, and has an underlying green tone. I used it on the panelling on my kitchen walls.’

9. Farrow & Ball Setting Plaster

Sherlock also champions pale pink as a neutral base. ‘I would also consider pale pink to be a neutral and use it a lot - it’s very flattering on skin tone, and you can use it as a base in a room and mix it with lots of other stronger colours. I used Farrow & Ball Setting Plaster in my studio and love it,’ she says.

10. Bauwerk Limewash Sandstone

A truly restful space is built on a foundation of organic textures rather than bold pops of colour. ‘We used Bauwerk Limewash in Sandstone, chosen for its warm undertones and subtle variation, which adds softness and depth without dominating the room,’ explains Marcelina Janiszewska, head of interior design at London-based architecture and interior design studio Project London.

‘The bedroom was designed around a neutral palette to create a calm, restful atmosphere. Rather than relying on colour, interest comes from layering natural materials and textures. The limewashed walls are paired with a linen headboard, oak bed frame, grasscloth wardrobe inserts, a rattan pendant light, and small details such as bamboo handles, all contributing to an organic, relaxed feel.

To add contrast and personality, the neutral base is balanced with deeper tones such as terracotta and moss green, alongside softer textures, including velvet cushions and curtains.’

The magic of a neutral colour palette is that it doesn’t scream for attention; it acts as a quiet conductor, harmonising all of the elements of your interior. By trading flat, one-dimensional colour for shades with depth - whether that’s the chalky, touchable soul of a limewash or the rhythm of tonal layering - you head towards a beautiful home that feels timeless.

For those looking to dive deeper into the world of colour theory and application, we recommend this book: Farrow & Ball: How to Decorate – an essential guide to transforming your space with confidence. It would make a lovely gift for the interior obsessive, too.

item.title: Five luxury essentials for the ultimate guest bedroom experience
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: How to transform a simple guest stay into a restorative retreat through the art of the evening ritual.
item.content:encoded:
Creating a memorable guest bedroom is something that goes far beyond just the aesthetic appeal. While we often focus on the look of a spare room, true luxury is in the sensory details that enable a deep and restorative unwind.

Working with Swedish interior designer Louise Hjorth, Carpe Diem Beds has identified the five all-important pillars of a high-end guest experience that will make your friends and family feel like they are staying at a five-star hotel.

By prioritising support, texture and ritual, you can transform a simple stay into a night of pure bliss.

1. Prioritise ergonomic support

The most important part of any guest experience is the quality of the sleep itself. Louise Hjorth’s benchmark for comfort is the Skaftö or Sandö bed. A while back i acquired a Skaftö double bed from Carpe Diem Beds. I was amazed! It was, and still is, like lying down on fluffy clouds while at the same time having perfect support for the body. Now i sleep better than ever,’ she says.

To ensure a one-size-fits-all, luxury feel, a medium-firm mattress paired with a topper - like the Carpe Diem Beds Luxury Mattress Topper - provides the perfect ergonomic support needed to erase the physical tension of the day.

2. Scent the space

First impressions are often shaped by the atmosphere of a room. Before your guests arrive, establish a sense of ‘visual and mental quiet’ by lighting a scented candle – try the Gryning (Dawn) or the Skymning (Dusk) hand-poured candles by Carpe Diem Beds. These organic soy-wax candles, featuring notes of bergamot, magnolia and cedarwood, act as a sensory anchor to a room – signalling to the guest that they have entered a cocooning retreat away from the outside world.

3. Provide a tactile unwinding kit

True comfort is found in the materials we touch. To help your guests settle into their evening, provide a kit of luxury essentials. A 16-momme mulberry silk robe and a pair of Australian sheepskin slippers are more than just comforts; they are physical invitations to slow down and embrace the stillness of being at home. As Carpe Diem Beds describes, these accessories are designed as the ‘perfect finishing touch for your dream sleep sanctuary.’

4. Regulate sleep temperature

A common cause of poor sleep when you're away is not being able to nail the right temperature; it's tough to find that 'sweet spot' you've got at home. To solve this, Louise suggests dressing the guest bed in Vindstilla bed linen. Crafted from exquisite mulberry silk, these linens are naturally breathable and heat-regulating, ensuring your guests stay comfortable regardless of the season. It is the final touch that makes a bed hard to leave.

5. Ensure total darkness

To guarantee a deep REM cycle - the deep stage of sleep essential for processing memories and recovery - a guest room must offer total darkness. Along with double, blackout curtains, provide a silk sleep mask. This small, thoughtful essential is kind to the skin and protects the guest’s neutral melatonin production from early morning sun, allowing them to gently wake up on their own terms.

By focusing on these five luxury essentials, you aren’t just decorating a room; you are curating a total wellness experience. And as a final note from Louise: ‘Just remember to keep most of the decor within the same colour scheme. That way you will be able to add personality with personal items, patterns and colours without creating clutter.’

If you’re ready to elevate your hosting experience at home, explore the Unwinding Routine collection and handcrafted beds at carpediembeds.co.uk

item.title: Paint ideas: 21 creative ways to drench your home in personality and soul
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: From ‘unexpected’ pops of red to the power of colour drenching, discover how to reinvent your space with paint.
item.content:encoded:
Exploring fresh paint ideas is the quickest way to breathe life into a room; new pigment is the most transformative tool in any design kit.

Colour has a clever way of shifting the entire energy of a home. A moody, deep ink can add drama to a small corner, while a pale neutral can pull every drop of natural light and make a space feel twice the size.

Before the transformation begins, investing in a professional brush, like this angled sash brush from Amazon, will make life much easier when tackling those tricky corners and mouldings. Once your kit is ready, you can begin the fun. From subtle tonal shifts to bold statements, here are some of the most effective ways to work with colour.

1. Match walls and joinery

Aligning your built-in elements with your wall colour is a sophisticated way to expand a room’s proportions. ‘Painting joinery the same colour as the walls is a great way to make a room feel bigger and more cohesive. When cupboards, panelling or wardrobes blend into the wall colour, the eye isn’t interrupted by visual breaks, which creates a calmer and more spacious feel,’ says Sophie Pringle, founder of Surrey-based interior design studio Pringle & Pringle.

This monochromatic approach works beautifully with bespoke cabinetry, allowing the craftsmanship to speak through the texture rather than create high-contrast shadows.

2. Trial the unexpected red theory

If a scheme feels flat, a burst of primary colour can be the secret ingredient to lift it. ‘A small hit of red can bring warmth and balance to a scheme, particularly when a room leans towards cooler tones,’ says Pringle.

She notes that using it on smaller furniture or architectural details 'can create a focal point and gently enliven an otherwise calm palette.’ It’s the interior equivalent of a red lip; a bold, intentional choice that signals confidence and adds a surprising pop to an otherwise neutral backdrop.

3. Elongate with stripes

Stripes are a classic design tool used to manipulate a sense of scale. Vertical stripes can make a low ceiling feel significantly higher and more grand, while horizontal bands can make a narrow room feel more expansive.

While you could cut corners and go with striped wallpaper, painting them yourself gives you total control over the exact width and hue of each band. All you need is a steady hand and a roll of high-quality painters' tape, like this Frog Tape from Amazon, to keep those lines razor-sharp.

4. Drench the room in colour

Create a truly immersive atmosphere by painting every surface in the same shade to remove visual boundaries. 'Painting the walls, ceiling, woodwork, cornicing and skirting all in the same colour creates a seamless envelope that removes visual breaks, which in turn can make a room feel larger than it actually is,' says Carina Raymond, founder of London-based interior design firm, Studio Raymond.

This is a great solution for small kitchens where you may want to minimise visual clutter, make the space work hard despite its proportions, and create a cosy, cocooning effect.

5. Refresh the fireplace

A fireplace is the natural focal point of a room. Updating the surround with a fresh lick of paint in a different colour can tie an entire scheme together, turning a traditional feature into a more modern statement.

The most successful paint projects are the ones that reflect your personality rather than just following a fleeting trend. Whether you decide to colour-drench a cosy snug or add a hand-painted border to a doorway, these small acts of creativity are what turn a house into a home.

6. Highlight the door frame

The transition between rooms is an excellent place to add a considered choice that guides the eye. ‘Painting a door frame is a small detail that can make a big difference to how considered a room feels,’ says Pringle. She advises that you should ‘always paint the door the same colour as the door frame or architrave’ if you want your woodwork to feel entirely cohesive. This prevents a checkerboard effect of white frames against coloured walls, which can feel disjointed and unfinished.

7. Enhance with panelling

Wall panelling offers a wonderful opportunity to play with light and shadow, especially in traditional schemes or period homes. 'I like to paint both the walls and the panelling in exactly the same colour. This keeps the look fresh and modern, as the architectural texture of the panels still reads beautifully but without the contrast feeling too traditional,' says Raymond.

8. Elevate the ceiling

Treating the ceiling with the same intention as you would the walls can ensure a room feels professionally finished rather than forgotten about. 'I think of the ceiling as the ‘fifth wall’ of the room - when it’s treated with the same attention as the rest of the space, the whole scheme feels more complete and layered,' says Raymond.

For rooms with high ceilings, ‘painting the ceiling in a darker or contrasting tone is a wonderful trick... it visually brings the ceiling down slightly, making the room feel far more intimate and cosy,’ says award-winning interior designer Laura Stephens.

9. Coordinate woodwork with wallpaper

Pull a specific shade from your wallpaper and apply it to the surrounding trim to create an integrated look. When wallpapering a room, I often like to pick out one of the colours within the wallpaper and carry it onto the ceiling. It’s a simple trick, but it creates a much more playful and cohesive scheme because the colours start to talk to one another,' says Raymond.

10. Introduce a textured paint finish

Adding texture through paint adds a layer of depth that standard emulsions can’t match. For a chalky, mineral aesthetic, consider a limewash finish. While this specific texture may not suit all interior design styles, it would work particularly well in a rustic or Mediterranean-inspired space.

In rooms where practical performance is just as important as aesthetics, it is important to choose a finish that can withstand the elements. 'When panelling sits behind a bath or basin, durability is key, so I always recommend using an eggshell finish as it’s far more hard-wearing and easier to wipe down than a standard matt paint,' says Stephens. This is essential advice for anyone exploring bathroom ideas where moisture resistance and wipeability are non-negotiables.

11. Layer in different tones of the same colour

Using different depths of the same hue can subtly define a space without the jarring effect of high contrast. 'Using slightly different tones of the same colour across walls, woodwork and ceilings can create a wonderfully cohesive look while still adding depth and interest,' says Stephens. This technique is a fantastic way to introduce diverse shades for those who prefer a more subtle approach rather than making a bold statement.

12. Have fun with motifs and murals

Hand-painted details add a bespoke, artisanal quality that wallpaper cannot replicate. Whether it is a delicate botanical vine winding around a doorframe, a bold geometric pattern or something more lifelike, they allow for total creative freedom and give a home a totally unique and personal touch. This approach is a lovely way to spark the imagination in a children’s bedroom, or to be a topic of conversation in an unexpected place, such as in a cloakroom or a hidden larder.

13. Define with shapes

Utilise paint to create geometric shapes - such as a large circle behind a bed, a crisp rectangle to frame a sofa or a hand-painted archway - to act as an instant focal point. It is a brilliant way to add architectural interest to a plain room without the need for expensive construction. By playing with these sharp lines and curves, you can mimic the structural elegance of alcoves or high-end mouldings using nothing more than a brush.

14. Revitalise floorboards

Painting floorboards can brighten a dark room or add a rustic, lived-in feel to a property without renovating. It is a cost-effective alternative to replacing flooring and allows for a completely custom colour underfoot that ties the whole scheme together. If you are feeling particularly creative, why not mimic grand Victorian checkerboard tiles? Just be sure to opt for dedicated floor paint to ensure the finish can withstand daily foot traffic.

15. Transform old furniture

Before replacing an old chest of drawers or a dining table, consider the power of upcycling. A fresh coat of paint can turn a dated piece of furniture into a statement item that perfectly matches your new palette. A high-gloss finish can lend a modern, lacquer-like effect, while a chalk or eggshell finish offers a more traditional feel.

16. Zone the space

In open-plan homes, paint is the easiest way to define different areas without physical partitions. Using a specific colour for the dining nook and another for the seating area helps to organise the home visually. This is particularly effective for carving out a dressing area or a workspace; by painting an alcove or a recessed corner in a contrasting shade, you can create a dedicated spot for a desk or dressing table that feels separate from the rest of the room.

17. Decorate a dado rail

Painting a dado or picture rail in a contrasting or complementary shade highlights the room’s architectural bones. It breaks up the vertical plane of the wall and provides a perfect stopping point for two different colours, or perhaps a combination of paint and wallpaper, creating a traditional, layered look.

18. Frame with borders

Adding a painted border around a window or door adds a tailored, high-end finish. It frames the room’s features and adds a layer of intricate, designer detail that mimics the effect of an expensive trim.

19. Contrast with opposite colours

If you’re looking to inject a bit of energy and soul into a room and aren’t afraid to go all-out, try playing with complementary colours – those sitting directly opposite each other on the colour wheel. Pairing a blue tone with a zesty orange, for instance, creates an instant visual hun that standard tonal schemes just can’t match.

20. Refurbish kitchen cabinets

Refreshing kitchen cabinets with a durable, specialist paint can completely modernise the heart of the home. It is a sustainable way to renovate that provides a fresh look without a full rip-out. If you really want to play on current trends, play with green kitchen ideas; a soft sage can feel calm and pretty, while a deep forest green adds a sense of heritage and drama.

21. Disguise radiators

Painting radiators is a transformative trick that can go one of two ways: to make a radiator vanish, paint it the exact shade and finish as your walls – this is a lifesaver in small bathrooms where a bulky white unit can break up a scheme. However, if you want to be more intentional, try matching the radiator to something else in the room, such as the joinery. This shifts it from an eyesore into a structural feature, making it feel like a built-in part of the room's cabinetry or woodwork.

The most successful paint projects are the ones that reflect your personality rather than just following a fleeting trend. Whether you decide to colour-drench a cosy snug or add a hand-painted border to a doorwar, these small acts of creativity are what turn a house into a home. The beauty of paint lies in its versatility; if you crave a change of mood for the next season, the transformation is only a brushstroke away.

If you’re feeling inspired to test out the unexpected red theory that we mentioned above on a mirror frame or a chair, try this Annie Sloan Chalk Paint in Capability Red from Amazon – it’s the ideal starting point and requires almost no prep and offers a brilliant, rich pigment.

item.title: Is this the most beautiful cookware ever made? Why I’m clearing my kitchen shelves for Le Creuset’s new petal collection
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: I’ve found the one spring showstopper your kitchen is missing to get you ready for hosting season.
item.content:encoded:
This latest Le Creuset drop has made me so excited for spring hosting, especially with the new season officially arriving next weekend.

I’m always on the hunt for the best non-toxic cookware, and the new Jardin collection hits that sweet spot where wellness meets style. It's truly a beautiful marriage of form and function that elevates the everyday ritual of cooking.

I’m planning to keep the Petal Shallow Casserole Dish from the brand out on my kitchen island from now until the autumn because it’s honestly too pretty to hide in a cupboard. And there’s one particular colour that has made me fall in love...

Cast Iron Petal Shallow Casserole in Pêche

I am completely obsessed with the new 'Pêche' shade; it’s a soft, sun-kissed peach that feels warm and fresh without being overly sugary. The lid features a stunning embossed petal relief inspired by the French anemone, which makes the whole piece look like an artisanal sculpture on your stovetop. It is the perfect size for serving up a spring pea risotto or a light Sunday roast directly to the table, and because it's Le Creuset, you know it’s a lifetime investment that will stay looking this beautiful for decades.

RRP£309
Shop now

I’ve found that while deep pots are great for winter stews, this shallow shape is what I actually use for spring hosting. The wider base means you get much better results when you’re cooking chicken or browning seasonal vegetables. I love that I can whip up something delicious and then carry the whole thing straight to the table.

Because I’m a bit of a perfectionist when it comes to a theme, I’ve already started thinking about how this will look for the long weekend/ I’m planning to use the casserole dish as the anchor for my Easter table decorating ideas, surrounded by simple linen napkins and fresh spring blooms. When I’m not using it to host, I’ll be giving it pride of place on my open shelving – it’s far too pretty to be hidden behind a cupboard door.

If you’re looking to refresh your own collection, these are the other standout pieces from the new launch I’ve added to my wishlist:

Stoneware Petal Mug

RRP£25
Available from Le Creuset
Shop now

Cast Iron Petal Soup Pot

RRP£345
Available from Le Creuset
Shop now

Stoneware Jardin Oval Serving Platter

RRP£80
Available from Le Creuset
Shop now

Stoneware Jardin Set of 2 Egg Cups

RRP£23
Available from Le Creuset
Shop now

Cast Iron Petal Shallow Casserole in Garnet

RRP£309
Available from Le Creuset
Shop now

Stoneware Jardin Petite Casserole

RRP£27
Available from Le Creuset
Shop now

I’m more than ready to swap out my dull cookware for something that brightens up the everyday. I’ve already carved out a spot in my kitchen for this cheery dish, so that it’s ready for the first bank holiday lunch of the year. If you’re looking to refresh your space, why not look to the textiles too? Some colourful table linen, like this floral tablecloth from M&S, would look beautiful layered beneath your culinary creations – it’s such an easy way to pull the whole look together.

item.title: Everyone asks why my home smells amazing whenever they visit – I can’t gatekeep the White Company candle responsible now it’s finally in the sale
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: My all-time favourite candle has finally been discounted in The White Company sale, and it’s the one staple I rely on to make my home feel like a luxury sanctuary year-round.
item.content:encoded:
I’ve been refreshing my browser all week in anticipation, but the wait is officially over; just as my favourite candle was about to burn out. It has finally been discounted as part of The White Company sale.

I am always being asked by my friends why my home always smells so utterly delicious. And the secret lies in a decade-long obsession with fragrance layering. After testing hundreds of different waxes over the years, I've found that the Coast candle is easily one of the best scented candles out there.

I’ve been using this scent to welcome spring for years, but I always wait for the sale to stock up on the Coast Large Candle. While the smaller sizes are lovely, there is something about the scale of the three-wick version that feels like a true investment for the home. With the discounts now live, this is the particular star of the show I am buying in bulk:

The White Company Coast Large Candle

I always keep this candle in pride of place on my coffee table, and my little ritual is to light it about 20 minutes before guests arrive so the scent fills the living room and hallway. It’s the perfect winter-to-spring transition scent, with a blend of sea salt, fig and cashmere. Plus, it would make a lovely gift packaged in the brand’s signature white box - and who doesn’t love receiving a luxury candle?

RRP£65
Shop now

Because the design is so beautifully minimalist, it sits perfectly within any interior design style. This oversized three-wick vessel is a great size too; it pulls a look together, whether you are using it for coffee table styling ideas or just sitting it on a mantelpiece, it makes a statement.

For me, a candle looks best when it’s just part of the furniture – I usually nestle mine next to a stack of books or on top of a decorative tray. It’s an effortless way to begin scentscaping your home, using different fragrances to anchor the mood of each room.

This approach os using scent to define a space is a sentiment shared by our houses editor, Charlotte Dunford, who notes: ‘Creating an inviting interior counts on much more than just visual appeal; engaging the sense of smell is equally as important. My own home is dotted with candles which lend each space its own unique character: a fresh scent for the kitchen, something energetic to inspire in my office, and calming notes to accompany evening soaks in the bath.’

If you are looking for a few other fragrances to scent different zones in your home, I’ve found a few other options in The White Company sale that you should try:

Capri Signature Candle

RRP£25
Available from The White Company
Shop now

Cassis Diffuser

RRP£35
Available from The White Company
Shop now

Fresh Grapefruit Botanical Candle

RRP£35
Available from The White Company
Shop now

Peony Home Spray

RRP£20
Available from The White Company
Shop now

Winter Clear Ribbed Large Candle

RRP£70
Available from The White Company
Shop now

Coast Fragrance Oil

RRP£15
Available from The White Company
Shop now

Adding a beautiful scent to your home is a simple, everyday luxury that truly elevates the atmosphere of your space. Sales are the best time to stock up on your signature home fragrance – for me, that means ensuring I have a backup of the Coast candle ready to go.

To make your investments last, I always recommend letting the first burn reach the edge of the glass to prevent tunnelling. I also keep this candle care set from Dunelm nearby, which helps to keep my prized candles in tip-top condition and prevent soot from ruining my white walls.

item.title: 11 small hallway ideas that prove you don’t need square footage to make a statement
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: How to elevate a narrow entrance with slimline forms and clever light-reflecting finishes.
item.content:encoded:
First impressions really matter, and even the tiniest hallways can be a chance to show off your personal style. With a few clever small hallway ideas, you can turn that tight space into a warm and inviting introduction to your home.

Creating a functional yet beautiful entrance requires a delicate balance of light, proportion and smart storage. The best hallway ideas lean into the architectural quirks of the space – whether that’s utilising high ceilings to draw the eye upward or using a bold, oversized mirror to play an optical illusion.

Incorporating a few functional elements like these brass wall hooks from Dibor is an affordable way to lift coats and bags off the floor, instantly reclaiming precious space. Once you have cleared the path of daily clutter, you can begin to explore more transformative design strategies - from rhythmic patterns to clever paint effects - that turn a modest passageway into a grand entrance.

1. Hang a statement mirror

In a narrow corridor, a mirror is more than a vanity tool to check your hair before you leave the house for the day; it’s a spatial magician. To mimic the grandeur of a window, choose an arched, multi-panneled mirror that reaches toward the cornicing. This creates a focal point that breaks up the monotony of a long wall while reflecting light into the depths of the home.

M&S Scallop Ribbed Wooden Wall Mirror

RRP£149
Available from M&S
Shop now

Pooky Baloo Mirror

RRP£175
Available from Pooky
Shop now

Dusk Traditional Arched Mirror

RRP£100
Available from Dusk
Shop now

2. Layer vertical stripes to lengthen the space

A striped runner acts as a visual pathway, lengthening the space. By continuing this pattern from the floor up the stairs, you create a seamless flow that leads the eye upward, making a tight footprint feel far less restricted and much more purposeful.

3. Adopt the ledge console solution

Traditional console tables can sometimes be too deep for a narrow hallway. Instead, look for slimline floating shelves or bespoke narrow ledges as part of your hallway furniture ideas. These provide a surface for floral arrangements and small accessories without the visual weight of furniture legs, keeping the floor area clear and breathable.

Melody Maison Oxblood Red Scalloped Wall Shelf

RRP£34.95
Available from Amazon
Shop now

Garden Trading Sapperton Mirror with Shelf

RRP£190
Available from Garden Trading
Shop now

The Cotswold Company Chester Storage Shelf and Hooks

RRP£199
Available from The Cotswold Company
Shop now

4. Elevate walls with art

'When it comes to longer walls, breaking them up with artwork is key,' says Cotswolds-based interior designer Sean Symington. Whether it’s a rhythmic gallery or a single statement piece, art introduces a sense of narrative that prevents a transition space from feeling like an afterthought, ensuring a narrow corridor feels intentional and layered.

5. Anchor the space with checkerboard flooring

In a room with restricted space, the floor is your largest uninterrupted surface, making it the perfect tool for creating an illusion of width. Nothing defines the most effective hallway flooring ideas like the timeless appeal of a black-and-white checkerboard. When these tiles are laid on the diagonal, the diamond orientation draws the eye to the widest corners of the room rather than the narrow side walls, effectively pushing the boundaries of the space outward.

6. Refine walkways with slender consoles

In narrow corridors, there often isn’t room for much more than the essentials. If you find a radiator is already occupying the wall where you'd like a console, opting for a slim-profile console table or a shallow radiator cover allows you to reclaim that surface. These slender additions transform a functional necessity into a stylish ledge for keys and lighting, providing the utility of a hallway table without obstructing the walkway.

The White Company Cartmel Console Table

RRP£325
Available from The White Company
Shop now

OKA Talasa Console Table

RRP£695
Available from OKA
Shop now

Dusk Penny Ash Console Table

RRP£199
Available from Dusk
Shop now

7. Embrace bold jewel box colours

Don’t be afraid of vibrant hues in windowless hallways. A bold, sunny yellow or a grass green can turn a cramped passage into an intentional design choice. Use the door frame as a natural frame for the rooms beyond, creating a sophisticated colour story as you transition through the home.

8. Conceal clutter with under-stairs storage

The space beneath the staircase is often wasted. Transform it into a functional nook with a built-in cupboard. Painted in a uniform, cheery colour - to mimic the image above, try a warming yellow like Yellow Ground from Farrow & Ball - it disappears into the architecture, providing essential storage for bulky coats and umbrellas while maintaining a tidy appearance.

To ensure this hidden space doesn't become a chaotic 'catch-all', keep the interior organised with tiered shoe racks, pull-out drawers and dedicated areas for smaller accessories.

9. Elevate the ceiling with statement lighting

If your hallway is narrow but tall, use that height to your advantage. Hanging a large statement lantern or a textured woven pendant draws the eye up to the ceiling and away from the close-proximity walls. It makes the whole area feel bigger by reminding you that the space goes up, even if it doesn’t go wide, and a beautiful light is often the first thing that you see when you enter a home.

Pooky Regular Stella Pendant Light

RRP£185
Available from Pooky
Shop now

M&S Gemball Glass Ribbed Pendant Light

RRP£179
Available from M&S
Shop now

Cox & Cox Three Arm Ayla Pendant

RRP£295
Available from Cox & Cox
Shop now

10. Create depth with textured wallpaper

If paint feels too flat, a subtle, small-scale patterned wallpaper can add incredible depth to a space. Using paper with a slight texture or a delicate botanical print creates a soft focus effect. This blurs the sharp lines where the walls meet, making the boundaries of a narrow hallway feel less rigid, more inviting and adding heaps of character.

11. Paint the staircase for visual interest

If your hallway is too narrow for rugs, look to the stairs themselves. Painting the treads and risers in alternating colours - or adding a contrasting stripe edge - adds a bespoke, artisanal touch that requires zero extra space but offers maximum personality.

Transforming a cramped hallway is ultimately about making deliberate choices that prioritise both style and flow. By shifting the focus away from the floor towards the walls and ceiling, you can turn a functional thoroughfare into a sophisticated introduction to your home’s interior. Whether you choose to lean into bold colour or stay with classic neutrals, the key is to ensure every element - from the flooring to the light fixtures - is working to open up the perspective.

To complete the atmosphere, placing a tall, slender reed diffuser, like this Rituals reed diffuser from Amazon, on a windowsill or console is a lovely way to greet guests with a signature scent without cluttering your small space.

item.title: The only Mother’s Day cake recipe you need – from the baker behind a royal wedding
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: Celebrate spring with this elegant Mother’s Day cake recipe by royal baker, Fiona Cairns – the ultimate floral centrepiece for your afternoon tea.
item.content:encoded:
As the designer of the Prince and Princess of Wales's 2011 wedding cake, this Mother's Day cake recipe by Fiona Cairns brings a touch of royal elegance to your celebrations.

While many opt for Mother's Day gift ideas such as flowers and jewellery, there is something heartfelt about a home-baked cake. This lemon wreath cake is beautifully decorated with iced florals, a sophisticated upgrade to the classic sponge, ensuring your gift is as delicious as it is beautiful.

This recipe features a wonderfully moist sponge doused in lemon syrup for a signature crunch, topped with edible flower decorations. To ensure your presentation is as polished as the bake, try serving it with a silver cake server, like this Sophie Conran X Arthur Price one from Amazon – it comes presented in a lovely gift box and would make the perfect accompaniment to your Mother's Day surprise.

Overview

Serves: 6

Ingredients

(For the cake)

  • 175g unsalted butter, softened, plus more for the tin

  • 175g self-raising flour

  • A pinch of salt

  • 2 eggs, lightly beaten

  • 175g golden caster sugar

  • Zest, finely grated, and juice of 1 large unwaxed lemon

(For the topping)

  • Juice of 1 large lemon

  • 100g white granulated sugar

(To decorate)

  • 150g icing sugar, sifted

  • Purple food colour

  • 20-25 crystallised flowers (In spring, try pansies, primulas or primroses, and in summer, roses, daisies, lavender)

  • 50g bag white royal icing (optional)

Method

  1. Preheat the oven to 180°C/fan 170°C/350°F/gas mark 4.

  2. Lightly butter an 18cm diameter, 7.5cm deep, round springform tin and line the base and sides with baking parchment.

  3. Sift the flour and salt into a bowl and set aside. Melt the butter in a small pan and set aside to cool slightly. Using the whisk attachment of a stand mixer or a bowl and a hand mixer, beat the eggs and sugar together until very light and fluffy (this may take 5 minutes). Blend in the melted butter; then very gently fold in the flour and zest. Finally, slowly fold in the juice.

  4. Pour the batter into the tin and bake for 30-35 minutes or until the cake springs back to the touch, or a skewer inserted into the centre comes out clean.

  5. Meanwhile, make the crunchy topping by simply mixing the juice and sugar in a small bowl. Immediately the cake comes from the oven, prick tiny holes all over it with a fine skewer or cocktail stick. Pour the lemon syrup evenly all over the surface. Leave to cool completely in the tin.

  6. To decorate, place the cake upside down onto your serving plate or cake stand. Tip the icing sugar into a small bowl and add 1.5-2tbsp water and a tiny amount of purple food colour. The icing should be thick enough to coat the back of a spoon. Spoon it over the cake and allow it to drizzle down the sides.

  7. Arrange the flowers in a circle, using the royal icing to affix them, if you like, then place a few in the middle of the cake. It’s as simple as that.

Shop our favourite cake stands

If you’re looking for the perfect pedestal to display your creation, we’ve rounded up a few of our favourite cake stands that will bring an extra touch of elegance to your table.

Neptune Malborough Cake Stand

RRP£85
Available from Neptune
Shop now

Mervyn Gers Ceramics Hand-Glazed Ceramic Short Cake Stand

RRP£70
Available from ABASK
Shop now

Williams Sonoma Famille Rose Cake Stand

RRP£78
Available from Fortnum & Mason
Shop now

Recipe and image sourced from Fiona Cairns

Whether you're hosting a grand afternoon tea or a quiet moment with a cup of Earl Grey, this pretty springtime cake is a beautiful way to show your Mother figure you care.

While this cake makes an irresistible edible centrepiece, no table is truly complete without a splash of colour. To really pull your celebration together, pair your bake with a colourful spring arrangement like this bouquet from Bloom & Wild.

item.title: Green kitchen ideas – 23 ways to style sage, olive, emerald and more
item.dc:creator: Charlotte Dunford
item.description: Green, in all its hues, is a perfect choice for a welcoming, soothing and inspiring kitchen. Here we explore 18 of the best green kitchen ideas.
item.content:encoded:
Incorporating green kitchen ideas is such an effortless way to bring a splash of colour into the heart of the home while shunning the safe, overused neutrals of the past. Whether you are leaning into the softest whisper of safe or a deep, dramatic forest hue, it is the ultimate choice for a space that feels both welcoming and restorative.

When it comes to broader kitchen colour ideas, green is a bit of a chameleon – it works just as well as a neutral as it does a statement. ‘Green is in general a calming, relaxing colour,’ says Francesca Wezel, founder of eco-friendly paint brand Francesca’s Paints. ‘Being the colour that represents nature, it’s a colour that makes us feel good and positive.’ This natural link is exactly why it’s become such a go-to for creating a kitchen that feels like an indoor sanctuary.

The secret to a sophisticated look lies in the layering. If you aren’t ready for a full overhaul, start small with high-quality accents like this stoneware cafetière from Le Creuset, which adds an instant, functional pop of colour. From painted cabinets to tactile tiles, there are endless ways to introduce this vitalising hue – here are 23 of our favourite ideas to get you started.

1. Create a layered look with varying tones of green

Green kitchens don’t have to stick to just one shade. Layering a range of hues on different surfaces throughout the room creates an interesting and sophisticated scheme.

'We painted this kitchen in varying shades of soft green, with the island in the deepest hue to ground the design. The mixed palette adds visual interest and pairs nicely with the wooden dining table and stone floor,' explains Richard Moore, design director at award-winning luxury kitchen brand, Martin Moore. 'Opting for different shades of green prevents the look from becoming too blocky, and it helps differentiate the various zones within the kitchen.'

2. Make a kitchen feel cosy with rich, dark green cabinets

Dark green kitchen cabinets are a bold yet soothing design choice, creating a cosy and enveloping atmosphere. Any hue, from a fresh pine to dense forest green, will work beautifully. However, deep green is an intense and all-encompassing colour, so pair it with elements of white or cream in smaller kitchens to avoid your scheme feeling too dark and overwhelming.

For a softer look, introduce deeper shades on kitchen accessories, linens or a small splashback rather than cabinets.

3. Add an accent with green shelving

Marrying form and function, open shelving in a kitchen is a great way to create extra storage space while also providing an area to display a curated collection of serveware, crockery or recipe books. Against a light-coloured wall, dark green shelves are the perfect burst of colour. Choose a shade that matches your green kitchen cabinets for a well-balanced and aesthetically pleasing look.

4. Opt for an olive green kitchen

From sage to khaki to forest, there’s a whole host of shades to choose from when it comes to decorating with green. Rich and earthy, an olive green kitchen feels warm and inviting. 'Olive green can be a neutral, dropping into the background, or it can be the shining star of a colour scheme,' explains Annie Sloan, colour and paint expert and founder of the eponymous paint brand. 'It is a bit of a dark horse in the sense that you can easily forget how much interest and sophistication it brings to a space.'

For a traditional yet rustic look, combine olive green kitchen paint with timeless fabrics such as a ticking stripe.

5. Embrace colour drenching for an immersive feel

For a truly impactful look, consider taking your chosen shade beyond the cabinetry. In this deVOL kitchen, the mint-green walls extend across the ceiling to create a seamless enveloping canopy. By layering this against deep green cabinets and emerald glazed tiles, the room feels cohesive.

6. Introduce a pop of green with a contrasting kitchen island

For those cautious about covering walls or painting all their kitchen cabinets in a bright green shade, painting just the kitchen island is an effective alternative. Pick a contrasting shade to create drama and interest, such as a velvety moss green against an otherwise monochrome kitchen. Keep the style of your kitchen island and the hardware used the same as on the main kitchen cabinets for a cohesive look.

7. Lean into moody, traditional panelling

For an atmospheric look that feels steeped in history, consider vertical tongue-and-groove panelling in a deep moss or olive green. As shown in the beautiful space pictured above, the rich green walls create a dramatic, moody backdrop that allows warm wood tones and brass fittings to sing.

Taking colour across the panelling, the shelf and the adjacent walls creates a sense of age and permanence. It's a brilliant way to add texture and architectural interest.

8. Pick a fresh, bright green for an uplifting kitchen

While dark greens are enveloping and cosy, fresh spring greens feel invigorating and uplifting. 'The darker kitchens we loved so much are still very popular, but it’s the fresher, brighter colours that are creeping in,' says Helen Parker, creative director at deVOL. 'We are now seeing lots of new kitchens coming through with warmer and brighter colours. Soft mellow yellows, punchy and bright greens and soft, delicate pinks, all giving us a light and airy feeling, especially when the kitchens have lots of open shelves for displaying our greenery, flowers and special loved objects, things that brighten up our homes and give us a feeling of new beginnings.'

Pair zesty greens with whites and natural wood for a refreshing, airy atmosphere.

9. Bring the outside in with a nature-inspired palette

Green is inherently connected to the natural world, from leafy trees to spring shoots to fragrant herbs. Embrace this theme and choose a nature-inspired palette of greens and browns for a soothing kitchen that brings the beauty of the outdoors in. For a truly natural look, opt for organic materials such as wood and wicker for kitchen lighting, worktops and furniture.

10. Surprise with unexpected pops of green

Some of our favourite green kitchen ideas contain only the smallest hint of green, but well-placed it can create maximum impact. 'People often want to incorporate bolder colours into a kitchen without overwhelming the space,' says Moore. 'Cupboard interiors are the perfect way to do this – they allow you to add a pop of colour into a neutral scheme, whilst still retaining an overall cool palette.' Try a pop of mint green against white kitchen cupboards for a classic, elegant take on this design idea.

11. Choose bright green cabinets for a joyful scheme

One of the most popular green kitchen ideas is to introduce colour through kitchen cabinets. To make coloured cabinets the star of the show, choose a bold green paint, such as apple green, and combine it with neutral walls and natural materials. Bright and full of energy, this shade will add a playful touch to your scheme, bringing a smile to your face each time you walk in. Pictured here, Suzie de Rohan Willner, CEO at TOAST’s kitchen cabinets are painted in Farrow & Ball’s ‘Emerald Green’.

12. Create a country-style kitchen with pastel green

Country kitchens are a highly sought-after style, often feeling comfortable and characterful. With its connections to nature and the English countryside, green is ideal for a country kitchen. A soft green paired with other pastel shades and natural or white wood is the perfect choice for a refined yet rustic interior. To add pattern and interest, opt for a sage green kitchen wallpaper in a charming, ditsy print.

13. Add a dash of 'unexpected red'

To inject a sense of personality into your kitchen scheme, look to the 'unexpected red' theory. As seen in this Naked Kitchens scheme, a bold crimson island creates a sharp, playful contrast against deep olive cabinetry. Red and green sit opposite eachother on the colour wheel, making them natural partners that bring out the best in one another.

By introducing a pop of red through an island or a single piece of furniture, you break up the green scheme and add a curated, designer feel that keeps the room from looking too coordinated.

14. Brighten a kitchen with a botanical wallpaper

Wallpaper has a transformative effect on any room, whether used to create a statement accent wall or to cover all four walls. Botanical or tropical wallpapers in natural shades of green bring personality and drama.

If picking your wallpaper first, take colours from the design to use on kitchen cabinets or accessories and plan the rest of your scheme in-keeping with the nature-inspired theme. Make sure the wallpaper you choose is suitable for use in a kitchen; today, there are plenty of washable, waterproof options available.

15. Establish a fresh, playful feel with green and orange

For refreshing and fun green kitchen ideas, combine a lively green with a bold, bright shade. As complementary colours, green and orange make the perfect pair for a playful and optimistic colour scheme. A vibrant spring green on kitchen cabinets or walls acts as the ideal base for bright orange accents such as a cafe curtain, linens or mugs, like these Le Creuset mugs from Amazon in the iconic volcanic colourway.

16. Create a colourful splashback with green kitchen tiles

Even the smallest addition of green can bring life and energy to a room. Whether you choose a soft mint or a deep emerald green, a tiled backsplash in a classic kitchen introduces a layer of colour and pattern while retaining an elegant feel. Tiles are also a brilliant choice for this high-traffic area as they are both durable and effortless to clean.

17. Introduce colour and pattern with kitchen fabrics

Often overlooked, fabrics are a great addition to kitchens and can bring a softness to your interior as well as a burst of colour. For green kitchen ideas involving soft furnishings, try a kitchen blind to add pattern or a curtain sink to introduce texture. Green is strongly associated with the energy and beauty of the natural world, so lean into this when choosing your fabric. Botanical, floral or scenic designs will all sit beautifully in a green and white scheme.

18. Pair green with marble and brass for an elegant kitchen scheme

While green may be the star of the show in your kitchen, it’s important to consider which colours and materials to pair it with to create your desired look. For an elegant kitchen scheme, combine a soft sage green on the walls or cabinets with marble surfaces and brass details, such as lighting or hardware. If changing your countertops isn't on the cards, consider something like this large marble worktop board from The White Company to get a similar look.

19. Clash bold colours for a joyful kitchen scheme

'More dynamic spaces such as kitchens lend themselves well to bolder colour choices,' says Sloan. 'A colour clash will always put me in a good mood because it feels cheeky and daring and mischievous and heightens the qualities of each colour to fabulous effect!'

For a playful palette, pair rich greens with sunny yellows, warming pinks or bright blues.

20. Pepper a green kitchen with pink accents

An ever-popular colour palette for any room, green and pink has an enduring appeal. 'If you’re looking to introduce this combination into the heart of the home, we always recommend using green as the base colour and layering it with pops of pink for an eye-catching contrast,' explains Amanda Telford, marketing manager at tile brand CTD Tiles. 'You can do this through cabinetry, tables and doors, the result is a truly timeless look that will stay in style for years to come.'

21. Embrace dark shades with a forest green kitchen

Utilitarian spaces are perfect for darker colour palettes, and a forest green kitchen adds plenty of character. Keeping the forest green in the lower portion of the kitchen adds depth without feeling small and dingy.

22. Create a serene sanctuary with soft sage

For a kitchen that feels light and airy yet full of character, sage green is a timeless choice. Because it contains a hint of grey, it acts as a soft neutral that changes beautifully throughout the day.

To keep the look sophisticated, pair sage cabinetry with crisp white marble worktops and unlacquered brass hardware. It's an ideal shade for those who want to move away from grey or white without committing to a high-contrast palette.

23. Paint your window or door frames

If you want to bring the outdoors inside without saturating the walls or cabinets, look to your joinery. Painting window or door frames in a soft leaf-green - as seen in this deVOL kitchen - perfectly frames the outside, making the glazing feel like a living landscape. It is a subtle and unexpected way to introduce colour that feels light and architectural, especially when paired with neutral walls and dark, contrasting cabinetry.

A palette that evolves with the seasons, green kitchens feel just as invigorating on a bright spring morning as they do cosy and enveloping by candlelight inthe depths of winter. By embracing these botanical tones, you aren't just redecorating a room; you are creating a space with soul that feels connected to the landscape beyond the window.

If you are looking for a simple way to trial the trend, look to your textiles for a low-commitment refresh that adds instant charm. This white and green stripe tea towel from Daylesford is the perfect example, offering a country-inspired accent that looks beautiful draped over an oven handle.

item.title: 11 ways to make your bedroom feel like a luxury hotel
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: Turn your bedroom into a luxurious retreat with Carpe Diem Beds, where expert design and comfort bring the hotel experience to your home.
item.content:encoded:
Transform your bedroom into a five-star retreat with expert tips from Carpe Diem Beds, bringing the luxury and comfort of a hotel stay into your everyday life.

What lingers in your mind long after a holiday ends? Maybe it’s the breathtaking views, the exquisite cuisine, or the cultural landmarks you explored. But for many, the true indulgence of a getaway is the hotel bed, its sumptuously soft linens, perfectly fluffed pillows, and the feeling of sinking into pure comfort at the end of the day.

The best part is you don’t have to wait for your next getaway to enjoy that level of luxury. By incorporating the right elements, you can bring the hotel sleep experience straight to your home.

Carpe Diem Beds, renowned for its Scandinavian craftsmanship and innovative sleep technology, offers expert insights on achieving that hotel-inspired look and feel.

By focusing on premium materials, sophisticated design, and small yet impactful details, you can transform your bedroom into a space that exudes elegance and tranquillity. Here’s how:

1. Layer decorative pillows

A hotel bed always looks effortlessly stylish, and a key element is the careful layering of decorative pillows. Mix different sizes, textures, and fabrics to add dimension and a plush, inviting look. Opt for neutral or muted tones for a timeless feel, or introduce subtle patterns for a touch of personality.

2. Invest in high-quality bedding

Luxury hotels prioritise comfort, and it all starts with the bedding. Opt for high-thread-count sheets—ideally 300 or higher—for a smooth, ultra-soft feel. Carpe Diem Beds offers a range of premium bed linens, such as the Vindstilla and Havsbris collections, designed to enhance your sleep with breathability and unmatched comfort.

3. Add a headboard

A headboard serves a practical purpose when sitting and relaxing in bed, but it also adds elegance and frames the entire bed and sleeping area.

It is a perfect way to elevate the look and feel of the bedroom. You can go premium and majestic, or play it down with a simple, minimalist headboard.

4. Maintain crisp, fresh linens

One of the hallmarks of a five-star hotel bed is its impeccably fresh and crisp sheets. Achieve this at home by lightly misting your bedding with a mix of water and fabric softener, then smoothing out wrinkles by hand. Regularly laundering and properly storing your linens will also keep them feeling brand new.

5. Choose plush pillows and duvets

High-quality pillows and duvets make all the difference in achieving a hotel-standard sleep experience. Opt for breathable, luxurious materials like eco-labelled down or silk, which provide cloud-like softness while regulating temperature. Carpe Diem Beds’ premium pillow and duvet collection ensures you get the perfect balance of comfort and support for restorative sleep.

6. Incorporate colour thoughtfully

While hotels often use neutral tones to create a serene atmosphere, adding a splash of colour can make your bedroom feel both elegant and personalised. A statement headboard, accent pillows, or a luxurious throw in deep blues, soft greys, or warm earth tones can elevate the space while maintaining a sophisticated look.

7. Add a luxurious bedroom rug

A plush, high-quality rug can instantly elevate your space, adding warmth and comfort underfoot. Choose a neutral-toned or subtly patterned rug that complements your bedroom aesthetic, making every morning a little more indulgent.

8. Create atmosphere with lighting

Lighting is a subtle yet powerful way to recreate the ambience of a luxury hotel suite. Layer light sources by combining a statement ceiling fixture with bedside lamps and warm-toned wall sconces. Opt for dimmable bulbs to adjust the mood from bright and energising in the morning to soft and soothing at night.

9. Keep clutter to a minimum

Hotel bedrooms always feel serene, in part because they’re free of unnecessary clutter. Streamline your own space by incorporating discreet storage solutions such as upholstered benches, under-bed drawers, or built-in wardrobes. Keeping surfaces clear enhances both the sense of calm and the feeling of indulgence.

10. Personalise with luxury touches

The best hotel suites combine comfort with distinctive personality. Add your own luxurious touches at home, whether it’s a vase of fresh flowers, a tray with a scented candle, or a piece of artwork above the bed. These details create a welcoming space that feels like a retreat designed just for you.

11. Focus on sleep quality

Beyond design, the true hallmark of a five-star retreat is restorative sleep. Investing in the right mattress is essential. Carpe Diem Beds’ handcrafted designs, with their innovative spring systems and luxurious natural materials, are created to support the body and promote deep, rejuvenating rest. This ensures your bedroom isn’t just beautiful, but a place of genuine renewal.

By integrating these carefully curated design elements, you can bring the comfort, elegance, and opulence of a luxury hotel bedroom right into the heart of your own home.

Contact Carpe Diem Beds today to get design help with your bedroom and to make your home feel like your favourite hotel.

item.title: Scandinavian bedroom styling ideas: How to achieve comfort and function
item.dc:creator: The English Home
item.description: Transform your space with expert tips from Carpe Diem Beds, and bring the simplistic beauty of Scandinavian design into your home.
item.content:encoded:
Scandinavian design has long been admired for its effortless elegance, clean aesthetic, and focus on functionality.

Rooted in the Nordic countries’ deep appreciation for nature and light, this style embraces simplicity while maintaining warmth and comfort – ideal features of a bedroom scheme.

We spoke to Helen Jensen, VP of Brand at Carpe Diem Beds, about the key features of Scandinavian bedroom design and how to bring this minimalist style into your home.
