Helen shares her insights on what defines Scandinavian style, the importance of texture and colour, and expert tips for creating a cosy, functional bedroom.

What is Scandinavian style?

Scandinavian style is best known for its minimalism and functionality. According to Helen, “It’s very minimalistic. You will often see in Scandinavian homes a combination of simplicity but still functionality.”

This design ethos places importance on clean lines and open spaces. “We like airy spaces. We don’t clutter them with a lot of furniture,” she explains. It’s all about creating room to breathe, both visually and physically, in a home.

When it comes to colour, neutral tones dominate the Scandinavian aesthetic. “It’s often very neutral tones. You see that in houses in Scandinavia. I think that’s the common denominator of the Scandinavian style,” Helen notes. This palette of muted colours contributes to the calm and serene atmosphere that characterises the design.

Three elements that define a Scandinavian-inspired bedroom

Helen identifies three core elements that define a Scandinavian-inspired bedroom:

  1. The Continental Bed: In Scandinavia, beds often feature two mattresses stacked on top of each other, known as a continental bed. “If you compare it to beds in the UK, in Scandinavia, we like what we call a continental bed – beds where you have two mattresses on top of each other," she explains. This design provides added comfort and is typically upholstered, which is distinct from the traditional white mattresses common in the UK.

  2. Simple Headboard: Scandinavian style avoids over-the-top decorations. A simple headboard is key. Helen emphasises, “People like to style it with a simple headboard - again, still very simple - not a lot of heavy fabrics, too many buttons, and so on.”

  3. Neutral Colour Palette: The muted tones extend to the entire bedroom, not just the bed. “The bed style is typically a continental bed, adding a simple headboard, and then having everything in a neutral colour palette," says Helen. These soft, natural colours create a peaceful and inviting space.

The role of colour and texture in a cosy Scandinavian bedroom

Texture plays a crucial role in making a Scandinavian bedroom cosy. “Textiles are so important,” says Helen, explaining how the last few years have seen an explosion of new textures and fabrics. “Everybody’s talking about tactility and mixing and matching many different fabrics and materials. There are so many options right now.”

For a cosy bedroom, Helen recommends combining various textures, such as wooden surfaces, metals, and textiles like bouclé, linen, and wool. “Texture is really important, especially in the bedroom, where you really want to create a cosy feeling,” she says. This can include everything from plush fabrics to natural materials that invite relaxation.

Colour-wise, Scandinavian design sticks to muted, natural tones. “Nobody should have a crazy neon pink or neon yellow in the bedroom. Those aren't really soothing colours that help you unwind,” says Helen. Instead, you'll find shades of beige, grey, and other calming colours. "Especially in the bedroom, we stick to more natural tones," she adds.

Bringing Scandinavian bedroom style to an English home

Helen suggests that you can incorporate Scandinavian bedroom style into an English home with a few key elements. First, start with the right bed. A continental bed is a great place to begin, but for the ultimate Scandinavian look, Helen recommends a floating bed, like those from Carpe Diem’s Iconic Collection. “The fact that you don’t have heavy legs underneath the bed makes it feel really airy, minimalistic, and truly Scandinavian,” she says.

In addition to the bed, think about the colour palette and incorporating an upholstered bed, which allows for more texture. Helen’s advice for achieving the Scandinavian aesthetic is simple: “Think simple, functional, and cosy - that would be the three key elements to incorporating Scandinavian style into an English home.”

Scandinavian styling tips for a cosy and stylish bedroom

To create a cosy and stylish Scandinavian bedroom, Helen offers these expert tips:

  1. Choose Minimalist Furniture: Don’t overcrowd the space with too much furniture. “Seeing the style of the foundation is important, but it also goes beyond the bed. It’s about carefully choosing the right bedside tables and perhaps an ottoman or a bed bench,” she suggests. Keep the pieces minimal and functional.

  2. Focus on Quality Bedding: Scandinavian homes often feature duvets instead of blankets, and these duvets are typically thicker than what’s found in the UK, but still light and breathable. “We either go with goose down or mulberry silk to make it truly premium,” Helen explains. She recommends investing in high-quality bedding with a good thread count to enhance the cosy feeling.

  3. Mix and Match Textures: “What brings back the cosiness is the styling of the bed – the duvet, the pillows, the fabrics, the textures,” Helen explains. In Scandinavian design, the balance of textures and colours creates warmth and comfort, making a minimalist space feel inviting.

Bringing Nature Indoors for a Scandinavian Feel

A key part of Scandinavian design is the connection to nature. Incorporating natural elements into your bedroom enhances the aesthetic and contributes to a sense of calm and well-being. Consider adding plants, wooden accents, or natural fibre rugs to bring texture and warmth to the space.

Even small touches, such as a potted plant on a bedside table or a wooden bench at the end of the bed, can make a big difference. Maximise natural light by keeping window treatments minimal, highlighting textures and the neutral colour palette central to Scandinavian style.

Blending these organic elements with classic Scandinavian furniture and fabrics helps create a serene, inviting bedroom that feels harmonious and comfortable.

Ultimately, Scandinavian bedroom design is about simplicity, functionality, and comfort. By focusing on clean lines, neutral colours, and a balance of textures, you can create a serene, cosy space perfect for rest and relaxation.

Discover your perfect sleep experience at carpediembeds.co.uk or visit the London flagship store in Marylebone to explore the collection firsthand.

item.title: Farmhouse decor ideas – 21 ways to master the art of layered, country styling
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: From chunky timber to ditsy florals, here is how to layer in that lived-in, farmhouse charm for a house that feels as good as it looks.
item.content:encoded:
Whether you’re tucked away in a country cottage or bringing a slow living vibe to a city terrace, the latest farmhouse decor ideas are all about that homely feel. Simply put, farmhouse decor is a style that celebrates rural life, focusing on practical, sturdy furniture, natural materials and a cosy, lived-in atmosphere.

This shift towards comfort is a fresh change from more rigid interior design styles that can feel a bit too stiff. Today’s farmhouse look is much more relaxed; it’s about mixing heritage pieces with your own modern life. The best examples feel quietly layered with timber, linen, stone and soft colour palettes.

The beauty of this aesthetic is that it starts with the small tactile details that bridge the gap between utility and style. We’ve been eyeing this ceramic candle holder from Neptune; it has that lovely, slightly irregular finish that looks like it was plucked straight from a rural pantry. Once you’ve had fun with the smaller details, you can begin to layer in the broader architectural and decorative elements that truly bring this aesthetic to life.

1. Source a reclaimed timber refectory table

The dining room is the hardworking heart of the English home, and nothing anchors it quite like a substantial dining table. ‘Finding a beautiful table to place at the heart of the home creates a strong sense of timelessness and character,’ says Kelly Ross, studio lead at Somerset-based interior design studio Sims Hilditch. ‘The richness and natural grain of timber bring a sense of storytelling and authenticity.’

Award-winning interior designer Laura Stephens notes that older timber has a depth of patina that simply can’t be replicated: ‘It becomes part of the life of the house, ageing alongside the family who live there.’ To keep the look informal, try Laura’s tip of pairing it with a mix of chairs or a simple dining bench on one side.

2. Layer nature-inspired neutrals

Move away from stark, gallery white in favour of a palette that feels evolved and intentional. Layering chalky neutrals like stone, lichen and ochre creates a soft, luminous backdrop that shifts beautifully with the changing British light.

‘Introducing a neutral palette with colours, stones and fabrics will bring a subtle sense of outdoors,’ notes Ross. ‘These tones will harmonise effortlessly within the natural vernacular of a farmhouse.’

3. Install a bespoke boot room

A farmhouse isn’t just a look; it’s a lifestyle that often involves muddy dogs and Wellington boots. ‘We see the boot room as the using hero of a farmhouse home – practical, hardworking, but still beautifully considered,’ says Jojo Bradley, founder of the eponymous Cheshire-based interior design studio. ‘It’s about robust joinery, natural stone floors, and plenty of woven baskets.’

Hampshire-based interior designer Emma Sherlock highlights the importance of keeping the space orderly: ‘Well-designed cabinetry is key for storing miscellaneous items such as dog leads, coats, and treats. I often incorporate a bespoke bench into the cabinetry, providing useful storage for wellies underneath while also offering a comfortable seat for putting them on.’

Dusk Poppy Seated Storage Cabinet

RRP£129
Available from Dusk
Shop now

Cox & Cox Round Umbrella Stand & Boot Rack

RRP£95
Available from Cox & Cox
Shop now

The Cotswold Company 5 Coat Peg Shelf

RRP£95
Available from The Cotswold Company
Shop now

4. Frame the kitchen with a classic range

If you are searching for authentic country kitchen ideas, a classic range cooker like an Aga is the non-negotiable centre piece. Beyond its cooking ability, it anchors the room. ‘A farmhouse kitchen isn’t complete without the gentle warmth of an Aga cooker at its heart,’ says Bradley.

‘It creates a natural gathering point, somewhere to pull up a Windsor chair, share a pot of tea, and embrace that unhurried comfort that defines English country style.’

5. Mix heritage florals with modern stripes

The most successful farmhouse interiors feel evolved rather than themed. Sherlock explains that these patterns act as complementary opposites: ‘The structured, geometric nature of stripes provides a grounding force to the soft, whimsical feel of floral prints. The space isn’t instantly decoded, which gives it depth and intrigue.’

Ross adds that ‘stripes provide a clean, structured contrast that complements intricate, vintage patterns, preventing them from feeling out of place in today’s interiors.’

6. Opt for unlacquered brass hardware

In a world of mass-produced finishes, unlacquered brass is the ultimate choice for a home that feels gathered over time. ‘Unlacquered brass develops a natural patina over time, creating warm, earthy tones that complement wood, stone, and other rustic materials,’ Ross notes. ‘This ageing process gives the space character and a lived-in, authentic feel.’

7. Soften spaces with heavy-weight linens and patterned textiles

Texture is everything in a farmhouse. ‘Natural fabrics such as linen, wool and cotton add softness and warmth, particularly when layered across upholstery, curtains and table linens,’ says Stephens. ‘It’s these gentle layers, rather than bold decoration, that give cottage rooms their inviting, comfortable feel.’

Anboise Aylin Merino Throw

RRP£170
Available from Anboise
Shop now

The White Company Tibetan Sheepskin Cushion

RRP£70
Available from The White Company
Shop now

Camomile London Washed Linen Cotton Ticking Stripe Tablecloth

RRP£49
Available from The Roost
Shop now

8. Expose and soften architectural bones

Don’t hide the history of your home. ‘English country homes are often defined by quirky features such as worn flagstones, stone walls, and exposed beams,’ says Sherlock.

Bradley suggests celebrating these raw materials: ‘Exposed brickwork brings an honesty and quiet texture to farmhouse interiors that you simply can’t replicate with new materials.’

Sometimes, timber beams can feel quite dark and dominating. ‘Limewashing timber beams is a lovely way to soften them while still allowing their character to shine through,’ Stephens suggests. ‘The chalky finish gently lightens the structure of the room and helps everything feel more harmonious, particularly in smaller cottages where darker beams can otherwise feel quite heavy.’

9. Reinstate a traditional walk-in pantry

Modern kitchens often hide everything, but the farmhouse look celebrates the art of organisation. Reinstating a traditional walk-in pantry complete with open shelves is a joy for the home cook. It’s a space to display artisanal ingredients and stoneware jars, turning everyday essentials into a visual display of texture.

Leach Pottery Sourdough Jar

RRP£52
Available from TOAST
Shop now

Neptune Melbury Stoneware Storage Jar

RRP£29
Available from Neptune
Shop now

Nkuku Kali Tall Storage Bar

RRP£20
Available from Nkuku
Shop now

10. Curate a gallery of botanical art

‘One of my favourite ways to decorate cottage walls is with botanical artwork arranged in a simple grid,’ says Stephens. ‘It brings a lovely sense of order and calm while also echoing the natural landscape outside." For a personal touch, Laura suggests using pressed flowers from the surrounding countryside framed in simple antique brass or pale wood to keep the look cohesive.’

11. Integrate freestanding kitchen elements

Avoid the rigidity of a fully fitted kitchen suite. Stephens prefers a ‘looser approach,’ using freestanding pieces like a butcher’s block or a dresser to help the space feel as though it has developed naturally. ‘When paired with pretty patterned tiles, perhaps a Delft-style design, it creates a kitchen that feels comfortable, characterful and quietly layered,’ she notes.

12. Bridge the gap with indoor greenery

Bringing the outdoors in is less about a total greenhouse overhaul and more about simple, restorative touches. ‘More and more, we are recognising just how important our connection to nature is for our wellbeing,’ says Sherlock. She suggests that this can be as easy as ‘floral wallpapers, green paint colours, or simply fresh flowers on our kitchen table’ to nurture a sense of calm.

For an authentic farmhouse feel, skip the formal arrangements and opt for a few oversized branches clipped from the garden or a simple potted herb on the windowsill.

13. Create a calm, textural bedroom

The farmhouse bedroom should be a place of quiet calm. Create a peaceful sanctuary by using a classic iron bedframe or a sturdy wooden bed as your starting point. Layer this with natural textures - chunky wool rugs, linen sheets and cotton quilts - to create a space that feels stripped back but incredibly warm.

By warming visual clutter and focusing on these slow materials, you allow the quality of the craftsmanship to take centre stage.

14. Embrace the utility of a Belfast sink

A sunken Belfast sink is the ultimate symbol of farmhouse utility. Paired with high-end bridge mixer taps, it creates a blend of grit and glamour. These deep porcelain sinks are designed for heavy-duty tasks, from scrubbing garden veg to washing the dog, yet they possess a timeless aesthetic that anchors the room in tradition.

15. Lay reclaimed terracotta or flagstone flooring

Flooring provides the organic grounding that a farmhouse needs. Ross advocates for these materials because ‘laying reclaimed terracotta or flagstone adds both warmth and character to any room, creating a timeless and inviting atmosphere.’ This type of flooring is highly practical and perfectly suited to hardworking spaces.

16. Soften lighting with pleated shades

Lighting in a rural home should never be harsh or clinical. Soften the atmosphere by using pleated silk or linen shades on table lamps and walls. These shades cast a warm, diffused glow at eye level, creating pools of light that can make a room feel cosy and intimate.

Rowen & Wren Bonnie Cotton Lampshade

RRP£48
Available from Rowen & Wren
Shop now

The White Company Addison Cone Ceiling Shade

RRP£65
Available from The White Company
Shop now

Pooky Smaller Wobster Table Lamp

RRP£245
Available from Pooky
Shop now

17. Panel the walls with tongue-and-groove

Add a little architectural interest to the walls with tongue-and-groove panelling. When painted in saturated, ‘dead-flat’ colours - like a deep navy or forest green - it creates a cocooning. High-end feel. This is particularly effective in snugs, boot rooms and bathrooms where the vertical lines of the timber add tactile warmth and a sense of structure.

18. Introduce a curated dresser

A farmhouse kitchen is rarely complete without a dresser. Rather than a place for clutter, use it to display a curated mix of china and silver. It turns a functional piece of furniture into a visual story of your life and travels - from wandering around antique markets to special momentos from adventures abroad- making the room feel personal and layered.

19. Create a fireside snug

In every farmhouse, there should be a corner dedicated entirely to comfort. Create a fireside snug area using jewel-toned velvet accents, perhaps a deep emerald armchair or a rich anvy ottoman. This provides a sophisticated retreat that feels particularly indulgent when the fire is roaring in the winter.

20. Incorporate woven rush or sisal textures

For a durable and organic foundation, woven rush or sisal rugs are the gold standard. They add a wonderful, golden warmth to the floor - especially needed with chilly flagstone flooring - and are tough enough to withstand high traffic.

Don’t stop at the floor, bring this honey-toned texture upwards. Mixing in woven baskets for log storage and smaller seagrass trays for coffee table styling ties the organic theme together. Soften these ‘crunchy’ textures with plenty of ditsy-print cushions.

The White Company Round Seagrass Tray with Glass Base

RRP£70
Available from The White Company
Shop now

Neptune Orissa Woven Jute Floor Cushion

RRP£149
Available from Neptune
Shop now

Laura Ashley Natural Gold Natural Hand Woven Jute Rug

RRPfrom £80
Available from Next
Shop now

21. Embrace the charm of café curtains and sink skirts

Finally, maintain a bright, airy feel by introducing café curtains. While they work beautifully on windows to offer a touch of privacy, they are equally effective at breaking up heavy cabinetry.

Instead of standard doors, try hanging a gathered-pattern fabric - perhaps a small-scale floral or a classic ticking stripe - to hide pots and pans under a Belfast sink or across a larder shelf. It’s a simple, nostalgic touch that adds a layer of soft, movable texture and prevents the kitchen from feeling overly fitted.

The charm of a farmhouse interior is that it never tries too hard. It’s a look that doesn’t ask for perfection; instead, it welcomes the happy muddle of real life. By leaning into the original character of your home and layering in pieces that tell a story, you create a space that feels less like a passing trend and more like a forever home that gets better as time passes.

To instantly shift the mood and bring the essence of the English countryside indoors, we love this Vegetable Patch scented candle from Neptune, its bright, green notes of tomato leaf and basil evoke the earthy freshness of a country garden in summer.

item.title: Fancy a sneak peek inside the private rooms of Queen Elizabeth II? Here’s how to get your once-in-a-lifetime ticket for this summer’s limited tours
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton
item.description: To celebrate the centenary of her birth, this summer, visitors to Scotland’s official royal residence will have a rare and special chance to glimpse inside Queen Elizabeth II’s private rooms
item.content:encoded:
For the first time in 100 years since she was born, this summer, the public will be given the unique chance to glimpse inside the real private rooms of Queen Elizabeth II at her official Scottish residence in Edinburgh.

One of the key royal sights in Britain to visit in 2026 to celebrate her centenary year, the Palace of Holyroodhouse will be opening the doors of the rooms used by Queen Elizabeth II and Prince Philip during their visits to Scotland, for the public to peek inside at long last.

The news comes as part of a year of celebrations to mark this milestone, as Queen Elizabeth II was born 100 years ago this year. The major event will take place in London at Buckingham Palace's The King's Gallery, with the largest exhibition of her fashion ever staged, but these very special tours at the official royal residence in Edinburgh are Scotland's answer to celebrations.

The baroque palace that stands at the end of Edinburgh's famous Royal Mile has a fascinating history, and has been the site of many of the most dramatic events in Scottish history, including the notorious murder of David Rizzio, Mary, Queen of Scots' Italian secretary. The official Holyrood Palace guidebook is available on Amazon and delves deeper into the history of this important place, from Mary, Queen of Scots, to Bonnie Prince Charlie and Queen Elizabeth II herself.

Centenary tours at the Palace of Holyroodhouse - how to get tickets

The private apartments used by Queen Elizabeth II at Holyrood Palace will be open for the first time in 2026, and available only this year.

Running for 100 days from 21 May to 10 September 2026, the tours will see small groups take behind-the-scenes tours, led by expert guides, to discover the history of the spectacular rooms and see for themselves the real places that Queen Elizabeth II and Prince Philip lived during their visits.

Subscribers to the Royal Collection Trust emails will be given priority access to book tickets at an exclusive offer price from Monday 9 March, with tickets going on general sale on Thursday 12th March. Book your tickets via the Royal Collection Trust website here.

What can you see inside the rooms?

Dating back to the 17th century, the rooms have been used by successive monarchs and members of the Royal Family since the reign of Queen Victoria.

The lucky ticket holders will be able to admire incredible, historic objects from the Royal Collection as well as some pieces from the personal collections of Queen Elizabeth II and Prince Philip, many of which reflect their longstanding love and affection for Scotland.

With her mother a Scotswoman, it is no surprise Queen Elizabeth II loved Scotland, joining a long line of British monarchs who enjoyed spending time here very much.

The Palace of Holyrood in particular was the setting for many of the most significant occasions during her reign. While visiting Edinburgh and Scotland, the Queen and her husband would occupy this lovely suite of private rooms that sit on the palace's east side. They offer stunning views of the gardens and Holyrood Park and, though modestly decorated, offer a rare and very special glimpse into the personal, private 'lived-in' spaces of the royal family, where they would take a private moment between their busy schedules of official duties.

The tours will include the Royal Breakfast Room, hung with tapestries at the request of Queen Elizabeth's grandparents, King George V and Queen Mary, and the Queen's actual Dressing Room. The latter in particular offers visitors the chance to see how the Queen would have prepared for official engagements, and there will be three particular ensembles of her wardrobe on display, all with special significance and Scottish heritage, which visitors will discover.

The tour will finish in the Sitting Room, where the late Queen would work and review the papers and documents presented to her in the Government red dispatch boxes. This room would also be used for private audiences and resting between engagements - she would often watch horse racing here, one of her favourite hobbies.

Throughout the rooms, visitors will also be able to admire highlights from Prince Philip's private art collection. He was Patron of the Royal Scottish Academy of Art and a keen collector of Scottish contemporary art, with many works by 20th-century Scottish artists adorning the walls of the palace and reflecting his passion for Scotland's landscapes and wildlife.

The Curator at the Palace of Holyroodhouse, Emma Stead, said, 'Queen Elizabeth II’s well-known love for Scotland will be given fresh context through this unique and special access to the private apartments, where visitors will enjoy a new perspective into both the formal and more informal use of Edinburgh’s royal palace.’

Admiring these beautiful rooms will be a rare chance to understand the private lives of one of the most famous couples in the history of the royal family, married for over 73 years. This lovely book, Elizabeth and Philip: A Story of Young Love, Marriage and Monarchy, available on Amazon, dives deeper into the love story of the royal pair and their extraordinary relationship.

item.title: How to make a small bathroom look bigger, according to interior experts
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: Make even the smallest bathroom feel twice the size with these expert-approved tips and tricks.
item.content:encoded:
Not everyone is blessed with grand proportions, but a modest footprint doesn’t have to limit your style. In fact, some of the most impactful interiors are those that embrace a compact layout with confidence. By focusing on light and clever tricks, you can easily learn how to make a small bathroom look bigger while creating a space that feels curated rather than cluttered.

The most successful small bathroom ideas prioritise a sense of ‘visual flow’. In a period property, this often means ditching bulky freestanding units in favour of wall-hung vanities. Even the use of colour can trick the eye; when boundaries are blurred, the walls seem to recede, giving even a tiny ensuite some much-needed breathing room.

This sense of openness is often found in the smaller details. Keeping your surfaces clear is essential for a tranquil atmosphere. Opting for something like this wall-mounted soap dispenser from Amazon saves precious space around a basin. With these tactile touches in place, we turn to our panel of interior designers to share their definitive rules of maximising every square inch of bathroom space.

1. Commit to a cohesive palette

When working with limited square footage, the most effective way to create a sense of calm is to reduce visual noise and stick to a unified scheme. ‘Stick to a limited palette of materials and a cohesive colour palette,’ advises Cotswolds-based interior designer Sean Symington.

‘The key is not breaking the room up too many times. For example, a deep navy floor tile paired with a similar tone on panelling with a complementary wall tile in the shower creates something that feels considered and cohesive rather than busy. Every time you introduce a new material or colour, you fragment the space.’

2. Install wall-to-wall mirrors

Using glass on a grand scale is perhaps the fastest way to trick the eye into seeing double the actual floor area. ‘Full-height, wall-to-wall mirrors would be top of my list,’ says award-winning interior designer Laura Stephens.

‘They instantly amplify scale and light and create that slightly magical feeling of the room extending beyond its footprint. I’m a great believer in using reflection deliberately rather than timidly. In a small bathroom, wall-to-wall mirrors, ideally full height, can be transformative. They completely dissolve the boundaries of the room and double both light and colour, which is especially effective if you’ve been brave with your scheme.’

3. Lean into character and personality

If you cannot change the physical dimensions of a room, you can certainly change how the eye perceives its boundaries by introducing rich detail. Rather than trying to make a small bathroom disappear, Melissa Hutley, co-founder of London and Surrey-based interior design studio Hutley & Humm, prefers to distract the eye with curated interest.

‘Rather than trying to make a small bathroom disappear, we prefer to lean into its character. Giving the space personality immediately distracts from its size,’ she says. ‘A small wall filled with a carefully curated collection; whether that’s plates, framed photographs in a consistent format, or a mix of paintings and prints, which creates visual interest and draws the eye around the room. Wallpaper can work beautifully too, provided the room is well ventilated and not prone to excessive steam. Pattern adds depth and softness, which prevents a compact bathroom from feeling stark.’

Pooky Tina Wall Fitting

RRP£169
Available from Pooky
Shop now

Hastshilp Cara Brass Toilet Roll Holder

RRP£98
Available from The Roost
Shop now

Villa Bologna Valentina Bath Soap Dish

RRP£64
Available from Villa Bologna
Shop now

4. Play with directional tiling

Using vertical lines is a brilliant way to lift a low ceiling, making a compact room feel instantly loftier. Sophie Pringle, founder of Surrey-based interior architecture and design studio Pringle & Pringle, advocates for this kind of visual continuity to remove the breaks that often make a small space feel boxy.

'Keeping finishes simple and cohesive can make a small bathroom feel far more spacious,' she notes. While she often suggests tiling all walls in the same material to create a seamless look, using vertical stripes or orientation can also trick the eye into seeing more loftiness.

'Tiling all the walls in the same material helps blur boundaries and creates a seamless look, especially if the floor tiles continue into a walk-in shower with a single glass screen instead of a bulky enclosure.'

5. Layer your reflective surfaces

By combining mirrors with strategic lighting, you create a sense of depth that makes the wall feel as though it is receding. ‘Mirror panels can be transformative in a small bathroom. Covering even a modest section of a wall with mirrored panels expands the sense of space and amplifies the light,’ says Hutley.

‘For maximum impact, we like to mount decorative mirrors and wall lights on top of the mirrored surface as it creates layering and adds elegance rather than feeling purely functional.’

‘I also love mounting wall lights directly onto mirror panels. The reflection of the light creates a wonderful glow and adds a sense of depth and atmosphere in the evenings,’ Stephens adds.

Cox & Cox Antique Brass French Folding Mirror

RRP£150
Available from Cox & Cox
Shop now

Dusk Niamh Wavy Wall Mirror

RRP£45
Available from Dusk
Shop now

M&S Monroe Wall Light

RRP£49.50
Available from M&S
Shop now

6. Embrace bold, jewel tones

There is a common misconception that dark colours shrink a room, but in reality, they can provide a sophisticated sense of envelopment. ‘I wouldn’t say you need to avoid particular colours purely because a bathroom is small. In fact, I often think the opposite, small spaces are brilliant places to be bold,’ says Stephens.

‘Once you’ve maximised reflection and lighting, you can absolutely lean into rich, jewel-like colours like deep teal, claret, sapphire and even warm terracotta. Strong colour gives a room confidence and intention. A beautifully saturated scheme can actually blur the edges of a space and make it feel cocooning rather than cramped.’

Christy Prism Turkish Cotton Hand Towel

RRP£14
Available from Christy
Shop now

Liberty Ianthe Bath Mat

RRP£45
Available from Liberty
Shop now

Decor Walther Cut Crystal Soap Dish

RRP£120
Available from ABASK
Shop now

7. Use shine to your advantage

The finish of your materials is just as important as the colour, as high-sheen surfaces act as secondary mirrors to bounce light. Stephens highlights that even when you aren’t using a mirror, the texture of your tiles and glass surfaces matters immensely.

‘Glazed tiles are another quite hero,’ she notes. ‘Their subtle sheen bounces light around beautifully, particularly when paired with decorative shades or warm metallic fittings. Even a modest amount of gloss can make a compact room feel much more expansive.’ This effect, paired with layers of light, ensures the room feels bright and surprisingly generous.

8. Opt for bespoke architectural storage

Standard furniture can often protrude into the walkway of a small bathroom, making it feel narrower than it actually is. While ready-made, slimline mirrored cabinets are a useful off-the-shelf option, Pringle suggests looking into the walls themselves for a bathroom storage solution that sits flush against the architecture.

‘Mirrors are a great opportunity to add both reflection and storage,’ she explains. ‘Rather than relying on a standard medicine cabinet, we often ask builders to create a recessed niche in the wall sized to fit a mirror we love, then hinge the mirror to form a bespoke cabinet. If storage isn’t required, choosing the largest mirror possible can help visually expand the room.’

Ultimately, transforming a modest bathroom is about shifting the focus from what the room lacks to the quality of what it holds. By balancing these expert design ideas, you can turn a functional necessity intoa sophistiacted retreat.

To ground your new scheme with a final flourish of luxury, consider the sensory experience alongside the visual. Elevating a daily ritual with a high-end product like this geranium hand and body wash from Bamford brings five-star spa-like elegance to even the smallest vanity.

item.title: The hidden essential – Why the most elegant kitchens are filtering from within
item.dc:creator: Sally Coffey
item.description: This spring, refresh your home and health, one sip at a time, with the Waterdrop X12 Alkaline Mineral Reverse Osmosis System.
item.content:encoded:
As the soft April sunlight filters through the window, illuminating a meticulously arranged Easter table, the essence of an English spring truly comes to life.

For you and your home, this season represents more than just a change in paint palettes; it is a deep-seated evolution of lifestyle quality. In this season of vitality, we invite you to introduce a gift of technology and nature to the 'heart of the home' - your kitchen.

Aesthetic Integration: The Invisible Artisan

The Waterdrop Filter X12 Alkaline Mineral Reverse Osmosis (RO) System's slim, under-counter tankless design fits seamlessly in any kitchen, while its fewer exposed connections limit the possibility of secondary bacterial growth in the system.

Its design also perfectly aligns with the modern English pursuit of space and light. It tucks sophisticated filtration technology beneath the surface, ensuring the visual harmony of the cabinetry remains undisturbed, allowing the focus to return to pure tranquillity.

Unlike traditional, bulky under-sink systems, the X12’s compact footprint reclaims valuable storage space, ensuring your cabinetry remains a place of organisation and calm. Perhaps most impressive is its simplicity; designed for the modern homeowner’s schedule, the system is remarkably easy to install.

With intuitive instructions, most can complete the setup in just 30 minutes, bringing pure, mineral-rich water to your tap without the need for a complex renovation.

By reducing exposed connections, the X12 also eliminates the risk of secondary bacterial growth, allowing the visual harmony of your kitchen to be matched only by the purity of its performance.

The Taste of Provenance: Retaining Natural Minerals

As the soul of water lies in its natural minerals, the X12 retains beneficial essential elements such as calcium and magnesium while efficiently removing impurities. Whether brewing a delicate pot of Earl Grey tea or preparing the foundation for a spring feast, the crisp, sweet taste of mineral-rich water awakens the authentic flavours of ingredients, supporting a healthier drinking experience that feels as vital as the season itself.

This commitment to health is backed by rigorous science. The Waterdrop Filter X12 is widely regarded as the most effective method for purifying drinking water. It acts as a sophisticated shield for the home, drastically reducing dangerous contaminants and modern pollutants – including at least 98% of PFOA and PFOS (‘forever chemicals’), lead, bacteria, and organic compounds – ensuring that every drop is as safe as it is refreshing.

Sustainable Elegance: A Greener Future

The Waterdrop Filter X12 is more than a commitment to family health; it is a dedicated step toward a sustainable lifestyle. From the first glass of water in the morning to the pure preparation of ingredients for children’s meals, the system’s 1200 GPD rapid flow ensures fresh, mineral-rich water is always at hand. Featuring an industry-leading 3:1 pure-to-drain ratio, it maximises water efficiency while significantly reducing environmental impact.

The Waterdrop Filter X12 aims to bring the principles of sustainable living into every home. By reducing reliance on bottled water, it offers a solution that is not only safer and more convenient, but also more environmentally friendly – helping families make a positive choice for both their health and the planet. This is the promise of a purer, more eco-conscious future, delivered one glass at a time.

Find out more at waterdropfilter.co.uk

item.title: 7 playful outdoor easter decorating ideas to transform your garden into a seasonal sanctuary
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: From rustic textures to heritage prints, elevate your outdoor Easter celebrations with our curated guide to effortless, garden-ready styling.
item.content:encoded:
The arrival of Easter marks a subtle but significant shift in the British landscape, as our gardens begin to bloom and soften with new growth. Embracing outdoor Easter decorating ideas is a wonderful opportunity to extend the warmth of the home beyond its four walls, turning terraces and gardens into a welcoming spot for a long-overdue catch-up in the sunshine.

Whether you’re planning a big family lunch or just want to add a bit of cheer to your garden gate, Easter decorating ideas always take their cue from nature. Think about mixing soft, earthy tones with rustic textures and fun pops of pastel. By layering simple details with a few festive nods, you can create a look that feels effortless, stylish and perfectly at home.

To get the look right, start with one or two pieces that have a handmade feel. This printed paper Easter egg garland from Papersmith is a lovely place to begin – it’s understated and adds a touch of craft to your garden that you’ll want to bring out year after year (just don’t leave it out in the rain!) Read on for our favourite ways to decorate your outdoor space for the spring festivities.

1. Adorn your entrance with a wreath

Your front door is the first thing guests see, so make it a focal point. Swap your winter greenery for a light and airy wreath made of pussy willow, eucalyptus and tiny speckled eggs.

To give it a high-end finish, tie it off with a long, trailing silk ribbon in a soft sage of primrose yellow – it’s a simple way to add a bit of movement and luxury to your doorway.

Interflora Vibrant Spring Wreath

RRP£75
Available from Interflora
Shop now

Lights4fun 50cm Pre Lit Easter Egg Wreath

RRP£34.99
Available from Lights4fun
Shop now

Wildflower & Berry Handmade Dried Wheat Wreath

RRP£60
Available from Not On The High Street
Shop now

2. Layer your tablescape

If the weather is kind enough for an outdoor lunch, make the table feel like an event. ‘There’s something about that first spring lunch in the garden. If you’re hosting al fresco for Easter, this is your moment to get your table ready for the season,’ explains Sue Jones, co-founder and creative director at luxury interiors brand OKA. ‘I like to pair patterned plates and playful serveware with linens.’

To keep it looking elevated, Claire Horn, interior designer at eco-friendly lifestyle brand Nkuku, suggests a thoughtful touch: ‘The Easter table should feel layered and intentional rather than cluttered. Use seasonal decorations, such as decorative eggs or bunnies, as place settings by resting one on each napkin with a sprig of greenery.’

Liberty Mitsi Wiltshire Blossom Double-Sided Scallop Placemat

RRP£45
Available from Liberty
Shop now

Fortnum & Mason Gingham Eau de Nil Napkins

RRP£22.95
Available from Fortnum & Mason
Shop now

Meri Meri x Liberty Paper Honeycomb Bunnies

RRP£42
Available from Mori
Shop now

3. Soften your seating

Even on a sunny day, the spring air can be a bit nippy. To make sure no one wants to leave the table, pile on the textures. ‘Whether you’re hosting guests around a table or opting for a more laidback gathering on loungers, don't forget to add a few shower-resistant cushions,’ says Jones. ‘They transform any outdoor seating area into a welcome retreat and make it look effortlessly inviting.’

OKA Pushpa Outdoor Cushion

RRP£75
Available from OKA
Shop now

Dibor Green Gingham Bench Cushion

RRP£68
Available from Not On The High Street
Shop now

Neptune Wide Stripe Outdoor Bolster Cushion

RRP£85
Available from Neptune
Shop now

4. Utilise your greenery

Don’t limit the decor to just the table; let it spill over into the rest of your garden. ‘If you have trees or shrubs in your garden, consider using them as decorating opportunities,’ says Horn. ‘Hanging egg decorations can instantly create a magical, storybook feel. I recommend choosing a cohesive palette or opting for natural materials like mango wood, which look refined and blend seamlessly with the natural surroundings.’

5. Elevate your display

Using height is a great trick to make a display feel more professional and less flat. Try using cake stands to throw off more than just the dessert. As Horn notes, ‘Cake stands are excellent for adding height to a tablescape. They make a great centre piece when filled with seasonal treats or even a nest of moss and eggs.’ It’s an easy way to create a focal point on an outdoor sideboard or coffee table.

6. Sculpt with spring bulbs

Group a few terracotta pots or wicker baskets of different sizes together on your patio or garden steps. Fill them with spring classics like white daffodils, hyacinths and delicate snake’s head fritillaries.

For a polished, ‘designer’ finish, cover the soil with a thick layer of green moss; it looks much cleaner than bare dirt and gives the pots a lush, woodland feel.

7. Illuminate the twilight

As the afternoon turns into evening, lighting is what keeps the atmosphere alive. Instead of big overhead lights, go for a cluster of hurricane lamps and lanterns at different heights. ‘Placing lanterns around the garden along pathways or clustered beside the table adds warmth and intimacy,’ says Horn. ‘The soft glow against spring greenery instantly elevates the atmosphere and gives the entire space a sense of occasion.’

The White Company Naunton Lantern

RRP£65
Available from The White Company
Shop now

Lights4fun Glass Bunny Dome with Bunny Micro Lights

RRP£29.99
Available from Lights4fun
Shop now

Neptune Rosewood 5 Metre Festoon Lights

RRP£79
Available from Neptune
Shop now

You might find yourself basking in the warmth of a rare April afternoon or simply admiring the view from the comfort of your breakfast table; either way, these small additions ensure your garden feels like a true extension of the home. By focusing on natural materials and a few thoughtfully chosen accents, you can create a space that feels both festive and timeless.

To finish the look with a final flourish of seasonal charm, consider adding this grass-effect bunny ornament from Amazon, a playful yet charming addition that looks wonderful nestled among spring bulbs or standing guard by the garden gate.

item.title: The hunt is over – these are the best luxury Easter eggs of the year
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst

item.content:encoded:

Whether you're tucking a beautifully wrapped shell into a hamper for a loved one or treating yourself to a bit of indulgence, these finds are also the secret to some of the most stylish Easter table decorating ideas. There's something in using edible decor; imagine a sculptural egg as a centre piece or tiny pralines as a favour at each place setting.
