item.title: Seven coffee table styling ideas the experts swear by for an instant living room upgrade
item.dc:creator: Charlotte Dunford
item.description: One way to refresh your living room is through coffee table decor. We’ve consulted industry experts for their coffee table styling ideas.
item.content:encoded:
Your coffee table is the heart of your sitting room seating arrangement, and incorporating some clever coffee table styling ideas is the fastest way to make your space feel finished rather than just furnished.

When you’re hunting for fresh living room ideas, it’s easy to get bogged down in the big-ticket items like sofas and rugs, but the coffee table is where you can add those smaller, personal touches. Think of it as a small-scale stage for your favourite books and those luxury scented candles that you’ve been saving.

If you’re starting with a blank canvas, we recommend a versatile foundation like this wooden coffee table from The Cotswold Company, which offers a classic look at a reasonable price. Once you have your base sorted, you can begin layering items to create a space that feels uniquely yours. And to get you started, we’ve asked a few industry experts to share their top coffee table styling tips.

Expert advice on coffee table decor and styling

Keep displays considered and curated

Suzy Humphreys, founder of family-run, luxury British homeware brand, Layered Lounge, says: 'When styling a coffee table, it’s essential to remember that it is a practical piece of furniture and overcrowding can make it very difficult for guests to use, and the last thing you want is someone putting down their glass of red wine on one of your precious books. I like to group a couple of objects, such as a vase, tray, candle and book in the centre or on the side of the table that is least likely to be used by guests. It gives your table a curated look without being overly fussy.'

Use your coffee table as an extra surface for entertaining

Sarah Peake, founder and creative director of boutique interior design studio, Studio Peake, says: 'Coffee tables can offer so much to a space. You can change it up on a daily basis, and it will subtly change the feel of a room – it’s very therapeutic. I like to place a tray in a contrasting material or colour on top – this is a perfect spot for a ceramic bowl full of nibbles or can act as a wipeable surface to rest a drink.'

The White Company Small Porto Heart Dishes

RRP£30
Available from The White Company
Shop now

OKA Bennet Leather Solitaire Set

RRP£95
Available from OKA
Shop now

Rebecca Udall Iris Ceramic Carafe & Pair of Tumblers

RRP£80
Available from Rebecca Udall
Shop now

Choose colours and patterns that complement the rest of the room

Caroline Downing Nadel and Rosie Axford, founders of luxury home furnishings brand Wicklewood, say: “The key to interior joy is balancing aesthetics with function, especially when it comes to a high-traffic item like a coffee table. It’s a great place to embrace colour and pattern, choose hues and motifs which punctuate the rest of the space. And we love a ‘wow’ piece on a coffee table – after all, it’s usually at the centre of the room – like a unique basket or hand-painted ceramic vase collected on our travels.”

Plan your coffee table decor in groups of three

Irene Gunter, founder of London-based interior design studio, Gunter & Co, says: 'The number of items on your coffee table should be determined by its size. If you’re unsure (and it’s relatively small), an easy solution is to use the power of three: choose one taller item, such as a plant, a slightly smaller object, and then add a small pile of books. In addition to varying the height of objects, don’t be afraid to mix and match different textures, too. The contrast between materials creates visual interest.'

Choose coffee table decor that adds character and personality

Georgie Wykeham, interior designer and founder of London-based Georgie Wykham Designs, says: 'I always think that more is more when it comes to styling a coffee table! I load them up with beautiful, colourful books, a tray with a lovely, large candle, ornaments and a vase of flowers. Add things that you love, and that show a bit of personality.'

Paddywax A Dopo Misted Lime Scented Candle

RRP£25
Available from Selfridges
Shop now

OKA Decorative Pomegranate

RRP£35
Available from OKA
Shop now

Addison Ross Navy Blue Small Round Scallop Tray

RRP£80
Available from Addison Ross
Shop now

Embrace a variety of textures

Amy Dalrymple, creative director of global interior design studio, Dalrymple Studio, says: 'For coffee tables in smaller rooms where the sofa is quite close to the coffee table, a soft upholstery ottoman is perfect, often styled with a tray for drinks or with beautiful coffee table books. We also love to bring wood textures into a space through a coffee table, creating a focal point for the room. If working with a wooden coffee table, it’s nice to break up the finish by using colourful accessories – think about coffee table books with colourful spines or interesting ceramics that add a different texture.'

Add colour and interest with seasonal bouquets

Andrea Waters, head of brand at renowned British pottery brands, Portmeirion & Spode, says: 'No matter what size you have, incorporating your own sense of style to your coffee table often adds that finishing touch to the room, and can also really help to elevate your living space. Vases of flowers and foliage bring a burst of colour to a room and can complement your interior style in less decorated spaces. Choosing a statement vase will certainly draw attention, as well as add a stunning centrepiece to your home. Pair this with a small bouquet of flowers or a simple greenery display, and you are guaranteed to pull focus to the table in a simple and understated way.'

Ferm Living Shell Organic-Shape Stoneware Vase

RRP£75
Available from Selfridges
Shop now

Doing Goods Miracle Fish Vase

RRP£175
Available from Liberty
Shop now

Oliver Bonas Lola Green Ceramic Tall Vase

RRP£22.50
Available from Oliver Bonas
Shop now

Opt for curved coffee table decor to soften your scheme

Simon Temprell, interior design manager at premium British lifestyle brand, Neptune, says: 'Your coffee table is a focal point in your room, so it must reflect the style of your décor. Think of it as a little altar with some of your favourite things set upon it. Decorative bowls can be a great way to add curves to your coffee table and can sit comfortably on top of a stack of books or magazines and trays.'

Styling a coffee table isn't just about mimicking a showroom or Pinterest board; it's about creating a space that feels genuinely curated. By grouping together your favourite objects and experimenting with different textures, you can keep your living room feeling fresh without needing a total overhaul.

If you're looking for that final hero piece to anchor your new arrangement, a classic coffee table book is a styling essential (though this should usually reflect your own personal interests). We love this book: A New English Style: Timeless Interiors from Amazon, by one of our favourite interior design studios, Salvesen Graham.

item.title: I’m banning grey - These are the pieces I’m raiding from the M&S homeware sale for a ‘soft English’ bedroom makeover
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: Ditch clinical grey for a more restorative aesthetic with these characterful pieces designed to bring the warmth and texture of the English countryside indoors.
item.content:encoded:
While I usually feel the pull of the famous cookies in the in-store bakery, it’s the home section in M&S that has really caught my attention lately, with lots of design-look items on offer. My trip to my local branch last weekend confirmed that the M&S homeware sale is in full swing – now is the perfect time to snap these elevated pieces for a fraction of the price.

Living on the edge of the Cotswolds, I've realised my clinical grey bedroom feels totally at odds with the landscape outside my window. To truly mimic those surroundings, I’m ditching cold, ‘millennial’ tones for the soft English look – a nature-led aesthetic that leans into a palette of limestone, raw plaster, and weathered wood. This is the era of the ‘new neutrals’; we’re trading characterless grey and all-over beige for more earthy tones like oatmeal, clay pink and moss greens, and my recent scrolls are full of bed styling ideas centred on this palette.

I've curated my best of the best below, focusing on the bedroom pieces that I know, from my daily checks, are the true standouts for anyone looking to master this soft English look – and I’m using the M&S sale to kickstart my revamp. If you’re looking for living room ideas in this aesthetic, I’m also eyeing up this rattan floor lamp that would bring instant texture and warmth to a corner.

Watercolour Stripe Ruffle Edge Lamp Shade

RRP£19.50
Available from M&S
Shop now

Pure Cotton Floral Embroidered Bolster Cushion

RRP£35
Available from M&S
Shop now

Aida-Alouette Damask Sateen Bedding Set

RRPfrom £39.50
Available from M&S
Shop now

Josephine Pendant Light

RRP£45
Available from M&S
Shop now

Borg Fleece Throw

RRP£35
Available from M&S
Shop now

Wide Wood Round Wall Mirror

RRP£149
Available from M&S
Shop now

Flow Wooden Skirt Shade Table Lamp

RRP£79
Available from M&S
Shop now

Pure Cotton Floral Kantha Stitch Cushion

RRP£12.50
Available from M&S
Shop now

Brushed Cotton Twill Bedding Set

RRP£45
Available from M&S
Shop now

While my personal shopping bag is already quite full, I always like to check in with the rest of the team to see what pieces have that lasting editorial appeal. Katy Mclean, decorating editor at The English Home, has a particularly savvy approach to the seasonal shifts, often using the sale to prep for the year ahead. ‘The M&S sale is always full of temptations for me, and it offers a great chance to stock up on some spare bedding sets at a great price, and to lean into that coveted English style of decorating,’ she says. ‘

‘I always look for those landscape-led neutrals like limestone and plaster that feel so grounded. My top tip is to buy out of season. I'll bag the snuggly brushed cotton bedding in the spring sale for a fraction of the price, ready to create that cosy, nature-inspired sanctuary when winter returns.’

Artisan 1 Drawer Bedside Table

Katy Mclean's top sale pick: 'This bedside table offers the look of a far more expensive hand-crafted piece at an affordable sale price. I’m obviously an advocate of handmade pieces, but I recognise this is not always in budget, and this piece offers a luxury aesthetic that I admire. I love the bobbin legs, which give it plenty of character, whilst also being compact with room for my essentials – a glass of water, a favourite photo and a good lamp for reading, plus a spot to store my current pile of books and a useful drawer too!'

RRP£299
Shop now

The secret to achieving this soft English look in a bedroom lies in the layering of textures. This look isn’t about matching sets; it’s about the collected feel of an English country house that has evolved over decades. For 2026, we are moving away from the perfectly done and matching look in favour of spaces that feel like they have been curated over time.

When styling these bedroom finds, aim for contrast to build depth: pair the crisp, white of a cotton sheet with the rugged, organic texture of a rattan mirror to ground the room. Or sit a structured, embroidered bolster cushion against more relaxed, tumbled linens. By mixing these varied surfaces - rather than sticking to one flat finish - you mimic the natural irregularities of the landscape, turning a simple bedroom into a soulful, soft English sanctuary.

In my own bedroom, I’m using the sale to introduce smaller, characterful vignettes to liven up the room and lift the currently rather grey space. I currently have a spherical, clear glass shade, which has been a nightmare for collecting dust. I’ve just purchased this fabric pleated shade in a deep olive green. Because it’s fabric, it diffuses the light into a warm, golden glow rather than a harsh glare, giving the room a more refined feel that suits the Cotswold light.

Ultimately, a bedroom refresh is about so much more than just the aesthetic; it’s about creating that ‘cannot wait to crawl into bed’ feeling. As we move into the nighter months, these little treats from the M&S homeware sale are the perfect way to help our homes shed their heavy winter coats and transition as seamlessly as the seasons themselves.

Whether you are giving your bedside table a tidy with a ceramic dish like this one for a hint of natural texture or giving your bed a total linen makeover, the key is to lean into what truly makes you feel the most at home. Bag these discounted finds now while you can, they won’t be in stock for long – and you’ll save enough for an extra pack of Percy Pigs.

item.title: This is your rare chance to see the Royal family’s most precious heirloom at Buckingham Palace exhibition this spring, worn by 62 royal babies
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton
item.description: Visitors will have the chance to see a rare piece of royal history up close as the Royal Collection announces that Queen Elizabeth II’s christening gown will be part of her centenary exhibition at Buckingham Palace this spring
item.content:encoded:
How many people can say that the Christening gown they wore was nearly 200 years old, and was first worn by Queen Victoria's eldest daughter? Well, unsurprisingly, very few.

Queen Elizabeth II is one of 62 royal babies to have worn this particular christening gown, which will go on display as part of the special exhibition to celebrate the centenary of her birth at Buckingham Palace this spring, Queen Elizabeth II: Her Life in Style, which is the largest exhibition of her fashion ever staged.

It is a rare appearance for the gown, which dates back to 1841 when Princess Victoria was christened, and it is thought to be the first time it has ever been on display to the public, making this a rare chance to see such rare royal history up close.

The exhibition will also feature pieces like the late Queen's glittering Coronation gown, as well as some of the most memorable dresses and outfits she's ever worn, from ballgowns to tweed coats and plastic raincoats, tracing the journey of her remarkable legacy through her iconic clothes.

If, like me, you'll be first in line to see the exhibition, which opens on 10 April 2026 and will run until 18 October 2026, the companion book to the exhibition makes for some great pre-reading and allows you to really immerse yourself in her world. Queen Elizabeth II: Fashion & Style by Caroline de Guitat (who also curated the exhibition) is available on Amazon celebrates the late Queen as a fashion icon of the 20th and 21st centuries, and even includes a tribute by Dame Anna Wintour CH, DBE.

Queen Elizabeth II's christening gown

The christening gown itself has been carefully passed down through consecutive generations of royal babies since it was first worn by Princess Victoria in 1841, and was worn by Princess Elizabeth - the future Queen Elizabeth II - at her christening in May 1926, when she was just one month old.

Caroline de Guitat commented, ‘The christening robe was the most significant garment worn by Princess Elizabeth in her infancy and is an exceptional example of British craftsmanship – something Queen Elizabeth went on to champion throughout her life. As the earliest piece of clothing worn by the Queen, and by so many royal babies before and after her, it holds a special place in her wardrobe. We are delighted to be able to share it with as many people as possible in this centenary year.’

What is the christening gown made of?

Queen Elizabeth II's christening gown is made of cream Spitalfields silk from East London, and is overlaid with fine Honiton lace made in Devon. It was created by Janet Sutherland, Queen Victoria's Scottish dressmaker and ‘Embroiderer to the Queen’.

Of course, silk is naturally susceptible to degradation, and the robe became fragile over time. It was repaired in the 1960s. In 2004, Queen Elizabeth decided it had become too delicate and made the decision to commission a replica, which has been worn for every subsequent royal christening since.

To go on display as part of the exhibition, the robe has undergone 100 hours of careful conservation by the Royal Collection Trust's textile conservator, Cecilia Oliver, in advance of going on display, including meticulous repairs to holes and being gently handwashed section by section.

Alongside the robe, visitors to the exhibition will also be able to see a handwritten note by Queen Elizabeth II herself, which shows her recording some of the babies who have worn the christening robe. This was a tradition that started with her grandmother, Queen Mary, after the robe came into her care from Queen Victoria.

Cecilia Oliver commented, ‘The robe has been lovingly cared for over the generations – we even found a note on its box instructing that it be washed in natural spring water after use, which helps explain its remarkable condition despite such frequent use. However, after 185 years and 62 christenings, it was in need of conservation. It has been an honour and a privilege to work on such a special historic garment.’

The robe will be on display as part of the exhibition at the King's Gallery at Buckingham Palace, from 10 April 2026. Tickets are available from the Royal Collection Trust's website here.

So, who were all the other babies that wore the christening robe? Some will have been Queen Victoria's children who went on to marry into the royal families of half of Europe. The extraordinary lives of her children and grandchildren are exploredin greater detail in Children of the Empire by Michael Farah, available on Amazon. Written entirely in first person, this is a fascinating deep dive into the minds of 47 children and grandchildren and the dramas of their lives, from haemophilia, coups, Nazis and even execution. It certainly gives the beautiful christening gown a new meaning!

This article was first published on discoverbritain.com

item.title: WIN! A pair of VIP tickets to Toby’s Garden Festival in Devon, worth £200
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: The English Home is delighted to partner with Toby’s Garden Festival to offer one lucky reader a truly unforgettable day out. We are giving away a VIP Experience package for two for Saturday, 2nd May 2026.
item.content:encoded:
Returning to the magnificent Powderham Castle in Devon on Friday 1st and Saturday 2nd May, this year’s Toby Garden Festival centres around the theme of Discovery. Whether you are looking to unearth fresh planting ideas or gain expert advice, the festival promises to spark curiosity in gardeners of all levels.

Festival founder Toby Buckland will be joined by a host of familiar faces, including Arit Anderson (BBC Gardeners’ World) and Chris Hull (BBC Garden Rescue). Beyond the expert talks, visitors can explore over 200 exhibitors, more than 35 specialist nurseries, and the celebrated Artisan Barn. New for 2026, the Seed & Soil Lab offers bespoke planting advice, while the Mini Woofstock Dog Show ensures your four-legged companions are part of the festivities.

What's included in the prize?

  • Festival entry

  • Exclusive all-day access to Powderham Castle’s stunning 14th-century library and music room

  • Morning tea, coffee and biscuits

  • Canapés and a light buffet lunch

  • Afternoon tea

  • A glass of fizz

  • A VIP gift bag

To enter, simply fill in the form below. Competition ends 23:59pm GMT on Tuesday 31 March 2026:

View this article on our website to enter

item.title: The best robot vacuum cleaners, tried and tested for hands-free cleaning
item.dc:creator: Ellis Cochrane
item.description: We put the best robot vacuums through their paces to see if these automated cleaners can truly tackle everything from fine dust to stubborn pet hair.
item.content:encoded:
Investing in one of the best robot vacuums can significantly lighten your cleaning load, allowing you to return home or wake up to clean floors. Many now combine vacuuming with mopping, saving time and storage space. While dry-only models are cheaper, spending more on a multi-functional unit, especially one with HEPA filtration for dust and allergies, often makes better sense.

Depending on the layout of your home and the nature of the messes, you may still need to utilise a traditional vacuum alongside a robot option. Even the best vacuum cleaners can sometimes miss certain spots or struggle with corners and skirting boards, depending on their specific design. Other common issues include units getting stuck or losing their suction, but routinely checking for blockages or cleaning the filter should resolve most of these problems.

As you shop for a robot vacuum, there are a few terms that you will want to keep an eye out for. For example, “entry-level models tend to use basic gyroscopes or camera-only systems, they can struggle in low light or cluttered spaces,” explains Solenco's managing director, Trevor Brewer. Whereas “more advanced robots use LiDAR-based mapping, which creates an accurate, room-level map regardless of lighting conditions.” Alternatively, hybrid systems may combine LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) with additional sensors for obstacle avoidance.

At a glance - the best robot vacuum cleaners for 2026

  • Best robot vacuum overall: eufy E25

  • Best value robot vacuum: Hoover HG4 Hydro Robot Vacuum Cleaner & Mop

  • Best robot vacuum and mop: Roomba Max 705 Combo robot + AutoWash dock

  • Best robot vacuum for thick carpets and rugs: Dreame L10s Ultra Gen 2 Robot Vacuum Cleaner and Mop with Self-Cleaning and Auto-Empty

  • Best robot vacuum for homes with pets and kids: ECOVACS DEEBOT X11 OmniCyclone Bagless Robot Vacuum & Mop

  • Best robot vacuum at avoiding obstacles: Samsung Bespoke AI Jet Bot Combo AI+ 3-in-1 Cleaning with Steam Plus

  • Best beginner robot vacuum: Shark Matrix Plus 2-in-1 Robotic Vacuum Cleaner

How we tested

The robot vacuums were tested on a variety of floor types, from laminate and tile to carpet and a thicker pile rug, as well as on the same kinds of common messes, to ensure a fair test.

Each one was rated on cleaning performance, navigation, battery life, noise, app usability, maintenance, ease of use and value for money.

The best robot vacuum cleaners for 2026

1. eufy E25

Overall rating: 5/5

Best robot vacuum overall

  • Type: Mop combo

  • Suction power: 20,000 Pa

  • Battery life: 216 mins (vacuum only), 125 mins (vacuum and mop)

  • Navigation type: LiDAR

  • Dustbin size: 3 litre dustbin, 2.5 litre clean water tank and 1.8 litre dirty water tank

Even though it might be the second most expensive robot vacuum that I tried, at their current prices anyway, I was thoroughly impressed by one of eufy’s latest models. At 20,000 Pa, it is one of the highest suction powers that you will find on the market, and this helps it to work equally well on hard floors and carpets/rugs. This extra pressure was particularly obvious when scrubbing laminate and tile floors, using very little water and, in turn, leaving them dry in seconds. It also dealt with long hairs, without it getting tangled, too.

I liked how quiet it was, and the accompanying app has plenty of options. Even after testing it for over a week, I felt like I had not even scratched the surface of what was possible. And because it is not completely circular, with its blockier design, it can better navigate corners and edges, instead of missing them.

As for the slight negatives, both the vacuum and the station were prone to fingerprints and dust, as well, which was a bit annoying, especially when you consider the price. As was how you can only use eufy’s refillable cleaning solution cartridges going forward, which will incur additional costs.

RRP£849
Shop now

2. Roomba Max 705 Combo Robot + AutoWash Dock

Overall rating: 4.5/5

Best robot vacuum and mop

  • Type: Combo

  • Suction power: 13,000 Pa

  • Battery life: 180 minutes

  • Navigation type: LiDAR (and AI)

  • Dustbin size: 75 days of auto-emptying debris (exact dimensions not provided by the brand)

Many will no doubt be familiar with the Roomba brand, and for good reason. Currently down to £549, from £999, I found it to be well worth the investment. Avoiding and navigating around everything from cables and items of furniture to boxes and storage, thanks to a mixture of LiDAR and AI, I did not need to worry about it colliding or anything getting caught up in the mechanism. I was also incredibly impressed that it got right up to and along skirting boards and in corners thanks to its PerfectEdge extension. It was easily the best at this by quite some margin.

Its heated mopping worked particularly well on dried dirt and messes, lifting even the most stubborn of stains, while the nifty mop cover allows it to move from hard floors to rugs and carpets, and back again, without you having to worry about it wetting the rug or getting up to manually change it back and forth. And again, the voice control and app were both receptive, allowing me to control it with ease or not even needing to get up to do so.

I was quite concerned when the box first arrived. Despite all of the robot vacuums that come with docking stations coming in sizeable boxes, the Roomba one was incredibly heavy. So, if you have any mobility struggles or are a bit older, you may need some assistance bringing it into your home and unpacking it.

RRP£999
Shop now

3. Dreame L10s Ultra Gen 2 Robot Vacuum Cleaner and Mop with Self-Cleaning and Auto-Empty

Overall rating: 4.5/5

Best robot vacuum for thick carpets and rugs

  • Type: Mop combo

  • Suction power: 10,000 Pa

  • Battery life: 240 minutes (in standard mode)

  • Navigation type: AI

  • Dustbin size: 0.3 litres (80ml water tank capacity)

For only around £100 or so more than the Hoover, I would highly recommend spending a bit more on the Dreame. Not only is it one of the most attractive designs, with its polished white look, but it felt unobtrusive in a way that many of the others did not, and there were plenty of spots in my home where I could set it up out of the way.

It excelled at vacuuming, with its powerful suction, even if it only had 10,000 Pa. It struggled a bit around skirting boards, but I have resigned myself to most not getting right into the corners. And the mop, while working on most messes, did need a few extra passes on especially tough or ground-in stains. I also found that long hairs were prone to tangling in the mechanism. Yet, it had the added bonus of a ramp setup. This means that if there is a slight incline in your home or a ramp from one room to the other, you can program this in, and it will handle them with ease.

Additionally, it was a standout on my shaggy pile rug. It has not looked that refreshed since I brought it home, and I have ended up forgoing my usual vacuum for this one going forward. The smart vacuum voice control also worked well, even as someone with a stronger - namely Scottish - accent, and you can link it to Google Home, Siri or Alexa if you prefer.

RRP£479
Shop now

4. Hoover HG4 Hydro Robot Vacuum Cleaner & Mop

Overall rating: 4/5

Best value robot vacuum

  • Type: Mop combo

  • Suction power: 4000 Pa

  • Battery life: 120 minutes

  • Navigation type: Advanced Laser Navigation System

  • Dustbin size: 0.26 litres

With the ability to vacuum and mop, even if its mopping is quite basic compared to some of the more expensive models, it works particularly well on hard floors and thinner rugs and carpets. It did struggle with debris on coarser or thicker rugs, but I was quite astonished by how much dirt it picked up from floors that looked clean to the eye.

Both bins are easy to empty, and I was a big fan of the understated yet sleek design. The charging dock also tucks in neatly, not taking up much room and could be a fantastic option if you do not necessarily need a docking station. At its full price of £299, you might be better off spending a little bit more on some of the others on this list. Yet, it is currently on sale for £199, which makes it a much more attractive purchase and a good option if you are on a budget or new to robot vacuums.

However, one of my biggest issues with it was how long it took to charge. Taking over four and a half hours to charge fully, this means you need to plan ahead. It also had some issues connecting to my home WiFi. This might not be the case for all, but for being a Wi-Fi robot vacuum, it had quite a few issues communicating with my router. After several hours of trying to change the frequencies to have them link, I ended up having to reconfigure my router, so this might not be the best model if you want one which is simple to set up and use straight away.

RRP£299
Shop now

5. ECOVACS DEEBOT X11 OmniCyclone Bagless Robot Vacuum & Mop

Overall rating: 4/5

Best robot vacuum for homes with pets and kids

  • Type: Mop combo

  • Suction power: 19,500 Pa

  • Battery life: 369 min (PowerBoost charge on) or 206 min (with PowerBoost charge off)

  • Navigation type: dToF (Direct Time-of-Flight)

  • Dustbin size: 220ml

Another interesting piece of kit, the ECOVACS premium bagless combo performed well across a range of different floor types, with it being particularly successful at dealing with long hair and pet hair. At 19,000 Pa it has one of the strongest suction powers, and this is observable when running it, without it being too noisy.

It might not be the best option if you have a lot of clutter or cables and wires on your floor, as it was rather hit or miss as to whether it would avoid them or roll towards them. I did, however, appreciate the on-demand hot water tank, which helps the roller to scrub away even the dirtiest of messes, including dried-on mud and spilt wet coffee granules.

My only real bugbear was its mapping. Upon first starting up the robot vacuum, it took considerably longer to map a room than the alternatives. It also didn’t seem to like black or dark rugs. In fact, I had to map the room without the rug for it to work, which was not ideal. I did put the rug down a few days later, and it went over it without issue, so I am not sure if this is a slight glitch, but it felt more complicated than it needed to be. The docking station also takes up quite a bit of space, so you will need to have a designated area where you want to set it up.

RRP£1,119
Shop now

6. Samsung Bespoke AI Jet Bot Combo AI+ 3-in-1 Cleaning with Steam Plus

Overall rating: 4/5

Best robot vacuum at avoiding obstacles

  • Type: Combo

  • Suction power: 6000 Pa

  • Battery life: 180 minutes

  • Navigation type: dToF LiDAR

  • Dustbin size: 0.25 litre dustbin and 0.1 litre water tank

One of the most expensive robot vacuums currently on the market - even with it being discounted to under £1000 - the Samsung Bespoke AI Jet Bot Combo combines three cleaning devices into one. While many of the others that we tested vacuumed and mopped, this also steam cleaned the floor, so it could save you from having to invest in a separate steam cleaner. Initially, I thought that this would be handy on my hard floors, but I was pleasantly surprised to discover that the steam cleaning could be used to wash and sanitise rugs, too, with the robot vacuum then utilising hot air to dry them.

As you might expect from a brand such as Samsung, their app is intuitive with an extensive number of features. I especially appreciated the live Cleaning Report, which allows you to check how it is getting on even if you are out or in another room. You can also use the app to schedule cleans and switch between cleaning methods, with plenty more options to completely tailor it to your space or spaces. But depending on how tech-savvy you are, you may need to take some time to get to grips with the sheer amount of choice.

One of the biggest selling points of this specific robot vacuum, in addition to its impressive docking station, is its use of AI. And it handled obstacles, clutter and changes in flooring with no problems at all. Even though it has a lower suction power than some of the others, it did not struggle with dirt and debris. However, as someone with longer hair, this did get caught in the mechanism quite a few times and caused the vacuum to beep until I removed it, which was rather frustrating. It was also one of the heaviest, along with the Roomba, and required more assembly. So, it took considerably longer to get it up and running and had some fiddly parts, which might be off-putting for some.

RRP£999
Shop now

7. Shark Matrix Plus 2-in-1 Robotic Vacuum Cleaner

Overall rating: 4/5

Best beginner robot vacuum

  • Type: Mop combo

  • Suction power: Number not provided by brand

  • Battery life: 110 minutes

  • Navigation type: LiDAR

  • Dustbin size: 0.31 litres (300ml water tank capacity)

One of the quietest that I tested, it handled everything from dirt and debris to hair, spills, and wet and dry mud without me needing to expend any elbow grease. And unlike some other vacuum/mop combos, the pads oscillate in such a manner that they give the floor a proper scrub instead of just wetting the floor. I also really liked the overall look of it, with it coming in a white or a black colourway, depending on your personal preference. Like most robot vacuums, you can opt for the standard or auto emptying version of it, and while I have been using the standard variation in my own home, I got a chance to test the auto emptying bagless design, as well, and found it to be one of the best. And it is only £50 more to upgrade to this self-emptying style from Shark at the moment.

While the vacuum got right into the skirting board and edges and felt like it was using air to do so, the mop function was not as successful. So, you may still need to go in with a traditional mop to reach these areas, which obviously is not ideal. Again, this might not be the best option in homes or rooms with one flooring type or the other, as it is unable to lift its mopping pad to move from hard floors to carpets or rugs. As someone with a large rug in their living room, this proved a bit problematic for it.

I was not particularly impressed with the accompanying app either. It left quite a lot to be desired and felt more complicated than it needed to be. However, it is a good option if you want a robot vacuum which is relatively easy to set up and start using straight away.

RRP£379
Shop now

Robot vacuums FAQs

“When it comes to pet hair, brush design and airflow control are far more important than headline suction figures,” Brewer affirms. “More advanced robots use anti-tangle or hair-separating brush designs, which guide hair into the airflow path before it wraps around the roller. These systems dramatically reduce maintenance while maintaining strong pickup on both hard floors and carpets.

“Edge performance also matters: robots with better corner reach and effective side-brush placement tend to collect more pet hair along skirting boards and furniture edges. Finally, good filtration and sensible bin capacity are essential, as pet hair fills dustbins and filters faster than general household dust."

A self-emptying robot vacuum base is worth it with shedding pets because the on‑board bin fills quickly, and bagged bases reduce dust when you empty them.

,

“Robot vacuums are excellent for everyday maintenance, such as cleaning and keeping floors consistently tidy with minimal effort. However, they don’t fully replace a traditional vacuum, which is still better for deep cleaning thick carpets, upholstery and stairs, as well as spot-cleaning heavy or ground-in dirt,” argues Peter Tyson’s appliance category manager, Steven Kent. “Many households use a robot vacuum alongside a cordless or upright vacuum for best results.”

,

As you might expect, this will vary from model to model. However, “a typical robot vacuum can run on average for about 30 mins to 2 hours before needing to be charged, while some newer and energy-efficient models can run for up to 3 hours,” according to Katie Lillywhite, small appliance expert at AO.com. But the good thing is, if a vacuum runs out of charge before completing a task, it should automatically return to its dock to recharge before resuming any cleaning.

“It is also worth noting that the battery life can degrade over time, meaning the run time will also decrease. The average lifespan of a battery is 2-5 years before needing to be replaced,” Lillywhite adds.

,

With self-emptying bins costing more, on average, they may be worth the additional expenditure “for larger homes, pet owners, and anyone who reacts to dust,” says Lillywhite. Other trade-offs include “a larger dock that needs clear floor space, and a loud 10–20 second emptying cycle you’ll want to schedule for daytime.” In most cases, it will be down to personal preference and budget.

,

If you are looking for a quiet robot vacuum or have an open plan space, you will want to look for one with a quiet mode, which cleans at a lower suction, thus reducing the noise level. A lower decibel rating can also translate to quieter cleaning.

,

The experts agree that it is recommended to replace the filters on your robot vacuum every 3 months and the brushes every 6-12 months. This is, of course, dependent on usage, with homes with pets potentially needing to do so more often.

And if you notice that the performance has dipped or either the filters or the brushes appear to be damaged or broken, you should look to replace them sooner.

You should also maintain brushes, filters, and dustbins for optimal performance. This can be done with regular cleaning, and some robot vacuums even come with various tools and accessories to help you do so.

,

Traditionally, “they are designed to clean carpets of a low to medium pile and are less efficient on deeper pile carpets,” Kent remarks. “Using the app for the robot vacuum cleaner, you can identify zones that have a deeper pile carpet, and for them you can program it to use maximum suction to improve the cleaning performance.”

Unfortunately, even the best robot vacuums cannot clean stairs, at least at the moment anyway, so you will need to use a traditional vacuum to do so.

While robot vacuum cleaners are revolutionary for maintaining day-to-day cleanliness, they are most effective when viewed as a partner to your existing cleaning routine, alongside one of the best corded vacuums instead. As our testing showed, even the most advanced AI can struggle with deep-seated grit in thick carpets or the tightest corners of a staircase.

If your robot vacuum includes a mopping function, we recommend using a specialist solution like this WoldoClean Robot Mop Cleaning Solution from Amazon. By using the right maintenance tools alongside your automation, you can ensure your floors stay sparkling, and your robot lasts for years to come.

item.title: Just dropped: the new arrivals our editors can’t stop talking about this month
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.content:encoded:
