item.title: 8 best clothes steamers, tried and tested
item.dc:creator: Priya Raj
item.description: We put clothing steamers from the most popular brands to the test to see which came out on top.
item.content:encoded:
Ironing is an unavoidable chore that must be done. But what if it could be simplified without the hassle of setting up and taking down a bulky ironing board?

Clothes steamers are increasingly popular among seasoned travellers and busy professionals who need wrinkle-free clothes at their fingertips. They can also be an excellent addition to a typical family home, making it easy to keep garments looking crisp and fresh for everyday wear. In fact, many households now view these tools as essential laundry companions, much like how the best steam cleaners have become the go-to for deep-cleaning floors and upholstery. But steamers don’t come without their user difficulties: weak steam, water leakage and heavy appliances are just a few.

So we’re putting some of the best clothes steamers from the most popular brands like Morphy Richards, Russell Hobbs and Steamery to the test to see if they’re worth the investment, and whether they can really tackle creases as well as the best steam irons.

At a glance - the best clothes steamers for 2026

  • Best overall: Lakeland Compact EasyFold Steamer

  • Best folding steamer: Beldray Handheld Portable Garment Steamer

  • Best for shirts: Russell Hobbs Steam Genie 2 in 1

  • Best affordable clothes steamer: BEAUTURAL Handheld Garment Steamer

  • Best powerful but compact steamer: Tefal Pure Pop

  • Best for an iron-like steam: Morphy Richards TurboSteam 2 in 1

  • Best aesthetically pleasing steamer: Steamery Cirrus 3 Iron Steamer

  • Best for delicate fabrics: Tower CeraGlide Garment Steamer

How we tested

When testing these clothing steamers, we took into account the following factors:

  • Leakage and ease of adding water

  • Heating time

  • Strength of steam

  • Comfort (was this easy to handle)

  • Value for money

  • Versatility (does this work on different types of fabric)

  • Comparison to an iron (does it give the same kind of crease-releasing effect as an iron)

Some steamers are created to be lightweight and easy to travel with, but might not necessarily be the most heavy-duty steamers with strong crease-releasing abilities and more suited for a light refresh when travelling.

To test this, we tested each steamer on a cotton shirt, a pair of linen trousers and a satin top to see how versatile it is, and what severity of crease it can smooth out.

The best clothes steamers

1. Lakeland Compact EasyFold Steamer

Overall rating: 5/5

Best steamer overall

  • Water capacity: 160ml

  • Weight: 900g

  • Warranty: 3 years

A steamer for travelling is a must-have in the case of every seasoned traveller, and so testing a foldable steamer was a high priority. A small detail in this steamer, which makes a big difference, particularly if you need to steam a large garment, or a few at one time, is the ability to set this to omit a continuous steam. In contrast, most other steamers require you to keep a button held down to keep the steam coming.

The steam from this was powerful enough to steam creased linen, ideal for those moments where perfectly pressed clothes become creased and crumpled in transit. We also found it to be one of the lightest options, making it perfect when your luggage allowance is tight.

RRP£29.99
Shop now

2. Beldray Titanium Handheld Portable Garment Steamer

Overall rating: 5/5

Best folding clothes steamer

  • Water capacity: 100ml

  • Weight: 810g

  • Cable length: 1.9 metres

  • Warranty: 1 year

  • Steam output: 18g/min

When testing this steamer, our first impression was how easy it was to fill with water and fit back into the body – there was no water spill at all. Unlike others, this has an on-off button for the steamer, rather than having to hold a button down. A downside to note is that this steamer is extremely loud when in use.

While this is a light, compact and folding steamer, during testing it became obvious that this can still do the job of a heavy-duty steamer. It worked well on a cotton shirt and linen trousers. The small size means it can take longer to steam than one of the bigger models.

RRP£27.99
Shop now

3. Russell Hobbs Steam Genie 2 in 1

Overall rating: 5/5

Best clothes steamer for shirts

  • Water capacity: 150ml

  • Weight: 1.21kg

  • Cable length: 3 metres

  • Warranty: 2 years

  • Steam output: 20g/min

This was the only steamer we tested where the water tank was attached to the device, so like an iron, this is to be filled up directly into the steamer rather than popping out a water tank. In spite of the size, this is fairly lightweight at 1.2 kg, making it comfortable to handle. While this heated up fairly quickly, it took around 15 seconds for the water to feed through and for the steam to start pumping out. Something which caught our eye immediately during testing was that at three metres, this was by far the longest cable.

If you’re looking for a heavy-duty steamer and in particular need something which can press your shirts like an iron, this is a wonderful choice. The heated soleplate of this is pretty identical to an iron, so it was no real surprise during testing that it performed like one too. It took around 20 seconds to steam a whole shirt. We then lay the shirt over a towel to quickly press the cuffs and collar for that iron-like finish.

RRP£59.99
Shop now

4. BEAUTURAL Handheld Garment Steamer

Overall rating: 4.5/5

Best affordable clothes steamer

  • Water capacity: 260ml

  • Weight: 930g

  • Cable length: 1.9 metres

  • Warranty: 2 years

  • Steam output: 30g/min

This isn’t the most powerful steamer we tested, but by far one of the best options in this price range. One of the first things we noticed about using this steamer was the length of the cable. At under two metres, it can be difficult to comfortably steam and may require using an extension lead.

Considering the price and size, this steamer packs a punch and delivers a wonderfully powerful continuous steam, which worked relatively well on satin, cotton and linen. Although not as effective as some of the pricier options in our roundup, the price and quality of this steamer are hard to compete with.

RRP£22.99
Shop now

5. Tefal Pure Pop Slim Handheld Clothes Steamer

Overall rating: 4/5

Best powerful but compact steamer

  • Water capacity: 120ml

  • Weight: 1kg

  • Cable length: 2 metres

  • Warranty: 2 years

  • Steam output: 20g/min

A compact steamer is a must for a few reasons. Whether a small option is needed due to wrists which can’t carry much weight, or just a lack of space to store another household appliance. Luckily, this small but mighty steamer means no compromising.

This heated up really quickly, and with minimal noise. A few water droplets did spill out while fitting into the water tank.

This handled a creased cotton shirt surprisingly well. Where this majorly differs from other steamers is in the velvet-textured padding around the metal soleplate. This helps smooth out the fabric of the garment, but it does mean you need to hold the edge of the garment in one hand to get that pull effect. Overall, we were impressed with how well this worked for a small appliance, though the weight means it wouldn’t be an ideal choice for travelling.

RRP£49.99
Shop now

6. Morphy Richards TurboSteam 2 in 1

Overall rating: 4/5

Best for an iron-like steam

  • Water capacity: 200ml

  • Weight: 1.2kg

  • Cable length: 2.5 metres

  • Warranty: 2 years

  • Steam output: 35g/min

Our first impression when testing this steamer was that it was by far the heaviest and biggest steamer we tested, but not necessarily the most powerful steam. That said, the hot iron plate helps make up for where the strength of the steam lacks, and the appliance really is what it says on the tin.

During testing we found it wasn’t as effective as the other 2-in-1 that we tested, but it was the closest thing too an actual iron, making it ideal for someone who still prefers an iron, but occasionally needs a steamer for more delicate clothing or fabrics and doesn’t want lots of appliances cluttering up the home, just one thing that works really well.

RRP£49.99
Shop now

7. Steamery Cirrus 3 Iron Steamer

Overall rating: 4/5

Most aesthetically pleasing steamer

  • Water capacity: 85ml

  • Weight: 860g

  • Cable length: 2.3 metres

  • Warranty: 2 years

  • Steam output: 22 g/min

Aesthetics aren’t the first thing everyone looks for in a steamer, but this is just as functional as it is elegant. Unlike others which were tested, the water tank is inserted upright, so there is virtually no risk of leaking water when filling or use, and it has a satisfying magnetic clip – so there’s no second-guessing if you’ve inserted it back into the body correctly.

It’s comfortable to handle, though slightly on the heavy side for a steamer of this size. It’s ready to use in around 25 seconds and emits a strong steam. The soleplate doubles as an iron, and while it’s not as effective as some of the heavy-duty steamers we tested, this is ideal for pressing cuffs and collars of cotton shirts.

RRP£150
Shop now

8. Tower CeraGlide Garment Steamer

Overall rating: 4/5

Best for delicate fabrics

  • Water capacity: 200ml

  • Weight: 1.2kg

  • Cable length: 2.5 metres

  • Warranty: 1 year

  • Steam output: 35g/min

While the brand states this takes around 35 seconds to heat, we found during testing that this took after 10 seconds. The power cable has a decent length compared to other steamers, where you might find yourself accidentally pulling on the plug.

This was not the easiest steamer to operate, though for some garments it reigns supreme. On a shirt, it didn’t give the desired pressed effect of an iro,n which we were looking for, and found that it worked better on a satin blouse. The steam this gives off is strong, but it didn’t feel as hot, and so it wasn’t as effective as releasing creases in an air-dried cotton shirt.

RRP£29.99
Shop now

Clothes steamer FAQs

It’s best to start with the basics, which is determining the best way to stream. Atit Chopra, product development director at Beldray says, “For the best results, clothes should be steamed whilst hanging rather than laid flat. Hanging garments helps fibres relax, and creases drop out naturally.”

From testing this range of steamers, it was clear that using one hand to pull on the fabric slightly helped in getting creases out in a timely manner. “Holding the steamer 15–30 cm away from the fabric and moving steadily over the garment avoids scorching and provides smoother results,” says Catrin Parry, the cleaning and laundry buying lead at Lakeland. And when it comes to preventing any further creases, the best practice is to allow the garment to cool and dry fully before wearing.

,

According to Chopra, most other fabrics are generally fine – like cotton, linen, wool and synthetic fabrics. That said, always err on the side of caution and check the garment label before you begin steaming. “Some fabrics that are generally not suitable for steam include those with a ‘do not steam/iron’ symbol,” Parry adds. It’s also advised to be careful with any garments that have any plastics or embellishments, as these could melt under extreme heat.

,

Contrary to popular belief, steaming clothes is not a way to skip out on a dry cleaning bill. “Steaming doesn’t replace professional dry cleaning for deep cleaning, stain removal or heavily structured garments,” Parry explains, adding that it can help refresh delicate items like silk and linen, which might be too delicate to dry clean frequently.

Chopra concurs, adding that steaming can help reduce odours without the agitation in washing - and will help extend the time between those trips to the dry cleaners, which no doubt your wallet will thank you for.

,

A clothes steamer is often a hefty investment, and so maintaining it properly means it’ll be worth the investment. Nell Lainton, marketing assistant and blog contributor at Morphy Richards, recommends using distilled water and emptying the steam after each use.

Chopra explains this can reduce limescale build-up, which affects the consistency of the steam if left to fester and build up. Parry adds on an extra maintenance step: “Clean the steam holes regularly to avoid mineral build-up."

Ultimately, the right clothes steamer is a game-changer for anyone looking to save time without sacrificing style. But for a truly professional look, it’s often the small details that make the biggest difference.

While you're upgrading your laundry routine, you might also want to pick up something to quickly remove lint and pilling from your favourite knits, like this rechargeable fabric shaver from Lakeland. By pairing the power of steam with the right maintenance tools, you’ll ensure your wardrobe stays looking brand new for years to come.

item.title: Small kitchen storage ideas – 8 design secrets for a curated, clutter-free space
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: Fighting for counter space? We’ve rounded up the most ingenious small kitchen storage ideas to help you reclaim your room and master the art of the edit.
item.content:encoded:
Creating a kitchen that feels both functional and quintessentially British often comes down to how we handle the squeeze. Finding the right small kitchen storage ideas isn’t just about tidying up; it’s about making sure your favourite teapot and everyday spices havea home that feels intentional rather than cramped.

We often think we need more square footage, but usually we just need to look at things differently and work with what we have. When playing around with small kitchen ideas, the focus should remain on hidden opportunities – such as the tops of cabinets or narrow gaps beside a fridge. By choosing pieces that work hard while maintaining a beautiful aesthetic, even the most modest rooms can be transformed into a culinary hub.

This guide explores how to move from a cluttered workspace to a space that feels thoughtfully curated. From the charm of an open dresser to a handy hidden larder, these tips are all about clearing off your counters – and if you want a quick way to start, this under-shelf spice rack from Amazon is a brilliant, low-cost way to use the empty air inside your cupboards. Afterall, the heart of the home should be a place to enjoy, not a constant battle with stacks of plates and kitchenalia.

Elevate storage with floor-to-ceiling cabinetry

When floor space is tight, the only way to go is up. Custom cupboards that reach right up to the ceiling make use of that often-dusty gap at the top of the room. As Helen Parker, creative director at deVOL Kitchens, says: ‘If ceilings are high, then use this space too and create high storage with tall wall cupboards; they may be harder to reach, but the space can be invaluable.’

Make the most of wall space with open shelves

Standard wall cabinets can sometimes feel a bit heavy in a small room. Replacing them with open shelves lets the room breathe and provides the perfect home for everyday items like stacked breakfast bowls, glass storage jars filled with grains or your most-used cookbooks.

‘If ceiling height is restricted, open shelves can work in place of wall cabinetry - they can keep the room feeling lighter while still offering easy access to the things you reach for the most,’ explains Fred Horlock, design director at Neptune. ‘Features like zig-zag shelf fittings allow you to position shelves at precisely calibrated levels so that none of the cupboards' height goes to waste.’

For those tricky architectural features, Parker adds: ‘A small indent in a wall is a perfect place to fit some slim shelves, and when painted in the same colour as the walls, they almost disappear. Slim shelves can give you such good storage for glassware and tall, shallow objects and also create a nice visual where wall space and spots for ornamentation are in short supply.’

Nkuku Meera Mango Wood & Iron Gallery Shelf

RRPfrom £160
Available from Nkuku
Shop now

M&S Set of 3 Small Stacking Glass Storage Jars

RRP£12
Available from M&S
Shop now

Emma Bridgewater Black Toast Wooden Kitchen Shelf

RRP£115
Available from Emma Bridgewater
Shop now

Banish worktop clutter with an integrated appliance garage

The quickest way to make a kitchen feel smaller is to cover the counters in gadgets. An appliance garage is a clever storage unit - often a small cabinet sitting directly on the worktop - that allows the toaster, kettle, coffee machine and any other appliance to stay plugged in but kept neatly in one area. It’s essentially a parking spot for your appliances.

Emily Newmarch, design consultant at Kitchens By Holloways, explains that these units are key to maintaining a 'clean, streamlined aesthetic' in a compact room. By using pocket doors that slide back into the cabinetry, you can keep the station open during the morning rush without blocking the rest of the kitchen.

'These solutions allow everyday appliances to remain easily accessible without interrupting the overall flow of the kitchen,' she says. 'A dedicated worktop area accommodates secondary appliances such as toasters and coffee machines, with carefully positioned electrical sockets to ensure practicality.'

Transform empty space with classic peg rails

For a flexible and charming setup, a simple wall-mounted peg rail is a great addition to a compact kitchen. These versatile rails are particularly effective when placed under open shelves or wall cabinets, allowing you to hang frequently used items - such as colanders, aprons, measuring jugs and even decorative items - within arm’s reach.

Unlike bulky cupboards, a row of sturdy timber pegs takes up almost no physical space while turning your kitchenware into a practical display. This classic approach adds a touch of artisanal, Shaker-style charm to a plain wall, making even the smallest room feel lived-in yet organised.

Garden Trading Hambledon 6 Peg Shelf

RRP£75
Available from Garden Trading
Shop now

Dibor Set Of 2 Woven Hanging Storage Baskets

RRP£45
Available from Not On The High Street
Shop now

Burford Garden Co. Oak Shaker Peg Rails

RRP£65
Available from Burford Garden Co.
Shop now

Utilise overhead space with a traditional hanging rack

If the walls and floors are full, look to the ceiling. A traditional hanging rack over a kitchen island or above an oven is a wonderful way to store bulky pots and pans. It frees up cupboard space and gives the space a country kitchen feel. Just be sure to hand it at a height that is reachable and won’t be bumped into.

Trade a traditional pantry for a vertical pull-out larder

In a small kitchen, horizontal wall space is scarce. Instead of trying to squeeze in a traditional wide pantry, consider going vertical with a slimline pull-out larder. These clever units can be as narrow as 15cm, fitting into those awkward gaps next to a fridge or oven, yet offer an immense amount of storage by utilising the height of the room.

Because the entire unit slides out towards you, it eliminates the ‘black hole’ effect of deep cupboards where ingredients go to be forgotten. Horlock notes that such features make ‘everything visible and easier to reach,’ as you can access jars and tins without sacrificing the significant floor area required by a traditional walk-in pantry or standalone cupboard.

Organise culinary essentials with in-drawer dividers

There is something very calming about a drawer where everything has its home. By using adjustable in-drawer dividers, you can create a bespoke grid that fits your specific collection of tools, from long rolling pins to cutlery. This keeps items from sliding around every time you open the drawers and turns a chaotic ‘junk drawer’ into a curated display, making it much easier to find what you need in the heat of cooking up a storm.

Rebecca Udall Rattan Cutlery Tray

RRP£48
Available from Rebecca Udall
Shop now

Lakeland Chrome Plated Bakeware Organiser

RRP£12.99
Available from Lakeland
Shop now

Neptune Orford Tray

RRPfrom £75
Available from Neptune
Shop now

Optimise the footprint with staggered worktops

This is a clever trick for tight spaces that involves varying how far your counters stick out into the room. ‘Staggering the depth of your worktops is another good option, as it allows you to create small shallow areas for seating or slim cupboards,’ says Parker. ‘You need depth for sink and appliances, and if your kitchen is long and thin, a staggered worktop is the perfect solution for a little breakfast bar, as galley kitchens rarely have enough width for a kitchen table.’

Ultimately, the best storage solution for small kitchens is a bit of honest editing. Once you’ve cleared the clutter, you can focus on pieces that truly elevate the room; for instance, swapping mismatched plastic boxes for wicker baskets, like this wicker basket from Lakeland, is a simple way to make your kitchen cupboards look like a high-end larder.

No amount of cabinetry can hide a surplus of gadgets you simply don’t use, but a few well-chosen accessories can make your space feel organised and calm. By focusing on what truly matters, any kitchen – no matter how small – can feel spacious, organised and full of character.

item.title: The best non-toxic cookware everyone is switching to for safer, cleaner cooking at home
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: Upgrade your kitchen with our edit of the best toxin-free cookware, from high-tech hybrid pans to heritage cast iron classics.
item.content:encoded:
In the modern home, the transition to a healthier kitchen often begins with a thoughtful evaluation of the tools we use every day. Choosing the best non-toxic cookware is no longer a niche interest; it’s a practical way to ensure that the quality of ingredients isn’t compromised by what they are cooked in.

Essentially, non-toxic cookware uses stable, natural materials that won’t leach chemicals into your food. Many traditional non-stick pans rely on synthetic coatings containing PFAS (often called ‘forever chemicals’), which can break down under heat. By switching to alternatives like stainless steel, cast iron or high-quality ceramic, you ensure that the only thing ending up on the plate is the flavour you intended.

Non-toxic cooking can start straight from the prep; consider a wooden chopping board, like this one from Lakeland, which offers a natural alternative to plastic. With all of that in mind, we have chosen the following pieces for their safety and timeless design.

Our Place Always Pan 2.0

RRP£125
Available from Our Place
Shop now

Le Creuset Cast Iron Round Casserole Dish

RRPfrom £219
Available from Le Creuset
Shop now

Stellar Profile Uncoated Chef Pan

RRP£50
Available from Stellar
Shop now

De Buyer Mineral B Pro Frying Pan

RRPfrom £60
Available from Borough Kitchen
Shop now

Greenpan Premiere Skillet

RRP£135
Available from Wayfair
Shop now

Falcon Enamelware 5-Piece Enamel Bake Set

RRP£85
Available from Amazon
Shop now

Zwilling Stainless Steel Grill

RRP£59.95
Available from Zwilling
Shop now

HexClad Hybrid Pot With Lid

RRP£114
Available from HexClad
Shop now

Beyond choosing the right pans, creating a truly non-toxic kitchen is often about the small, daily habits that preserve the integrity of your tools. For instance, even the most durable ceramic or stainless steel surfaces benefit from gentle heat; starting a pan on a medium setting rather than full flame prevents oils from carbonising and sticking to the surface. It’s also worth swapping out plastic utensils for silicone or FSC-certified wood, ensuring that no microplastics are introduced to your meals during the cooking process.

Our Place Utensil Essentials

RRP£70
Available from Our Place
Shop now

KitchenAid 4pc Birchwood Tool Set

RRP£28.98
Available from Amazon
Shop now

Le Creuset Craft 5-piece Utensil Set

RRP£72
Available from Le Creuset
Shop now

Ultimately, investing in these pieces is as much about sustainability as it is about health. While traditional non-stick frying pans are often seen as disposable items to be replaced every few years, materials like cast iron, stainless steel and ceramic are designed to endure. By creating a smaller collection of high-quality, toxin-free essentials, you can reduce kitchen waste and move toward a more intentional ‘buy once, buy well’ philosophy.

A healthy kitchen is a holistic environment, and the care of your surfaces matters as much as the pans themselves. To keep your new investment in peak condition without using harsh chemicals, consider a natural cleaner like this Ecover washing up liquid from Amazon.

item.title: David and Victoria Beckham’s Holland Park hallway proves monochrome will always feel timeless – and it’s easier to recreate than you think
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: The Beckhams’ hallway is an absolute masterclass in drama and monochromatic styling.
item.content:encoded:
It comes as no surprise that David and Victoria Beckham’s hallway is extremely opulent; there is something wonderfully attainable about its classic composition, and it is a masterclass in the power of a strictly monochromatic foyer.

If you’re hunting for hallway flooring ideas with the same curated edge, the secret is in the contrast. The Beckham’s famously opted for an oversized black-and-white marble checkerboard – a nod to the quintessentially British Victorian terrace.

View this post on Instagram

This hallway has become an icon in its own right, frequently serving as the chic backdrop for the couple’s celebrated ‘OOTD’ Instagram snaps. To protect the stone while keeping that crisp, monochromatic aesthetic, add a cast iron doormat like this one from Garden Trading, which will anchor the setting without distracting from the floor’s geometry.

Shop black-and-white hallway accessories

Ruggable Jaque Checkered Black Flatwoven Rug

RRP£139
Shop now

The White Company Cowley Table Lamp

RRP£95
Shop now

Pacific Black Heritage Pine Wood Half Moon Console Table

RRP£90
Shop now

M&S Metal Ribbed Multi-Shade Chandelier

RRP£99
Shop now

OKA Volu Decorative Vase

RRP£100
Shop now

Dusk Bobbin Round Wall Mirror

RRP£60
Shop now

To nail the look in your own home, keep your accessories strictly edited so that your guests get that ‘wow factor’ as soon as they step through the door. It’s all about a small selection of hero pieces that make a statement without being overly cluttered. Think an oversized light fitting overhead, a pair of statement table lamps on a slim console with a big, detailed hallway mirror above.

The best part? You don’t need to have a multimillion-pound townhouse fit for a 90s girl band member or a football legend to make this work. Whether you’re working with a grand entrance hall or a compact hallway, it’s all about the mood you create.

By sticking to a disciplined palette and choosing quality over quantity, you can give any home that high-end, drama-filled feel found in the Beckham’s Holland Park residence. To really lean into that sense of luxury, don’t just design for the visual – scents are the invisible accessory that can elevate a space as much as a piece of furniture or art. A glass reed diffuser like this one from Jo Malone will have your hallway feel like a multisensory experience and smell as luxurious as it looks.

item.title: Behind the crown and away from the cameras - how Queen Elizabeth II spent her days in surprising ways and why a century-old tradition ends with King Charles III
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton
item.description: From corgis and horses to the more unusual stamp-collecting and even pigeon racing, in the centenary year of her birth, we’re celebrating the off-duty Queen Elizabeth II
item.content:encoded:
An animal-lover since she was a small child, our late queen surrounded herself with corgis for decades: she is believed to have owned at least 30 during her reign, and in popular culture Queen Elizabeth II and her canine companions are inseparable still. “My corgis are my family,” she once said.

But did you know Queen Elizabeth II also enjoyed the company of other animals, including horses and even pigeons? Or that she also had a penchant for stamp-collecting and Scottish country dancing?

As we celebrate the centenary of her birth in April 2026, which is being marked by Buckingham Palace with the largest exhibition of her fashion ever staged, we thought it was time to take a closer look at the life of the off-duty queen.

If you're interested in understanding her extraordinary life further, David Cannadine has gone deeper in his book, Queen Elizabeth II: A Concise Biography of an Exceptional Sovereign, released in 2025. The respected historian brilliantly unpicks the life of the most depicted person ever to have lived, tracking the monumental global changes that occurred during her life, and how she remained a steadfast, dutiful figure throughout her 96 years.

Cannadine writes in his work that Queen Elizabeth II's hobbies were her way of escaping from the pressures and demands of being queen. So, how did Queen Elizabeth II spend her time when she wasn't performing royal duties?

Queen Elizabeth II's love of dogs

Queen Elizabeth II's love of dogs went back to childhood, when her parents, the future King George VI and Queen Elizabeth (later the Queen Mother) added a corgi called Dookie to the family. For her 18th birthday, the Princess received a corgi of her own, Susan, who bobbed along on her honeymoon. A loyal dog, Susan is said to have bitten a policeman, a detective and a sentry.

Most of the Queen’s 30 or so corgis or corgi crossbreeds over the years were descended from her line. Their names ranged from the traditional (Emma, Holly, Monty) to the unusual (Brush, Honey, Whisky, Sherry, Spick, Span and Bisto Oxo, to name a few).

The Queen’s dogs, who Princess Diana called “the moving carpet” because they followed the Queen everywhere, had their own room at the Palace, where they slept in bespoke wicker beds. Supper was at 5pm, and might have been fresh rabbit, chicken and rice, rustled up by royal chefs. The Queen liked to feed them herself and take them on daily walks. They made the Queen feel more at ease with strangers, and were a sort of therapy, which the late Prince Philip called her “dog mechanism”.

Queen Elizabeth II's love of horses

A close rival for the Queen’s affections were her horses, another childhood passion. Her grandfather, George V, gave her a Shetland pony called Peggy for her fourth birthday. She kept some horses for riding at Frogmore Stables at Windsor Castle, and was often spotted trotting in Windsor Great Park with Terry Pendry, her head groom, who said she was “a fountain of knowledge in all things equine”. Sanction, the last horse the Queen rode before she switched to ponies, was a favourite.

Queen Elizabeth II also loved to watch horse racing, attending Royal Ascot almost every year since 1945. She even entered her own horses there, celebrating four winners in 1957. The Queen’s thoroughbreds, which were foaled at Sandringham, her country home in Norfolk, were ridden to victory 20 times by jockeys wearing the royal purple and scarlet silks.

Queen Elizabeth II and Scottish country dancing

One of the Queen’s racehorses shared its name with another of her passions. Eightsome Reel, who ran at Ascot in 2019, is also a Scottish country dance. The dance, which involves groups of couples stepping gaily to particular patterns, has been around since the 17th century. A ‘reel’ is one of the faster, livelier dances, which all have evocative names like Dashing White Sergeant.

The Queen, who was patron of the Scottish Country Dance Society since 1947, before she acceded the throne, took part once a year at Balmoral, her Scottish holiday home. Every summer, she threw a thank-you party for the castle staff and local community in the grand Ballroom. The Ghillies’ Ball, a tradition started by Queen Victoria, and still happens under King Charles III, is a merry affair with pipers, punch and plenty of tartan. The men in the Royal Family wear kilts made of the Balmoral tartan in red and grey; the women, including the late Queen, wear matching sashes. She is said to have had “great rhythm”.

Queen Elizabeth II's secret hobby - pigeon racing

Her Majesty’s generous patronage hinted at more private passions. She also lent her support to the Royal Pigeon Racing Association and National Flying Club (pigeons not planes). The royal hobby stretches back to 1886, when Albert, Prince of Wales, the future George VI, received racing pigeons from the King of Belgium. He housed them at Sandringham.

The late Queen kept about 200 pigeons there. Some were for breeding, but most were for racing, where the birds are released from a distance and have to fly home as fast as possible. The pigeons, which had a leg ring marked with the Queen’s cipher, used to compete in one or two club races each week and all national races of the season. They won all the main competitions across the UK.

Since succeeding the throne, King Charles III has ended royal patronage of the Royal Pigeon Racing Association and National Flying Club after 138 years. As patron of the Royal Society for the Protection of Birds he was responding to pressure from animal welfare groups regarding the sport's cruelty. Instead, he has adopted 31 retired laying hens from the British Hen Welfare Trust, which will live and roam freely at Highgrove Gardens, his Gloucestershire home.

Queen Elizabeth II's love of stamp collecting

Similarly specialist was the Queen’s love of stamp collecting. The Royal Philatelic Collection, which fills about 300 albums and 200 boxes, is kept in a vault at St James’ Palace, another royal residence. Started by Queen Victoria’s second son, Prince Alfred in 1864, it was once valued at £100million. A rare Mauritian stamp is worth £2million alone, while the Queen spent £250,000 on a set of Penny Blacks, dated the day they came into circulation in 1840.

Of course, Queen Elizabeth II stamps are no longer issued, but some vintage collections are still available to buy, like this collection of 100 Elizabeth II stamps or Queen Elizabeth II stamp catalogues like this one.

So Queen Elizabeth II was a woman of many talents, it seems, but what about her predecessors? Did you know that Queen Victoria was a keen diarist, or that Mary, Queen of Scots, enjoyed needlework? From Henry VIII to King Charles III, here are some of the most surprising royal hobbies you definitely didn't know about.

This article was first published on discoverbritain.com

item.title: The April 2026 edition of The English Home is on sale now!
item.dc:creator: The English Home
item.description: Discover the latest issue of The English Home including regular features, exclusive interviews and interiors inspiration, on sale now.
item.content:encoded:
Our April edition celebrates the arrival of spring and offers inspiration on how to refresh homes for the new season.

Inside, discover the best new fabric and wallpaper collections, how to add character into your home with paint, and experts share their advice on how and where to source antiques. Plus, enjoy our curated shopping edits, from beautiful Easter decorations to pretty butter dishes.

Our four homes this edition include a Victorian rectory and a farmhouse in the Lake District.

Elsewhere in the issue, we explore the best things to make, see and do in April, we continue our new series where we delve into the homes of our favourite interior designers (this time focusing on dining spaces), and talk to interior designer Peter Mikic about his English home.

Purchase a copy of the April 2026 edition.

Follow us on Instagram or sign up to our newsletter to stay up to date with our various exciting announcements.

item.title: I’ve finally found a dog bed that doesn’t ruin my aesthetic – and it might be the most stylish pet buy of the year
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: If you’re tired of dog beds that feel like a design compromise, OKA’s new architectural rattan frame is the heritage-inspired solution your sitting room actually needs.
item.content:encoded:
I’ve always found that the biggest challenge when curating a home is integrating pets without cluttering the aesthetic. My English Bull Terrier is the heart of my home, yet his accessories tend to feel like a compromise against the calm.

In my search for a solution, I spotted that OKA has launched one of the best dog beds I've seen this year. This elegant rattan pet bed is the perfect answer for anyone needing a design that offers ultimate support for a dog without disrupting a scheme.

While I initially considered this budget-friendly pet bed from Dunelm, my focus shifted the second this OKA piece dropped. Its bold, structural silhouette and slight antique look is really what I needed to ground my dog’s sleeping nook.

OKA Rattan Mattaban Pet Bed

Simple yet sophisticated, this hand-woven rattan frame makes for a structural base for layering plush cushions and throws, providing a touch of organic warmth without overwhelming a room. Available in two sizes, the medium is perfect for spaniel-sized dogs, while the large is best suited to medium dogs – like my bulky Bull Terrier, for instance.

RRPfrom £295
Shop now

I’ve always loved rattan furniture and accessories thanks to the organic feel and warmth they add to a room. So when it comes to styling, I’m moving away from hiding my dog bed away in the utility room and putting it right where it can be seen and admired. It turns a ‘dog corner’ into a lovely little spot that feels like a part of the room.

The raised frame would work brilliantly in a hallway or a kitchen, keeping my dog above the cold flooring and draught; he's the type of dog to hunt out the warmest spot in the house, so I know he’d love being elevated off the ground.

It’s a sentiment shared by Charlotte Dunford, The English Home’s houses editor. ‘As the owner of an Australian Shepherd who likes to hop from bed to bed - he now has three to choose from spread through various rooms of the house - I’ve learnt first-hand that choosing a dog bed is all about that careful balance between support and style,’ she says.

‘This new offering from Oka appears to marry both beautifully. The elevated base is practical in helping to keep dogs cool and avoid overheating, while the elegant rattan design has a timeless appeal, and I can see it sitting happily in an elegant, traditional living room and rustic country kitchen alike.’

Shop more of our favourite new luxury pet accessories

OKA Akino Pet Cushion

RRP£150
Shop now

Daylesford Tweed Dog Coat

RRP£145
Shop now

Emma Bridgewater Personalised All Over Dogs Large Pet Bowl

RRPfrom £39
Shop now

Ultimately, the best thing about this new launch is that it feels as substantial as a high-end armchair. I can style the bed just as I would any other piece of furniture, and it looks even more elevated when paired with the right textures.

This OKA pet bed would look especially put-together draped with an earthy-green blanket like this pheasant-printed one by Joules, and a heritage-look dog toy like this tartan bone from Fortnum & Mason, for a country-inspired setup for your pup.

item.title: Built to withstand the beautiful chaos of family life - a Victorian renovation that proves practicality can be playful
item.dc:creator: Charlotte Dunford
item.description: Bold patterns and rich hues have transformed the ground floor of this family home in London, curated and layered with confidence by designer Laura Stephens.
item.content:encoded:
Once lacking character and cohesion, this late-Victorian family home in South East London has been reinvigorated by a dramatic transformation across its entire ground floor. The redecoration had been a long time coming, with the owners waiting until their three sons reached their teenage years before embarking on the project.

While the owners were willing to explore new interior design styles, practicality was key to ensure the home was suited to busy family life. The finished schemes display a careful balancing act, pairing the beautiful with the functional to create a home that is both inviting and durable.

'The clients were open to our ideas stylistically, but very clear that the house needed to be resilient to daily wear and tear, and the inevitable footballs flying around, so nothing could be too precious,' explains interior designer, Laura Stephens, whose considered, colour-led approach made her the perfect choice to lead the project.

With structural alterations off the cards and several existing elements to work around, Laura relied on colour, pattern and texture to infuse each space with new character.

'We took cues from how the clients had decorated previously to understand their taste, but part of our role was gently encouraging them to be braver,' she explains. 'We wanted to push boundaries slightly by introducing bolder schemes and stronger patterns, while still ensuring everything felt practical and liveable.'

The kitchen required a particularly creative approach. 'The cabinetry had to remain, and as it was a bright white, I immediately knew that an emerald green, jewel-toned zellige tile would work beautifully as the splashback,' explains Laura.

Colour was key to ensuring a natural flow between rooms. 'Little Greene's Clay was the thread that tied everything together,' Laura says. The paint has been used in its Light, Mid and Dark forms across the ground floor in a variety of ways, from the walls of the snug to the ceilings of the hallway and living room.

'We also repeated key colours throughout: green appears in both the kitchen and snug, and again in the living room through the Fermoie-print curtains. Yellow accents introduced in the kitchen are echoed in the hallway wallpaper, albeit in a slightly more mustard tone.'

As well as the kitchen cabinets and original flooring running throughout, several treasured pieces remained. 'There were a few pieces of artwork and some antique chairs, which we chose to re-cover and rework, giving them a new lease of life within the updated scheme,' explains Laura.

Alongside these pieces, a new set of homewares brings layers of character. Custom sofas for the living room were designed by Laura Stephens Interiors while other pieces were sourced from brands known for their expert craftsmanship, such as Palefire, Sinclair Till and A Rum Fellow. 'One of my favourite pieces is the large, radius-cornered mirror above the fireplace from Reid & Wright, which adds real presence,' says Laura.

While Laura's clients trusted her judgement, there were moments of hesitation. 'The Caddie colour on the living room walls was definitely a brave choice for them and something they would never have previously considered,' Laura explains. Yet, with some persuasion, and examples of how the shade could work alongside other tones and patterns, the clients agreed to it.

The rich caramel colour and a well-curated mix of old and new now fills the room with warmth, creating a cosy space for the family to unwind. 'What was once an underused and unloved space is now an elegant, welcoming living room,' she says. 'It finally feels like a space the family genuinely wants to spend time in.'

This project is testament to the power colour, pattern and texture have in bringing personality into your home, especially when structural alterations aren't possible. It's a masterclass in how to decorate a Victorian terrace for 21st-century living, allowing period features and proportions to sit happily alongside more contemporary, colourful elements.

item.title: The Mother’s Day gift guide for the mum who has everything – timeless homeware she’ll adore
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst

item.content:encoded:
With Mother’s Day on the horizon, it’s the perfect time to celebrate the women who bring love and warmth into our lives. When searching for Mother’s Day gift ideas, look for the small things that make her daily life a little brighter.

A successful day often comes down to the balance between thoughtful gestures and well-chosen Mother’s Day gifts. Whether it’s breakfast in bed, a handwritten note or a slow morning spent in the garden together, these moments are best paired with a present that fits her lifestyle.

item.title: Tired of trend fatigue? These interior design styles promise lasting elegance and relevance
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: From modernism to traditionalism, this expert-led guide breaks down the iconic styles that define a timeless home.
item.content:encoded:
Finding the right interior design style is about pinning down a look that makes you actually want to spend time in a room. It isn’t about following a strict rulebook; it’s about figuring out what clicks for you so your home feels timeless instead of just trendy for a season.

Most iconic interior design styles fall into two main camps: modern and traditional. But within those themes are more specific styles that represent a particular mood or era. As many designers will tell you, the magic usually happens in the mix; it is quite the norm to blend different looks to create a home that feels balanced and less one-note.

To help you find your footing, we’ve gathered insights from leading British interior designers on eight defining styles. Whether you’re ready for a full-scale renovation or just want a few pieces to refresh a corner, these styles offer clear direction.

Traditional

Few aesthetics are as synonymous with the English home as traditional design. While it’s a style built on history and heritage, it doesn’t have to feel like a museum. Today, the best traditional rooms find a perfect balance between a classic look and 21st-century comfort.

‘Traditional today is about respecting architecture and craftsmanship, but giving it air,’ says Cotswolds-based interior designer Sean Symington. “It’s rooted in heritage, proportion and detail, yet it should still feel relaxed, liveable and relevant to how we actually live now.’

The secret is in the finishing touches. As award-winning interior designer Laura Stephens explains: ‘The details are what truly define it. Window dressings are particularly distinctive with beautifully considered curtains. Joinery is another hallmark: panelled walls, turned legs, carved details and thoughtful architectural mouldings. These elements give traditional interiors their depth and sense of permanence.’

To easily achieve this, look in your own home, aside from furniture, look to textiles and layer floral linens and velvets with pinch-pleat curtains. Adding oil paintings, brass picture frames and sconces with pleated shades provides the depth that Stephens mentions. Smaller touches like hand-painted ceramics, silver-topped jars and embroidered cushions will help the space feel lived in. A patterned rug under a sofa will add the ‘air’ that Symington recommends.

This look is more about just decoration; it’s about the bones of the house, too. Interior designer Henriette von Stockhausen of VSP Interiors believes that scale and symmetry create a framework where you can then layer ‘textiles, pattern, antiques and art to build depth and visual richness.’ For her, the best rooms tell a story, ‘revealing personality, memory and a quiet confidence that comes from authenticity rather than display.’

To keep the look fresh for 2026, Symington suggests mixing furniture from different eras so a room feels ‘alive rather than staged.’ It is this timeless quality that makes the style so popular. As von Stockhausen puts it: ‘Traditional design endures because it is not trend-led. It is built upon proportion, material integrity and craftsmanship - qualities that do not date. Enduring traditional interiors are those that feel settled rather than styled.’

Neptune Cecile Gathered Linen Wall Light

RRP£125
Shop now

Anbôise The Sienna Square Cushion

RRP£95
Shop now

Harrods Fine Bone China Toile Vase

RRP£65
Shop now

Modernism

While traditional design looks to the past, Modernism is all about the power of the present. It’s a style often misunderstood as being cold or empty, but in 2026, the modern home has evolved to feel much more expressive and personal.

‘Modernism today is about clarity and confidence,’ says Carina Raymond, interior designer and founder of Studio Raymond. ‘It’s rooted in architectural integrity - clean lines, strong forms and purposeful design - but in contemporary interiors it has evolved to feel warmer. We use colour drenching and sculptural lighting to soften the poetry of the space.’

In your own home, focus on furniture that feels like sculpture, like a low-slung leather sofa or a honed marble coffee table. Introduce smaller accents like blackened steel bowls, matte ceramic vases and bold block-coloured accessories – think a single cobalt blue tray or an emerald green lacquered box.

To prevent these spaces from feeling soulless, Raymond suggests a careful balance of materials: ‘Natural timber, stone, steel and glass. The key is restraint. We paired smooth architectural surfaces with tactile upholstery to prevent the scheme from feeling cold.’

Even the traditional neutral palette of modern interiors is being reimagined. ‘At Studio Raymond, we often use saturated tones in a tonal, enveloping way. Deep greens, oxbloods or inky blues can feel incredibly modern when applied with discipline. It’s less about the colour itself and more about how confidently it’s used.’

Ultimately, Modernism survives because its foundation is based on logic rather than fashion. As Raymond concludes: ‘Modernism will always endure because it’s rooted in architectural principles rather than trends. It adapts - becoming warmer, bolder or more restrained depending on the moment - but its foundation is timeless.’

Jonathan Adler Cobalt Bowl

RRP£150
Shop now

The Conran Shop Form Curved Mini Vase

RRP£30
Shop now

HAY Arcs Candle Holder

RRP£39
Shop now

Transitional

If traditional design is the heart of the English home and modernism is its architectural edge, transitional design is the bridge between the two. It is a style that doesn’t choose sides, instead finding beauty in the balance between the past and the present.

‘Transitional design is about balance, combining the structure and detail of traditional interiors with the simplicity and clarity of contemporary design,’ explains Sophie Pringle, interior designer and founder of Pringle & Pringle. This sense of harmony is echoed by Alicia Meireles, creative director at OWN LONDON, who defines the looks as adding elegance of classic architecture with the clarity and restraint of contemporary living. It draws on both, creating interiors that feel layered yet calm, timeless yet current.’

To create this ‘conversation’ between eras, mix furniture silhouettes like a clean-lined linen sofa paired with an antique pedestal side table. For smaller accessories, look for textured ceramic lamps with simple shades and modern abstract art housed in traditional gold-leaf frames.

The hallmark of this interior design style is a considered mix of old and new, where cleaner silhouettes are set against traditional architecture. Symington describes it as a style of ‘tension,’ where contemporary touches are paired with classic detailing so that neither feels dominant. ‘Layering pieces from different eras so it never feels one-note,’ he points out, is a deeply personal way to decorate. ‘Artwork, collected objects, things with meaning - that’s what makes it feel comfortable and genuinely lived in.’

To avoid the common misconception that transitional interior design is just safe or undecided, these designers stress the importance of discipline. ‘Transitional interiors succeed when they appear effortless, even though they are underpinned by careful planning,’ says Miereles. Symington agrees, noting that success comes down to intention. ‘Transitional works when there’s a strong starting point and a disciplined edit, so the old and new are in conversation, not competing for attention.’

M&S Splatter Effect Urn Table Lamp

RRP£99
Shop now

Rosie Harbottle The Arrival of Spring A3 Print

RRP£85
Shop now

Oliver Bonas Green Ceramic Gluggle Jug Extra Large

RRP£45
Shop now

Minimalism

Minimalism is often misunderstood as a lack of decor, but in the modern home, it is a deliberate choice to let light and space do the talking. It is a style defined by what is left out as much as what is put in, resulting in a home that feels calm, refined and quietly confident.

‘At its core, minimalism is not about emptiness but about intention,’ says Meireles. ‘Every element is carefully considered, and the absence of visual noise allows proportion, light and materiality to take precedence.’ This style is immediately recognisable by its ‘uninterrupted surfaces’ and ‘seamlessly integrated’ joinery, creating a sense of weightlessness where objects appear carefully placed rather than just accumulated.

To achieve this ‘purity of design,’ the materials must be flawless. Merieles highlights the importance of glass and reflective surfaces to enhance light, along with finishes like polished concrete or plaster. These are favoured in this interior design style because ‘they can be applied without visible trims, grout lines or unnecessary junctions.’

To create this look, your wishlist should focus on a few high-quality hero pieces rather than a room full of accessories. Consider furniture with a structural silhouette - a low-slung modular sofa in a heavy linen or a dining table made from oak or stone. Storage is also your best friend here; look for sleek, handleless sideboards and cupboards that can hide away clutter, leaving only one or two vases or pieces of art on display.

While pure white remains the most iconic colour for this aesthetic, often paired with black for a sharp, modern identity, the success of the room relies on what lies beneath the surface.

The biggest misconception is that this look is simple or inexpensive to create. ‘Achieving true minimal detailing requires rigorous planning and technical coordination. Concealed junctions, integrated lighting and seamless finishes demand precision and craftsmanship. Simplicity often requires more engineering, not less.’

It is this invisible effort that ensures a minimalist home feels like a serene sanctuary rather than a cold gallery.

The White Company Larton Scalloped Marble Bowl

RRP£45
Shop now

Rebecca Udall Pearl Photo Frame

RRP£48
Shop now

The White Company Sandstone Candle Plate

RRP£15
Shop now

Maximilism

If minimalism is about the edit, then maximalism is about the collection. Far from being chaotic, a successful maximalist home is a vibrant celebration of personal history. It is a style that encourages you to surround yourself with things you love, using every surface as an opportunity for expression.

‘Maximilism today is about confident storytelling,’ says Raymond. ‘It’s layered, expressive and immersive, but crucially it’s curated rather than chaotic.’ She suggests using colour drenching - painting a whole room in one saturated tone - to make bold colours feel sophisticated rather than overwhelming. Georgina Cave, founder and creative director of Cave Interiors, agrees, defining the style as ‘intentional abundance. She notes that modern maximalism ‘celebrates individuality through curated collections, rich textures and visual storytelling.’

Building this look in your home requires a fearless approach to textures and objects. Cave recommends a playful mix of materials like ‘velvet or rattan’ or ‘marbles next to woods,’ while filling shelves with books and china. Raymond suggests adding depth by mixing matte and gloss finishes, such as lacquered surfaces paired with heavy linens.

Stephens adds that patterned fabrics are the final essential layer. ‘Pattern is layered thoughtfully, such as small prints combined with larger-scale designs,’ she says. ‘It’s about combining materials so the scheme feels rich rather than flat.’

The biggest misconception is that this style is unconsidered. In reality, it requires a disciplined eye. ‘The strongest schemes are meticulously edited where every object earns its place,’ Raymond states. This authenticity is why the style remains so relevant; it allows a home to feel fun and personal rather than carbon-copied.

Jonathan Adler Basketweave Square Tray

RRP£50
Shop now

OKA Lakadema Leopard Candle Holders

RRP£45
Shop now

Liberty Peacock Garden Rectangular Velvet Cushion

RRP£135
Shop now

Mid-century modern

Mid-century modern remains one of the most popular aesthetics for the contemporary home. Born in the post-war era, it was a movement that prioritised function, flow and an optimistic way of living – principles that feel just as relevant today as they did all those decades ago.

‘Mid-century modern interiors are rooted in the design movements that emerged in the 1950s, combining functional architecture with sculptural form,’ says Meireles. ‘Today, the style continues to feel fresh because it champions clarity of purpose. Furniture and architecture are guided by practicality, yet there is a strong aesthetic identity defined by confident lines.’

To get this look right, focus on ‘honest’ materials that defined the era. Meireles points to warm timbers like teak and walnut as the defining hallmark, often paired with fibreglass or chrome. You can introduce this into the home through smaller, curated accessories – think sculptural ceramic vases in matte, earthy tones, statement wall clocks or spherical glass lighting. Textiles are a great place to introduce the era’s bold palettes. Look for geometric print cushions in mustard, olive or burnt orange and low-pile wool rugs.

This interior design style prioritises ‘purpose-driven design,’ which Meireles believes aligns perfectly with 21st-century values of sustainability. ‘Form was shaped by practicality. This relevance continues today.’ When sourcing, look for hero pieces like a teak sideboard or an arched floor lamp – items that serve a clear daily function while acting as a statement piece.

M&S Chenille Geometric Jacquard Fringed Cushion

RRP£25
Shop now

The White Company Wanstead Portable Table Lamp

RRP£135
Shop now

TOAST Jacquard Star Wool Blanket

RRP£138
Shop now

Art Deco

Art Deco style is an unapologetic celebration of decadence and symmetry. Born in the 1920s and 30s, it brought a sense of theatre to the home through bold shapes and luxury, metallic finishes.

‘Art deco today is a celebration of elegance and cohesion,’ says Marcelina Janiszewska, head of design at Project London. ‘Furniture and interiors are defined by strong lines, both angular and curvaceous, and every piece is considered in relation to the whole.’ David Hartley, senior architect at OWN LONDON, adds that this style is ‘disciplined in its symmetry,’ using ornamentation with ‘control and precision rather than excess.’

To bring this style into your home, focus on high-shine surfaces and bold geometry. Janiszewska suggests signature traits like hexagonal and circular motifs, combined with metallic accents in antiqued brass or bronze. You can introduce this aesthetic through curated accessories – think silk bedcovers or plush velvet cushions and decorative tassels or fringing. These tactile, soft touches provide a welcome contrast to those sharper edges.

Lighting is a vital part of the look. Janiszewska recommends a sculptural geometric fixture or a tiered chandelier to act as a statement piece. For a sophisticated palette, use creams and beiges as a base, contrasted with black, blush pinks or jewel tones.

Many assume that this style must be overly lavish to work, but it is actually very flexible. As Janiszewska explains, ‘a single brass lamp or a sculptural fixture can inject the essence of Art Deco without overwhelming the room.’ This is why the look is still so popular; it allows you to add a few standout pieces that make a space feel full of character.

M&S Monroe Rectangular Wall Mirror

RRP£149
Shop now

Jonathan Adler Basketweave Glassware Set

RRP£135
Shop now

Pooky Calico Front Facing Wall Light

RRP£125
Shop now

Japandi

Creating a home that feels like a quiet sanctuary is the core of the Japandi aesthetic. By combining sleek lines of Japanese design with the rustic warmth of Nordic style, it creates spaces that are clean but never cold. It is a look that values quality over quantity, focusing on a few perfect items rather than a room full of clutter.

‘Japandi is a thoughtful synthesis of Japanese and Scandinavian design principles,’ says Meireles. ‘It is less about aesthetic trends and more about atmosphere, resulting in a space that feels calm, grounded and purposeful.’ Janiszewska agrees, noting that this fusion os styles celebrates ‘thoughtgul living, where every object and surface serves both practical and aesthetic roles.’

To bring this look into your home, focus on natural and tactile materials. Meireles suggests using warm wood, stone and clay to anchor the scheme. To bring this look through curated accessories, look to linen cushions, wool throws and hand-thrown stoneware. When it comes to lighting, a large paper lantern or a wooden floor lamp will give off a soft glow. As for the colour palette, use foundations of cream, beige and taupe, with a few accents of charcoal or deep green to add a gentle contrast.

Many assume this look is just another minimalist trend, but it differs in its focus on comfort and texture. While minimalism can feel ‘austere’ or clinical, Japandi is rooted in the Scandinavian concept of hygge, which prioritises cosiness and well-being. As Meireles explains, ‘true Japandi design is about balance and depth, where simplicity is supported by quality materials and meaningful detail.’

This blend of Japanese ‘wabi-sabi’ (the beauty of imperfection) and Nordic warmth is why this style remains so popular; it creates a mindful home and a sanctuary away from the outside world and busyness of everyday life.

OKA Stonewashed Linen Cushion Cover

RRP£75
Shop now

Nkuku Dharini Linen Lampshade

RRP£175
Shop now

M&S Evelyn Bobbin Side Table

RRP£149
Shop now

Whether you are a maximalist at heart or prefer the quiet of a Japandi retreat, the best homes aren’t built on trends; they’re built on personality. By focusing on quality materials and pieces that tell a story, you can create a home that feels personal and curated for your individual style.

For an even deeper dive into these looks, this book from Amazon is a great resource to have on hand. Beyond the inspiration inside, it’s the kind of book that sits perfectly on a shelf or a curated corner in a home.

item.title: The most inspiring country kitchen ideas for a space that feels collected, cosy and full of personality
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: Create the ultimate country kitchen with design elements that deliver on practicality and purpose, and are full of charm.
item.content:encoded:
The kitchen should be the beating heart of a country home, and the best country kitchen ideas focus on creating a space of conviviality and sociability, a place to gather with family, to cook, entertain and relax in.

This space must be as practical and purposeful as it is stylish, with an aesthetic that reflects the intrinsic beauty and simplicity of rural life. By pairing weathered textures with classic silhouettes, you can design a timeless country kitchen that feels like it has evolved gracefully over decades.

Below, we explore the essential elements involved in creating this look, from classic cabinetry, kitchen mantels, walk-in pantries and clever storage, to natural materials and rustic textures, with expert advice on how to build a space that is hardworking and beautiful.

13 country kitchen ideas for a touch of rustic charm

1. Hide the clutter with a fabric skirt

A fabric skirt is a quintessential element of a country kitchen, offering a soft, tactile alternative to traditional cabinetry. Replacing hard cupboard doors with gathered linen or heavyweight cotton adds a sense of relaxed, lived-in charm while cleverly hiding any unsightly appliances or bulky storage.

This design detail is particularly effective under a Belfast sink or across a freestanding larder. Whether you opt for a classic ticking stripe, a dainty floral or a simple weave, this detail introduces a layer of pattern and movement that breaks up the rigid lines of a fitted kitchen.

This charm can also extend into the utility room, where a linen skirt can conceal the more functional elements of the room. By pairing the textile with discreet tension rods, you can keep sturdy baskets, laundry tins and glass jars tucked away neatly and out of sight.

Scandalo al Sole Hook And Loop Sink Skirt

RRPfrom £36.90
Shop now

VonHaus Seagrass Set of 4 Storage Baskets

RRP£9.99
Shop now

West Elm Round Metal Curtain Rings

RRP£19
Shop now

2. Craft cabinetry and choose enduring surfaces

From heritage detail to rustic simplicity, there is much to consider with cabinetry. Traditional Shaker-style panelled doors have timeless appeal, countertop cupboards add an air of grandeur, while aged wood delivers a crafted, modern-rustic aesthetic.

A kitchen is a long-term investment, and quality is key. Paula Carter, marketing design manager of The White Kitchen Company, advises: 'At the heart of a great country kitchen is beautifully crafted cabinetry. Solid-wood units, built using traditional joinery techniques, offer durability and longevity and can be sanded back and repainted when a refresh is wanted.'

Kitchen spaces work hard, and work surfaces must perform. Fred Horlock, design director of Neptune, suggests, 'Solid-oak worktops bring warmth and beautiful texture to a country-style kitchen, but be mindful that they need regular care, especially around high-use areas like sinks. Light, delicate marbles can add a sense of grandeur to a country kitchen, but remember that real stone needs sealing and a little extra attention.'

If need be, 'try pairing stone on prep areas with oak on an island to create a more 'collected' feel that adds charm and character to a country kitchen,' he adds.

3. Lay characterful flooring

A kitchen floor has to be aesthetically pleasing but perform well, too; after all, muddy dogs and boots are part of country life, so think carefully about the material and colour. Natural stone and earthy, warm terracotta look great, but require some maintenance, explains Louisa Morgan, marketing director at Mandarin Stone: 'Natural stone brings a unique, organic charm that perfectly complements the rustic elegance of country-style interiors, but it does require sealing and a little ongoing care to maintain its beauty.

Porcelain tiles are a low-maintenance, highly durable alternative, resistant to stains, moisture, and wear.' Wood is also an option, particularly for country homes where a degree of elegance is required, and comes in a range of finishes, too. Alex Main, director of The Main Company, says: 'Wood is versatile and complements period homes with ease, adding richness, colour and texture. Reclaimed or hand-aged wood offers a unique surface texture that gets better with age, without compromising on functionality.'

For an extra layer of warmth, high-performance jute or sisal rugs can also work beautifully, providing a rugged, natural texture that stands up to heavy foot traffic.

Casa by JJ Blue Scallop Jute Rug

RRP£192
Shop now

Ruggable Fiore Natural Sage Re-Jute Rug

RRP£179
Shop now

IKEA STRÖG Rug

RRP£35
Shop now

4. Weave in colours and textures

Texture is key in creating country charm in kitchens, and popular finishes to achieve this include riven stone, aged terracotta, wood, and a cluster of wicker accessories. When choosing colours, take inspiration from the bucolic English countryside and opt for pale neutrals, warm ochres, moorland pinks and shades of green.

Country kitchens can have a classic or modern aesthetic, and the devil really is in the detail, so bear this in mind when combining different components to create a visually cohesive design.

Helen Parker, creative director of deVOL Kitchens, advises: 'For a classic country kitchen, I may include wood and stone worktops, aged-brass door furniture, and dark colours on walls and cupboards.'

To opt for a more modern country style, Parker suggests lighter worktops, maybe Carrara marble, lighter colours and a plainer palette, and bringing less contrast but more texture to the whole with natural accessories.'

Dibor Large Woven Veg Hopper

RRP£59.50
Shop now

Cisco & The Sun Stoneware Salt and Pepper Set

RRP£35
Shop now

Cole & Mason 2 Piece Barkway Board Bundle

RRP£65
Shop now

5. Curate clever storage

From larder cupboards and walk-in pantries to open shelving, practical storage options are plentiful. 'Pantries are always popular as there is such pleasure in opening the door to reveal the rows of neat shelving with attractive jars and bottles of produce, all clearly visible and within easy reach, explains Merlin Wright, design director at Plain English Design.

A cupboard alternative would also work, he suggests: 'These days, a pantry can either be a tall cupboard, fitted or freestanding, with wooden racks on the backs of doors and pull-out trays below the counter for products with awkward or less attractive packaging.'

For confined areas, open storage makes sense. 'If everything in a home, including the utilitarian objects, is beautiful, there is no reason to hide things away,' explains Jodey Collorick, creative director at Kelmscott Studio. 'On a more practical note, open shelves often work better than cupboards, disposing of the need to negotiate cupboard doors in a tight space.'

TOAST Speckle Enamel Storage Jar

RRP£25
Shop now

Emma Bridgewater Kitchen Life Utensils Pot

RRP£38
Shop now

M&S Wooden Egg Holder

RRP£10
Shop now

6. Illuminate with layered lighting

Lighting can add character and period elegance, and task lighting is key, particularly if it is adjustable. 'For central island lighting, solid metal shades offer a bold, sculptural silhouette, perfect for making a statement,' advises Sheena Lawrence, co-owner of Jim Lawrence Lighting.

'Alternatively, glass designs provide a timeless solution for honouring a property's heritage without visually interrupting the space's flow. With wall lights, consider adjustable options, like articulated sconces or spotlights, so you can angle the light where it's needed most.'

Combine these with decorative table lamps to create a softer atmosphere. Alice Moylan, founder of Beauvamp Studio, says, 'I love to add a touch of softer lighting to a country kitchen with a table lamp, creating a bit more ambience. As long as you are careful to keep any electrical cables and bulbs away from the sink or cooking appliances, these little touches can really add character and style to your country space.'

OKA Lucas Wall Lamp with Shade

RRP£150
Shop now

Rowen & Wren Barnaby Brass Table Lamp

RRP£124
Shop now

The White Company Lansdown Glass Triple Pendant

RRP£295
Shop now

7. Gather around a farmhouse table or island

Everyone convenes around the kitchen table, so think practically when selecting - from compact, antique, scrubbed top tables to modern extendable designs, there are plenty of options. Consider, too, what seating will work best, from classic carver chairs that require more room to benches that can be neatly tucked underneath.

If there is room, add an island. 'A well-planned kitchen island can transform your space into the heart of the home. Add an overhang and a few stools, and it becomes a natural gathering spot — ideal for casual breakfasts, evening chats, or helping with homework while dinner simmers,' says Paula Carter, marketing design manager of The White Kitchen Company.

Remember, practicality is key - open shelving at the ends or underneath provides easy access to cookbooks, baskets or mixing bowls, all while adding to the relaxed, lived-in feel that makes a country kitchen so special. It's the balance of beauty and utility that makes a kitchen not just a room, but a favourite place to gather.

8. Showcase with a dresser or sideboard

A dresser has long been considered a key piece of any country kitchen, offering the perfect combination of closed cupboard storage, drawers and utility space with open shelving on top, as well as creating a standalone focal point that is full of heritage charm.

Antique dressers have an attractive, timeworn aesthetic, but can be sizeable, so for compact kitchens or those with an open-plan dining area, consider a slender-proportioned sideboard instead.

'A sideboard can set the country tone and ground a space with quiet presence,' explains Paul Staden, founder and creative director of Pilgrim House. 'Blending timeless proportions with clean lines, refined, Shaker-style designs offer a contemporary yet classic look that exudes country charm and sophistication. Versatile pieces, they are also great workhorses for storing table linen, china or even everyday essentials.'

9. Pepper the room with antiques

Integrating antiques is the ultimate way to ground a country kitchen, stripping away the sterile feel of a new installation and replacing it with a sense of history. From a scrubbed pine farmhouse table to a weathered butcher’s block, these storied pieces provide a tactile connection to the past.

‘I love incorporating vintage items into a kitchen,’ says Rebecca Hughes, founder and creative director of Rebecca Hughes Interiors. ‘Mixing furniture from a range of periods is a brilliant way to create a layered look, making a kitchen feel like it has organically evolved over time.’

Smaller ‘found’ objects like copper pans and stoneware pots displayed on open shelving and hooks can also add layers of character.

DEMMEX Solid Copper Saucepan

RRP£104.80
Shop now

Victor Black Kitchen Scales

RRP£95
Shop now

TOAST Willow Pottery Cutlery Drainer

RRP£38
Shop now

10. Anchor the room with a range cooker

A range cooker lies at the heart of any country kitchen, and size can be a deciding factor, as Graham Duke, sales director at Everhot, explains: 'Range cookers - from smaller models which fit even the cosiest of spaces, through to powerhouse-size designs- remain a timeless and stunning focal point in any kitchen.'

Developments in technology mean a range cooker today will work harder all around. 'The latest models have Wi-Fi control and smart tariff integration, minimising energy usage and seamlessly integrating with renewable energy sources,' Duke adds.

Look out for those with extra functions, too. 'Modern range cookers often boast multi-functional cavities, including high-tech functions like AirFry, and Steam & Infuse cooking accessories, advises Sarah Whitfield, product manager at Belling and Stoves.

11. Highlight original features in a country kitchen

A true country kitchen is defined by its embrace of tradition, built on a foundation of craftsmanship and hard-wearing materials that only improve with age. For those lucky enough to have original details, the rule is simple: don’t cover them up. Let exposed brick, beams or stone walls sing, as their earthy textures provide a natural backdrop.

As Tom Howley, creative design director of the eponymous British kitchen company, explains, ‘a true country kitchen is all about warmth and authenticity. Classic details like Shaker-style cabinetry, exposed brick or stone walls, butcher block countertops and a butler sink create a space that feels both timeless and lived in, comfortable, practical and full of character.’

Integrating these country kitchen ideas, from the ceramic curves of a butler sink to the rugged patina of masonry, ensures the room functions as a hardworking, modern family kitchen without sacrificing its historic character.

12. Bring the outdoors in

No country kitchen is complete without bringing a little piece of the outdoors indoors. Whether your garden is home to a border of ready-to-cut flowers or a wildflowers meadow, you can add an instant feel of the country to your kitchen by placing them in a beautiful vase to act as a centrepiece on a large, rustic table or island.

The White Company Coombe Frill Vase

RRP£40
Shop now

M&S Large Twist Handle Ceramic Vase

RRP£35
Shop now

TOAST Rob Towler Slipware Bud Vase

RRP£32
Shop now

13. Decorate country kitchen walls with decorative plates

Add a dash of farmhouse style to your kitchen by decorating walls with a collection of beautiful plates. A popular way to decorate kitchens in the 50s and 60s, this trend has since seen a resurgence in popularity. Opt for plates with hand-painted details and floral motifs for a country cottage feel.

HAY La Pittura Ceramic Plate

RRP£49
Shop now

Pottery Barn Chambray Tile Stoneware Appetiser Plates

RRP£36
Shop now

Villa Bologna Personalised Monogram Plate

RRP£85
Shop now

Whether you are planning a full-scale renovation or simply looking to refresh your current setup with a few heritage-inspired touches - perhaps starting with a classic whistling kettle like this one from Smeg - these elements provide a roadmap for creating an enduring and welcoming scheme.

For those working with more compact footprints, discover how to maximise your space with our guide to small kitchen ideas, where you can still incorporate many of these tips to create a small kitchen with plenty of country charm.

item.title: I wasn’t planning to romanticise Easter this year, then Emma Bridgewater made me fall in love with nostalgia all over again – the pieces I cannot live without
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: From a return of the cult-favourite Mini Eggs print to charming new Hot Cross Bun patterns, this is the nostalgic kitchen refresh we’ve been waiting for.
item.content:encoded:
Lately I have been drawn to objects, colours, and patterns that feel familiar and comforting. In a world that often feels fast and fleeting, revisiting the charm of the past makes our homes feel warmer, more personal, and full of meaning.

As we hurtle towards Spring, I have been thinking about Easter table decorating ideas and discovered the Emma Bridgewater Easter collection. The cheerful patterns and timeless charm perfectly capture the joy of tradition while showing how nostalgia can feel fresh and meaningful again.

I am besotted with the new mini eggs collection, and I predict this will sell out fast, so I have stocked up, particularly as oftentimes Emma Bridgewater collections that sell out are never to be seen again. I’ve also spotted this Charbonnel et Walker Easter Bunny with Milk Chocolate Mini Eggs from Selfridges, which would be the perfect party favour to keep the speckled egg theme going.

Shop the collection

Mini Eggs 1/2 Pint Mug

RRP£26
Shop now

Egg Hunt Medium Pasta Bowl

RRP£28
Shop now

Hot Cross Buns Medium Dinner Plate

RRP£25
Shop now

Mini Eggs 2 Mug Teapot

RRP£50
Shop now

Easter Toast Mini Eggs Tea Towel

RRP£13
Shop now

Mini Eggs Runner

RRP£40
Shop now

The real charm of this collection lies in its almost child-like playfulness, the designs feel less like formal dinnerware and more like a joyful celebration of spring. To keep that spirit alive when styling, I’m ditching the stuffy, formal place settings in favour of a more effortless look, styling some plain linen napkins (upon the Mini Eggs table runner) with these felt animal decorations from The White Company as napkin rings.
