It’s a style that perfectly captures the ‘nostalgiacore’ aesthetic that Charlotte Dunford, houses editor at The English Home, identifies as a staple of the British home. ‘For me, Easter brings back fond childhood memories of hot cross buns served warm from the oven, slathered in butter and always on the same little plate adorned with bunnies and springtime flowers," Charlotte says.

‘While the ‘nostalgiacore’ trend is doing the rounds, it is far from a passing craze. There has always been an element of nostalgia in classic English interiors—a desire to bring the past into the present and fill our homes with pieces that speak to us and offer warmth and comfort.’

‘Upon seeing the creamware crockery in the new seasonal collection from Emma Bridgewater, I was immediately transported back to those childhood days,’ Charlotte continues. ‘With patterns ranging from traditional hot cross buns to folksy, sponge pattern eggs, these colourful designs have a charming appeal. I can easily picture them peppered amongst a melange of mismatched crockery on a country kitchen dresser, ready to be enjoyed time and time again.’ Her top pick for a classic country look is the Hot Cross Bun plate, an essential for any country kitchen ideas moodboard.

Of course, with such whimsical designs, the danger is that the table can quickly feel a little too ‘twee’. To keep the look elevated, avoid a perfectly matched set, and go for a mismatched look, pairing them with your usual understated, everyday crockery.

This new collection is ready to shop now on the Emma Bridgewater website, but you’d better hurry, with limited runs, they are bound to become future collectors’ items. Looking for more seasonal inspiration? Pair your new pieces with this classic Easter biscuit recipe.

item.title: 15 easter table decorating ideas designers swear by for a beautifully styled spring table
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst, Charlotte Dunford
item.description: Discover top tips and Easter table decorating ideas from the experts to make your tablescape the star of the show this spring.
item.content:encoded:
Spring is the season for gathering, and there is no better time to embrace the art of hosting.

Creating a memorable setting starts with a thoughtful foundation, and exploring various Easter decorating ideas allows you to really celebrate the themes of the season of new beginnings, seasonal blooms, pretty pastels and bunny motifs in tow.

Whether you’re hosting family and friends for a big Sunday lunch, an evening dinner party or a relaxed brunch, we’ve curated an edit of our favourite Easter table decorating ideas for our celebrations.

15 easter table decorating ideas

As we transition into a new season, the focus shifts from cosy interiors to the bright and communal feeling that arrives with spring. It’s a moment that calls for celebration and a perfectly dressed table to welcome friends and family.

‘I’ll take any excuse to make a table beautiful, and Easter is no exception. It’s such a hopeful time of year - the phrase “full of the joys of Spring” feels entirely apt here,’ says interior designer Katherine Pooley. Here we share 15 ideas to impress your guests and make this Easter one to remember.

1. Layer mix-and-match linens

Table linens are an effective way to add pattern, colour and texture and can be the perfect finishing touch to your tablescape. 'Mixing and matching layers of tablecloths, placemats, and napkins signals to your dinner companion of one or the heaving table of twenty that you’ve been looking forward to them coming and what lies ahead,' says Tori Murphy, founder of the eponymous English textile brand.

Liberty Mitsi Wiltshire Blossom Double-Sided Scallop Placemat

RRP£45
Shop now

M&S Cotton with Linen Gingham Ruffle Tablecloth

RRP£45
Shop now

The White Company Esme Napkins

RRP£35
Shop now

2. Add a whimsical touch with Easter table decorations

From velvet bunnies to fluffy chicks to miniature eggs, there’s a whole host of Easter table decor available to bring a fun, seasonal touch to your decorating scheme. 'You can have so much fun with pastel colours, flowers and Easter décor at this time of year,' explains Ellie Moore, founder of Dress For Dinner. 'We love decorating with pastel bunnies and nests of eggs to delight guests.'

Dunelm Twig Tree with Pack of 12 Floral Hanging Egg Decorations

RRP£25
Shop now

Talking Tables Set Of Five Bunny Table Decorations

RRP£14.95
Shop now

The White Company White and Yellow Table Chick Cosies

RRP£25
Shop now

3. Opt for seasonal-inspired tableware

For elegant Easter table decorating ideas, complement linens and floral arrangements with spring-themed crockery. 'Don’t be afraid to fully embrace the occasion,' says Rebecca Udall, founder of the eponymous British lifestyle brand. 'Ruffled tablecloths and napkins, cabbage crockery and textured placemats and chargers all touch on the right side of kitsch – they make a table feel more relaxed.'

Emma Bridgewater Mini Eggs Flat Serving Plate

RRP£60
Shop now

M&S Cabbage Large Serving Bowl

RRP£15
Shop now

Emma Bridgewater Windblown Daffodils Set Of 2 Napkins

RRP£22
Shop now

4. Pepper an Easter table with seasonal blooms

If you’re looking for simple Easter decorating ideas, try incorporating spring flowers into your tablescape. 'Keep flowers seasonal and slightly wild. Easter is about new growth, and I really like including planted blooms on my tables at easter that are in the early stages of growth, such as narcissi and hellebores,' says Hattie Hansard, interior designer at Joanna Wood. 'Placing blossom branches and pussy willow in large vases also works beautifully. Avoid anything too tight or formal.'

For a more curated look, consider placing small bud vases along the table. 'A few stems of ranunculus, daffodils, tulips or cow parsley in individual glass vessels create movement and delicacy without blocking conversation,' says Lucy Pateman, founder of The Table Stylist. 'This scattered style feels relaxed and allows each guest to enjoy the flowers up close.'

Rebecca Udall Violet Bud Vase Trio

RRP£35
Shop now

HAY Blue Splash Roll Neck Medium Vase

RRP£59
Shop now

Sass & Belle Warm Toned Mini Bud Vases

RRP£10
Shop now

5. Choose uplifting patterns with a spring feel

Fresh and fun, gingham is the ideal choice for spring interiors and Easter decorating. 'We suggest selecting table linens in your chosen colour scheme and adding these to your table to create a beautiful foundation,' says Kate Fairlie, founder and director of Truffle Tablescapes. 'This year, as with last year, ginghams are big news and are perfect for Easter. A stylish gingham tablecloth, placemats and/or napkins (layer up or choose one hero piece) in a pretty spring colour are a great way to form the basis of your Easter tablescape.'

Dusk Gabriella Set of 4 Gingham Napkins

RRP£10
Shop now

Lovely Linen Pure European Linen Check Table Runner

RRP£38
Shop now

100% Cotton Light Blue White Plaid Gingham Ruffled Table Cloth

RRP£30.84
Shop now

6. Personalise with delicate stationery

Incorporate charming menus and place names to bring a sense of occasion to your Easter feast; opting for relaxed textures and soft scripts keeps the setting feeling intimate rather than overly formal.

'A handwritten place card, illustrated menu, or even a simple tag tied with ribbon or twine adds personality and charm while helping the table feel thoughtfully composed,' says Lucy. 'I often like to use textured natural paper, or cards edged with a delicate brush of colour to echo the palette of the table. For Easter, small details such as hand-drawn florals, scalloped edges or a tiny pressed flower can make menu cards feel beautifully seasonal.'

Papier Spring Daffodils Menus

RRPfrom £29.50
Shop now

Smythson Scalloped Place Cards

RRP£25
Shop now

Aliroo Design Easter Carrot Place Setting

RRPfrom £3.50
Shop now

7. Dress your table with fresh colours and light materials

'There’s something so fun about creating an Easter table. With Christmas and other occasions, it’s about drama and rich colourings, but there’s a freshness and a feeling of lightheartedness that radiates from a spring table,' says Gemma Martinez de Ana, creative director at Bonadea.

'Keep the palette and colours fresh and uplifting. Adding a lot of green foliage but keeping the flowers quite neutral helps to bring that feeling of new life and growth. Again with the linens, I veer towards ginghams, stripes and patterns all in a pastel shade, nodding to the summer season ahead and the restorative freshness of spring.'

Rebecca Udall Colette Striped Linen Tablecloth

RRP£280
Shop now

Dunelm Spring Floral Table Runner

RRP£8
Shop now

OKA Tulipe Tablecloth

RRP£145
Shop now

8. If your home is a neutral haven, opt for muted yet fun decorations

For neutral homes, going all-out with bright or pastel colours that are synonymous with this festive period may seem a little frightening. Instead, opt for Easter decorations that celebrate a calming colour palette. Focus on layering creams, soft oatmeals, and warm stones through your linens to create a sophisticated base that feels seasonally appropriate without disrupting the scheme of your home.

M&S 2 Pack Raffia Woven Bunnies

RRP£10
Shop now

The White Company Ribbed Glass Egg Bunny Decorations

RRP£20
Shop now

Lights4fun 15cm Artificial Easter Egg Nest

RRP£10.99
Shop now

9. Impress guests with a seasonal centrepiece

If you’re looking for DIY Easter decoration ideas, a seasonal centrepiece could be the perfect choice. From ceramic eggs displayed in a large pedestal bowl to a bouquet of cut flowers fresh from the garden, the options are endless. 'Easter is a time when you can really have some fun with your tablescaping,' says Suzy Humphreys, founder of Layered Lounge.

'Each year, I place a vase of beautiful faux flowers in the middle of the table and hang our elegant carved wooden eggs on them. Using faux flowers instead of branches adds an extra element of interest, and the eggs are great if you prefer a neutral colour palette in the home.'

10. Layer in soft lighting

Do not wait for the sun to set to illuminate your display; layering different light sources adds a sophisticated depth to your tablescape, regardless of the hour. By mixing traditional waxes with modern lighting, you can ensure your setting feels cosy and considered, no matter the spring weather.

'I like to introduce candlelight, or wireless lamps down the table, even at lunch,' says Hattie.' Candles add warmth and atmosphere, and at Easter, you still cannot be sure of sunny weather, silver candlesticks with tapered cream candles make it feel like a special occasion.'

Addison Ross Sage Lacquer Rechargeable Bobbin LED Lamp

RRP£125
Shop now

Pottery Barn Easter Egg Candles – Mixed Set of 4

RRP£59
Shop now

M& Set of 4 Pastel Dinner Candles

RRP£6
Shop now

11. Pick linens and tableware in sunshine hues

For a bright, cheery Easter table decorating scheme, opt for a palette of bold sunshine yellows. 'Inject some springtime happiness and egg hunting joy through colour and pattern,' says Lucy Hood, co-founder of Tableday. 'This is easily achieved on a tablescape by coordinating your napkins, candles, and flowers in a bright, cohesive colour scheme. Yellow is our go-to for the happiest of Spring colours.'

12. Decorate with saturated tones

Take inspiration from the bold yellows of spring daffodils, vivid pinks of camellias and bright blues of forget-me-nots for your Easter tablescape. 'Spring signifies renewal and the joy of seeing nature bloom in vibrant colour,' says Farheen Allsopp, co-founder of Faro Home. 'This Easter, we have created frills and ruffles, swathes of dip-dyed pink with brightly coloured glassware, hand-painted candlesticks and ornaments for a pretty, elegant and unique tablescape.'

Whether you want to go bold or keep it more understated, Sara Miller, founder of the eponymous British lifestyle brand, notes that colour is the ultimate mood-setter. 'From vivid tulips in full bloom to pastel hues, Easter is characterised by colourful splendour. Adding pops of bright, vibrant colour to your decor can help create a welcoming atmosphere,' she says. 'But, even if you're a little more cautious with colour, you can still incorporate bright accents subtly - from your napkins or your salad servers, through to your olive oil vessels and butter dish.'

13. Take the lead from seasonal fauna

For a fun Easter table decorating idea, try incorporating animal-themed decorations and tableware into your scheme. 'When it comes to an Easter table, we’re all about letting animals take centre stage,' says Rosie Axford, founder and marketing director at Wicklewood. 'With a Wicklewood twist — swap chicks and bunnies for our farm yard candlesticks and iconic frog and rooster pitchers and embrace the menagerie!'

The Best Room Hand Painted Bunnies Ceramic Candle Holders

RRP£24.95
Shop now

The White Company Felt Spring Animal Decorations

RRP£35
Shop now

Villa Bologna Yellow Polka Dot Bunny

RRP£25
Shop now

14. Style with seasonal produce and celebrate nature’s bounty

Embrace the beauty of the garden by letting fresh ingredients take centre stage. Instead of traditional floral arrangements, look to the vegetable patch for a unique take.

‘I love using what’s in season for the styling itself. Fresh vegetables like artichokes, asparagus, leeks and heritage carrots bring such a beautiful shape and colour to an Easter table,’ says Lucy. ‘They can be styled as relaxed centrepieces, tied into simple bundles for each place setting or arranged in bowls as edible decoration.’

15. Bring the outdoors in

To truly capture the essence of the season, look beyond the tabletop and consider the wider environment. Creating a seamless flow between your home and the natural world helps the setting feel alive and expansive. ‘Whenever possible, make use of natural light and views of the garden or countryside,’ says Lucy. ‘Even in cooler weather, a table near a window with fresh greenery can capture the spirit of spring.’

Once you’ve tried your hand at decorating the perfect Easter table, remember that this celebration is all about spending time together. ‘Guests remember how a gathering feels as much as how it looks. Prepare what you can in advance, keep menus seasonal and uncomplicated, and allow space for relaxed conversation,’ adds Lucy.

So, if you’re looking for recipe inspiration, try baking these delicious traditional biscuits to gift on your Easter table as seasonal favours or this slow roast lamb for a show-stopping centrepiece.

item.title: What really inspired Wuthering Heights – tragedy, trauma and the violent destruction of the English idyll
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton
item.description: Here, we look at why the historic context of Wuthering Heights is more important, and much darker, than you could have imagined
item.content:encoded:
Behind the bodice-ripping romance of Emerald Fennell’s new Wuthering Heights, brought to life vehemently by Margot Robbie and Jacob Elordi, there lies a salient commentary on Victorian society that, when enjoyed with a glass of wine and a gaggle of girlfriends, is in danger of being missed.

Of course, Fennell’s adaptation of Emily Brontë’s beloved story can and should be enjoyed as a standalone piece of art, thanks to its maximalist, dream-like aesthetic and passionate acting of the principal actors.

But, a little historic context will help you understand the story and the novel for what it really is - a brilliant, ahead-of-its-time reflection of Victorian social class, and the massive, cataclysmic effects of the Industrial Revolution on rural Britain.

Of course, Emily’s own short and tragic life played into her storytelling, too. To get the most comprehensive understanding of Emily’s passions, experiences and influences, this biography by Winifred Gerin is considered to be the best; an impressive feat for a figure who, despite being one of Britain’s most famous novelists, led a frustratingly mysterious life.

Emily Brontë, whose life was plagued by tragedy after he mother died when she was very young, first published her debut and singular novel in 1847. The story is actually set more than 50 years earlier, spanning 1771-1802, harking back to the heyday of the Romantic movement in literature and art - a calculated choice on her behalf.

So what was happening at the time Emily was writing Wuthering Heights, and did it influence her?

Well, the short answer is, yes, of course it did. A good novel is never just a good story, though it can be enjoyed as such, and Wuthering Heights is unremovable from its historical and social context.

The story’s timeline overlaps with the beginnings of the Industrial Revolution and the expansion of the British Empire, enormous events in the 19th-century, that had massive, irreversible consequences on British society.

Set during the shift from agriculture to industry, the novel explores the decline of the traditional landed gentry (the Earnshaws) and the rise of the working-class, self-made man (Heathcliff).

Although Brontë’s native swirling, haunting Yorkshire Moors where Wuthering Heights was set, and where filming for Fennell’s adaptation took place (see the real-life filming locations here), seem far removed from the sooty factories of the Industrial Revolution, they were actually close to its heartlands. The now tranquil town of Haworth, where the Brontë sisters lived, the daughters of a Clergyman, and where the Bronte Parsonage Museum is now, had a death rate that equalled that of London in 1853, with the average age of death just 24. Brontë herself died of Tuberculosis, aged just 30.

In the novel, Brontë uses the stark contrast between the two main houses, the poor, dark, groaning walls of Wuthering Heights and the grand, colourful Thrushcross Grange, to reflect the societal, economic and class shifts that the Industrial Revolution brought about, and her view of them as destructive of rural society,.

She makes commentary on the immorality of wealth and the rich; wealthy characters are snobbish and badly portrayed in the novel, and, despite her hopes, Catherine’s lot in life is not improved by marrying Edgar. In the story, the eventual triumph of a new generation - the young Cathy and Hareton - reflects Brontë’s desire for a blending of old rural traditions with the benefits of new ‘civilised’ values.

Heathcliff’s mysterious origins have a point too. He was picked up in the great industrial city of Liverpool, black-haired and speaking ‘some gibberish’. Liverpool was famed for its Transatlantic slave trade until about 1807, and historians of the novel have often linked these two events together. In the novel, however, he is also guessed to be ‘a little Lascar’ (an Indian sailor or military servant), a ‘gipsy brat,’or an ‘American or Spanish castway’, all of which could have been possible in such an international city, with maritime links across the empire.

The novel’s late 18th/early-19th-century setting ties in with the Gothic tradition and Romantic movement popular at that time, reflected in its themes of death, the wild, isolated settings and even the supernatural. Brontë has created the character of the dark, mysterious Heathcliff and his obsessive, destructive passion as a quintessentially Byronic hero.

And what female novelist of the 19th-century wrote a novel with a female protagonist without making a comment on the status of women in society? Wuthering Heights highlights how marriage was used as an economic transaction to secure property, leaving women in a vulnerable, submissive position. Cathy has to choose her husband based on her social upbringing, rather than the man she loves and you can guess Emily Brontë’s opinion of that because, no spoilers, it doesn’t work out very well for her. Brontë, of course, never married.

Emily Brontë challenged traditional, narrow Victorian minds with her novel, which is probably why she published it first under a male pseudonym, and it was met with outrage and comments on its ‘vulgar depravity and unnatural horrors.’ But still she did it, and what a feat that was.

Although the point of Emerald Fennell’s Wuthering Heights adaptation is certainly more Gothic pleasure than societal commentary, and don’t get me wrong, I love it for that, an understanding of its historic context brings a whole new level of meaning to the story. Fennell cleverly weaves these ideas into her film that you may not have noticed otherwise, from the rich splendour of Cathy’s married life to the dark, brooding world of Heathcliff’s, and I think Emily Brontë would have loved it, really.

item.title: Le Creuset has just unveiled a Mediterranean-inspired new colour – and it might be the dreamiest shade the brand has ever released
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: Ditch the winter palette and lean into the bright: Bleu Riviera is the coastal-inspired cure for a dull countertop.
item.content:encoded:
Le Creuset fans are famously always on the edge of their seats waiting for the next big hue to drop, and the latest arrival might just be their most wanderlust-inducing yet.

If your kitchen, like mine, has been mirroring the grey English weather, Le Creuset’s newest launch is here to change the forecast. For those looking for the best non-toxic cookware to invest in, the pioneer of colourful cookware has officially unveiled its new Colour of the Season: Bleu Riviera. If you’ve found yourself longing for the discontinued fan-favourite Caribbean Blue, then Le Creuset’s new Colour of the Season launch will certainly get you excited.

While I was already swooning over this deep, mossy green Thyme mug - which I added straight to my basket - this morning’s reveal has me dreaming of an entirely different landscape, launching today, this spirited blue-green shade transported me back to my Amalfi coast holiday, a bold turquoise shade that can only be found tucked away in a sun-drenched Mediterranean cove.

Shop the collection

Cast Iron Round Casserole

RRP£355
Shop now

Stoneware Heritage Rectangular Dish

RRP£55
Shop now

Stoneware Mug

RRP£17
Shop now

Cast Iron Shallow Casserole

RRP£275
Shop now

Stoneware Signature Storage Jar

RRP£34
Shop now

Stoneware Petite Casserole

RRP£26
Shop now

It seems I'm not the only one feeling the pull of the ocean. Alison Davidson, editor of The English Home, is also championing the return of coastal tones. ‘After a few years in the doldrums, teal is having a resurgence and is perfect for this time of year when we are all looking ahead to holidays and turquoise seas,’ she notes. ‘This vibrant blue/green, aptly named Bleu Riviera, will add a wonderful pop of colour to your kitchen.’

While the shade is bold, it feels incredibly soulful in the home. I love seeing bright tones like this pop against white tiles and sandy linens for a beach house look, or paired with yellows and corals for a high-energy, maximalist vibe.

Spanning Le Creuset’s most beloved shapes, the star of the show has got to be the Dutch Oven, but it is at a higher price point, so for those wanting to inject a touch of teal into their kitchenware, this teal Dutch Oven from Amazon is very similar, but a fraction of the price.

item.title: 8 must-try pancake recipes for Shrove Tuesday – from zesty lemon to savoury bakes
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: Flip, stack, and savour these delicious Pancake Day 2026 recipes.
item.content:encoded:
There is a simple, nostalgic joy in the quiet sizzle of a better hitting the pan and the sweet aroma of citrus, maple syrup, chocolate spread and more as every indulgent topping fills the home.

It comes but one day a year, and while there is nothing saying you can’t enjoy them year-round, Pancake Day reminds us all of this much-loved tradition. Whether you prefer the nostalgia of a simple sugar-dusted classic or want to experiment with savoury, Shrove Tuesday is the perfect excuse to gather in the kitchen

From thin English crêpes to fluffy Japanese stacks, our guide features eight recipes to suit every taste. Before you start, remember, the secret to a perfect flip starts with precision, so we recommend measuring your ingredients with a reliable set of scales, like this set of digital kitchen scales from Amazon, in a charming butter-yellow hue.

Classic Crépe Recipe

Ingredients

  • 110g/4oz plain flour, sifted

  • 2 large free range eggs

  • 75ml/3fl oz water

  • Caster sugar

  • Lemon wedges

  • Pinch of salt

  • 200ml/7fl oz milk

  • Fresh lemon juice

Method

  1. Sift the flour and salt into a large mixing bowl with a sieve, make a well in the centre of the flour and break the eggs into it. Then begin whisking the eggs. Next, gradually add small quantities of the milk and water mixture, still whisking. The batter should eventually be smooth, with the consistency of thin cream.

  2. Now melt the 50g/2oz of butter in a pan. Spoon 2 tbsp of it into the batter and whisk it in, then pour the rest into a bowl and use it to lubricate the pan before you make each pancake.

  3. Proceed by getting the pan really hot, then turn the heat down to medium. 2 tbsp of batter is about the right amount for an 18cm/7in saucepan- use your Falcon ladle so it can be poured into the hot pan in one go.

  4. As soon as the batter hits the hot pan, tip it around from side to side to get the base evenly coated. It should take only half a minute or so to cook; then flip the pancake over with a pan slice or palette knife - the other side will need a few seconds only - then simply slide it out of the pan onto a plate.

  5. To serve, sprinkle each pancake with freshly squeezed lemon juice and caster sugar, fold in half, then in half again to form triangles, or else simply roll them up. Serve with extra sections of lemon.

Recipe sourced from Falcon Enamelware.

Must-haves for the perfect classic crépe

From achieving a silky-smooth batter to extracting every drop of zesty flavour, these essential tools are the secret to mastering the timeless lemon-and-sugar classic.

Dualit Hand Mixer

RRP£89.99
Available from Amazon
Shop now

M&S Enamel 24cm Large Mixing Bowl

RRP£12
Shop now

Dunelm Handheld Juicer

RRP£6
Available from Dunelm
Shop now

Classic American Pancakes

Ingredients

  • 130g plain flour, sieved

  • 1 tsp baking powder

  • ½ tsp salt

  • 2 tbsp golden caster sugar

  • 130ml whole milk

  • 1 large egg, lightly beaten

  • 2 tbsp melted butter (allowed to cool slightly), plus extra for cooking

  • Maple syrup

  • Handful of berries

Method

  1. Place the flour, baking powder, salt and caster sugar into a large bowl. In a separate bowl, lightly whisk together the milk and egg, then whisk in the melted butter.

  2. Pour the milk mixture into the flour mixture and, using a fork, beat until you have a smooth batter. Allow the batter stand for a few minutes.

  3. Heat a non-stick frying pan over a medium heat and add a knob of butter. When the butter has melted, add a ladle of batter.

  4. Once the top of the pancake begins to bubble, turn it over and cook until both sides are golden brown and the pancake has risen. Repeat until all the batter is used up.

  5. Serve with a drizzle of maple syrup and berries, or your favourite toppings of choice.

Recipe sourced from Miele.

Fluffy Japanese Soufflé Pancakes

Ingredients

  • 150g self-raising flour

  • ½ tsp baking powder

  • ¼ tsp bicarbonate of soda

  • ¼ tsp sea salt

  • 1tbsp vegetable oil

  • 180ml whole milk

  • 2 eggs, divided

  • 2tbsp. caster sugar

  • ¼ tsp. white wine vinegar

  • Oil spray

Method

  1. Combine the flour, baking powder, bicarbonate of soda and salt in a bowl. Add the milk, egg yolks, and oil, whisk well and set aside until needed.

  2. Using an electric whisk, start whisking the whites and the vinegar, and when frothy, add the sugar a little at a time until fully incorporated.

  3. Gently fold in the whites into the batter and set aside.

  4. Using a large non-stick frying pan over a low heat, grease two non-stick crumpet rings and place them in the pan. Fill the rings about two-thirds full with the pancake batter, put a lid on and cook for about 10 minutes on a very low heat.

  5. Flip the rings very carefully and cook for an extra two minutes.

  6. When ready, keep warm and serve with cream and maple syrup, or whipped coconut cream, ground cardamom, passion fruit and coconut chips.

Recipe sourced from Miele.

Gluten-free and Vegan Banana and Berry Pancakes

Ingredients

  • 145g jumbo oats

  • 300ml oat milk

  • 1 ripe banana

  • 2 tbsp maple syrup

  • 1 tsp baking powder

  • Coconut oil (to cook)

Toppings:

  • Coconut yoghurt

  • Blueberries

  • Raspberries

  • Strawberries

  • Pecans

  • Desiccated coconut

Method

  1. Mash the banana and mix with the oat milk and maple syrup.

  2. Place the oats into a food processor or blender along with the baking powder and blitz until the oats are a flour consistency.

  3. Slowly add the banana mixture to the oats and blitz until combined.

  4. Heat a little coconut oil in a cast iron pan over a low heat.

  5. Use a ladle to spoon out one scoop of the pancake batter.

  6. Allow to cook for 1-2 minutes then use the wooden spatula to flip the pancake and cook for a further 1-2 minutes on the other side

  7. Repeat until all the batter is used. To serve, top with coconut yoghurt, blueberries, raspberries, strawberries, pecans and desiccated coconut and enjoy!

Recipe sourced from STAUB and Naturally.B.

Salmon and Avocado Crêpes

Ingredients

  • 150g plain white flour or gluten-free flour

  • 3 eggs

  • 320ml oat milk

  • 50ml water

  • 15g butter (melted and cooled)

  • Pinch of salt

Toppings:

  • Spinach

  • Smoked salmon

  • 1 avocado

  • Plain yoghurt

  • Chilli flakes

  • Fresh coriander

  • Juice of half a fresh lemon

Method

  1. Add the flour and salt to a bowl and mix until combined.

  2. In a separate bowl, add the eggs and beat well.

  3. Add the oat milk, water and butter to the eggs and whisk together.

  4. Pour the egg mixture into the flour mixture a little at a time and whisk until a smooth batter is formed.

  5. Cover the batter with cling film and place in the fridge to chill for 1 hour.

  6. Remove the batter from the fridge and stir slightly.

  7. Heat a little butter to grease a cast iron pan over a low heat.

  8. Use a ladle to spoon out one scoop of the batter and use the wooden spreader to create a circular crepe shape.

  9. Cook for 1 to 2 minutes until the edges turn golden and use the wooden spatula to help flip.

  10. Cook for a further 30 seconds.

  11. Repeat until all the batter is used.

  12. To serve, top with the spinach, smoked salmon, slices of avocado, plain yoghurt, a sprinkle of chilli flakes, some fresh coriander and a squeeze of fresh lemon juice and enjoy!

Recipe sourced from STAUB and Natually.B.

Must-have crêpe frying pans to perfect your pour and flip

The perfect crêpe is all down to the pan, so we’ve handpicked three top-tier options to help you achieve a flawless, paper-thin finish every time.

MasterClass Ceramic Non-Stick Eco Crepe Pan

RRP£19.50
Available from Dunelm
Shop now

Lakeland Fusion 26cm Crepe Pan

RRP£21.99
Shop now

Bobikuke Non-Stick Crepe Pan

RRP£14.99
Shop now

Pomegranate, Lime and Coconut Pancakes

Ingredients

  • 2 limes

  • 2 eggs

  • 2 tbsp oil

  • 25g Doves Farm Organic Coconut Flour

  • ¼ tsp Doves Farm Bicarbonate of Soda

  • 50g pomegranate seeds

  • Rum, optional

  • Oil, for frying pan

  • Maple/sugar-free syrup, to serve

Method

  1. Finely grate one of the limes, and squeeze the juice of both.

  2. Put all the lime into a mixing bowl standing on a measuring scale, add a splash of rum if used and add water to make 100ml/100g of liquid.

  3. Break the eggs into the bowl, add the oil and whisk together.

  4. Add the flour and bicarbonate of soda and beat until the batter thickens.

  5. Lightly oil a non-stick frying pan and put it over a gentle heat.

  6. Drop large tablespoons of the batter into the pan and spread the mixture out to make 7.5/10cm/3-4” wide circles. Scatter some pomegranate seeds onto each pancake.

  7. Cook over a medium heat until the pancake is fully set and the base golden, about 4-5 minutes, then very carefully turn over and cook the other side for 2-3 minutes.

  8. Transfer the pancakes onto a warm plate and repeat until the batter is used.

  9. Serve warm with maple or sugar-free syrup.

Recipe sourced from Doves Farm.

Fresh Lemon Pancakes and Breakfast Blinis

Ingredients

  • 100g self-raising flour

  • 1 tsp baking powder

  • 1 tbsp castor sugar

  • 300ml almond milk

  • 50g plant-based butter (melted)

  • Zest of one whole lemon

  • 1 medium ripe banana

  • 1 tbsp self-raising flour

  • 50ml sunflower oil

Method

(Lemon pancakes)

  1. Sift the flour and baking powder into a bowl and add the sugar. Stir.

  2. In a separate bowl, add 300ml Almond Milk with the zest of one lemon.

  3. Using a Hand Mixer to combine, spoon the flour into the milk and continue to mix until the batter is smooth.

  4. Melt the plant butter in a small dish and stir it into the batter.

  5. Place the batter in the fridge and allow it to rest for an hour. This 'resting' period allows the gluten in the flour to absorb the liquid and gives the batter a thicker consistency, producing a lighter pancake.

  6. For best results, use a flat pancake pan over medium heat. Ensure the pan is hot, then add a teaspoon of sunflower oil, circling the pan to evenly coat the bottom of the pan with oil. 7. Pour away any excess oil into a separate side dish to use for the next pancake.

  7. Spoon a ladle of the batter onto the hot, oiled pan and again, circle the pan to ensure the batter reaches the sides. Place back onto the hob.

  8. Once the sides of the pancake start to brown, gently lift around the edges and underneath with a flat spatula and gently turn over to brown the other side.

(Breakfast Banana Blinis)

  1. Mash a banana in a bowl.

  2. Take half of the Lemon Pancakes batter made in the above recipe and add to the banana with 1 tablespoon of flour and mix well.

  3. Follow steps 6-8 in the above, but use a tablespoon in place of a ladle to make blini-sized pancakes.

  4. Serve alongside the Lemon Pancakes and enjoy!

Recipe sourced from Dualit.

Must-have tools for better spreading and flipping

A good pan is only half the battle. These accessories help you manage the batter like a professional, from traditional wooden spreaders for that thin, even layer to flexible spatulas that make flipping effortless.

OXO Good Grips Precision Batter Dispenser

RRP£27.40
Shop now

KitchenAid Stainless Steel Flex Turner

RRP£13
Shop now

KitchenCraft Crepe Spreader

RRP£5.99
Shop now

Buckwheat, Spinach and Ricotta Pancakes

Ingredients

Buckwheat Pancakes:

  • 100g Doves Farm Organic Buckwheat Flour

  • pinch salt

  • 2 eggs

  • 300ml milk

  • butter for cooking

Spinach & Ricotta Filling:

  • 1 small onion

  • 25g butter

  • 300g spinach

  • 250g ricotta

  • ¼ tsp salt

  • ¼ tsp black pepper

  • Pinch ground nutmeg

Topping:

  • 1 tbsp olive oil

  • 50g parmesan, grated

  • 25g pine nuts

Method

(For the filling)

  1. Finely dice the onion and add to a frying pan.

  2. Add the butter and put the pan over a gentle heat.

  3. Cook gently until the onion is translucent.

  4. Add the spinach, cover and continue cooking until the spinach has wilted.

  5. Remove the pan from the heat, tip the spinach into a mixing bowl and leave to cool.

  6. Open the ricotta and drain off any liquid.

  7. Tip the ricotta into the cooled spinach.

  8. Add the salt, pepper and nutmeg and stir to combine.

(For the pancakes)

  1. Put the flour into a large bowl, add the salt and stir to combine.

  2. Break the eggs into the bowl, add 150ml of milk and beat to a smooth paste.

  3. Stir in the remaining 150ml of milk to make a thin batter. You can use the batter immediately or refrigerate for up to 12 hours if you wish.

  4. Put a little butter into a frying pan and get the pan nice and hot so the butter lightly covers the surface.

  5. Stir the batter, then spoon or pour some into the hot pan, rolling it out to the edges.

  6. Cook the pancake, loosening the edges, until the base is golden.

  7. Turn it over and cook the other side.

  8. Transfer the pancake to a plate.

  9. Repeat until the batter is used.

  10. The pancakes can be left, covered and chilled, for up to a day before proceeding.

  11. Pre-heat the oven.

  12. Lay the pancakes out on a clean work surface.

  13. Divide the spinach mixture between the pancakes, spreading it in a line across each pancake.

  14. Roll up the pancakes and transfer them to an ovenproof dish.

  15. Sprinkle the olive oil over the pancakes.

  16. Dust the pancakes with the grated parmesan.

  17. Scatter the pine nuts over the top.

  18. Bake until the pancakes are heated through, about 10-15 minutes.

Recipe sourced from Doves Farm.

With these recipes and a few key tools, you are ready to make the most of this Shrove Tuesday. Whether you are whistling up a quick batch or a grand family spread, a reliable mixer ensures a silky batter everytime: for those looking to upgrade their kitchen for year-round baking, explore our curated guide to the best stand mixers.

item.title: The English Home US: April issue on sale now
item.dc:creator: The English Home
item.description: The April US edition of The English Home is full of ideas for the year ahead.
item.content:encoded:
In our April edition, discover a wealth of clever design ideas and fresh inspiration for creating an inviting, stylish home for the season ahead.

Inside, we explore the art of breaking decorating rules with confidence, discover an enchanting wallpaper collection rooted in American folklore and dive into our definitive guide on the enduring impact of colour in English interiors. Plus, we share the best buys this season, including bird motifs and beautiful mirrors.

Elsewhere in the issue, discover the best things to do, make and eat in April, take a look inside some of London's most exclusive members' clubs and star of The Forsytes and Death by Lightning, Tuppence Middleton tells us about her English home.

We also explore four quintessentially English homes, including a once-neglected Victorian farmhouse in Nottinghamshire and honey-hued Cotswold stone manor that dates back to 1790.

Buy the April 2026 issue at your nearest Barnes & Noble, or subscribe today.

item.title: Everyone’s talking about the Wuthering Heights ‘dark romance’ trend – here are 8 ways to get it right
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: Whether you’re a lifelong Brontë devotee or just craving a dose of cinematic drama, here is how to master the moody, manor-born look at home.
item.content:encoded:
There is a perennial allure to the Yorkshire moors, a landscape defined by its savage beauty, and for literary fanatics, the ghosts of the Brontë sisters.

Yet, as Emerald Fennell’s “Wuthering Heights” reimagines the classic tale for 2026, our collective obsession with the Gothic look has undergone a cinematic shift. Gone is the dusty, outdated and monochromatic feel of the traditional Victorian manor; in its place, a world of saturated shadows, bruised palettes and all-out decadence. For those seeking dark decorating ideas for your own home, this film is a treasure trove of inspiration.

To bring this dark romance aesthetic into your home is to embrace a style that is moody and unapologetically bold, the perfect antidote to plain white walls and those looking to inject character. These eight tips will help to inspire you to master this sense of Gothic maximilism into your home.

1. Focus on deep, moody hues for a drama-filled room

To achieve the immersive romance of the moors, move away from safe, white woodwork. Colour drenching - painting walls, skirting and evening ceilings in a single shade - is a great way to create a seamless, cocoon-like atmosphere that feels instantly cinematic. For something a little less in your face, you can achieve a similarly striking effect through more traditional painting of the walls, too.

The goal is to move away from high-contrast neutrals and select hues that feel energetic. Think bruised purples, earthy browns, poison greens and even the Cathy-inspired palette of flesh-toned pinks, like Margot Robbie’s character’s bedroom, adds an intimate layer.

However, the secret to mastering this look isn’t just about the darkness, but the depth of the finish. ‘Beautiful gloom is really about depth rather than darkness. It isn’t about painting everything black and turning down the lights; it’s more about creating atmosphere,’ explains Hattie Hansard, interior designer at Joanna Wood. ‘I’d go for layered, tonal colours like peat brown, lichen green, stormy dark blue, or softened charcoal and keep them all matte, chalky finishes rather than anything glossy.’

Superfresco Together Forever Easy Multi Surface Matt Emulsion Paint 2.5l

RRP£42
Shop now

Dunelm Bottle Green Eggshell Emulsion Paint 1.2l

RRP£32
Shop now

William Morris At Home Midnight Bramble Gallery Emulsion Paint 2.5l

RRP£42
Shop now

2. Master the Gothic look through curated accessories

If you aren’t ready to whip out the paint and rollers, you can still channel the Wuthering Heights aesthetic through strategic styling. Choose pieces that carry enough visual weight to anchor a lighter room.

As Lara Rich, creative lead for product at Furniturebox, suggests, your furniture and accessories can do the heavy lifting. 'Accent colours typical of the era, such as rich purples, navy and forest green, are a great way to emulate the Gothic Victorian period.

'A statement piece of furniture like a bold-coloured, velvet chair in purple or emerald green can seamlessly blend modern and functional with the colours and textures of the classic Wuthering Heights.'

For an even subtler approach, focus on the smaller details. Layering deep red or oxblood cushions, heavy velvet throws, and tarnished brass accessories can add drama and romance without the need for a total re-decoration.

OKA Blood Orange Plain Velvet Cushion Cover

RRP£50
Shop now

M&S Large Double Navy Cotton Velvet Quilted Bedspread

RRP£149
Shop now

OKA Morni Mirror in Antique Brass

RRP£425
Shop now

3. Introduce ‘bruised’ florals

While a traditional cottage-core look champions light, airy chintz, Fennell’s Gothic is defined by saturated, overblown botanicals set against a deep, shadowy background. These bruised, dark florals feature patterns with lilies, roses and thistles in shades of dark plum, violet, dusty pink and deep burgundy. To get the look, opt for large-scale wallpaper, fabrics and soft furnishings where the petals feel heavy and tactile.

Dunelm Vintage Floral Black Wall Mural

RRP£60
Shop now

Liberty Strawberry Thief Velvet Cushion

RRP£125
Shop now

M&S X National Gallery Sateen Floral Bedding Set

RRPfrom £69
Shop now

4. Layer heavyweight, decadent textiles

In Fennell’s Wuthering Heights, textiles are a sensory experience. On screen, we see floor-to-ceiling drapes that puddle on the floor and intricate lace that feels both romantic and ghostly. To recreate this, move away from light linens and toward fabrics with more structure and weight.

‘Texture was an integral part of this era, so consider adding bursts of highly tactile, rich texture,’ says Laura. “Velvet is a timeless way to blend classic and modern; it truly never goes out of style. Luxury materials like silk, satin and velvet are welcome in 2026.'

To prevent the look from feeling too dated for modern homes, Steven Dempsey, style consultant at Shutterly Fabulous, suggests a layered approach. ‘Focus on layering rather than excess. Hanging a sheer lace layer behind minimalist blinds or curtains is a simple way to create romance while keeping it modern.’

Dunelm Recycled Velour Eyelet Curtains

RRPfrom £35
Shop now

OKA Costellini Velvet Ottoman in Perfect Navy

RRP£250
Shop now

Dunelm Alessia Velvet Pleated Lamp Shade

RRP£45
Shop now

5. Lean into antiques

Focus on furniture that feels solid and historic. Traditionally, this means looking for Victorian-style antiques in dark woods like oak or mahogany, which have a natural depth that you don’t get with modern, white-washed furniture. Look for pieces with carved detail, heavy cured legs or aged brass hardware that have a sense of history about them.

While heading out to antiques emporiums and second-hand shops is the most authentic way of sourcing pieces like this, you can also buy furniture that is inspired by days gone by. As Hattie advises, mixing old and new can make the room feel like it has been put together over many years. ‘Mixing periods is key. A contemporary sofa alongside a nineteenth-century chest keeps the room feeling alive rather than museum-like.’

French Bedroom Gustavian Dark Writing Desk

RRP£995
Shop now

Dunelm Darlington Large Sideboard

RRP£699
Shop now

OKA Briallen Side Table in Antique Gold

RRP£295
Shop now

6. Create pockets of low-light drama

Lighting is the most powerful tool for changing the mood of a room. In a Gothic-inspired scheme, you will want to avoid flooding the space with bright, uniform light. Instead, use multiple sources at different heights to create a sense of mystery and intimacy.

Hattie explains that lighting is crucial because one overhead light will flatten everything. Instead, she suggests that you ‘build the room throughsmaller pools of light: table lamps with pleated shades, picture lights on textured walls, candles clicking against plaster.’

M&S Winnie Velvet Table Lamp

RRP£69
Shop now

Rowen & Wren Winnie Wall Candle Holder

RRP£44
Shop now

OKA Mini Grisewood Wireless Wall Lamp and Shade

RRP£95
Shop now

7. Incorporate the natural world

Bringing the outdoors in is key for a home inspired by the moors. You can achieve this by focusing on raw, earthy materials that feel like they were pulled directly from the landscape. ‘These natural colours and materials are archetypal of the era. Furniture doesn't have to be a statement to embody this Gothic feel; you can focus on the natural woods and stone used that draw inspiration from the landscape,’ says Laura. ‘Deep, textured woods like oak, walnut, mahogany and rosewood add dimension to the room, along with stone table tops.’

To keep the space feeling warm, Hattie suggests focusing on textures with visible depth and movement. She notes that you don’t need literal stone walls to evoke the moors, instead materials like ‘brushed or fumed oak, reclaimed timber, and coir carpets all add quiet regions. For upholstery, she recommends robust fabrics like heavy linen, brushed wool or mohair, noting that ‘honed limestone, tumbled marble or textured ceramics feel more considered than anything too polished.’

8. Hang a moody portrait

No Victorian-inspired room is complete without a dramatic portrait to anchor the space. In a modern home, this doesn’t have to be a family heirloom; find something that carries some emotional weight. Choose an oil painting - either an original or a high-quality reproduction - that features a deep colour palette.

A large-scale portrait in a chunky, tarnished gold frame creates an instant sense of history and can provide a nice contrast against contemporary furniture. Placing a theatrical piece of art against a colour-drenched or floral papered wall will give your room a cinematic finishing touch.

9. Opt for flesh-toned pinks and gilded accents

While the ‘beautiful gloom’ of the moors is captivating, not everyone will be keen on committing to a darker palette. For those who prefer a lighter and more obviously romantic touch, you can channel the femininity of Cathy’s bedroom by opting for flesh-toned pinks and antique gold accents. This softer palette feels sophisticated and playful, especially when layered with ornate features.

As Steven notes, focusing on these specific details allows you to dabble in the drama. ‘Carved furniture, sculptural lamps and ornamental details such as vintage frames, brass finishes and heritage-style accessories can all help achieve the aesthetic, while allowing one or two key pieces to take centre stage.’

By mixing these warmer pinks with brass or gilded finishes, you introduce a romantic glow and a lighter alternative to the Wuthering Heights look.

Toast Hand Stitched Organic Velvet Quilt

RRP£260
Shop now

Dunelm Crown Arched Full Length Wall Mirror

RRP£255
Shop now

OKA Coleridge Velvet Armchair in Apricot

RRP£1,295
Shop now

By blending these deep tones with the weight of storied accessories and luxurious textiles, you can transform your home into a sanctuary that feels plucked straight from a nineteenth-century manor, reimagined with a 2026 edge. As it turns out, a touch of Gothic drama is exactly what a modern home needs to find its soul.

For more moody inspiration, discover our guide to dark kitchen ideas, learn how to decorate a Victorian terrace or walk the moors yourself with a tour of Wuthering Heights filming locations. You can also pick up the original story of Wuthering Heights by Emily Brönte on Amazon, it will look beautiful displayed on your bookshelf once you've finished reading.

item.title: Stop scrolling – these 4 Yorkshire hotels make living out your Wuthering Heights daydreams shockingly easy
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton
item.description: As we all fall in love with Cathy, Heathcliff and the Yorkshire Moors that inspired Emily Brontë, these 4 Yorkshire hotels are the perfect place to live out your Wuthering Heights fantasies...
item.content:encoded:
We've always known that the Yorkshire landscapes that inspired the Brontë sisters were worth visiting, but Emerald Fennell's new "Wuthering Heights" movie adaptation has brought their heather-clad hills and romantic postcard villages to the attention of the world.

As the region becomes the next big beneficiary of screen tourism, following in the path of Bridgerton in Bath, travellers can look to some of Yorkshire's cosiest inns to provide a warming base after a day exploring the Moors, with roaring fires, hearty food and enormous beds you can climb into.

But, we think we can do one better. As set-jetting inspires travellers to explore Emily Brontë's hometown and inspirations in Yorkshire, we've put together a list of Wuthering Heights-inspired hotels, either because they are close to the real Wuthering Heights filming locations, or because they evoke the same sense of gothic, Victoriana maximalism that audiences will experience in the new film.

Screen tourism has taken Britain by storm in recent years, as directors look the country's beautiful landscapes and cultural heritage to provide the real backdrop of their storytelling. The CEO of the official UK tourism company VisitBritain commented that "Our research shows that more than 9-out-of-10 potential tourists are keen to visit the real-life film and TV sets of our vibrant destinations and stunning countryside. We look forward to rolling out the red carpet in West Yorkshire to future ‘set jetters’ when “Wuthering Heights” hits the cinema screens.”

If, after watching the new film, you are inspired to visit Yorkshire's windswept moors and do some brooding, Heathcliff-style, yourself, we have chosen 5 of our favourite Yorkshire hotels you should book to live out your Wuthering Heights fantasies...

1. Holdsworth House Hotel, Halifax

Just a twenty-minute drive from Haworth, where the Brontë Parsonage Museum is and where the Brontë sisters lived and wrote, and surrounded by their beloved Moors, this 4-star independent hotel is housed in a beautiful Jacobean mansion, and even offers its very own Brontë Country Escape Package.

View this post on Instagram

The package includes an overnight stay with breakfast, tickets to the Brontë Parsonage Museum, a souvenir guide and a walking map of Haworth, so guests can walk in the footsteps of the authors and see for themselves the landscapes that inspired them.

The hotel also has direct links to the film because it hosted Wuthering Heights director Emerald Fennell, and members of the production team on an early location recce in the area, and where stars Margot Robbie and Jacob Elordi dined and stayed following their final day of filming on the shoot.

Book your stay at Holdsworth House Hotel via Expedia here.

To celebrate the release of the film, in collaboration with Airbnb, Holdsworth House Hotel has also created a replica of Cathy Earnshaw's bedroom from Wuthering Heights and is offering three lucky couples the chance to stay their, for free. Bookings open Friday 20 February - set your alarms!

2. Simonstone Hall, Hawes

Another hotel with direct connections to Emerald Fennell's Wuthering Heights is the country house hotel Simonstone Hall in Hawes. The cast, crew and Emerald Fennell reserved the entire 20 rooms of the hotel for two and a half weeks while filming on the Moors nearby, and ran through scripts and performed interviews onsite at the hotel.

Making the most of their spotlight, Simonstone Hall is offering Wuthering Heights-themed stays in February and March 2026 so guests can experience what Margot Robbie and Jacob Elordi did during their stay.

The romantic two-night getaway for two includes Champagne and chocolate-covered strawberries upon arrival in your room, a two-course candelit dinner with wine pairings, and brandy and truffles to enjoy by the fire before bed. The stay is for a principal room which includes a superking bed, a large bath, a rainfall shower, and views of the surrounding Moors; modern luxuries we think Cathy would certainly have approved of, and essential after a day exploring the windswept filming locations nearby.

Book your stay at Simonstone Hall via Expedia here.

3. The Devonshire Arms Hotel, Skipton

Set in the grounds of the 30,000-acre Bolton Abbey Estate, a hauntingly Gothic cathedral ruined in the Dissolution that oozes Wuthering Heights style, The Devonshire Arms Hotel is in the Yorkshire home of the Duke and Duchess of Devonshire.

Certainly fit for the luxurious tastes of Cathy Earnshaw, the 18th-century restored hotel has two distinctive wings available to guests, the original wing with its traditional rooms rich in period charm and featuring four-poster beds, and the newer wing for those seeking more contemporary comfort. There are also two dining rooms inside the hotel, as well as a tranquil spa with a vitality pool, sauna, steam room and Rasul chamber.

The Devonshire Arms offers unrivalled access to all the Yorkshire Dales has to offer, and is a stylish base for exploring the Wuthering Heights filming locations, as well as the nearby Brontë sites like their house museum, and Top Withens.

Book your stay at The Devonshire Arms via Expedia here.

4. The Black Swan, Olstead

View this post on Instagram

This charming little country inn feels very Brontë-esque, neslted in the North York Moors National Park and near the impressive Byland Abbey ruin. But, it holds a very special secret.

Behind its historic walls and cosy interiors, The Black Swan is a Michelin-starred, 4 AA Rosette restaurant with rooms by chef Tommy Banks.

Inside, the decor is simple and traditional, and a rugged Heathcliff drinking from a frothy tankard after a day searching for Cathy on the Moors wouldn't look out of place here. But the menus are packed full of creative flare and offer modern, exuberant dining that go hand in hand with Fennell's vision for the her Wuthering Heights adaptation.

The nine bedrooms are named after the surrounding Dales and woodland and perfectly mix the traditional character of the inn, with the luxury the inn exudes - a welcome spot to retire to after a delicious meal.

Book your stay at The Black Swan via Expedia here.

If, like us, Wuthering Heights has inspired you to explore more real-life filming sets and places to stay in Britain, here is our guide to the very best Bridgerton-style hotels in the south of England - the perfect place to escape from the gossip of the ton.

item.title: Forget the sad beige aesthetic – KitchenAid’s new Colour of the Year is the character-filled colour your kitchen actually needs
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: Moving away from muted tones, the reveal of KitchenAid’s Colour of the Year, Spearmint, brings a refreshing, modern-vintage energy to the heart of the home.
item.content:encoded:
Owning a stand mixer has long been the ultimate status symbol for the home baker, the definitive stamp of a well-appointed country kitchen.

When it comes to investing in the best stand mixer, KitchenAid is the brand you want to turn to. They aren’t modestly priced, no, but take it from us that once you buy one, it will last a lifetime and then some.

KitchenAid has just launched its 2026 Colour of the Year – Spearmint, and it’s a total breath of fresh air. It strikes that perfect balance between modern and vintage, vibrant enough to make a statement, yet soft enough to feel timeless. It’s the kind of shade that instantly ‘breathes life into a space, filling it with a soothing yet invigorating energy,’ says Brittni Pertijs, colour, finish and material manager at Whirlpool, part of the team behind the new colour.

KitchenAid Artisan 4.7l Stand Mixer

The KitchenAid Artisan Tilt-Head 4.7L Stand Mixer in Spearmint comes with a sleek stainless-steel bowl, but it’s easily personalised. A library of optional bowls, from ceramic to quilted and hammered textures, lets you tailor the look to your décor. Also new for 2026 is a sophisticated sand finish. This soft matte alternative to the traditional gloss offers an understated, contemporary feel, ensuring your mixer looks more like a curated piece of furniture than a simple appliance.

RRP£699.95
Shop now

If you feel like your kitchen countertop is lacking that bit of ‘oomph’, upgrading your existing stand mixer to a more vibrant hue could be the perfect solution. It’s an easy way to introduce a focal point without a full overhaul of your appliances and accessories.

Alison Davidson, editor of The English Home, has noticed over the last few months that green has become the colour of the moment, as evidenced by Colour of the Year, Spearmint - a colour that first came to prominence in the Art Deco era and has continued in popularity through the years. ‘Greens are tied to nature, so are calming and make us feel grounded. This particular shade, a pale, bluey-green is the perfect way to add a hint of colour without being overpowering,’ she adds.

The Spearmint colourway works so beautifully because it’s a ‘chamelon colour’ – it feels right at home alongside moody charcoals or rich navy cabinetry of a modern scheme, yet looks equally charming against the soft neutrals and classic timbers of a classic country kitchen scheme. It sits in that middle point between a punchy, warm block shade and a cool pastel.

More mint green kitchenware to shop now

Smeg 50’s Style Jug Kettle

RRP£119.95
Shop now

Lakeland 8-in-1 Only Pan

RRP£59.99
Shop now

Liberty Betsy Tana Lawn™ Cotton Napkins Set of Two

RRP£44
Shop now

Dunelm Retro Gloss 4 Slice Toaster

RRP£45
Shop now

M&S Splatter Mug

RRP£6
Shop now

Dunelm Amalfi Stoneware Utensils Jar

RRP£12
Shop now

Of course, while we have all totally fallen for the Spearmint aesthetic, we know that this design icon is a significant investment. If you’re looking to capture the same look on a more modest budget, this sage stand mixer from Lakeland is a great alternative at less than half the price. And just in time too, with Pancake Day arriving this Tuesday, there’s no better excuse to get the mixer whirring, try our go-to recipes for the perfect Shrove Tuesday stack.

item.title: Just in time for Valentine’s Day, British Museum raise millions to keep Henry VIII’s 24-carat Tudor Heart necklace
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton
item.description: The 24-carat gold necklace, named the ‘Tudor Heart’ is engraved with the Tudor Rose and Katherine of Aragon’s pomegranate symbol and offers a rare glimpse into Henry VIII’s first marriage
item.content:encoded:
The British Museum have raised a whopping £3.5 million to acquire the dazzling 'Tudor Heart', a 24-carat-gold pendant engraved with a Tudor Rose and the pomegranate symbol of Katherine of Aragon that has links to Henry VIII and his first wife.

The Tudor Heart is a heart shaped gold pendant that is believed to have been created for a tournament held in October 1518 to mark the betrothal of Henry VIII and Katherine of Aragon's daughter, Princess Mary, to the French heir.

The dazzling pendant features the Tudor Rose united with Henry VIII's first wife, Katherine of Aragon's pomegranate symbol and offers a rare glimpse into their 24-year marriage (the longest of all of Henry VIII's six marriages) that ended, famously, with divorce, Henry's break from Rome and a second marriage to Anne Boleyn.

In October 2025, the British Museum launched a fundraising appeal so it could permanently acquire the Tudor Heart. In February 2026 it was announced that it had reached its fundraising goal, after receiving £360,000 in public donations from grants, trusts and arts organisations.

The Tudor Heart necklace will now remain permanently on public display at the British Museum.

View this post on Instagram

What is the Tudor Heart necklace?

Henry VIII regularly commissioned London goldsmiths to create special 'costume' jewellery for him for state occasions which would have been worn by members of the court.

Beneath the two united symbols and an 'H' and 'K' a banner reads tousiors, the old French word for 'always' and a poignant declaration of an intended eternal connection.

This is one of the only objects that survives from the king's marriage to Katherine of Aragon, with most having been lost over time. The necklace is a symbol of a long-lasting, and, at the time, successful marriage that shaped the Tudor court and politics at the time. Katherine was a trusted partner of Henry's, sometimes acting as Regent in his absence. The necklace symbolises the strength of their union before their marriage was annulled in 1533.

Where was the Tudor Heart found?

The Tudor Heart was discovered in 2019 by a metal detectorist in a field in Warwickshire. It was then reported under the Treasure Act that requires all finders to report any potential treasure and are rewarded for their contributions. Here we explore the biggest treasure hoards found in Britain.

Although I know it's not quite the same, all this talk of Tudor jewellery has got me inspired to add a little bit of historic magic to my own jewellery collection, and I absolutely love these Anne Boleyn 'B' necklaces - a subtle reminder to everyone that you know your Tudor history.

This article was first published on DiscoverBritain.com

item.title: 13 of the prettiest hotels in the Cotswolds to visit this year
item.dc:creator: Priya Raj
item.description: From charmingly quintessential inns to grand manor houses, these Cotswold hotels boast interiors that rival the world’s finest.
item.content:encoded:
Once a serene weekend escape for city dwellers, the Cotswolds has blossomed into a hub of luxury, boasting hotels with exquisite interiors and world-class charm.

Located in central South West England, the Cotswolds is a picturesque region dotted with enchanting towns and villages, including Chipping Norton, Cirencester, and Broadway. While it’s famed for its rolling landscapes, farm shops, and celebrity residents, the Cotswolds has also become synonymous with some of the country’s finest hotels.

The commonality between the hotels in our roundup is that while they are certainly luxury, they still feel approachable and comfortable much like what you’d expect from a countryside hotel.

Whether you’re seeking a boutique retreat or a grand estate, here’s our handpicked selection of the prettiest hotels in the Cotswolds.

The prettiest hotels in the Cotswolds

Buckland Manor

With just 15 guest rooms, Buckland Manor near Broadway in the Worcestershire Cotswolds offers an intimate and luxurious escape. Each room is thoughtfully designed for maximum comfort and features its own unique charm. Some rooms boast natural exposed wooden beams, while others are adorned with rich oak panelling, creating a romantic and timeless ambiance.

Broadway, WR12 7LY

bucklandmanor.co.uk

Calcot Hotel & Spa

The idyllic Calcot Manor & Spa in Tetbury is set within 220 acres of cascading Cotswolds countryside. The 34 rooms are comfortable yet elegant, offering generous family suites, cottages, or a small bolthole perfect for a solo stay.

Staying at Calcot is not just about the rooms. The facilities include an award-winning spa, indoor and outdoor gym and a calendar of country activities like walks and crafts workshops. For a one-stop property for the whole family, this is a must-stay.

Gloucestershire, Tetbury, GL8 8YJ

calcot.co

Cowley Manor

Cowley Manor is as much of an ‘escape’ as you can imagine. A short distance from Cheltenham, the manor has 36 rooms which have been meticulously designed by Dorothée Meilichzon. The bold spirit of this country house hotel is reflected in its blend of historic charm with contemporary design, featuring dark wood panelling, lacquered furniture, geometric fabrics, and vibrant art.

The common areas of the house are all ideal for a romantic getaway, or a relaxing weekend spent in the cocktail bar, library, lounge and games room.

Cowley, Cheltenham, GL53 9NL

cowleymanorexperimental.com

Ellenborough Park

Set within 90 acres of stunning grounds, this 15th-century manor house exudes grandeur in every detail. Each room at Ellenborough Park boasts its own unique personality, with Cotswold-inspired colour schemes like Blossom, Wildflower, Countryside, Tudor, Chrysanthemum, Gingham, and Rustic, carefully curated by interior designer Ilze Reinke during the 2023 refurbishment.

The update also introduced modern amenities, luxurious bathrooms, and high-quality linens, ensuring a truly five-star experience.

Southam Lane, Cheltenham, GL52 3NJ

ellenboroughpark.com

Estelle Manor

Hot off the heels of their success with Mayfair’s Maison Estelle, Estelle Manor opened in 2023 for guests looking for a spot of countryside luxury. The members club is set in a Grade-II listed Landmark House on an 85-acre plot. The 108 rooms are complimented by a range of private cottages and houses – with options for numerous group sizes.

The rooms have a similar theme running throughout – fireplaces, rich colours and beautiful rugs while the cottages each nod to a moment in design – some with an antique, eclectic theme and others like The Woodland Cottage with a more distinct Western style.

Eynsham Park, Oxfordshire, OX29 6PN

estellemanor.com

Foxhill Manor

Foxhill Manor, an Arts and Crafts manor built in 1909, is an opulent, boldly luxurious retreat nestled within the 500-acre Farncombe estate. With sweeping views over the Cotswolds and the Vale of Evesham, this boutique hotel offers a relaxed approach to country life and makes the most of the surrounding area.

Each bedroom and suite is individually designed, unique, and brimming with attention to detail, offering various charming features such as giant free-standing baths, cottage-like decor, and four-poster beds with stunning views of the Cotswolds.

Farncombe House, Farncombe, Broadway WR12 7LJ

foxhillmanor.com

No. 131

No. 131 in Cheltenham, a town renowned for its prominence during the height of Regency England, offers a perfect blend of heritage and modern luxury. Formed from several listed townhouses, this spirited haven seamlessly combines Georgian elegance with modern British design and contemporary art.

The hotel has strived to preserve the original features, and include nods to the Regency-era history of the building with period bathrooms and antiques. That said, the hotel maintains the modern comforts you’d expect from a luxury experience, including a sumptuous Japanese restaurant and one of the town's best late-night bars Gin & Juice – which is a laidback bar with live music and over 400 types of gin.

131 Promenade, Cheltenham, GL50 1NW

no131.com

The Lygon Arms

For a beautiful hotel the whole family can enjoy, including the family pooch, The Lygon Arms offers a countryside destination with comfort as its priority. The hotel has natural wooden elements and tartan as a common thread throughout the guest bedrooms which are in the main house as well as a detached cottage.

The 72 rooms have been individually designed for a luxurious stay. The hotel has welcomed a host of notable guests over its 650 year history, including King Edward VII, Sir Michael Redgrave, Elizabeth Taylor, and Oliver Cromwell is even known to have occupied a room in the 1600s.

The Lygon Arms, Broadway, WR12 7DU

lygonarmshotel.co.uk

The Painswick

Set in an 18th-century building, The Painswick in Gloucestershire has been open since 2015. With rooms ranging from cosy nooks to luxurious suites, there is something for every person and budget. The boutique hotel is set in Painswick, a small village bursting with culture and scenic views, ideal for exploring on foot or by bicycle.

Interiors in the guest rooms widely range, from neutral canopy headboards and Parisian-inspired interiors to bold colours with exposed wooden beams on the ceiling for a cosy natural look.

Kemps Lane, Painswick, Gloucestershire, GL6 6YB

thepainswick.co.uk

The Pig in the Cotswolds

Understated in name but luxurious in style, The Pig chain of hotels perfectly embodies a charming and rustic country style. The chain acquired Barnsley House in 2023, a Grade II listed, 17th-century former rectory close to the popular Cotswold hotspot Bibury.

The country-chic interiors of the guest rooms have features like floral wallpapers, wooden bed frames, plush velvet furnishings, and window treatments set on worn wooden floors with touches of exposed brick.

Judy, the interior designer of The Pig chain said: “I wanted it to be homely, as if you were staying with your great-aunt, a feeling of faded grandeur.” The eclectic style is achieved by collecting vintage pieces, as well as reworking pieces which might otherwise be thrown away. In fact, if she finds something she likes but doesn’t necessarily have a place for it, she’ll buy it with the view that it will one day find a home.

Barnsley, Cirencester, GL7 5EE

thepighotel.com

The Rectory Hotel

Built in the early 18th century, behind The Rectory Hotel's Georgian facade, constructed with Cotswold limestone, hides 18 rooms with a relaxed feel and contemporary finish. Originally serving as the rectory to All Saints’ Church, the building has seen additions and alterations over time. Other than the rooms, it boasts an honesty bar on the landing, inviting guests to help themselves to a cosy nightcap, restaurant, an outdoor pool and traditional English gardens to explore.

Crudwell, Malmesbury, SN16 9EP

therectoryhotel.com

Thyme

For those longing to escape the urban rush, Thyme offers a tranquil haven. Described as an ‘English country idyll’, Thyme dates back to the Roman era, and the current building is a fully restored manor house and farm, carefully and sensitively renovated by the Hibbert family. The bucolic interiors, as founder and creative director Caryn Hibbert explains, are inspired by the soft colours found in the gardens to create “harmony and fluidity between inside and out.”

Guests can indulge in farm-to-table meals at the Tithe Barn, savour exquisite cuisine at The Swan restaurant, or enjoy refreshments in the drawing rooms and cocktail bar, The Baa.

Southrop, Lechlade GL7 3NX

thyme.co.uk

Wild Thyme & Honey

Wild Thyme & Honey is a tranquil boutique hotel tucked away in the idyllic Cotswolds village of Ampney Crucis. Originally a 16th-century inn, it has been carefully renovated into a 24-room countryside escape.

The guest rooms feature neutral, warm and relaxed interiors with exposed brick walls, wooden beams, plush fabrics, and soothing tones for a cosy atmosphere. The award-winning restaurant, The Crown, enhances the homely charm with natural elements, a canopy effect over tables, and an open fireplace.

Ampney Crucis, Cirencester, GL7 5RS

wildthymeandhoney.co.uk

item.title: There is a reason Liberty’s new teapot collection is already causing a stir among country style fans - and I am obsessed with their quintessential English charm
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: These hand-painted teapots are the quintessential additions every curated country kitchen needs.
item.content:encoded:
There is a timeless appeal to the classic English farmhouse kitchen. It’s an evocative image of rural life, defined by the steady warmth of a cast-iron range oven, rugged stone flooring and open shelving packed with a lifetime’s worth of collected treasures.

When it comes to finding country kitchen ideas, success is found by leaning into the past rather than chasing the future, and in this setting, there is no such thing as being too kitsch. It’s all about celebrating that sentimental clutter, which is exactly why Liberty’s new Ceramic Inspirations teapot collection has totally won me over, from an Aga and apothecary cabinet to the ever-familiar yellow Colman’s mustard jar; each piece could easily be mistaken for a vintage family heirloom.

My favourite from the collection has to be the French Stove teapot, which stirs an immediate sense of nostalgia for my Italian grandmother’s vintage cooker; it is a wonderfully whimsical piece that even features a minute steel saucepan and moka pot, perfectly mirroring the permanent fixtures of her own well-loved kitchen. While that specific design captured my heart at first, I’ve found myself lovestruck by the entire collection; every item feels like a storied treasure for the home.

Shop the collection

Aga Ceramic One-Cup Teapot

RRP£95
Shop now

French Stove Medium Ceramic Teapot

RRP£115
Shop now

Sewing Machine Large Ceramic Teapot

RRP£155
Shop now

Apothecary Large Ceramic Teapot

RRP£155
Shop now

Marmtea Medium Ceramic Teapot

RRP£115
Shop now

Colman’s Mustard Tin Medium Ceramic Teapot

RRP£115
Shop now

Lyle’s Golden Syrup Tin Medium Ceramic Teapot

RRP£115
Shop now

Farrah’s Harrogate Toffee Tin Medium Ceramic Teapot

RRP£115
Shop now

These earthenware teapots celebrate quintessential English eccentricity, functioning more as sculptural curiosities than mere kitchenware. Each piece is a masterclass in miniature details - from the tiny, hand-painted cat resting on an Aga to the slice of toast atop a marmite jar - offering a sense of personality that can inject character into any room. They are designed to be lived with, talked about and passed down through the generations, feeling quite at home displayed proudly on a pine dresser or as a storied focal point on a pretty tablescape.

Whether you are hunting for a quintessential gift for a tea devotee - perhaps paired with this box of English breakfast tea from Liberty - or curating your own assembly of weird and wonderful kitchenware, these charming silhouettes are designed to be lived with.

From the morning’s first pour at the breakfast table to taking pride of place on a farmhouse dresser, they are a delightful reminder that the most functional objects can also be full of character - just be sure to snap them up before this limited collection goes out of stock!

item.title: The equestrian aesthetic: How to style your home for the Year of the Horse
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: From the rich patina of saddle leather to the power of a single hero painting, we share our favourite ways to bring a sophisticated equestrian spirit into your home for 2026 and our favourite accessories to shop now.
item.content:encoded:
As we move deeper into 2026, the Lunar calendar welcomes the Year of the Fire Horse, a rare cycle defined by speed, courage and transformation. In Chinese tradition, the horse symbolises perseverance and vitality, with the Fire Horse appearing only once every sixty years to signal a period of bold momentum.

Yet, for an English home, the horse is more than a seasonal symbol; it is a permanent cornerstone of British style. Whether in a Cotswold barn or a Georgian manor, equestrian motifs provide a storied connection to the landscape, heritage and sporting life.

While equestrian design has deep roots in tradition, this year's aesthetic is about far more than just country charm; it is a modern, high-energy style that balances power with refinement.

In this guide, we share inspiration for creating a balanced, 'horsey' home that feels curated rather than themed. We have gathered expert insights on how to harness this year's momentum, along with some of our favourite equestrian-inspired pieces to shop now.

1. Create a sophisticated focal point with statement art

While modern interiors often lean toward the subtle, a large-scale equestrian painting remains one of the most powerful ways to anchor a room. Instead of filling a space with multiple small trinkets, consider using a single hero piece to establish the home’s character and provide a sense of heritage.

“Think like a curator, not a decorator”, says Kunal Trehan, interior designer and founder of Touched Interiors. “Look for abstract or expressive equine art, monochromatic photography or bronze forms that feel collectable, and position them as you would any artwork, layers with books and objects, so the story feels permanent rather than seasonal or symbolic.” Visit local antique dealers or specialist collectors' fairs to find an authentic treasure with genuine provenance.

This entry hall, designed by VSP Interiors, demonstrates this perfectly. By hanging a large, traditional equine oil painting as the central "anchor," the room gains an immediate sense of history and grandeur.

2. Utilise tactile materials like leather and brass

Incorporating materials that reference the craftsmanship of saddlery is a considered way of adding a subtle equestrian nod to interiors that feels luxurious. “Leather valet trays, stitched coasters, dark wood, or even brushed brass or aged bronze hardware can instantly elevate a room,” adds Kunal. “These additions feel timeless and luxurious, bringing an equestrian sensibility through tactility rather than decoration.”

3. Go bold with a statement equestrian wallpaper

Equestrian wallpaper offers a dramatic way to transform a room. Whether used for an entire wall or a strategic accent wall, it can set a unique mood and act as a conversational centrepiece.

Select the scale carefully by choosing large-scale murals or bold horse portraits for feature walls in open-plan spaces where the design has space to do the talking. In narrower spaces like hallways, cloakrooms or accent walls, repetitive motifs like stylised silhouettes, galloping forms or geometric shapes can add movement without overwhelming the room.

Traditional spaces benefit from vintage sketches, sepia-toned prints of Toile de Jouy that add warmth.

For more contemporary schemes, consider high-contrast monochromatic prints, block prints or minimalist watercolour washes.

Use playful pony prints for nurseries or creative reading nooks, while sophisticated dark-toned wallpapers can make studies or bedrooms feel cosy and enveloping.

4. Style your space with horse-themed accessories and textiles

Accessories provide the finishing touches that breathe life into a space. Sculptural elements, such as bronze, stone or high-gloss horse busts, work well on shelving or console tables. Textiles also play a crucial role in grounding the aesthetic, wool throws and cushions featuring horse motifs or more subtle bridle-stitch embroidery add essential layers of comfort.
