Plus, accessories are a great way to inject this look without committing to a statement wallpaper or a large-scale piece of art, and can be moved from room to room for a quick interiors refresh. "The key is restraint. These pieces act as quiet accents rather than statements, allowing you to nod to the Year of the Horse without disrupting the existing scheme,” explains Kunal.

5. Source authentic equestrian antiques

To achieve a look that feels storied rather than staged, source original pieces with genuine provenance. Visit local antique dealers or specialist collectors' fairs to discover treasures that bring a unique sense of history to a space.

Seek out original oil paintings from the 18th or 19th century to create an immediate focal point with character. Bronze or hand-carved wooden horse busts found at antique fairs offer a more personal, collectable feel.

You can also repurpose functional hardware, such as antique brass stirrups for bookends or vintage leather crops and bits for wall-mounted displays. For landings or nurseries, a traditional wooden rocking horse provides a quintessential sense of soulful storytelling.

5. Balance energy with earthy neutrals

Lastly, for a less direct approach, the Fire Horse energy is high-intensity, so balance this by using a soft, neutral foundation. Stepping away from literal interpretations, this is about creating an atmosphere through hidden cues rather than motifs.

"With the surge of ambitious energy that comes with the Year of the Horse, it’s a great moment to focus on creating balance at home, making the most of our downtime before we start racing towards our goals,” Natalie Simone notes, creative director at Morris & Milne. “I suggest leaning into warm, earthy tones and cosy, tactile textures to ground that fiery Horse energy. Think layered textiles, natural materials and soft lighting, with pops of red added sparingly for luck and vitality."

Our favourite pieces to shop now to incorporate the Year of the Horse into your home

Whether you are looking for a subtle nod to the theme or are ready to fully commit to the look, these are the items that belong in a real home, balanced, stylish and delivering exactly the right amount of statement.

Brown & Cream Embroidered Horse Velvet Cushion Cover, Oliver Bonas

RRP£25
Shop now

Stable Companion Vintage Style Horse Print with Boy, Casa by JJ

RRP£160
Shop now

Acrylic Horse Objet, Jonathan Adler

RRP£995
Shop now

Ponies Wool Blanket, J.J.Textile

RRP£120
Shop now

Pair of Emerald Green Horse Bookends, Villa Bologna Pottery

RRP£120
Shop now

item.title: Where was Wuthering Heights filmed? 5 real-life Yorkshire filming locations from the upcoming adaptation of Emily Brontë’s novel
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton
item.description: As the much-anticipated Wuthering Heights film starring Margot Robbie and Jacob Elordi hits our screens this week, we explore some of the real-life filming locations in Yorkshire’s Brontë country that you can visit yourself.
item.content:encoded:
Based on the 1847 Gothic novel by Emily Brontë, director Emerald Fennel’s maximalist take on one of the best-loved love stories of all time tells the tragic tale of the intense, destructive love between Catherine and the orphan Heathcliff, all set on the swirling Yorkshire Moors.

When will Wuthering Heights be released?

Wuthering Heights will be released in cinemas worldwide on Friday, 13 February 2026.

Watch the trailer for Wuthering Heights at the link below:

What is Wuthering Heights about?

The singular novel written by the middle Brontë sister, Emily Brontë, Wuthering Heights was released to much criticism in November 1847, under the male pseudonym Ellis Bell, hiding her true identity because the novels examined ‘unfeminine’ subject matter for the prude Victorian reader.

Covering themes of destructive love, mental and physical cruelty, and shattering Victorian social conventions, Wuthering Heights’ impact on Victorian literature was monumental. Emily Brontë’s novel redefined the Gothic genre with its raw depiction of obsessive love. Although it initially failed to impress, with critics condemning it as ‘savage’ and ‘vulgar’, in true tragic Victorian style Wuthering Heights achieved posthumous success to become the much-loved literary classic it is today, with countless reimaginings on the page, stage, screen and even a 1978 debut single from the then unknown artist, Kate Bush.

Wuthering Heights is a tragic love story about the all-consuming, destructive, almost supernatural love between Catherine Earnshaw and her father’s ward, Heathcliff, a foundling he adopted on the streets of Liverpool, described in the novel as ‘dark-skinned’, though his exact origin is never revealed.

After Catherine marries for social status and wealth, Heathcliff seeks brutal revenge on everyone who wronged him, leading to a cycle of abuse obsession, and Romeo and Juliet-level family feuds across the estates of Wuthering Heights and Thrushcross Grange.

Where was Wuthering Heights filmed?

Just like her sisters Charlotte and Anne, Emily Brontë was inspired by the swirling, windswept Yorkshire Moors that surrounded her hometown of Haworth.

Although many of the interior shots were filmed in London’s Elstree studio, of course, director Emerald Fennel looked to the rugged Yorkshire Moors to do the majority of the filming and it provides a gothic, dark-skied and rain-drenched setting that perfectly captures the mood of the story. Here are some of the real-life Yorkshire filming locations you can visit yourself.

1. Old Gang Lead Mines, Swaledale, Yorkshire Dales National Park

Yorkshire’s early 19th-century Old Gang Smelt Mill has a starring role in Fennel’s Wuthering Heights, with its ruined buildings and towering chimneys; the remains of Yorkshire’s Victorian lead mining industry. Suitably brooding and gothic, the ruined mine and the moorland track leading to Surrender Bridge features in the first few scenes of the film, with a horse-drawn carriage trundling through a narrow valley. Later in the film too, Cathy and Heathcliff are seen here walking through the mist (there is a lot of mist in the film). The dramatic, ruined buildings of the mines provide the bleak, windy backdrop for many of the key scenes in Wuthering Heights.

There are plenty of hikes aross the Yorkshire Moors that will take you via the Old Gang Lead Mines, where you can work around the ruined buildings of the smelt mill and peat store.

2. Bouldershaw Lane, Swaledale, Yorkshire Dales National Park

Nearby, the wide and open Bouldershaw Lane was used for a pivotal scene featuring Cathy in a white wedding gown, carrying a bouquet. This is just a short climb from the Old Gang Mine and can easily be included in a circular route of the area.

3. Low Row Village, Yorkshire Dales National Park

This small, typical moorland village in Swaledale features traditional Yorkshire stone cottages and sweeping moorland views, and captures the wild feel of Wuthering Heights in its entirety. The village was chosen as a filming location for the 2026 adaptation for its unspoiled houses and quiet lanes, and features in many of the village scenes in the film. Visitors here can walk to the ‘wily, windy moors’, and experience the real moorland that so inspired Emily and her sisters, before stopping at the 17th-century Punchbowl Inn, perfect for a post-walk pint.

4. Melbecks Moor, Swaledale, Yorkshire Dales National Park

This wide, sweeping moor was possibly used for many shots that show the principle characters looking out over the views of the surrounding landscape, in particular the brooding Heathcliff. The exposed, high moor is incredibly remote and is also strewn with remnants of lead mining including old buildings, roads and spoil heaps.

Melbeck Moor is in Upper Swaledale which is known for having Britain’s highest pub, Tan Hill Inn - another perfect pitstop after a day of brooding on the moors.

5. Booze Moor, Arkengarthdale, Yorkshire Dales National Park

Arkengarthdale is the northernmost dale in the Yorkshire Dales National Park, and the Wuthering Heights cast and crew had a base in Langthwaite, one of the few villages in the wild, remote valley, and home to the Red Lion Inn, where many shots of ITV’s We visit some of the other All Creatures Great and Small filming locations here. were filmed. From the village, the production would hike up to Booze Moor where the open Moorland was the background for many exterior shots in the film.

item.title: Inside the archive: Sanderson launches its new National Trust collection, inspired by Brockhampton
item.dc:creator: Katy Mclean
item.description: An exclusive preview of a new collaboration between Sanderson and the National Trust with a collection of prints, weaves and embroideries inspired by Herefordshire medieval manor, Brockhampton.
item.content:encoded:
Sanderson has had much success with its first collaboration with the National Trust, a collection which drew inspiration from a range of properties across the country, launching new incarnations of different period styles.

Readers may remember our exclusive first look in the February issue; now, the wait is over. Sanderson’s new National Trust collection is officially live, bringing the unique charm of its muse property to life, and we’re thrilled to share it with you once again."

The property at the heart of this exciting new offering is Brockhampton, a medieval manor house in Herefordshire. 'We were drawn to Brockhampton as it's quite a humble dwelling, as opposed to some of the grander properties in the National Trust's portfolio. It's also its 600th anniversary year, and it is celebrating 75 years under the guardianship of the National Trust, too', Rebecca Craig, lead designer at Sanderson, reveals, adding that the property and its grounds were a rich pool of inspiration. 'We liked its connection to nature and sustainability.'

The manor is part of 687 hectare estate, which is used for farming with nature-friendly, traditional techniques. The grounds are also home to the largest orchard in the National Trust's care, which recently had more than 700 fruit trees reinstated. Much conservation work has also been achieved, including the establishment of wildflower meadows and halting the decline of endangered species such as finches and house martins.

The blossom on the trees in the orchard, the animals that graze on the farmland and the native wildlife all informed many of the designs. Craig explains that rather than pieces being based on documents at the property - of which there were none as it has been updated and added to over the years - the design team gathered inspiration from the site and used it to adapt designs from the Sanderson archive as well as create new ones.

'The Brockhampton Estate toile is obviously a very literal translation of all the elements of the estate - the moat, manor house, chapel, meadows, orchard and farmland all feature.' She adds that other pieces were created to celebrate and capture the spirit of the place. 'The blossoms are very "Sanderson", so we naturally had to include them, and much of the countryside nature has an affinity with our signature looks!'

Designs such as the Paradise Woods toile were adapted from archive documents: 'We changed all the exotic birds on the original to native British birds seen in the grounds', explains Craig. 'The team also explored whimsical and witty ideas: 'Moat Birds was another existing print document I have longed to use and it captured that spirit of Brockhampton perfectly', notes Craig.

Leaning into the medieval history and character of Brockhampton, there are designs that lend a nod to medieval embroideries and tapestries, with Craig's personal favourite being Medieval Embroidery. 'It's got lots of quirky elements,' she enthuses. 'It's got elements from an original archive document from the 17th century and a medieval sampler. It shows flowers, goats, birds, and the stitching work is a showcase of incredible diversity of technique, giving it a real, handcrafted look. It looks well-executed, which always pleases me when it comes to seeing the final piece.'

Though there are elements of the collection that are evoking history, the curation of designs is such that a contemporary look can be achieved too. Craig advises, 'You can really embrace the medieval manor route or take a fresh approach - perhaps using Barneby Stripe for a cleaner modern look'. She adds, 'The collection is very accessible, in easy colours and good scales. Each piece also co-ordinates with others so it can be layered up. It's great to have several options to suit whatever your tastes are.'

In addition to interpreting the beauty and character of Brockhampton and bringing it to life through fabrics and wallpapers to grace our interiors, the collaboration also benefits the ongoing work of the National Trust. 'From the sales of the Sanderson x National Trust II collection, a minimum of £95,000 will go to the National Trust to help look after nature, beauty and history for everyone, forever.'

All fabrics Sanderson X National Trust II collection

item.title: The Simba Hybrid Mattress topper has elevated our mediocre mattress into a high-end hotel-level sleeping experience
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton
item.description: We tested the Simba Hybrid Mattress topper for 14 nights, here’s what we thought…
item.content:encoded:
As someone who had never actually had a mattress topper before, I have to admit I was intrigued to know what all the fuss was about and why they consistently top the lists of the best mattress toppers on the market.

We bought our mattress new last year when we moved into our house, but it’s not a particularly extravagant one - a budget-friendly king-sized was all that our deposit-stricken bank accounts could afford, and it’s been perfectly ok for us. However, the thought of an extra layer of comfort, of course, is always appealing - especially for my much more mattress-sensitive fiancé - his sleep requirements are almost Princess and the Pea-level high maintenance.

So receiving the Simba Hybrid Mattress topper to our door felt like a really indulgent treat, and I was itching to climb (our bed is already high up, so plus the mattress and topper, it was a real climb) into bed the day it arrived and put it to the test.

Simba Hybrid Mattress Topper specs

  • ​​RRP: From £299

  • Size tested: King W150 x L200 x D6.5 (Cm) RRP £399

  • Other sizes available: Single, Double, King and Super King (Prices range from £299 to £499)

  • Type of topper: Hybrid (4 layers in total; supersoft sleep surface, Fresh Simbatex foam comfort layer, up to 1900 aerocoil springs, high-density Certipur foam base)

  • Trial period: 200 nights (collection free of charge)

  • Guarantee: 10-year manufacturer’s warranty

  • Washable: Plush, breathable cover removes for easy machine washing

Simba Hybrid Mattress topper – delivery and unboxing experience

Delivery of our mattress topper was quick and efficient, as Simba offer free next-day delivery for all UK mainland orders, if you order by 1 pm the day before. However, the unboxing experience wasn’t so simple. The topper comes rolled up in a box, just as a mattress would, but it is much heavier than it looks. Our bedroom is the converted attic space, so lugging it up three flights of stairs wasn’t easy, and I would have struggled alone.

The topper arrived in a cardboard box, and inside was a vacuum-sealed plastic bag, bound up with tape. It was not as easy as we thought to unwrap it, as there was a lot of packaging to deal with.

But, when it was out of the cover, it unravelled out of its roll easily and inflated with a satisfactory hiss. We were amazed that something that looked so big - a king-sized topper - could have fit in that size box, but the tell-tale sign had been the weight of it.

The topper has elastic straps to attach it to your mattress, which it did snugly and with no problem. This also stops it slipping when you’re sleeping on it.

The topper also has a removable cover, so it’s easy to wash to keep it fresh.

What is the Simba Hybrid Mattress topper made of?

The Simba Hybrid® mattress topper felt like a level of luxury we needed, and it’s paid off with our sleep, too. The toppers feature a layer of Simba’s patented conical Aerocoill® microsprings that compress inwards as you move around, which provides a responsive level of support and comfort you just don’t get with a mattress alone. It’s cooling too, as the compression from the springs pushes air up through the topper, keeping it feeling fresh all night long.

1900 microsprings sound, well, painful and uncomfortable. But they’re actually just the size of a paperclip, so you can’t feel them at all. They’re also all underneath the topper’s plush cushioning layer of Simbatex® foam, which has five times more airflow than a normal memory foam mattress. It is highly flexible (ideal for sleepers who move around a lot) and feels luxurious. Even better is that their foam is CertiPUR® verified, which means it’s totally free from harmful materials like mercury or lead.

Is it worth buying the Simba Hybrid Mattress topper?

Despite the slightly hefty price tag, the Simba Hybrid topper will transform a standard mattress into an extremely luxurious and comfortable sleeping surface. Climbing into bed with that extra level of softness has been a real moment for the past two weeks we’ve had the topper, and we have both noticed significant improvements in our sleep. The coolness of the topper is also really noticeable, and I think come summertime, we will be extra grateful for it, especially being in the attic room that gets particularly warm.

For our inexpensive mattress, the topper has provided an extra level of support and comfort that has elevated our sleeping in ways we didn’t know we needed. After a tiring day, it’s a squishy, cushioned haven that relieves any aches and pains, and is supportive without being too firm. Without sounding cliché, it feels like sleeping in an expensive hotel bed, and I am genuinely excited to get into bed every day. Now we have the topper, I don’t know how I’d sleep without it.

item.title: 30 best Valentine’s Day gifts for 2026
item.dc:creator: Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: We share our roundup of some of the best Valentine’s Day gifts for him and her, from food and drink to flowers to Valentine’s day cards.
item.content:encoded:
Valentine’s Day is a wonderful excuse to spoil the ones you love with romantic offerings.

Here is a round-up of some luxurious Valentine’s Day gifts for loved ones, from food and drink to fragrance. If you’re looking for more inspiration, don’t miss our Valentine’s cocktail recipes, ten of the best UK florists and places to stay for a romantic getaway.

Best Valentine's Day gifts for 2026

A small, thoughtful gift for the home such as decorative plate or statement candlestick can be a perfect idea for celebrating Valentine’s Day and will bring a smile to your partner’s face every time they use it.

When is Valentine's Day 2026?

Valentine’s Day is celebrated annually on 14th February, and this year it falls on a Saturday.

What is the most popular gift on Valentine's Day?

When it comes to the most popular gifts for Valentine’s Day, it’s all about thoughtful gestures that show how much you care. Classic choices such as flowers, chocolates, and jewellery never go out of style.

However, more personal gifts like hand-written cards, bespoke homeware, or experiences to enjoy together are becoming increasingly favoured.

Ultimately, the best gift is one that reflects your partner’s personality and the unique bond you share.

item.title: Romantic weekend getaways: 13 of the best places to stay in England
item.dc:creator: Charlotte Dunford, Amber-Rose Hurst
item.description: From restored country manors to castles that were once graced by English royalty, we share 13 of the best romantic weekend getaways.
item.content:encoded:
Whether for a Valentine’s Day treat, or just an impromptu break, there’s a whole host of options for an elegant and relaxing weekend away in England.

From restored country manors to castles that were once graced by English royalty, we share 13 of the best romantic weekend getaways that are sure to impress.

For more inspiration for 14 February, discover our Valentine's Day gift guide.

13 romantic weekend getaways around the UK

Ashdown Park Hotel and Country Club, East Sussex

Located in the heart of Ashdown Forest, the inspiration for A. A. Milne’s Winnie The Pooh, Ashdown Park Hotel and Country Club is set in 186 acres of lakes, secret gardens, and woodland trails. Whether you prefer to relax under the tallest Wellingtonia tree in Sussex, take a stroll through the peaceful walled garden, or enjoy the sunshine on the jetty by the lake, Ashdown is a romantic slice of paradise.

Inside, 106 bedrooms and suites, each named after a tree species found in the grounds or forest, make the perfect place to relax before dinner at the two AA rosette Anderida Restaurant.

ashdownpark.com

Beaverbrook, Surrey

A late Victorian mansion, Beaverbrook was built among acres of Surrey parkland in 1866 and during the 1900s belonged to Lord Beaverbrook. With rooms available at The House, The Garden House and The Coach House, there’s a style to suit every type of guest. For those looking for a truly indulgent overnight stay, the Elizabeth Taylor suite is as glamorous as its namesake, with a four-poster king size bed, private balcony, and luxurious roll top bath.

For an elegant evening drink before dinner, The Robin’s Nest Bar offers a comforting retreat and boasts beautiful interiors designed by Nicola Harding.

beaverbrook.co.uk

Bedford Lodge Hotel & Spa, Newmarket

Originally a Georgian hunting lodge owned by the 6th Duke of Bedford, Bedford Lodge Hotel & Spa is now Newmarket’s only 4-star hotel. Playing an important part in history, the lodge was also home to Anna Maria Russell, Duchess of Bedford (wife of the 7th Duke), who is credited with creating the quintessentially English concept of Afternoon Tea in 1845. Now, the hotel houses the two AA rosette Squires Restaurant which specialises in modern dining and uses locally sourced produce where possible.

bedfordlodgehotel.co.uk

Bovey Castle, Devon

Located near the market town of Moretonhampstead, Bovey Castle is the perfect spot for romantic weekend getaways. The castle is set within Dartmoor National Park, surrounded by 275 acres of beautiful English countryside. There’s an array of exciting experiences on offer, from archery to clay pigeon shooting, or guests can explore nearby walks through ancient woodland, some of which lead to idyllic wild swimming spots.

Bovey castle houses 60 individually designed bedrooms, each decorated to feel luxurious, warm, and elegant.

boveycastle.com

Burleigh Court, Cotswolds

A boutique manor house hotel filled with country charm, Burleigh Court is set in the heart of Stroud’s picturesque Golden Valley, making it the perfect spot for a romantic weekend getaway. Burleigh houses 18 rooms, with styles ranging from classic to cosy to superior.

The perfect place to retreat from the busyness of everyday life, the hotel offers in-room treatments, nearby countryside walks and locally sourced food at the two AA rosette restaurant.

burleighcourtcotswolds.co.uk

Ellenborough Park, Gloucestershire

Steeped in history and nestled in the rolling Cotswolds, Ellenborough Park offers an enchanting escape for couples seeking luxury and tranquillity. Boasting 90 acres of glorious grounds, this timeless country house hotel has stood for over 500 years, blending heritage charm with modern comforts.

Indulge in exquisite seasonal dining, unwind in the serene spa, and explore the breathtaking countryside. With elegant rooms, crackling fireplaces, and warm hospitality, Ellenborough Park promises a memorable getaway filled with relaxation, fine food, and refined indulgence

ellenboroughpark.com

Farlam Hall, Cumbria

Tucked away in the picturesque Cumbrian countryside, Farlam Hall is the perfect setting for a romantic escape. This elegant country house hotel offers a spectacular twelve-course Valentine's tasting menu by Chef Patron Hrishi Desai, featuring French-inspired dishes such as truffle soup, Atlantic halibut, and Black Forest Gateau.

With its enchanting kitchen gardens, warm hospitality, and proximity to the Lake District and Hadrian’s Wall, Farlam Hall provides an unforgettable blend of fine dining and idyllic surroundings for a truly special celebration.

farlamhall.com

Grantley Hall, Yorkshire Dales

With views of the rolling Yorkshire hills, its own English Heritage-listed Japanese garden and 47 exquisitely decorated rooms, Grantley Hall has everything you need for a romantic weekend break. The luxury hotel and wellness retreat also houses five indulgent restaurants, three bars and a spa, so you can relax and unwind in style.

Grantley has a colourful past, with members of parliament, artists, entertainers, and royalty all having graced its halls. It was built towards the end of the 17th century in a Palladian architectural style.

grantleyhall.co.uk

The Grove Hotel, Hertfordshire

Set in the rolling Hertfordshire hills, and just 18 miles from London, The Grove Hotel offers the perfect country escape. The private resort features five dining outlets, three bars, a spa, golf course and selection of luxury event spaces.

Showcasing sleek and stylish interiors designed by Martin Hulbert, the traditional 18th-century building has a luxury modern feel. Art is a key feature throughout the interiors, from miniature sculptures to dried flower displays.

thegrove.co.uk

Thornbury Castle, Gloucestershire

A one-of-a-kind destination, Thornbury Castle in Gloucestershire was once a beloved retreat for Henry VIII and Anne Boleyn, and today guests can stay in the same room The King and Queen shared in 1535.

With exquisitely decorated rooms, a collection of beautifully kept gardens and a delectable menu, Thornbury Castle is the perfect place for a romantic weekend getaway.

thornburycastle.co.uk

Tylney Hall Hotel & Gardens, Hampshire

Sitting in 66 acres of Hampshire countryside, Tylney Hall Hotel & Gardens is a grand Grade-II listed Victorian building. With 112 bedrooms and suites, all decorated in a traditional style to remain in-keeping with the hotel’s heritage, guests can expect a comfortable, relaxing stay. One of the highlights of the country house hotel is the gardens designed by British Horticulturalist Gertrude Jekyll, including striking water features and lakes, an Italian garden, wild orchid meadow and azalea and rose gardens.

Dinner is a treat at the two AA rosette Oak Room Restaurant, while an elegant Afternoon Tea is served in the lounge which features an ornate ceiling carefully relocated, piece by piece, from The Grimation Palace in Florence.

tylneyhall.co.uk

The Vineyard, Berkshire

Just an hour from London, The Vineyard is a dream destination for wine lovers and romantics alike. This Valentine’s, the luxurious Taste of Valentine's Day package offers Champagne on arrival, a five-course dinner by chef Tom Scade, indulgent spa time, and an elegant overnight stay.

Home to a 30,000-bottle wine cellar, world-class dining, and an exceptional spa, The Vineyard is a 5-star luxury hotel that provides the ultimate romantic retreat, whether savouring fine vintages or unwinding in style.

the-vineyard.co.uk

The White House, Norfolk

Considered by many to be one of the best boutique hotels in Norfolk, The White House offers a serene retreat in the coastal countryside. A restored country manor, The White House’s bedrooms are decorated to ensure a peaceful, comfortable stay, making the hotel a great spot for private romantic weekend getaways.

The White House is situated in an area of picturesque farmland between Burnham Market and Brancaster Beach, and with guided walks, picnic hampers available to order, a local winery and more, there’s plenty to do and see on your stay.

stayatthewhitehouse.co.uk

item.title: Eight Valentines cocktail recipes for a romantic evening at home
item.dc:creator: Charlotte Dunford
item.description: We’ve compiled the recipes for our favourite Valentines cocktails, from ruby red concoctions to cocktails for two, for a romantic evening at home.
item.content:encoded:
Delight and impress with these elegant Valentine’s Day cocktails this February 14.

From fruity cocktails in ruby red tones to cocktails made for couples, these drinks are the perfect choice to impress and delight your special someone. Explore eight of the best Valentine's cocktail recipes below.

Eight cocktail recipes for a romantic date night

Frozen Margarita cocktail

Full of flavour and with an alluring rich red colour, this chilled cocktail is the perfect after-dinner drink.

Serves 2

Ingredients

  • 2 chilled glasses, (optional) salt or granulated sugar rimmed

  • 4oz good quality tequila, such as Patron Reposado

  • 10oz frozen raspberries

  • 8oz Ocean Spray Cranberry & Raspberry juice drink

  • Juice from 1/2 lime

  • Agave to sweeten, to taste

  • Lime rind, to garnish

  • Pomegranate seeds, to garnish

Method

  1. Put all ingredients into a smoothie blender and blend.

  2. Sweeten with the Agave to taste.

  3. Pour into chilled glasses (dipped in lime and salt or granulated sugar if desired).

  4. Twist some lime rind for decoration and top with a sprinkle of pomegranate seeds.

Recipe sourced from Ocean Spray.

Strawberry Fizz cocktail

The fresh strawberries and cranberry juice give this easy-to-prepare cocktail a beautiful ruby red colour and a sweet fruity taste.

Serves 1

Ingredients

  • 35ml vodka

  • Handful of fresh strawberries, plus extra for garnish

  • 50ml cranberry juice

  • 1 shot of sugar syrup

  • Prosecco, to finish

Method

  1. Take the strawberries and muddle them in a cocktail shaker.

  2. Add the vodka, sugar syrup, cranberry juice and a handful of ice. Shake well.

  3. Strain into a pre-cooled cocktail glass and top up with Prosecco.

  4. Garnish with a strawberry stuck on the rim of the glass.

Recipe sourced from Mark Hartstone at La Fosse at Cranborne.

Raspberry Martini cocktail

Undoubtedly elegant, this raspberry cocktail recipe is sure to impress your other half. The colourful garnish of a mint leaf and raspberry makes the perfect finishing touch.

Serves 1

Ingredients

  • 65ml Masons British Raspberry Gin

  • 35ml lemon juice

  • 15ml Lillet Rouge (or other sweet vermouth)

  • 20ml hibiscus syrup

  • Foamer (quarter pipette) or egg white

  • 3 mint leaves

  • 4 raspberries, plus 1 to garnish

Method

  1. Combine the raspberries, mint leaves and hibiscus syrup into a shaker and muddle.

  2. Add all other ingredients and dry shake.

  3. Add a handful of ice into the shaker and shake vigorously for 10-15 seconds.

  4. Fine strain into a coupe glass.

  5. Garnish with a fresh raspberry and mint leaf on a cocktail stick.

Recipe sourced from Masons of Yorkshire.

Bramble cocktail

Bursting with flavours of raspberry and blackberry, this Bramble cocktail recipe is sweet and fruity.

Serves 1

Ingredients

  • 50ml Bombay Bramble Gin

  • 25ml fresh lemon juice

  • 12.5ml sugar syrup

To garnish

  • Lemon wedge

  • Blackberries

Method

  1. Add all the ingredients into a rocks glass one by one.

  2. Top with crushed ice.

  3. Garnish with a fresh lemon wedge and blackberry.

Recipe sourced from Bombay Sapphire.

Iced Tea Gin cocktail

Simple yet sophisticated, the tea-infused gin in this recipe brings a unique and refreshing twist to this cocktail recipe.

Serves 1

Ingredients

  • 50ml Masons Tea Edition Gin

  • 25ml Monin cloudy lemonade

  • 100ml soda

To garnish

  • Lemon twist

Method

  1. Fill a highball glass with crushed ice.

  2. Add the gin and lemonade and mix.

  3. Top up the glass with soda and garnish with a lemon twist.

Recipe sourced from Masons of Yorkshire.

Grape Martinez cocktail

The deep burgundy colour of this Valentine’s cocktail makes it too tempting to resist!

Serves 1

Ingredients

  • 45ml G’Vine Floraison

  • 2 tsp lemon juice

  • 1 tbsp water

  • 10-15 red grapes, plus two for garnish

  • 1/2 tsp sugar

  • 30ml sweet vermouth

Method

  1. In a blender, combine the grapes, water, gin, lemon juice, sugar and sweet vermouth. Blend until smooth.

  2. Pour into a shaker filled with ice and shake for about a minute.

  3. Strain into a small glass. Garnish with two grapes on a toothpick.

Recipe sourced from maisonvillevert.com.

Seabird cocktail for two

The perfect choice for a romantic Valentine’s evening, this cocktail is made for sharing. The red grapefruit peel garnish is a chic final touch.

Serves 2

Ingredients

  • 100ml Salcombe Gin ‘Start Point’

  • 100ml fresh red grapefruit juice

  • 25ml lime juice

  • 25ml agave syrup

To garnish

  • Red grapefruit peel

  • ‘Seamist’ liquid garnish

Method

  1. Prepare your garnish before juicing the red grapefruit.

  2. Add the gin, grapefruit juice, lime juice and agave syrup to a shaker filled with plenty of ice and shake vigorously for 10 seconds.

  3. Double strain into two chilled Nick & Nora glasses.

  4. Garnish with red grapefruit peel on the side of the glass and 5 sprays of ‘Seamist’ liquid garnish.

Recipe sourced from salcombegin.com.

Devon Spritz cocktail

This Valentine’s cocktail recipe is easy to make and refreshing to taste. The hint of rose petals and lemon give it a delicate flavour.

Serves 1

Ingredients

  • 25ml Devon Coke vodka, or other Vodka

  • 50ml Luscombe Damascene Rose Bubbly

  • The Uncommon’s Bubble White Wine, to finish

To garnish

  • Grapefruit slice

Method

  1. Combine a single shot of Devon Cove Vodka with a double of Luscombe’s Damascene Rose Bubbly, into a wine or Champagne glass.

  2. Top up with The Uncommon’s Bubbly White Wine.

  3. Garnish with a thin slice of grapefruit.

Recipe sourced from Louis Xavier Lewis-Smith at Luscombe.

item.title: 11 of our favourite florists around the UK
item.dc:creator: Gaelle Biguenet
item.description: Whether you want to add a dash of colour to your home or find a special gift, here are the best places to buy beautiful bouquets.
item.content:encoded:
Flowers have such emotive power that they are used for any occasion, whether you want to express love, grief or celebrate a lifelong commitment. It takes a special kind of person who has a sensibility to colour, composition and form, to make such an evocative flower arrangement.

A skilled florist knows how to pick the right flowers and arrange them harmoniously to create a design that communicates the desired emotion. From regenerative farms to independent flower shops, here are 11 of the best places to buy beautiful bouquets.

For more gifting inspiraton, discover our Valentine's Day gift guide.

11 of the best florists in the UK

Lavender Green, London

Located in the heart of King’s Road, Lavender Green has been a leader in luxury floristry since 1990. Renowned for its trend-setting designs, the florist has worked with prestigious brands like Chanel, Ferrari, and Wedgwood, as well as celebrities and royalty. Led by Creative Director Sue Barnes and Managing Director Colin Gray, their expert team combines creativity and craftsmanship to create stunning floral arrangements for high-profile clients.

lavendergreen.co.uk

Bramble & Wild, Somerset

Nestled among independent businesses on Catherine Hill in Frome, Bramble & Wild has been crafting exquisite floral designs since 2011. Their signature style - rustic, romantic, and organic - reflects a commitment to sustainability, with recyclable packaging and composted stem cuttings. To ensure the freshest blooms, they collaborate with local growers and cultivate their own cutting patch.

With a passion for floristry and eco-conscious practices, Bramble & Wild remains a cherished presence in Frome’s artisan quarter, creating beautiful, thoughtfully sourced arrangements year-round.

brambleandwild.com

Grace & Thorn, London

It was the Fiddle Leaf Fig tree in her grandparents’ home that inspired Nik to open a flower shop. As a child, she was mesmerised by the idea that a tree could grow within the walls of a home. Each elegantly crafted bouquet at Grace & Thorn is borne out of this city girl’s wish to adorn English homes with the beauty of nature.

graceandthorn.com

Snapdragon, Edinburgh

Even through the bustle of Edinburgh’s Bruntsfield area, Snapdragon is hard to miss. Passersby are greeted by the small shop’s charming front, ornate with colourful bouquets and house plants. Rich aromas of freshly cut roses and scented candles lure you in, where you’ll find rustic bouquets made with local flowers.

snapdragonedinburgh.com

Little Garden, Leamington Spa, Warwickshire

This little gem is one of the few flower shops to strictly sell British stems. Their love of nature not only manifests in their beautiful, seasonal flowers – all of which are grown in their own Little Garden or at a local farm – but also through their efforts to protect it. Inside their Leamington Spa shop, you will find a vibrant array of indoor and outdoor plants, as well as skilfully hand-made bouquets.

littlegardenflowers.co.uk

Wildbunch Florists, Alresford

When she founded Wildbunch Florist, Juliet was driven by a humble mission: to provide fresh and local flowers to her community in the small town of Alresford. Since then, the little shop has blossomed into a leading florist in the Hampshire area. They now grow their own flowers – Dahlias in the summer and Narcissus in the winter – in the heart of the Hampshire countryside.

wildbunchflorists.co.uk

Smith & Munson, Spalding

This family-run farm has been growing and selling fresh flowers for five generations. Tulips and lilies are their specialty, and for every tone on the colour chart they have a flower, from the classic white and red British tulips to the Columbus Pink Double British tulips, which ooze romance.

smithandmunson.com

Theodora’s Flowers, Corsham

The 2021 and 2022 winner of Best Florist in Wiltshire, Theodora’s Flowers is run by a family of flower-enthusiasts. Each bouquet is tailor-made with passion and expertise accrued over 35 years. Whether you want to add an uplifting dash of colour in your home or buy a gift for someone special, their hand-made bouquets suit every taste.

theodorasflowers.co.uk

Stem & Green, Winchester

It was his frustration with the lack of sustainably grown flowers available on the market that spurred James Greig to start his own flower farm. The wide variety of traditional scented English garden flowers at Stem & Green are granted breathing space to grow and wilt with the seasons, free from harmful lights, chemicals and heating.

stemandgreen.co.uk

Petalon, Cornwall

What started out as a bicycle delivery business in East London slowly grew into a flower farm when Florence Kennedy and her family moved to the Cornish countryside. Instead of cycling to the flower market every morning, they now have a budding flower field of their own. Ordering from Petalon. contributes to protecting the environment, as 100% of their profits go to UK conservation projects.

petalon.co.uk

The Hampshire Florist, Otterbourne

With over 20 years of floristry experience, Kelly Earle brings her expertise to each bouquet and bespoke arrangement. She is known for her elegant and romantic signature style, which draws inspiration from the English garden and nature. Only the freshest blooms are used, and each bouquet is hand-tied and hand delivered.

thehampshireflorist.co.uk

item.title: Best waffle makers, tried and tested: From 3-in-1 snack makers to stovetop irons
item.dc:creator: Chris Jefferies
item.description: While a staple of the indulgent weekend breakfast, the elevated texture of a homemade waffle requires exact heat and timing - making the choice of the best waffle maker essential.
item.content:encoded:
Few things define a luxurious breakfast quite like the aroma of freshly cooked waffles; though perhaps more of a weekend treat or a special occasion indulgence, with one of the best waffle makers in your kitchen, you can produce a consistent batch every time.

When shopping for waffle makers, there are a few things to consider. If you’re a toasted sandwich fan, you can plump for a 3-in-1 waffle maker that can also cook paninis and toasties. Or if you’re focused on waffle perfection, there are models with variable browning control, so you can perfect that crisp exterior/bubbly interior combination.

As a devoted waffle maker owner (my vintage Salter 3-in-1 was recently retired after many years’ faithful service when I dropped it and broke its clasp), I was excited to see what the class of 2026 has to offer.

And to help you get the best waffles every time, we’ve got expert advice from Mark McShane, chef and founder of Food Hygiene Certificate.

At a glance - the best waffle makers for 2026

  • Best overall: Russell Hobbs Creations 3 in 1 Sandwich Maker

  • Best budget waffle maker: Salter Deep Fill Waffle Maker

  • Best waffle maker for families: Lakeland No Mess Electric Waffle Maker

  • Best compact waffle maker: Giles & Posner Bubble Waffle Maker

  • Best waffle maker for versatility: Salter 3-in-1 snack maker

  • Best stovetop waffle maker: ProCook Stovetop waffle maker

How we tested

Over a four-week period, we tested all six waffle makers using their provided recipes to feed a family of three. These ranged from sweet pudding waffles to savoury grated vegetable waffles. We measured all the waffle makers for size, weight and maximum temperature.

We also hosted a waffle party for dozens of neighbours, testing all six waffle makers side-by-side over two hours, cooking a total of 40 waffles using a generic 15% sugar batter. Finally, we tried a gluten-free recipe using corn flour instead of wheat.

The best waffle makers for 2026

1. Russell Hobbs Creations 3 in 1 Sandwich Maker

Overall rating: 5/5

Best overall waffle maker

Also available from Argos

  • Power: 750 W

  • Waffle capacity: 2

  • Plate type: Non-stick coated metal

At first, I was sceptical of this model, as it had a higher price and yet a smaller cooking surface than the Salter. However, its performance was consistently excellent on everything from sweet waffles to puff pastry baklava pockets, and from potato waffles to cheese toasties.

The non-stick was very reliable, the pre-heat temperature was second highest on test, and it was the joint quickest, despite being the cheapest to run, drawing just 750w. The Russell Hobbs fully justifies its RRP, and the smaller size makes it easier to store. Consider me a convert!

RRP£55
Shop now

2. Salter Deep Fill Waffle Maker

Overall rating: 4.5/5

Best budget waffle maker

Also available from Amazon

  • Power: 1,600 W

  • Waffle capacity: 2

  • Plate type: Non-stick coated iron

Temperature control is this waffle maker’s trump card. Whilst we settled on halfway between Mid and Max for sweet waffles, we found the Max setting very useful for achieving crispy potato waffles, which many other models on test simply couldn’t manage.

The lack of overflow gulley means you run the risk of making a mess, but once we had established a 3tbsp per waffle maximum, this wasn’t a major issue.

RRP£39.99
Shop now

3. Lakeland No Mess Electric Waffle Maker

Overall rating: 4/5

Best waffle maker for families

Also available from Amazon

  • Power: 1,200 W

  • Waffle capacity: 4

  • Plate type: Die-cast aluminium plates with Tefal non-stick coating

The largest waffle maker on test, this model from Lakeland packs in enough cooking space for four square waffles, plus an overflow gulley on each side.

After 3 minutes of pre-heating and 5 minutes of cooking, four crispy and fluffy waffles came out easily, even without any oil on the plates, thanks to the Teflon non-stick coating. The overflow area, whilst sizeable, is not unlimited - 3tbsp of batter per waffle is the maximum this waffle maker can hold.

RRP£59.99
Shop now

4. Giles & Posner Bubble Waffle Maker

Overall rating: 4/5

Best compact waffle maker

  • Power: 750 W

  • Waffle capacity: 1

  • Plate type: PFAS-free non-stick coated aluminium

The joint fastest waffle maker on test, this model popped out flexible pudding waffles in just 3 minutes, which could be rolled into a cone shape and filled with decadent toppings.

The supplied recipe requires some specialist ingredients, such as evaporated milk and custard powder, but we found this waffle maker worked just as well with a generic recipe or even the gluten-free alternative. However, this is the only waffle maker on test that can only do one at a time, which could be an issue when catering for parties or large families.

RRP£19.99
Shop now

5. Salter 3-in-1 Snack Maker

Overall rating: 3.5/5

Best waffle maker for versatility

  • Power: 900 W

  • Waffle capacity: 2

  • Plate type: Non-stick coated aluminium

A tried and tested favourite waffle maker in my household, having already tested one to destruction over the past 5 years (the retaining clip was the first part to go, but only after accidentally dropping it onto a tiled floor). This versatile model can handle everything from paninis to quesadillas and from cheese toasties to sweet waffles (2 at a time).

However, it had the lowest pre-heat temperature on test and struggled to turn out a crispy potato waffle. On the plus side, the removable plates make it easy to clean, and the non-stick coating performed consistently well.

RRP£34.99
Shop now

6. ProCook Stovetop Waffle Maker

Overall rating: 3/5

Best stovetop waffle maker

  • Power: N/A

  • Waffle capacity: 2

  • Plate type: Non-stick ceramic

The simplest waffle maker on test, and yet the one that requires the most skill to wield. As browning control is determined by the temperature of your (gas or electric) hob, there is no defined pre-heat time. You’ll need to use your nose and be prepared to burn a couple of waffles before you get the hang of it - our infrared thermometer proved to be an invaluable ally when cooking with this waffle iron.

Three tbsp of batter is enough to fill it up - be careful not to overfill, or you’ll get a nasty burned-on stain on your hob to clean up. That said, this was the smallest waffle maker on test by far, so it’s ideal if storage space is limited in your kitchen.

RRP£25
Shop now

Waffle maker FAQs

Once you’ve got the right recipe (most waffle makers come with a recommended one) and the right technique, you can knock out a perfect batch of waffles every time in under ten minutes, including pre-heating time.

Wet and dry ingredients should be “mixed separately and then combined gently to prevent over-mixing, which can produce tough and not so light and fluffy waffles”, advises chef Mark McShane.

“I would also recommend letting the batter rest for about 5–10 minutes to allow the flour to absorb moisture completely, and the raising agents to become active.”

“Temperature control is a must,” he continues. “Preheat your waffle maker fully so that the waffles can be cooked evenly and develop a golden, crispy outer layer while remaining soft inside.”

Once the waffle is brown and firm, you can pop it out of the waffle maker using a plastic or wooden spatula - avoid using metal implements as they can scratch the non-stick coating.

,

For a perfectly crisp, browned waffle, the cooking times on the waffle makers we tested ranged from three to eight minutes. Ignore the lights on the top; they are for pre-heating only.

However, if you’re unsure, just wait until the steam has stopped coming out of the waffle maker before opening.

,

Waffles aren’t just for breakfast, and indeed, if you’re investing in one of the best waffle makers on test, you’re going to want to use it as often as you can.

For this test, we tried a wide variety of recipes, from courgette and parmesan waffles, topped with fried eggs and pesto, to potato and cheddar waffles topped with pulled pork and cream cheese. Puff pastry baklava waffles with lemon and honey were another favourite.

“When it comes to toppings, think of the balancing,” suggests McShane. “A little butter, fresh fruits or syrup complements the waffle rather than overpowers it.”

,

If your waffle maker has removable plates, they can be washed by hand in a basin of warm soapy water, but don’t be tempted to pop them in the dishwasher as that will strip off the non-stick coating, as will scourers or wire wool.

If the plates are not removable, wait until the waffle maker has completely cooled down, then wipe off any excess batter with a damp kitchen cloth and a little washing-up liquid.

,

No - unless your waffle maker is described as a 3-in-1 snack maker or a waffle maker/pancake maker, then the plates are most likely built-in, which makes cleaning slightly more difficult.

,

It depends on the size of your family and how much available storage space you have in your kitchen. The Lakeland waffle maker we tested could make 4 waffles at a time, which would be ideal for a larger family.

However, you can just as easily cook for a family in a smaller waffle maker; it will just take a little longer, and you may want to keep your first few batches warm in an oven pre-heated to 50°C.

,

Pre-greasing your waffle maker with a little cooking oil or melted butter will help avoid sticking; however, all of the waffle makers we tested had non-stick coating that made this unnecessary. Eventually, you may need to introduce oil as all non-stick coatings will degrade over time.

,

It depends on what types of waffles you want to make. If you’re happy with simple, sweet waffles only, then a single temperature model will be sufficient, but if you want to branch out and make savoury waffles with grated courgette or potato, then it’s worth looking for a model with variable temperature control, such as the Salter Deep-Fill.

item.title: Airbnb are offering stays in Cathy’s candy floss-pink bedroom from Wuthering Heights - and it’s free
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton
item.description: To celebrate the release of the new Wuthering Heights film starring Margot Robbie and Jacob Elordi, Airbnb are offering fans the chance to book into Cathy Earnshaw’s real-life Yorkshire bedroom...
item.content:encoded:
To celebrate the release of the much-anticipated new film adaptation of Emily Brontë's Wuthering Heights, Airbnb are offering a few lucky guests the chance of a life time: to stay in an an exact replica of Cathy’s Thrushcross Grange bedroom in the film, and it's completely free.

Between Friday 27 February and Wednesday 4 March 2026, there are three separate stays up for grabs, for up to two guests each, with bookings opening on 20 February 2026 at 12 GMT here. And they are totally free.

The new film, which has been directed by Emerald Fennell (Saltburn, Promising Young Woman), and will be released in cinemas on February 13, has garnered much press attention for its maximalist, modern take on one of the best-loved novels of all time.

But Wuthering Heights is no stranger to divided opinions, having both enticed and appalled readers in equal measure since its release in 1847, when Emily Brontë published it under the pseudonym Ellis Bell.

What is Wuthering Heights about?

Telling the tragic love story of Cathy and Heathcliff all set on the swirling moors of Brontë's native Yorkshire, Wuthering Heights was to be Emily's only novel and it's themes of mental and physical cruelty and domestic abuse, brazenly challenged strict Victorian morality and shocked many, let alone the fact that it had actually been written by a woman.

Fennell's controversial take on the novel, with almost Barbie-esque Victoriana costumes and Charli XCX soundtrack, has caused quite the stir, but we're sure it is guaranteed to be a box-office hit thanks to the story's endless appeal and it's stellar cast of actors.

Your chance to stay in the Wuthering Heights bedroom courtesy of Airbnb

For fans of the film Airbnb are now offering a way to get even closer into the beloved story, by staying in an exact replica bedroom of the one Margot Robbie filmed in as Cathy just minutes from the moors where Emily Brontë herself was born, lived and found her inspiration.

The Airbnb listing says:

'I’m Cathy, the lady of the manor Wuthering Heights on the Yorkshire moors, but I’ve left my once-majestic, now crumbling home behind to live in luxury as I deserve, at Thrushcross Grange. Edgar and Isabella’s exquisite home is filled with beautiful things, delectable food and the finest clothes. Come stay in the bedroom they curated just for me, featuring elements that are uniquely… mine. If only Heathcliff could see me now.'

The special stay is within the grounds of Holdsworth House Hotel and Restaurant - a historic hideout in Halifax that once played host to the Beatles and which was home to the cast of Wuthering Heights during their filming.

The candy-floss coloured bedroom is a real look into Fennell's mind when she was imagining what her version of the story looked like and for the lucky guests that get to stay their, they will truly feel they are living in Cathy's world.

The stay also includes a special Wuthering Heights-themed curated itinerary inspired by Brontë's fictional world, from guided horseback riding across the surrounding Moors, a traditional Yorkshire afternoon tea, a visit to the Brontë Parsonage Museum where Emily and her sisters lived and wrote, an intimate candlelit dinner and a private listening session of the film's Charli XCX's soundtrack, all before being tucked into Catherine's marshmallow-like bed.

As the listing on Airbnb, which is hosted by Cathy herself, says: 'To wake with the mist on the moors and wander where my heart still yearns, this stay is not to be missed.'

Guests can book their stay from 12pm GMT on Friday 20 February here. Bookings are on a first-come, first-served basis. The experience is totally free and three couples will be selected to experience it. The stay is for two guests for three nights and it includes breakfast, lunch and dinner. Travel is not included.

This article was first published on DiscoverBritain.com

item.title: The best non-stick frying pans for 2026, tried and tested
item.dc:creator: Anna-Lisa De'Ath
item.description: Make everyday cooking a pleasure with our pick of the very best non-stick skillets and sauté pans.
item.content:encoded:
As one of the hardest-working pieces of equipment in any home cook’s armoury, a high-quality non-stick frying pan can be worth its weight in gold.

A well-made pan with an effective non-stick coating will not only improve the results of your cooking, whether that’s frying, sautéing or searing, but it will also make cleaning the pan quicker, easier and less messy.

Pans with a poor non-stick coating and ineffective conductivity can result in food sticking and uneven cooking, while bad construction and design can result in warped pans, or worse still, peeling non-stick coatings.

To discover which pans deserve their place in your batterie de cuisine, we put a wide range of non-stick frying pans through their paces in our home kitchen to see how well they performed across a number of everyday cooking tasks, how versatile they were and their durability.

At a glance - the best non-stick frying pans for 2026

  • Best overall: Our Place Always Pan® 2.0

  • Best non-stick frying pan for quality build: Kuhn Rikon Culinary Five-Ply Non-Stick Frying Pan

  • Best investment non-stick frying pan: Hexclad 3-Litre Hybrid Deep Sauté Pan with Lid

  • Best affordable non-stick frying pan: ProCook Elite Forged Non-stick Frying Pan

  • Best non-stick frying pan for scratch-resistance: Circulon ScratchDefense Multi-Layer Stainless Steel Extreme Non-Stick Induction Frying Pan

  • Best non-stick frying pan for sustainability: GreenPan Padova Smokey Sky Blue Two-Piece Frying Pan Set

  • Best lightweight non-stick frying pan: Kyocera Ceramic-Coated Non-Stick Frying Pan

  • Best non-stick frying pan for affordable luxury: M&S X Tom Kerridge Stainless Steel Non-Stick Frying Pan

  • Best large non-stick frying pan: Stellar Rocktanium Non-Stick Frying Pan

  • Best budget non-stick frying pan set: Cermalon Triply Non-Stick Three-Piece Frying Pan Set

How we tested

When testing this selection of non-stick frying pans, we took into account the following factors:

  • Non-stick performance (how well food releases)

  • Heat distribution and retention

  • Durability and scratch resistance

  • Ease of cleaning (including dishwasher safety)

  • Handle comfort and safety, and the general weight of the pan

  • Suitability for induction or other hob types

  • Oven safety (if applicable)

  • Value for money

Some non-stick pans have an excellent coating that allows food to slide off easily without the need for any oil, but others can tend to stick to the food when they're cooking. We dry-fried eggs to find out how good their non-stick performance was without using any oil.

A good frying pan should heat up evenly across the base, so food will cook uniformly and brown well all over. To test this. We made crêpes to check how evenly the heat spread across the pan. We also fried bacon to see how quickly the pan heated up and how well it seared.

After these initial tests, we used the pans for cooking a range of foods to see how the non-stick performed, as well as how easy it was to wash the pans. As most non-stick pans are recommended for use with a low to medium heat, we did all our cooking tests using a medium heat setting on the hob.

We also considered the materials the pans were made from, whether they were dishwasher-safe and oven-proof, if they were available in a range of sizes and any warranties and guarantees they may come with.

Best non-stick frying pans for 2026

1. Our Place Always Pan® 2.0

Overall rating: 5/5

Best overall

  • Material: Recycled aluminium

  • Handle material: Recycled aluminium

  • Coating type (PTFE, ceramic, hybrid): Ceramic

  • Size tested: 26.7cm

  • Induction compatibility: Yes

  • Oven-safe temperature: 230ºC

  • Dishwasher-safe: No

  • Weight: 1029g

  • Lid included: Yes

This stylish 10-in-1 pan is as functional as it is attractive, arriving with a modular lid, a steamer basket that doubles as a colander, and a clever beechwood spatula that "parks" directly on the handle. Constructed from 100% post-consumer recycled aluminium and finished with a non-toxic, PFAS-free ceramic coating, it is an excellent choice for the eco-conscious cook.

In our testing, the pan heated quickly and performed impressively; eggs and pancakes slid off the surface with almost no oil, and bacon cooked perfectly even.

While the 7cm-deep sides are ideal for curries and stir-fries, we did find the main handle a bit square and uncomfortable to grip. However, this is largely offset by the inclusion of a sturdy helper handle and dual pouring lips for easy decanting.

Although it requires hand-washing, the non-stick surface is so effective that cleanup is effortless without any scrubbing. Despite the higher price tag, its ability to replace several specialised pieces of cookware makes it a versatile, space-saving investment that justifies the cost through its performance and sustainable build.

RRP£125
Shop now

2. Kuhn Rikon Culinary Five-Ply Non-Stick Frying Pan

Overall rating: 5/5

Best non-stick frying pan for quality build

  • Material: Stainless steel/aluminium alloy

  • Handle material: Stainless steel

  • Coating type (PTFE, ceramic, hybrid): PTFE

  • Size: 24cm

  • Induction compatibility: Yes

  • Oven-safe temperature: 240ºC

  • Dishwasher-safe: Yes

  • Weight: 1075g

  • Lid included: No

This was one of the heavier pans we tested. Although it felt robustly made, the stainless steel handle wasn’t particularly comfortable to hold due to its straight top edge. The pan is made from a 3.2mm thick, five-ply material; this special combination of stainless steel and aluminium alloys distributes heat evenly over the entire surface right up to the rim. The closed rim had no sharp edges, making it easier to pour sauces, and the pan is available in 20cm, 24cm, and 28cm sizes for all hob types.

We found it heated up really quickly and retained that heat well. Dry-fried eggs didn’t stick at all, while pancakes and bacon cooked quickly, evenly, and browned well. After cooking, the non-stick surface was very easy to clean by hand, although the pan is also dishwasher safe. Overall, this pan felt very well made with pared-back looks suitable for a professional kitchen. Its lifetime guarantee gives confidence that, although it’s an investment purchase, it will perform well for many years to come.

RRP£112.95
Shop now

3. Hexclad 3-Litre Hybrid Deep Sauté Pan with Lid

Overall rating: 4.5/5

Best investment

  • Material: Stainless steel

  • Handle material: Stainless steel

  • Coating type (PTFE, ceramic, hybrid): Hybrid

  • Size: 30cm

  • Induction compatibility: Yes

  • Oven-safe temperature: 480ºC, lid oven-safe up to 200ºC

  • Dishwasher-safe: Yes

  • Weight: 1476g

  • Lid included: Yes

This frying pan is a serious piece of equipment, arriving in protective drawstring bags that signal its high quality. A favorite of chefs like Gordon Ramsay, its hybrid technology combines stainless steel with a TerraBond™ ceramic non-stick coating and tri-ply construction for quick, even heating. The stay-cool long handle and extra carrying handle provide stability, while high, straight sides reduce spatter when cooking large family meals.

The hybrid design delivered fantastic searing power, though our dry-fried egg needed slight encouragement before gliding. With a smear of oil, pancakes cooked evenly and browned nicely. This robust pan is tough enough for metal utensils and includes a well-made glass lid with a steam vent. While easy to wash by hand, it is also dishwasher safe.

Brian Hennessy, Head Chef at The Landmark London, says: “The best non-stick pans are HexClad... these are used in all the top restaurants and are truly worth the investment. The unique hybrid design combining stainless steel searing power with a non-stick surface for easy cleanup, makes them durable, metal-safe for the oven, as well as dishwasher safe.”

RRP£169
Shop now

4. ProCook Elite Forged Non-stick Frying Pan

Overall rating: 4.5/5

What to look for when buying a non-stick frying pan

Coating types: PTFE vs ceramic vs hybrid

In order to make them non-stick, frying pans need to have a special coating applied, which could either be ceramic, PTFE or a hybrid, to prevent food from sticking to the surface. Though some pans have three layers, others just have one layer of non-coating. How long this non-stick coating will last is very much dependent on how you care for the pan and how often it's used.

PTFE (polytetrafluoroethylene), commonly known as Teflon, is what makes the surface slippery when it’s not ceramic. PFOA, an acid which is used when making PTFE, has been linked to cancer, which deters many consumers, although it should be noted that the level of exposure is small. However, in response to consumer concerns, many PTFE pans are now PFOA-free. Simply put, PFOA-free means that the coating is free from the chemical PFOA, or perfluorooctanoic acid.

The long-term impacts of this chemical are not fully known, but lab tests have shown a connection with higher cholesterol as well as impacts on growth, reproduction and liver function. So most consumers prefer to avoid this if possible.

You may also see the term PFAS when looking to buy a pan. PFAS have been dubbed ‘forever chemicals' because they don’t break down easily in either the environment or in the human body. As a result, they accumulate over time, giving cause for concern.

PFAS-based coatings also release toxic fumes when overheated, and are still used in some cookware on the market today. However, today’s PTFE non-stick pans have been found safe to use under normal conditions. The key is to know how to use the pans correctly, as the potential dangers lie with sustained use over 260ºC. At these extremely high temperatures, the coatings can degrade, causing chemicals to be released.

Ceramic non-stick pans are PFOA- and PTFE-free. These are made by coating an aluminium or stainless steel pan with a ceramic layer to prevent food from sticking. Ceramic pans are often more heat-resistant than Teflon, but they have a reputation for shorter lifespans than PTFE pans and for the non-stick coating wearing off within a few years.

A hybrid non-stick coating combines traditional non-stick, PTFE/ceramic, with raised, laser-etched stainless steel ridges, which are often in a hexagonal or honeycomb-like formation. This design allows for high-heat searing on the metal ‘peaks’ while the non-stick ‘valleys’ provide easy food release and make cleaning easier.

The hexagonal steel grid protects the non-stick coating, allowing the use of metal utensils without scratching the surface. Although they are more durable and can withstand higher temperatures than ceramic/PTFE pans, hybrid pans can be slightly less slippery when cooking delicate ingredients, like eggs.

Material: aluminium, stainless steel base, hard-anodised

Most non-stick frying pans tend to be made from one of three materials – aluminium, stainless steel or hard-anodised metal. Aluminium has the benefit of being lightweight, which is important in larger pans. It has good heat conductivity and is usually budget-friendly. However, aluminium can warp and can be easily scratched. It can also react to acidic food, such as tomatoes.

Stainless steel, on the other hand, produces weighty pans which are very durable, and it doesn’t react to any type of food. But cheap stainless steel pans can transfer heat unevenly, giving disappointing cooking results.

Hard-anodised non-stick pans offer a good balance of durability, even heating, and easy food release, but their non-stick coatings can wear down through prolonged use; they often need hand washing and don’t work well at very high oven temperatures. They can also be heavier and more expensive than basic non-stick options. The process of anodising changes the surface of metal, such as aluminium, by adding a layer of oxidation. The anodised layer is strong, non-porous, and ideal for cooking ingredients at different temperatures.

Hob compatibility (gas, electric, induction)

If you are investing in a pan that you'll be using for years, it’s important to ensure it is suitable for every hob type, not just the one you have now, so that it will still be compatible should you move house or change your hob. Induction hobs only work with certain metals, so plan and choose a pan that is induction-friendly, in case you get an induction hob in the future.

Pan depth and diameter for different cooking tasks

When it comes to deciding what size pan to buy, take into account your daily cooking routine. If you’re cooking for a large family or like cooking stir fries, curries or stews, consider opting for a pan with a larger diameter and high sides. A large, heavy pan is also ideal for searing meat, like steak or chops.

However, if you have a smaller household, or just need a pan for frying eggs, making omelettes or flipping pancakes, then a smaller, lighter design might better suit your needs. Most frying pans come in a range of diameters, from 20cm up to 30cm, but take into account how high the sides are, if you plan to use the pan for making sauces or stir fries, for example, to avoid overspill when cooking.

Handle ergonomics and heat resistance

As well as taking into consideration the size and composition of your non-stick frying pan, it’s also important to choose one with an ergonomic, heat-resistant handle. While comfort is a plus, true ergonomic design for pans prioritises function and safety, helping you to maintain control when the pan is hot, heavy, or slippery.

A well-designed handle will balance the pan's weight, giving you maximum control over the pan and its contents when cooking. Handles with angles or stabilising ridges provide a better grip, preventing the frying pan from twisting or slipping in your hand, which is safer than smooth, round handles, which can easily slide in the hand, especially when the pan is full.

Textured or rubberised (TPE) coatings can improve grip and control, especially when frying with oil, where they could become greasy. Handles which are curved or contoured will fit better in the natural hand shape, thus reducing strain, especially when they are full. If you plan to cook on high temperature settings or intend to use your frying pan in the oven, then it’s essential you choose one with a heatproof handle.

Silicone is a good option, as it stays cool, while all-metal handles are oven-safe but can get very hot, so you’ll need to use oven gloves when handling them. For larger pans, or ones made from heavier material, choose one with a helper handle attached to the opposite side of the pan, which will provide leverage and a second point to hold for safer lifting.

Oven and dishwasher safety

For maximum flexibility, choose a frying pan that is also oven safe, allowing you to cook meat through after searing on the hob, or for browning food for a golden finish. The temperatures that non-stick pans can withstand vary, from 180ºC up to 260ºC, so always check the packaging before purchase to ensure you buy one that is fit for purpose. Be aware that the lid may not be able to withstand the same temperature as the body of the pan, and that not all non-stick frying pans are oven-compatible.

When it comes to cleaning your pans after cooking, many state that they are dishwasher safe, but be aware that larger pans won't necessarily fit into smaller dishwashers! And, while they might be dishwasher safe, it is always preferable to wash non-stick pans by hand, using warm soapy water, to help prolong the life of the non-stick surface.

Signs of wear and when to replace a non-stick pan

Although most non-stick pans are built to last, they may eventually show signs of wear and tear over time, especially if you don’t look after them properly. Using metal utensils or abrasive cleaners will eventually scratch and damage the non-stick coating of your pan, making it less efficient.

If the pan has been subject to sudden temperature changes, for example, putting a hot pan in cold water, you might find that the metal buckles, which means that the pan’s base will no longer have good contact with the surface of your hob, which will result in uneven cooking. If either of these things occurs, then you know it’s time to buy a new pan.

Care and maintenance guide for non-stick frying pans

Although many non-stick frying pans purport to be dishwasher-safe, most dishwashers use very hot water and strong detergents, which may damage the coating over time. So, to prolong the life of your pan, it is best to wash it by hand if you can. When doing so, avoid using abrasive cleaners and scourers to prevent the surface from getting scratched.

Always make sure the pan is thoroughly dry before putting it back in the cupboard.

Steven Carter-Bailey, food expert at ProCook, says “No matter the material or coating, a pan will last significantly longer with a few simple habits. Avoid putting pans in the dishwasher, as harsh detergents and high heat can degrade both coatings and metal over time. For pans with a coating, cook on a low to medium heat and avoid using metal utensils to avoid damage to the surface. These small changes protect performance and ensure more even cooking for years to come.”

Avoiding high heat to protect the coating

It’s important to avoid high heat when cooking with your non-stick pan, as high temperatures can impact the effectiveness of a non-stick coating. So, whenever possible, stick to cooking using low or medium heat levels.

Brian Hennessy, head chef at The Landmark London, explains,” To keep the long life of a non-stick pan, you need to allow it to cool naturally. Going from too hot to too cold will make the metal contract, thus removing the non-stick coating."

Best practices for storage

You may never have given much thought to how you store your pans, but it can harm them if not done carefully. To avoid scratching the non-stick coating, place a protective layer, like a pan protector, between non-stick pans when stacking or nesting them in your cupboard.

If you don’t want to buy a purpose-designed pan protector, you can use a clean tea towel, a fabric napkin, a paper plate, or even a couple of sheets of paper towel in between your pans.

Recommended utensils

While some non-stick frying pan manufacturers claim that their pans are safe to be used with metal utensils, it is best to avoid them if you can, as metal utensils with sharp edges can scratch the non-stick coating, shortening its lifespan. Whenever possible, it’s better to use softer tools, like silicone or plastic spatulas and wooden spoons.

Non-stick frying pans FAQs

Over the course of our tests, we found that ceramic pans gave the best results when dry frying eggs. They cooked quickly and evenly, and the eggs released from the non-stick surface and slid around much more smoothly than they did in many of the PTFE or hybrid pans.

,

Ceramic non-stick pans are usually considered a healthier, more eco-friendly choice as they are free from PFAS/PTFE, and they are ideal when cooking delicate food. Meanwhile, PTFE (Teflon) pans frequently offer superior durability and maintain their non-stick glide-ability over time, but they will eventually degrade. To prevent this, you need to care for them correctly by cooking on a low heat and avoiding metal utensils.

Ceramic's advantage is that it’s a non-toxic, mineral-based coating; it can be brittle and lose non-stick quality faster; and although PTFE is tougher, its manufacturing often involves ‘forever chemicals’ which many consumers wish to avoid.

,

Some non-stick pans can be used in the oven, but not all are suitable. Always check the packaging before you buy and stick to the recommended maximum temperatures. When taking the pan out of the oven, avoid sudden temperature changes. Allow your pan to cool down before washing, as plunging a hot pan into cold water may cause the pan to warp and buckle.

,

Although some manufacturer’s state their pans are dishwasher safe, it's always best to wash a non-stick pan by hand, using warm soapy water. Avoid using abrasive scourers to protect the non-stick surface from scratches, and dry the pan thoroughly before putting it away after use.

,

Many non-stick frying pans are now designed for use on induction hobs, but not all are suitable. Always check the packaging before you buy, to make sure your chosen pan will work with your induction hob.

Cesar Fernandez, culinary expert at Miele GB, explains 'When buying non-stick pans, quality and design are key. If you use an induction hob, look for pans with a full-surface induction base, which helps ensure even heat distribution and more precise cooking results. During cooking, allow pans to heat gradually on a medium setting rather than using maximum heat straight away.”

,

A good non-stick pan should last you for years, but its longevity relies on you looking after it correctly by avoiding scratching the surface, following the manufacturer’s washing instructions and cooking at low to medium temperatures.

,

While some non-stick pans, especially hybrid designs, claim they are suitable for use with metal utensils, it is always advisable to use wood, plastic or silicone utensils to help avoid scratching and prolong the life of your pan’s non-stick coating.
